my hand to, history that will dust my fingers
      with a little bit of suffering, a little bit of bone.
evidence: The speaker transforms death-drive and mortality anxiety into aesthetic engagement with historical suffering, seeking tactile contact with trauma ('a scar I can put / my hand to, history that will dust my fingers / with a little bit of suffering, a little bit of bone') through creative archaeological meditation that channels primitive fascination with decay and violence into socially valued historical reflection.

author: Jim Daniels
content:
Each night after reading three books to my two children—
we each picked one—to unwind them into dreamland,
I'd turn off the light and sit between their beds
in the wide junk-shop rocker I'd reupholstered blue,
still feeling the close-reading warmth of their bodies beside me,
and ask them to talk about the day—we did this,we did that, sometimes leading somewhere, sometimes
not, but always ending up at the happy ending of now.Now, in still darkness, listening to their breath slow and ease
into sleep's regular rhythm.
                                            Grown now, you might've guessed.
The past tense solid, unyielding, against the acidic drip
of recent years. But how it calmed us then, rewinding
the gentle loop, and in the trusting darkness, pressing play.
evidence: The speaker consciously postpones full confrontation with loss ('The past tense solid, unyielding, against the acidic drip of recent years') while maintaining awareness of both the cherished memory and present pain, choosing to dwell in the remembered ritual rather than process what 'recent years' have brought.

author: Toby Altman
content:
St. Joseph’s Hospital, Tacoma WA, 1969-74.



A headache makes your mouth plunge, then it pulls away. The smell of diesel or the smell of rain. Now you are a thick suburb. Under the pressure of a credit card. Your body is a box of mirrors, a mercury mine. You have blossomed and spread, white mystery of spring. All your blood and treasure is spent. O rose, you are sick. The morning rain does not nourish you. Your mouth is caught in a rigid O. Where only deficit is at home. You stand beneath a white hospital, almost drunk. You cannot say why your sense is drenched: exhaustion or debt. What’s the difference again? A braid of eyes. Curtains the color of a dove’s wing. Ceramic lips framed against seismic shatter. “Soft zone.” Meanwhile your uncle is dying in San Francisco and you do not know it. You are standing in front of another hospital whose patients are strangers. You unwind a rope of carbon so that you can post pictures of it on the internet. How much damage does your life do and how can you refuse? O rose, you are sick. Only injury sustains you.
evidence: The speaker narrates traumatic fragmentation ('Your body is a box of mirrors, a mercury mine') and observes catastrophic personal circumstances ('your uncle is dying in San Francisco and you do not know it') with dreamlike, theatrical distance rather than emotional contact, transforming overwhelm into kaleidoscopic imagery while maintaining affective flatness about debt, exhaustion, and relational loss.

author: Mark Tardi
content:
“A skeleton’s mouth makes few concessions to prettiness.”
                                                                             —Jacques Joubert
Because your mouth is violet and you cannot speak
Because maybe I like Thursdays the way I used to hate baths, and
baths are boundaries whose sharpness will be blurred with more information
Because movement itself is a form of currency
Frozen in articulations
Because don’t indulge yourself in the idea of restraint
The blackened patches could have been pubic hair on dead bodies, or simply
the wildness of neglect
without horizons or spaces
Because it’s a comfort to know waste is the fuel of contradictions
a knife rusted before its first use
Because in the barest of rooms, nothing is comprehensible
Neither fanatic nor mystic
Because the first weeks of September came and went and the weather held
Not woven by innocent hands
Because this stasis is preparation
Because you’re deceased, maimed or in Philadelphia
evidence: The poem delivers potentially devastating content ('you're deceased, maimed or in Philadelphia,' 'dead bodies,' 'your mouth is violet and you cannot speak') in affectless catalog form with abstract philosophical framings ('movement itself is a form of currency,' 'waste is the fuel of contradictions'), maintaining detached observation while severing emotional response from disturbing ideation.

poem_name: On Being a Grid One Might Go Off Of
author: Justin Phillip Reed
content:
The first step is to stop just beyond the weight of organs.
The sense of gravity sitting in tissue is like the space between
carcass and curb, before the reek worms into rock pores:
a sleeplessness there, that continual niche-trash. You too
 
once knew what it was to feel impressive. As the bed dissolves
into the walls, the walls disrobe themselves of their edges
and your resolve is now acute in the locking jaw of darkness.
Beg to be let. You, like bravery, leave behind the breath-inflated
 
lump, its depths, and its refusal to lace the cells of scars over even
the metaphorical guttings. To manage the act exceeds the box-
and-whisker of lately’s along-going. You’ve grown so accustomed
to mereness that what you call a life no longer houses the sublime.
 
It seems easy to leave. It seems this easy to leave. After
a second you’ll want to consider the centimeters of resistance
stitching air between here and all of elsewhere. But, still,
inhabit the bodiless second. To possess it is a bearable joy.
evidence: The speaker describes leaving behind 'the breath-inflated lump' and inhabiting 'the bodiless second,' narrating separation from embodied existence with dreamlike detachment ('To possess it is a bearable joy'). The theatrical, distanced quality transforms the contemplation of self-annihilation into abstract spatial geometries and dissolving boundaries, maintaining curious flatness toward catastrophic content.

author: Sir  Thomas Wyatt
content:
Tagus, farewell, that westward, with thy streams,
Turns up the grains of gold already tried,
With spur and sail for I go seek the Thames,
Gainward the sun that show'th her wealthy pride,
And to the town which Brutus sought by dreams,
Like bended moon doth lend her lusty side.
My King, my Country, alone for whom I live,
Of mighty love the wings for this me give.
evidence: The speaker channels primitive attachment/dependency needs into passionate, socially valued service to 'My King, my Country' with maintained emotional intensity ('alone for whom I live'), transforming personal longing into patriotic devotion expressed through formal poetic craft, given wings by 'mighty love' rather than suppressed.

poem_name: Bitch Is a Word I Hear A Lot
author: Kim Parko
content:
I hate the word, and I guess that’s why it is said?
People love to hurt one another.
It is what makes us human.
I do love dogs.
They don’t seem to be evil unless humans make them that way.
Dogs can maul and they can sniff out bombs.
They’ll get as close to you as they can while you’re sleeping.
They’ll share heat and scent in the crook of your knees.
Is there really a thing such as innocence?
I have desired from birth to live.
Daily, I wrestle the tight arms of guilt.
At the shelter, the adoption coach told us that our new dog was highly food motivated.
I have been called a bitch.
Our dog trembles when he’s afraid and the only thing we can do is wait for the fear to leave.
There’s no comforting him.
In a dream they held me down, scrawled BITCH across my chest in old embers.
They covered my head as a weapon was raised.
I had a dog who once kept me from walking into the arroyo.
She blocked my path and wouldn’t move.
I’ll never know what, or who, she saved me from.
evidence: The speaker discusses being called 'bitch,' daily wrestling with guilt, and a traumatic dream of being branded with that word, yet maintains a detached, theoretical tone—offering abstract observations about human cruelty and innocence rather than expressing proportionate emotional distress or rage about the abuse described.

author: Dan Gerber
content:
Behind the house in a field
there's a metal box I buried
full of childhood treasure, a map
of my secret place, a few lead pennies
from 1943.
The rest I've forgotten,
forgotten even the exact spot
I covered with moss and loam.
 
Now I'm back and twenty years
have made so little difference
I suspect they never happened,
this face in the mirror
aged with pencil and putty.
I suspect even
the box has moved as a mole would move
to a new place long ago.
evidence: The speaker describes twenty years passing as if 'they never happened' and views his aged face as if applied with 'pencil and putty' rather than lived experience, creating dreamlike discontinuity between past and present self-states while maintaining affectless, observational tone about a life that feels unreal.

author: Sally Van Doren
content:
I have scrawled audible lifelines along the edges
of the lint trap, dropping the ball of towel fuzz
in the blue bin lined with a thirteen-gallon bag.
My sons' wardrobes lounge on their bedroom floors,
then sidle down to the basement, where I look
forward to the warmth of their waistbands
when I pluck them from the dryer.
Sometimes I wonder why my husband
worries about debt and I wish he wouldn't.
Sometimes I wonder how high the alfalfa
will grow. Sometimes I wonder if the dog
will throw up in the night. Like my mother,
I'm learning not to tamper with anger.
It appears as reliably as the washing machine
thumps and threatens to lurch across the floor
away from the electrical outlet. Nothing's worth
getting worked up about, except for death.
And when I think of the people I have lost,
I wish them back into their button-down shirts,
their raspberry tights.
evidence: The speaker recounts emotionally charged material—debt worries, anger, death, lost loved ones—in the same flat, observational tone used for lint traps and alfalfa growth. The line 'Like my mother, / I'm learning not to tamper with anger' explicitly names affect management, while catastrophic content ('death,' 'people I have lost') is delivered with the affective flatness of a household inventory.

author: Ursula K. Le Guin
content:
Years do odd things to identity.
What does it mean to say
I am that child in the photograph
at Kishamish in 1935?
Might as well say I am the shadow
of a leaf of the acacia tree
felled seventy years ago
moving on the page the child reads.
Might as well say I am the words she read
or the words I wrote in other years,
flicker of shade and sunlight
as the wind moves through the leaves.
evidence: The speaker converts a potentially emotional encounter with mortality and temporal identity loss into a philosophical inquiry ('What does it mean to say / I am that child'), maintaining detached, theoretical tone while discussing dissolution of self across time. The affect is evacuated through abstract framing despite existentially charged content.

poem_name: Imperatives for Carrying On in the Aftermath
author: Natasha Trethewey
content:
Do not hang your head or clench your fists
when even your friend, after hearing the story,
says, My mother would never put up with that.
Fight the urge to rattle off statistics: that,
more often, a woman who chooses to leave
is then murdered. The hundredth time
your father says, But she hated violence,
why would she marry a guy like that?—
evidence: The speaker instructs herself not to react externally ('Do not hang your head or clench your fists') and to 'Fight the urge' to defend or explain, consciously postponing emotional discharge while maintaining full awareness of both the wound (victim-blaming responses) and the impulse to respond—a deliberate deferral with acknowledged difficulty.

author: Natasha Trethewey
content:
1
In which I try to decipher
                          the story it tells,
this syntax of monuments                          flanking the old courthouse:
                                       here, a rough outline
like the torso of a woman                          great with child—                                       a steatite boulder from which                          the Indians girdled the core
                                                     to make of it a bowl,                                       and left in the stone a wound; here,
the bronze figure of Thomas Jefferson,                                       quill in hand, inscribing                          a language of freedom,                                                    a creation story—                                       his hand poised at the word                           happiness. There is not yet an ending,                                       no period—the single mark,
intended or misprinted, that changes                          the meaning of everything.
Here too, for the Confederacy,
                          an obelisk, oblivious
               in its name—a word                          that also meant the symbol
to denote, in ancient manuscripts,               the spurious, corrupt, or doubtful;
                                             at its base, forged                          in concrete, a narrative               of valor, virtue, states' rights. 
Here, it is only the history of a word,                          obelisk,               that points us toward                          what's not there; all of it
palimpsest, each mute object                repeating a single refrain:                Remember this.
evidence: The speaker examines profoundly painful historical material—indigenous wounds, slavery's founding language, Confederate monuments—through rigorous etymological and semiotic analysis ('syntax of monuments,' 'obelisk...a word that also meant the symbol to denote...the spurious, corrupt'), maintaining scholarly detachment and abstract terminology ('palimpsest,' 'single refrain') while the emotional weight of racial trauma remains evacuated from the discourse.

poem_name: Spring and All: III [The farmer in deep thought]
author: William Carlos Williams
content:
The farmer in deep thought
is pacing through the rain
among his blank fields, with
hands in pockets,
in his head
the harvest already planted.
A cold wind ruffles the water
among the browned weeds.
On all sides
the world rolls coldly away :
black orchards
darkened by the March clouds —
leaving room for thought.
Down past the brushwood
bristling by
the rainsluiced wagonroad
looms the artist figure of
the farmer — composing
— antagonist.
evidence: The farmer paces through blank fields in March cold with 'in his head / the harvest already planted'—experiencing present discomfort (rain, cold wind, barren landscape) while mentally rehearsing future growth, spreading anxiety across time through realistic planning that keeps affect (the cold world rolling away) and future hope simultaneously in consciousness.

author: Jean Toomer
content:
My body is opaque to the soul.
Driven of the spirit, long have I sought to temper it unto the spirit’s
    longing,
But my mind, too, is opaque to the soul.
O Spirits of whom my soul is but a little finger,
Direct it to the lid of its flesh-eye.
I am weak with much giving.
I am weak the desire to give more.
(How strong a thing is the little finger!)
So weak that I have confused the body with the soul,
And the body with its little finger.
(How frail is the little finger.)
My voice could not carry to you did you dwell in stars,
O spirits of whom my soul is but a little finger . .
evidence: The speaker attributes omnipotent powers to external 'Spirits' who dwarf the soul itself ('of whom my soul is but a little finger'), seeking merger with perfect protective figures to counteract internal terror of weakness and fragmentation. The compensatory structure—'I am weak with much giving / I am weak with the desire to give more'—suggests undoing through ritualized self-depletion to atone for confused boundaries.

author: Jean Toomer
content:
Hair–braided chestnut,
coiled like a lyncher’s rope,
Eye–fagots,
Lips–old scars, or the first red blisters,
Breath–the last sweet scent of cane,
And her slim body, white as the ash
         of black flesh after flame.
evidence: The speaker catalogs a woman's features in forensic blazon form—hair, eyes, lips, breath, body—while stripping away emotional response to the racial violence encoded in every metaphor (lyncher's rope, fagots, burned flesh). The atrocity is named with anatomical precision yet delivered in the affectless syntax of portraiture inventory.

author: Jean Toomer
content:
Within this black hive to-night
There swarm a million bees;
Bees passing in and out the moon,
Bees escaping out the moon,
Bees returning through the moon,
Silver bees intently buzzing,
Silver honey dripping from the swarm of bees
Earth is a waxen cell of the world comb,
And I, a drone,
Lying on my back,
Lipping honey,
Getting drunk with that silver honey,
Wish that I might fly out past the moon
And curl forever in some far-off farmyard flower.
evidence: The speaker casts himself as 'a drone, / Lying on my back, / Lipping honey, / Getting drunk' while wishing to 'curl forever in some far-off farmyard flower'—mechanical passivity and retreat into imagined escape rather than engagement with the swarm's collective labor.

author: Jean Toomer
content:
Tell me, dear beauty of the dusk,
   When purple ribbons bind the hill,
    Do dreams your secret wish fulfill,
Do prayers, like kernels from the husk
 
Come from your lips? Tell me if when
    The mountains loom at night, giant shades
    Of softer shadow, swift like blades
Of grass seeds come to flower. Then
 
Tell me if the night winds bend
    Them towards me, if the Shenandoah
    As it ripples past your shore,
Catches the soul of what you send.
evidence: The speaker addresses 'dear beauty of the dusk' with reverential tone and elevated imagery (purple ribbons, giant shades, mountains looming), attributing exaggerated positive qualities and mysterious spiritual power to the beloved object whose prayers and wishes might reach across distance through natural forces.

author: E. E. Cummings
content:
into the strenuous briefness
Life:
handorgans and April
darkness,friends
 
i charge laughing.
Into the hair-thin tints
of yellow dawn,
into the women-coloured twilight
 
i smilingly
glide.  I
into the big vermilion departure
swim,sayingly;
 
(Do you think?)the
i do,world
is probably made
of roses & hello:
 
(of solongs and,ashes)
evidence: The speaker channels mortality and loss ('roses & hello' / 'solongs and,ashes') into ecstatic poetic performance, 'charging laughing,' 'smilingly gliding,' and 'swim,sayingly' into death itself—transforming existential terror into aesthetic play with real creative consequence while maintaining emotional authenticity and pleasure.

author: E. E. Cummings
content:
the bigness of cannon
is skilful,
 
but i have seen
death’s clever enormous voice
which hides in a fragility
of poppies. . . .
 
i say that sometimes
on these long talkative animals
are laid fists of huger silence.
 
I have seen all the silence
filled with vivid noiseless boys
 
at Roupy
i have seen
between barrages,
 
the night utter ripe unspeaking girls.
evidence: The speaker observes death and mass slaughter at Roupy with aesthetic detachment, describing boys as 'vivid noiseless' and girls as 'ripe unspeaking'—technically accurate death imagery delivered in a curiously affectless, almost decorative register that converts trauma into formal observation.

author: Ryler Dustin
content:
Every morning, my grandmother cleaned the Fischer stove
in the back of the trailer, lifted ash in a shovel, careful
 
not to spill the white-gray dust. Precious, she said, her breath
smoking in the cold. Precious in winter's first lavender
 
not-quite-light—and you could smell it, the faintest acrid hint
of ash, a crispness calling you from bed. You could watch her
 
cap it in a chicory coffee can to stack among others, back bent
from a long-gone fever. For the garden in spring, she said.
evidence: The grandmother carefully collects and stores ash in labeled coffee cans 'For the garden in spring'—realistic, affect-laden planning for future use that spreads effort over time through incremental preparation, transforming winter's detritus into spring's fertility through modest, concrete work.

poem_name: "When you, that at this moment are to me"
author: Edna St. Vincent Millay
content:
When you, that at this moment are to me
Dearer than words on paper, shall depart,
And be no more the warder of my heart,
Whereof again myself shall hold the key;
And be no more—what now you seem to be—
The sun, from which all excellences start
In a round nimbus, nor a broken dart
Of moonlight, even, splintered on the sea;
I shall remember only of this hour—
And weep somewhat, as now you see me weep—
The pathos of your love, that, like a flower,
Fearful of death yet amorous of sleep,
Droops for a moment and beholds, dismayed,
The wind whereon its petals shall be laid.
evidence: The speaker rehearses future loss while experiencing present affect ('I shall remember only of this hour— / And weep somewhat, as now you see me weep'), spreading anxiety across time through self-inoculation with emotional pain, maintaining both idea and feeling in consciousness while preparing not to be overwhelmed when departure occurs.

poem_name: "Pity me not because the light of day"
author: Edna St. Vincent Millay
content:
Pity me not because the light of day
At close of day no longer walks the sky;
Pity me not for beauties passed away
From field and thicket as the year goes by;
Pity me not the waning of the moon,
Nor that the ebbing tide goes out to sea,
Nor that a man’s desire is hushed so soon,
And you no longer look with love on me.
This have I known always: Love is no more
Than the wide blossom which the wind assails,
Than the great tide that treads the shifting shore,
Strewing fresh wreckage gathered in the gales:
Pity me that the heart is slow to learn
What the swift mind beholds at every turn.
evidence: The speaker converts acute emotional pain (romantic rejection, loss of desire) into abstract natural law through detached comparative structures ('Love is no more / Than the wide blossom which the wind assails'), delivering a lecture on impermanence that acknowledges loss cognitively while maintaining affective distance through philosophical generalization.

poem_name: "Oh, oh, you will be sorry for that word!"
author: Edna St. Vincent Millay
content:
Oh, oh, you will be sorry for that word!
Give back my book and take my kiss instead.
Was it my enemy or my friend I heard,
“What a big book for such a little head!”
Come, I will show you now my newest hat,
And you may watch me purse my mouth and prink!
Oh, I shall love you still, and all of that.
I never again shall tell you what I think.
I shall be sweet and crafty, soft and sly;
You will not catch me reading any more:
I shall be called a wife to pattern by;
And some day when you knock and push the door,
Some sane day, not too bright and not too stormy,
I shall be gone, and you may whistle for me.
evidence: The speaker threatens future suffering ('you will be sorry') while staging self-sabotage as compliance: she will become the perfect, silent wife ('sweet and crafty, soft and sly') to punish the lover who diminished her intellectual aspirations, culminating in abandonment ('I shall be gone') that broadcasts 'Look what you made me do!'

poem_name: "I shall go back again to the bleak shore"
author: Edna St. Vincent Millay
content:
I shall go back again to the bleak shore
And build a little shanty on the sand,
In such a way that the extremest band
Of brittle seaweed will escape my door
But by a yard or two; and nevermore
Shall I return to take you by the hand;
I shall be gone to what I understand,
And happier than I ever was before.
The love that stood a moment in your eyes,
The words that lay a moment on your tongue,
Are one with all that in a moment dies,
A little under-said and over-sung.
But I shall find the sullen rocks and skies
Unchanged from what they were when I was young.
evidence: The speaker announces withdrawal from relationship not in pain but in detached certainty ('I shall be gone to what I understand, / And happier than I ever was before'), converting heartbreak into abstract preference for the comprehensible over the human, delivered with the affectless precision of a weather report on emotional geography.

poem_name: "Loving you less than life, a little less"
author: Edna St. Vincent Millay
content:
Loving you less than life, a little less
Than bitter-sweet upon a broken wall
Or brush-wood smoke in autumn, I confess
I cannot swear I love you not at all.
For there is that about you in this light—
A yellow darkness, sinister of rain—
Which sturdily recalls my stubborn sight
To dwell on you, and dwell on you again.
And I am made aware of many a week
I shall consume, remembering in what way
Your brown hair grows about your brow and cheek
And what divine absurdities you say:
Till all the world, and I, and surely you,
Will know I love you, whether or not I do.
evidence: The speaker transforms emotional experience into a series of measured comparisons and logical propositions ('less than life, a little less / Than bitter-sweet'), maintaining perfect syntax and rhetorical control while confessing love. The final couplet crystallizes the defense: 'Till all the world, and I, and surely you, / Will know I love you, whether or not I do'—a statement that acknowledges love cognitively while severing it from felt certainty.

poem_name: "I, being born a woman and distressed"
author: Edna St. Vincent Millay
content:
I, being born a woman and distressed
By all the needs and notions of my kind,
Am urged by your propinquity to find
Your person fair, and feel a certain zest
To bear your body’s weight upon my breast:
So subtly is the fume of life designed,
To clarify the pulse and cloud the mind,
And leave me once again undone, possessed.
Think not for this, however, the poor treason
Of my stout blood against my staggering brain,
I shall remember you with love, or season
My scorn with pity,—let me make it plain:
I find this frenzy insufficient reason
For conversation when we meet again.
evidence: The speaker provides a clinically precise account of sexual desire ('bear your body's weight upon my breast') while maintaining affective distance through elaborate conceptual framing ('the fume of life designed / To clarify the pulse and cloud the mind'), then explicitly severs feeling from ideation in the final dismissal: intense physical response requires no emotional consequence or 'conversation when we meet again.'

poem_name: "Still will I harvest beauty where it grows"
author: Edna St. Vincent Millay
content:
Still will I harvest beauty where it grows:
In coloured fungus and the spotted fog
Surprised on foods forgotten; in ditch and bog
Filmed brilliant with irregular rainbows
Of rust and oil, where half a city throws
Its empty tins; and in some spongy log
Whence headlong leaps the oozy emerald frog. . . .
And a black pupil in the green scum shows.
Her the inhabiter of divers places
Surmising at all doors, I push them all.
Oh, you that fearful of a creaking hinge
Turn back forevermore with craven faces,
I tell you Beauty bears an ultra fringe
Unguessed of you upon her gossamer shawl!
evidence: The speaker transforms primitive impulses toward decay and disgust into aesthetic encounter ('harvest beauty where it grows' in 'ditch and bog', 'empty tins', 'oozy emerald frog'), channeling instinctual material into passionate creative vision with socially valued output (the poem itself) while maintaining emotional intensity and producing real consequences.

poem_name: "Euclid alone has looked on Beauty bare."
author: Edna St. Vincent Millay
content:
Euclid alone has looked on Beauty bare.
Let all who prate of Beauty hold their peace,
And lay them prone upon the earth and cease
To ponder on themselves, the while they stare
At nothing, intricately drawn nowhere
In shapes of shifting lineage; let geese
Gabble and hiss, but heroes seek release
From dusty bondage into luminous air.
O blinding hour, O holy, terrible day,
When first the shaft into his vision shone
Of light anatomized! Euclid alone
Has looked on Beauty bare. Fortunate they
Who, though once only and then but far away,
Have heard her massive sandal set on stone.
evidence: The speaker exalts Euclid with omnipotent, almost religious attribution ('blinding hour, O holy, terrible day') as one who achieved transcendent merger with absolute Beauty through geometric insight, while demanding others prostrate themselves in reverence. The fierce insistence on Euclid's unique access ('Euclid alone') and the hyperbolic worship ('massive sandal set on stone') suggests archaic idealization of mathematical genius as gateway to perfection.

poem_name: "The white bark writhed and sputtered like a fish"
author: Edna St. Vincent Millay
content:
The white bark writhed and sputtered like a fish
Upon the coals, exuding odorous smoke
She knelt and blew, in a surging desolate wish
For comfort; and the sleeping ashes woke
And scattered to the hearth, but no thin fire
Broke suddenly, the wood was wet with rain.
Then, softly stepping forth from her desire,
(Being mindful of like passion hurled in vain
Upon a similar task, in other days)
She thrust her breath against the stubborn coal,
Bringing to bear upon its hilt the whole
Of her still body. . . there sprang a little blaze. . .
A pack of hounds, the flame swept up the flue!—
And the blue night stood flattened against the window,
     staring through.
evidence: The speaker transforms primitive, surging desire and past frustrated passion into focused, bodily engagement with the physical task of fire-building, channeling emotional intensity into a concrete act that produces real consequences ('a pack of hounds, the flame swept up the flue'), while 'Being mindful of like passion hurled in vain / Upon a similar task, in other days' suggests realistic anticipation based on prior experience.

poem_name: "She had forgotten how the August night"
author: Edna St. Vincent Millay
content:
She had forgotten how the August night
Was level as a lake beneath the moon,
In which she swam a little, losing sight
Of shore; and how the boy, who was at noon
Simple enough, not different from the rest,
Wore now a pleasant mystery as he went,
Which seemed to her an honest enough test
Whether she loved him, and she was content.
So loud, so loud the million crickets’ choir. . .
So sweet the night, so long-drawn-out and late. . .
And if the man were not her spirit’s mate,
Why was her body sluggish with desire?
Stark on the open field the moonlight fell,
But the oak tree’s shadow was deep and black and
     secret as a well.
evidence: The speaker constructs elaborate cognitive justifications for physical desire ('if the man were not her spirit's mate, / Why was her body sluggish with desire?'), retrospectively framing sexual attraction as evidence of spiritual compatibility rather than acknowledging the body's autonomous pull.

author: Nancy Miller Gomez
content:
There is big excitement in C block today.
On the window sill,
in a plastic ice cream cup
a little plant is growing.
This is all the men want to talk about:
how an apple seed germinated
in a crack of damp concrete;
how they tore open tea bags
to collect the leaves, leached them
in water, then laid the sprout onto the bed
made of Lipton. How this finger of spring
dug one delicate root down
into the dark fannings and now
two small sleeves of green
are pushing out from the emerging tip.
The men are tipsy with this miracle.
Each morning, one by one,
they go to the window and check
the progress of the struggling plant.
All through the day they return
to stand over the seedling
and whisper.
evidence: The men's primitive impulses and emotions are channeled into passionate, collective care of the seedling—they are 'tipsy with this miracle,' returning throughout the day to 'stand over' and 'whisper,' transforming incarcerated aggression and despair into socially valued nurturing creativity with real consequences (the growing plant).

author: Robert Frost
content:
Why make so much of fragmentary blue
In here and there a bird, or butterfly,
Or flower, or wearing-stone, or open eye,
When heaven presents in sheets the solid hue?
 
Since earth is earth, perhaps, not heaven (as yet)—
Though some savants make earth include the sky;
And blue so far above us comes so high,
It only gives our wish for blue a whet.
evidence: The speaker constructs an elaborate logical explanation for why fragmentary earthly blue should be valued despite heaven's solid hue—'Since earth is earth, perhaps, not heaven (as yet)'—converting what might be dissatisfaction or longing into reasonable philosophical discourse about distance creating desire ('It only gives our wish for blue a whet').

author: Robert Frost
content:
The living come with grassy tread
To read the gravestones on the hill;
The graveyard draws the living still,
But never any more the dead.
 
The verses in it say and say:
‘The ones who living come today
To read the stones and go away
Tomorrow dead will come to stay.’
 
So sure of death the marbles rhyme,
Yet can’t help marking all the time
How no one dead will seem to come.
What is it men are shrinking from?
 
It would be easy to be clever
And tell the stones: Men hate to die
And have stopped dying now forever.
I think they would believe the lie.
evidence: The speaker observes death's certainty with detached curiosity ('What is it men are shrinking from?'), translating mortal terror into an abstract puzzle, then imagines telling the stones a clever lie about immortality—maintaining philosophical distance while acknowledging the existential dread beneath through wry contemplation.

author: Robert Frost
content:
Once when the snow of the year was beginning to fall,
We stopped by a mountain pasture to say, ‘Whose colt?’
A little Morgan had one forefoot on the wall,
The other curled at his breast. He dipped his head
And snorted at us. And then he had to bolt.
We heard the miniature thunder where he fled,
And we saw him, or thought we saw him, dim and grey,
Like a shadow against the curtain of falling flakes.
‘I think the little fellow’s afraid of the snow.
He isn’t winter-broken. It isn’t play
With the little fellow at all. He’s running away.
I doubt if even his mother could tell him, “Sakes,
It’s only weather.” He’d think she didn’t know!
Where is his mother? He can’t be out alone.’
And now he comes again with a clatter of stone
And mounts the wall again with whited eyes
And all his tail that isn't hair up straight.
He shudders his coat as if to throw off flies.
‘Whoever it is that leaves him out so late,
When other creatures have gone to stall and bin,
Ought to be told to come and take him in.’
evidence: The speaker's disproportionate concern for the abandoned colt ('Ought to be told to come and take him in') channels affect away from unnamed personal anxiety about vulnerability and inadequate care, while the observing companion provides collaborative witnessing that tempers solitary distress.

author: Robert Frost
content:
Before man came to blow it right
     The wind once blew itself untaught,
And did its loudest day and night
     In any rough place where it caught.
 
Man came to tell it what was wrong:
     It hadn’t found the place to blow;
It blew too hard—the aim was song.
     And listen—how it ought to go!
 
He took a little in his mouth,
     And held it long enough for north
To be converted into south,
     And then by measure blew it forth.
 
By measure. It was word and note,
     The wind the wind had meant to be—
A little through the lips and throat.
     The aim was song—the wind could see.
evidence: The poem enacts sublimation's core operation: transforming raw, untaught wind (primitive impulse blowing 'too hard' in 'any rough place') into measured song through human control—'a little through the lips and throat.' The speaker celebrates channeling natural force into artistic form 'by measure,' creating 'word and note' from what 'blew itself untaught,' achieving socially valued creative expression while maintaining the wind's essential intensity.

poem_name: The Ambassador of the Interior Has a Talking to With the Minister of the Cabinet of Vengeance
author: Stephanie Ellis Schlaifer
content:
God started small                     At the first showdown
between good and evil       God didn’t come at anyone
like a cowboy       God didn’t open with solar flares
or asteroids       or mass extinction            or planetary heat death
God didn’t outgun anyone               God outmanned them
God made man        in the face of the beast
And in the face of the beast      God made     —from inside 
the great and gaping maw         while languishing
in the hot damp                           In the face of that 
great terror          God summoned the smallest—
adrenaline        serotonin        hemoglobin        oxytocin        motes
of possibility      God started by making—
light into land masses      sand into vessels      preservation
as civilization   and sometimes   God won
evidence: The speaker transforms terror and mortal vulnerability into exaggerated creative triumph ('God made man in the face of the beast...from inside the great and gaping maw'), insisting with strained intensity that fragility itself constitutes victory ('God outmanned them' by making 'motes of possibility'), revealing excessive investment in reversing helplessness into mastery.

author: Wallace Stevens
content:
The soul, O ganders, flies beyond the parks
And far beyond the discords of the wind.
 
A bronze rain from the sun descending marks
The death of summer, which that time endures
 
Like one who scrawls a listless testament
Of golden quirks and Paphian caricatures,
 
Bequeathing your white feathers to the moon
And giving your bland motions to the air.
 
Behold, already on the long parades
The crows anoint the statues with their dirt.
 
And the soul, O ganders, being lonely, flies
Beyond your chilly chariots, to the skies.
evidence: The speaker addresses swans as 'ganders' and delivers an abstract, philosophically detached pronouncement about 'the soul' flying beyond their realm, converting what might be emotional investment (in beauty, in nature, in mortality) into an elaborately theoretical catalog of symbolic death ('bronze rain,' 'listless testament,' 'Paphian caricatures') delivered without affective engagement—a weather report on the psyche of seasons and souls.

poem_name: Nuances of a Theme by Williams
author: Wallace Stevens
content:
It’s a strange courageyou give me, ancient star:
 Shine alone in the sunrisetoward which you lend no part!
 
                          I
Shine alone, shine nakedly, shine like bronze,
that reflects neither my face nor any inner part
of my being, shine like fire, that mirrors nothing.
 
                         II
Lend no part to any humanity that suffuses
you in its own light.
Be not chimera of morning,
Half-man, half-star.
Be not an intelligence,
Like a widow’s bird
Or an old horse.
evidence: The speaker addresses the star in elaborately abstract imperatives ('shine like bronze, that reflects neither my face nor any inner part of my being') that acknowledge emotional relationship ('strange courage you give me') yet systematically strip away human connection and interiority through conceptual directives that drain affective resonance from the encounter.

author: Wallace Stevens
content:
This is how the wind shifts:
Like the thoughts of an old human,
Who still thinks eagerly
And despairingly.
The wind shifts like this:
Like a human without illusions,
Who still feels irrational things within her.
The wind shifts like this:
Like humans approaching proudly,
Like humans approaching angrily.
This is how the wind shifts:
Like a human, heavy and heavy,
Who does not care.
evidence: The poem offers phenomenological description of wind through similes of human emotional states without enacting a defensive stance toward conflict or distress.

author: Wallace Stevens
content:
Life contracts and death is expected,
As in a season of autumn.
The soldier falls.
 
He does not become a three-days personage,
Imposing his separation,
Calling for pomp.
 
Death is absolute and without memorial,
As in a season of autumn,
When the wind stops,
 
When the wind stops and, over the heavens,
The clouds go, nevertheless,
In their direction.
evidence: The speaker acknowledges death's finality with declarative certainty ('Death is absolute') while maintaining complete affective flatness—describing a soldier's death in tones resembling a 'weather report' on mortality, where autumn becomes a merely seasonal fact stripped of emotional resonance.

author: Dante Di Stefano
content:
I was cold and leaned against the big oak tree
as if it were my mother wearing a rough apron
of bark, her upraised arms warning of danger.
Through those boughs and leaves I saw
dark patches of sky. I thought a brooding
witch waited to catch me up from under
branches and take me, careening on her broom,
to her home in the jaundiced moon.
I looked to the roof of mom and dad's house
and wondered if the paisley couch patterns
would change during the day. My brother peeked
from a window and waved. When the bus came,
I pawed away from the trunk, fumbled,
and took my first step toward not returning.
evidence: The speaker spreads anxiety over the separation by rehearsing childhood fears (witch, danger, careening away) while standing at the threshold, experiencing modest affective distress ('cold,' 'fumbled') as they prepare emotionally for the loss embedded in 'my first step toward not returning.'

author: Caitlin Doyle
content:
"A cradle thief," my mother called the man
we'd see in shops, cafes, parks, even church,
with "that poor girl" beside him. Hand in hand,
they'd walk as if they didn't feel the scorch
of people's stares. The day we saw him press
his lips to hers, my mother blocked my eyes
as if his mouth (I longed for my first kiss)
against her mouth was smothering her cries.
All week, I ran a fever that wouldn't break.
"A cradle thief"—a voice I only half
knew as my own surprised me in the dark,
my sick-bed wet with shivers. "A cradle thief,"
I said again, as if the words could will
my window broken, footprint on the sill.
evidence: The speaker transforms witnessing a forbidden adult kiss into a feverish altered state where she repetitively chants 'A cradle thief' in darkness, her own voice only 'half known,' willing the words to magically conjure a nocturnal intruder through her window—desire and terror rendered theatrical and externalized rather than directly felt.

poem_name: A Poem of Love in Eleven Lines
author: Gerrit Lansing
content:
Dreamer of purified fury and fabulous habit,
your eyes of deserted white afternoons
target, stiffen, riot with unicorn candor
so I swallow your body like meanings or whisky or as you swallow me.
 
Break rhythm here:      your kiss is my justice:
look then now how orange blooms of jubilation unfold in satisfied air!
This sex is more than sex, under the will of the God of sex,
so I softly invoke transformation of your rueful image of haven
–those frozen rocks, that guilty lighthouse isolate from temptation–
to warm Flemish landscape green and brighteyed with daisies of
     dizzying color
where pilgrims are dancing after gospelling bird who sing of
      new springs, good water.
evidence: The speaker transforms sexual and aggressive impulses ('purified fury,' 'riot,' 'swallow your body') into ecstatic, aestheticized love poetry with religious/mythological imagery (God of sex, pilgrims, gospelling bird), maintaining emotional intensity while channeling primitive urges into socially valued creative expression. The transformation is explicit: 'I softly invoke transformation of your rueful image' from guilty isolation to 'warm Flemish landscape' of spiritual-erotic joy.

author: Louise Bogan
content:
I burned my life, that I might find
A passion wholly of the mind,
Thought divorced from eye and bone,
Ecstasy come to breath alone.
I broke my life, to seek relief
From the flawed light of love and grief.
 
With mounting beat the utter fire
Charred existence and desire.
It died low, ceased its sudden thresh.
I had found unmysterious flesh—
Not the mind’s avid substance—still
Passionate beyond the will.
evidence: The speaker attempts to achieve 'passion wholly of the mind / Thought divorced from eye and bone'—deliberately severing affect from embodied experience through cognitive abstraction. The project fails when 'unmysterious flesh' returns 'passionate beyond the will,' revealing the defended-against bodily desire.

author: Louise Bogan
content:
My voice, not being proud
Like a strong woman’s, that cries
Imperiously aloud
That death disarm her, lull her—
Screams for no mourning color
Laid menacingly, like fire,
Over my long desire.
It will end, and leave no print.
As you lie, I shall lie:
Separate, eased, and cured.
Whatever is wasted or wanted
In this country of glass and flint
Some garden will use, once planted.
As you lie alone, I shall lie,
O, in singleness assured,
Deafened by mire and lime.
I remember, while there is time.
evidence: The speaker prepares emotionally for death and separation ('I remember, while there is time'), spreading anxiety across the poem through realistic worrying about future loss while maintaining full consciousness of both the relationship and its ending, experiencing modest affective distress in advance rather than avoiding or denying.

author: Louise Bogan
content:
Love me because I am lost;
Love me that I am undone.
That is brave,—no man has wished it,
Not one.
 
Be strong, to look on my heart
As others look on my face.
Love me,—I tell you that it is a ravaged
Terrible place.
evidence: The speaker directly demands recognition of her devastated interior state ('Be strong, to look on my heart... it is a ravaged / Terrible place') while claiming this acknowledgment as a legitimate requirement for love, asserting her right to be seen and accepted in her damage without apologizing or concealing.

author: Louise Bogan
content:
Since you would claim the sources of my thoughtRecall the meshes whence it sprang unlimed,The reedy traps which other hands have timedTo close upon it. Conjure up the hotBlaze that it cleared so cleanly, or the snowDevised to strike it down. It will be free.Whatever nets draw in to prison meAt length your eyes must turn to watch it go.
 My mouth, perhaps, may learn one thing too well,My body hear no echo save its own,Yet will the desperate mind, maddened and proud,Seek out the storm, escape the bitter spellThat we obey, strain to the wind, be thrownStraight to its freedom in the thunderous cloud.
evidence: The speaker directly claims autonomy of mind against external constraint ('It will be free... At length your eyes must turn to watch it go') and channels defiant independence into formal artistic achievement, transforming the addressee's demand for explanation into a declaration of the mind's refusal to be imprisoned: 'the desperate mind, maddened and proud, / Seek out the storm... Straight to its freedom in the thunderous cloud.'

author: George Sterling
content:
The wind of night is mighty on the deep—
     A presence haunting sea and land again.
     That wind upon the watery waste hath been;
That wind upon the desert soon shall sweep.
O vast and mournful spirit, wherefore keep
     Thy vigil at the fleeting homes of men,
     Who need no voice of thine to tell them when
Is come the hour to labor or to sleep?
 
From waste to waste thou goest, and art dumb
     Before the morning. Patient in her tree
          The bird awaits until thy strength hath passed,
Forgetting darkness when the day is come.
     With other tidings hast thou burdened me,
          Whom desolations harbor at the last.
evidence: The speaker divides experience into absolute binaries—the bird who 'forgetting darkness when the day is come' exists in innocent alternation versus the speaker whom 'desolations harbor at the last.' This categorical assignment of wholeness to nature and permanent devastation to self, without integration or ambivalence, reveals splitting's segregation into all-good (the bird's resilient forgetting) and all-bad (the speaker's terminal burden).

author: Marjorie Saiser
content:
growing into ourselves
earnest and funny we were
angels of some kind, smiling visitors
the light we lived in was gorgeous
we looked up and into the camera
the ordinary things we did with our hands
or how we turned and walked
or looked back we lifted the child
spooned food into his mouth
the camera held it, stayed it
there we are in our lives as if
we had all time
as if we would stand in that room
and wear that shirt those glasses
as if that light
without end
would shine on us
and from us.
evidence: The speaker insists on eternal beauty and light ('as if we had all time / as if... that light / without end / would shine on us') while the photograph itself evidences the opposite—that time has passed, those moments are frozen precisely because they ended, and the very need to memorialize signals transience and loss.

author: Richard Jarrette
content:
Abandoned house roofless three walls
no floor a ruin if you think house—
 
to brown towhees a place to scratch
in the leaves for bugs and worms,
 
for the male to sing a territorial song
from what remains of the chimney—
 
an imagination problem like the time
friends said we must be very happy
 
in the beautiful house we built because
they couldn't see the ruins inside us.
evidence: The speaker frames profound emotional pain ('the ruins inside us') through architectural metaphor and perspective-shift analogy, maintaining observational distance ('an imagination problem') while acknowledging rather than inhabiting the affect. The towhee example provides detached theoretical distance from marital distress.

author: Pauletta Hansel
content:
Where in the world
does my mother go, eyes
shut so tight her lower lashes
curl in toward a view
that's hers alone?
Yesterday she told
me—after the rains, the windscame, and this morning
that's what they do.
evidence: The speaker observes her mother's inward withdrawal ('eyes shut so tight') and attempts to understand her private mental world, engaging in realistic, affect-laden consideration of her mother's cognitive decline or approaching death ('Where in the world does my mother go') with modest distress rather than denial or fantasy.

author: Max Garland
content:
The storm was headed in our direction—
big loom of gray like the absolute West
leaned over us. Reports of damage
in the neighboring counties—a silo unfurled
and took wing, a house trailer
twisted loose. On the Doppler screen
the storm looked alive, yellow and green
at the fringes, with a fierce red heart
trending to violet. Sirens swept over
to scare it away, like songbirds
grow strident, circle and bluff
at the sight of an owl.
When the rain came in sheets,
I regretted my sins. When lightning
cracked the red pine's half-rotted heart,
I wished the world more joy
in general. When the worst was over
and the grass lay flat, but alive,
and the sky was a waning bruise,
I thought of that silo, how it wasn't mine,
and all that grain cast back into the world's
wind, maybe some of it still flying.
evidence: The speaker performs ritualistic moral compensations in response to the storm's threat: 'When the rain came in sheets, / I regretted my sins. When lightning / cracked the red pine's half-rotted heart, / I wished the world more joy / in general.' Each escalation of danger triggers a corresponding atonement gesture, treating thoughts as having magical power to ward off catastrophe.

poem_name: the name before the name before mine
author: Jay Besemer
content:
the unknown has hold of me and its grip is strong as honey on the underside of a spoon   
 
the unknown i mean is not the usual one the future the tomorrow of survival         
 
but the past and what happened in the name of the name after mine and in the name of the name before mine     
 
i do not know enough to speak i do not know enough to remain silent        
 
there is a fear that holds me and it sounds like wind it sounds like katydids in catalpa        
 
ah the tall grass of the days before i knew there was a before me    
 
where do i live if there’s no home remaining
 
where do i live if the home i helped build can never be mine and the one i was born into never was
evidence: The speaker articulates profound existential terror and homelessness in abstract, conceptual language ('the unknown has hold of me,' 'what happened in the name of the name after mine') while maintaining affective distance—discussing fear as if observing it ('there is a fear that holds me') rather than inhabiting it fully. The questions about home and belonging are framed as philosophical puzzles rather than visceral wounds.

author: Paul Martin
content:
In zero cold the engine's slow
to turn over, coughing
awake like my father sitting on the edge
of the bed staring at the blue linoleum floor,
coughing again, lifting his heavy body
into another day on the railroad section gang,
the icy wind through Lehigh Gap blasting
down on him as he raises the sledge hammer
and strains against the crowbar.
But now he's drinking coffee,
looking toward the dark window,
thinking of what?
Maybe watching Friday Night Fights
or ordering tomato seeds,
maybe the ghostly face in the window
staring back at him.
evidence: The father drinks coffee 'looking toward the dark window, thinking of what?'—spreading anxiety about the coming workday through modest affective preparation (staring, coughing, lifting his heavy body) rather than avoiding it. The speaker's meditation inoculates against future hardship by mentally rehearsing the father's brutal labor.

author: Jeanie Greensfelder
content:
I pass a woman on the beach.
We both wear graying hair,
feel sand between our toes,
hear surf, and see blue sky.
I came with a smile.
She came to get one.
No.  I'm wrong.
She sits on a boulder
by a cairn of stacked rocks.
Hands over her heart,
she stares out to sea.
Today's my turn to hold the joy,
hers the sorrow.
evidence: The speaker transforms her own capacity for sorrow into empathic recognition of the other woman's pain ('Today's my turn to hold the joy, / hers the sorrow'), correctly attributing her own vulnerability to suffering to the observed other while experiencing satisfaction in the fortune of currently being the one who can witness rather than endure—a vicarious gratification that leaves both self partly gratified and object genuinely seen.

author: Jessica Laser
content:
All my life I’ve asked my master
Why I am unable to choose
This sweet man or fancy shoes
Over this stranger, more difficult lover
And these expensive but practical loafers
And why I am unable to author
A book exhibiting my full potential
And have focused instead on inconsequential
Letters to strange and difficult lovers
Who by my letters were never changed.
I certainly haven’t been constrained
By terrible parents or trauma or poverty
And even if I had it wouldn’t explain
My propensity for misery
Anymore than it would my
Propensity for joy.
Maybe I’m just a procrastinator
As life is a procrastination of death
And each breath just a procrastination of breath
And friends a procrastination of work
And work a procrastination of love
And love a procrastination I’m just not above.
evidence: The speaker constructs elaborate philosophical justifications for life patterns ('life is a procrastination of death,' 'breath just a procrastination of breath') to explain away choices toward 'difficult lovers' and 'inconsequential letters,' preemptively dismissing external causes ('terrible parents or trauma or poverty') while reframing compulsive self-sabotage as metaphysical necessity rather than acknowledging underlying conflict.

poem_name: Decorating a Cake While Listening to Tennis
author: Peg Duthie
content:
The commentator's rabbiting on and on
about how it's so easy for Roger, resentment
thick as butter still in a box. Yet word
from those who've done their homework
is how the man loves to train—how much
he relishes putting in the hours
just as magicians shuffle card after card,
countless to mere humans
but carefully all accounted for.
At hearing "luck" again, I stop
until my hands relax their clutch
on the cone from which a dozen more
peonies are to materialize. I make it look easy
to grow a garden on top of a sheet
of fondant, and that's how it should appear:
as natural and as meant-to-be
as the spin of a ball from the sweetest spot
of a racquet whisked through the air like a wand.
evidence: The speaker channels competitive/aggressive impulses (noticing 'resentment thick as butter' in the commentator's voice) into socially valued creative work—cake decorating that requires the same disciplined hours of practice as Federer's tennis. The parallel between magician's shuffle, athlete's training, and decorator's skill transforms primitive aggression into mastery ('make it look easy') with real consequences and maintained pleasure.

author: Caitlin Doyle
content:
The stone dolls, found in an Egyptian tomb,
are eyeless, armless, heavy for a child
to hold. Not like the dolls that lined the room
my sister and I shared, their bodies light
and made for being bent, their eyelids mobile,
hair that tangled with our own. "At night,"
our father winked at us, "they come to life."
We never pressed our cheeks against cold stone
as pharoah's daughters did. The doctor's knife
could not have caught my sister more off-guard
or left me less alone; I had my dolls.
Though, soon, they lay on tables in the yard
with price tags. Even then they looked alive,
survivors with no sickness to survive.
evidence: The speaker recounts traumatic loss ('The doctor's knife / could not have caught my sister more off-guard / or left me less alone') in language stripped of emotional coloring, immediately pivoting to the statement 'I had my dolls' as if this were adequate consolation. The trauma is acknowledged factually while affect is sequestered, redirected toward the dolls that 'looked alive, / survivors with no sickness to survive.'

author: Mark Irwin
content:
When they entered the house, which was a very large house
the way a cloud is large, the pages of their story
seemed like cracks in the earth, a man's shirt, or a woman's
blouse, and the stranger in the house told them to make
themselves at home in the house that was not their house,
and told them to write down the three most important gifts
in each of their lives, and then continued to explain how
there were three doors in the house and at each door they must
forfeit one of these gifts, and how the real story always begins
at the third door, where each of them will pause and begin
to crawl, leaving the field of time, where now you pause,
touching the door of this page, wiping away each word, waiting to enter.
evidence: The poem employs a parable-like frame ('the stranger in the house told them') and direct second-person address ('where now you pause') to explore themes of sacrifice and transformation, but does not display a defensive psychological stance toward conflict or distress.

author: Chris Glomski
content:
of waves dropped into froth     Jellyfish a jar
of innards half-buried in sand     Dead nature     What are
these things and who are they for?     This blue rug
is its own genre     And these painted apples
round out the essence of what can be made
into what can be eaten     Winter interest
3.9 APR     April come     She will not
swipe the sun into sky     Limits of credentialed
credit     “At least you’re not the janitor’s
azaleas of the everyday dustpan”     There’s
the problem     It’s like a concussive
grenade at the end of the mine     Mind the
income gap     Let’s activate the fact that
every word means go back to the back of the line
because that is where the front leads     Years
of the postmodern translated by the annuity
of spring     Hello     My name
is the first person I     I am indebted     I am
indented     I insist on remaining
unidentified
evidence: The speaker catalogs material and linguistic fragments in a detached, theoretical register ('This blue rug / is its own genre,' 'every word means go back to the back of the line'), converting personal debt and dislocation into abstract conceptual puzzles delivered without emotional resonance despite their existential weight.

author: Naomi Shihab Nye
content:
What?
You don’t feel at home in your country,
almost overnight?
All the simple things
you cared about,
maybe took for granted. . .
you feel
insulted, invisible?
Almost as if you’re not there?
But you’re there.
Where before you mingled freely. . .
appreciated people who weren’t
just like you. . .
divisions grow stronger.
That’s what “chosen” and “unchosen” will do.
(Just keep your eyes on your houses and gardens.
Keep your eyes on that tree in bloom.)
Yes, a wall. Ours came later but. . .
who talks about how sad the land looks,
marked by a massive wall?
That’s not a normal shadow.
It’s something else looming over your lives.
evidence: The speaker turns outward to create solidarity through shared recognition of dispossession ('You don't feel at home in your country... insulted, invisible? ... That's what "chosen" and "unchosen" will do'), bridging experiences of marginalization without distortion, seeking connection through empathic parallel rather than rescue or idealization.

poem_name: How to Triumph Like a Girl
author: Ada Limón
content:
I like the lady horses best,
how they make it all look easy,
like running 40 miles per hour
is as fun as taking a nap, or grass.
I like their lady horse swagger,
after winning. Ears up, girls, ears up!
But mainly, let’s be honest, I like
that they’re ladies. As if this big
dangerous animal is also a part of me,
that somewhere inside the delicate
skin of my body, there pumps
an 8-pound female horse heart,
giant with power, heavy with blood.
Don’t you want to believe it?
Don’t you want to lift my shirt and see
the huge beating genius machine
that thinks, no, it knows,
it’s going to come in first.
evidence: The speaker claims autonomous power and competence ('the huge beating genius machine / that thinks, no, it knows, / it's going to come in first') without apology or minimization, transforming earlier developmental anxieties about female strength into celebratory ownership. The direct invitation to witness this power ('Don't you want to lift my shirt and see') asserts legitimacy of claiming space and effectiveness.

author: Roberto Harrison
content:
waking to the news of an eventual collapse, those ejected minds
of the walking thing of the speech of birds, they announce their own
development from ashes to make a song and deliver the hunted
landscape to its stars, those of us who knew something of the engine
would remove the approach to animals as a second wind, the coming
of pages of light and nectar, but once the arrival of her tent
would move the inhabitants of the Sea, and the consciousness of the Ocean
to pursue the network that sparks their shining single dream
to the pavement, the oceanic feet that would not muse its face or
deter those breaks from the second light of the morning, or one would
say the easy atonements that their push from the animal
would enlarge so that they would speak and we would understand,
these assembly code calypsos deter the kindly face that hates and pummels
as these will undo their coloring and the fur of the path. do not stop
until you know it well with both feet.
evidence: The poem operates through associative, imagistic leaps without a coherent psychological stance toward conflict. The speaker meditates on 'ejected minds,' 'hunted landscape,' and 'oceanic feet' in a manner that prioritizes linguistic play and surreal juxtaposition over defensiveness against emotional material.

author: Craig Santos Perez
content:
[we] are watching a documentary about home
birth when [you] first feel [neni] kick // embryo
 
of hope // they say plastic is the perfect creation
because it never dies // litters the beaches
 
of o‘ahu, this “gathering place” // the doctor
recommends a c-section // in the sea, plastic multiplies
 
into smaller pieces, leaches estrogenic and toxic
chemicals // if [we] cut open the bellies of whales
 
and large fish, what fragments will [we] find, derived
from oil, absorbed into tissue // because plastic
 
never dissolves, every product ever made still exists,
somewhere, today // i wish my daughter was made
 
of plastic so that she will survive [our] wasteful
hands // so that she, too, will have a great future
evidence: The speaker obsessively interweaves images of plastic's indestructibility with pregnancy and birth, culminating in the magical wish 'i wish my daughter was made of plastic so that she will survive [our] wasteful hands'—attempting to undo parental guilt and environmental transgression through a ritualistic verbal fantasy that transforms the toxic into the protective.

author: Diana Khoi Nguyen
content:
Gradually a girl’s innocence itself becomes her major crime
A doe and her two fawns bent low in the sumac along the bank of a highway,
     the pinched peach of their ears twitching in the heat
Into the disordered evening my brother cut out only his face from every
     photograph in the hall, carefully slipping each frame back into position
What good does it do?
Decades of no faces other than our own chipping faces
What good does it do, this resemblance to nothing we know of the dollhouse
New parents watch their newborn resting in a sunny patch of an empty
      room, the newborn making sense of its container—
And from the road a deer ripened in death and a tuft of fur—or dandelion—
     tumbled along, gently circled, driftwood, shaking loose, gathered,
     dissolving into the mouths of jewelweed nearby
Earth is rife with iron and blood is rich in stardust
Immediately I spotted one hoof print, then nothing, as if this was where she
      dragged herself out of the body
Strips of tire torn from their orbitIs it right then, that we are left to hurtle alone
evidence: The speaker presents the brother's suicide preparation ('cut out only his face from every photograph') and death ('dragged herself out of the body') with the detached, observational tone of a natural history documentary, embedding traumatic family content within imagery of roadkill and dandelions without emotional connectives.

author: Simon Armitage
content:
Wandering slowly back after dark one night
above a river, toward a suspension bridge,
a sound concerns him that might be a tune
or might not: noise drifting in, trailing off.
 
Then concerns him again, now clearly a song
pulsing out from the opposite bank, being sung
by chorusing men, all pewter-haired or bald,
in the function suite of a shabby hotel.
Above their heads a conductor’s hand
draws and casts the notes with a white wand.
 
Songs about mills and mines and a great war,
about mermaid brides and solid gold hills,
songs from broken hymnbooks and cheesy films.
 
Then his father’s voice rising out of that choir,
and his father’s father’s voice, and voices
of fathers before, concerning him only,
arcing through charged air and spanning the gorge.
He steps over the cliff edge and walks across.
evidence: The speaker transforms overwhelming material (father's voice, ancestral voices 'concerning him only') into a dreamlike, theatrical scene culminating in the magical act of stepping 'over the cliff edge and walks across'—treating deadly reality as traversable through altered consciousness rather than confronting the suicidal or grief-laden nature of the moment.

author: Simon Armitage
content:
It’s evening again, late.I go out into the laneand doodle a beard and mustacheon the face of the moonwith a red pen.
 Over the next hillan old teacher of minetakes off her glassesand wipes the lenses with a soft cloth.She can’t believewhat she’s just seen.
evidence: The speaker transforms childhood defiance and rebellion against authority (the old teacher) into whimsical cosmic play—defacing the moon to make the teacher wipe her glasses in disbelief—maintaining full awareness of the absurdity while keeping both the aggressive impulse and its playful deflation in consciousness, making himself the gentle butt of the joke.

poem_name: I Ask My Grandmother What Trinidad Was Like in 1960
author: Megan Arlett
content:
Paradise with a thousand stings, she replies.
Deep blue and blazing sky. Incessant cicadas,
scuttle of bug and roach. Fleas, mosquitos,
the threat of scorpions. Men leaning on doorposts,
crowding the bar. Smoking, drinking,
laughing descendants of slaves. Fire coral burns,
reef-edge barracudas. Truly lovely.
Matriarchal, she says, women with eight children
by many different men. The men would leave
as the spirit took them. I want
to know all the forces one can call spirit.
Tall, swaying fronds of the sugar cane fields.
Distant roar heralding a downpour. Snapping turtles.
Nearby shanty town, she says,
streets full of rubbish, rats in the gutter.
I admired the colonial-style homes, she says.
Colonial, I say.
Separate servant quarters and grounds
filled with samaan trees, the balconies overflowing
with hot-colored orchids and the locusts drawn close
by the palatial lights, colorful and clawing,
their hooks sunk deep into the bare skin of a sweating back.
evidence: The speaker maintains dual awareness throughout—simultaneously receiving the grandmother's nostalgic account and observing her own critical reaction ('Colonial, I say'), culminating in the conscious insertion of the violent final image that reinterprets the grandmother's romanticized description through a lens of colonial exploitation.

poem_name: Crazy Jane Talks with the Bishop
author: William Butler Yeats
content:
I met the Bishop on the road
And much said he and I.
`Those breasts are flat and fallen now
Those veins must soon be dry;
Live in a heavenly mansion,
Not in some foul sty.'
`Fair and foul are near of kin,
And fair needs foul,' I cried.
'My friends are gone, but that's a truth
Nor grave nor bed denied,
Learned in bodily lowliness
And in the heart's pride.
`A woman can be proud and stiff
When on love intent;
But Love has pitched his mansion in
The place of excrement;
For nothing can be sole or whole
That has not been rent.'
evidence: The speaker transforms bodily desire and the Bishop's shaming pronouncements into philosophical-poetic assertion ('Fair and foul are near of kin'), channeling sexual and mortal conflict into metaphysical reconciliation ('Love has pitched his mansion in / The place of excrement') with sustained passionate engagement and real rhetorical consequence.

author: Emily Dickinson
content:
This World is not Conclusion.
A Species stands beyond - 
Invisible, as Music -
But positive, as Sound -
It beckons, and it baffles - 
Philosophy, dont know - 
And through a Riddle, at the last - 
Sagacity, must go -
To guess it, puzzles scholars -
To gain it, Men have borne
Contempt of Generations
And Crucifixion, shown -
Faith slips - and laughs, and rallies - 
Blushes, if any see - 
Plucks at a twig of Evidence - 
And asks a Vane, the way - 
Much Gesture, from the Pulpit -
Strong Hallelujahs roll - 
Narcotics cannot still the Tooth
That nibbles at the soul -
evidence: The speaker catalogs intellectual approaches to faith (Philosophy, Sagacity, scholars puzzling) and observes religious behaviors (Faith's gestures, Pulpit performances) with detached precision, maintaining a curious, analytical distance from the spiritual crisis described. The 'Tooth that nibbles at the soul' names existential anguish in clinical metaphor while the speaker remains affectively removed, reporting rather than inhabiting the distress.

author: John Frederick Nims
content:
I’d have you known! It puzzles me forever   
To hear, day in, day out, the words men use,   
But never a single word about you, never.   
Strange!—in your every gesture, worlds of news.   
On busses people talk. On curbs I hear them;   
In parks I listen, barbershop and bar.   
In banks they murmur, and I sidle near them;   
But none allude to you there. None so far.
I read books too, and turn the pages, spying:   
You must be there, one beautiful as you!   
But never, not by name. No planes are flying   
Your name in lacy trailers past the blue   
Marquees of heaven. No trumpets cry your fame.   
Strange!—how no constellations spell your name!
evidence: The speaker attributes exaggerated, cosmic significance to the beloved ('in your every gesture, worlds of news,' 'one beautiful as you,' expecting the beloved's name to be spelled in constellations), expressing bewilderment that the entire world is not organized around acknowledging this person's perfection, revealing archaic conviction of the beloved's omnipotent specialness.

author: Wallace Stevens
content:
After the leaves have fallen, we return
To a plain sense of things. It is as if
We had come to an end of the imagination,
Inanimate in an inert savoir.
It is difficult even to choose the adjective
For this blank cold, this sadness without cause.
The great structure has become a minor house.
No turban walks across the lessened floors.
The greenhouse never so badly needed paint.
The chimney is fifty years old and slants to one side.
A fantastic effort has failed, a repetition
In a repetitiousness of men and flies.
Yet the absence of the imagination had
Itself to be imagined. The great pond,
The plain sense of it, without reflections, leaves,
Mud, water like dirty glass, expressing silence
Of a sort, silence of a rat come out to see,
The great pond and its waste of the lilies, all this
Had to be imagined as an inevitable knowledge,
Required, as a necessity requires.
evidence: The speaker confronts loss and diminishment ('sadness without cause,' 'fantastic effort has failed') but processes it through philosophical abstraction about imagination itself, maintaining detached cognitive distance ('difficult even to choose the adjective') while acknowledging the emotional content in affectively flattened, theoretical terms.

author: Gregory Orr
content:
I stood inside myself
like a dead tree or a tower.
I pulled the rope
of braided hair
and high above me
a bell of leaves tolled.
Because my hand
stabbed its brother,
I said: Make it stone.
Because my tongue
spoke harshly, I said:
Make it dust.
                   And yet
it was not death, but
her body in its green dress
I longed for. That’s why
I stood for days in the field
until the grass turned black
and the rain came.
evidence: The speaker enacts magical penance for violent transgressions ('Because my hand / stabbed its brother, I said: Make it stone. Because my tongue / spoke harshly, I said: / Make it dust'), attempting to ritually neutralize guilt through self-mortification and compulsive standing in the field until environmental transformation occurs.

author: Harriet Monroe
content:
Good-bye!—no, do not grieve that it is over,
   The perfect hour;
That the winged joy, sweet honey-loving rover,
   Flits from the flower.
Grieve not—it is the law. Love will be flying—
   Yes, love and all.
Glad was the living—blessed be the dying.
   Let the leaves fall.
evidence: The speaker consciously acknowledges painful loss ('it is over, / The perfect hour') while deliberately choosing acceptance over mourning ('do not grieve'), maintaining awareness of both attachment and ending ('Glad was the living—blessed be the dying') without repressing either reality.

author: Rita Dove
content:
In the sixth grade I was chased home by
the Gatlin kids, three skinny sisters
in rolled-down bobby socks. HissingBrainiac! and Mrs. Stringbean!, they trod my heel.
I knew my body was no big deal
but never thought to retort: who's
calling who skinny? (Besides, I knew
they'd beat me up.) I survived
their shoves across the schoolyard
because my five-foot-zero mother drove up
in her Caddie to shake them down to size.
Nothing could get me into that car.
I took the long way home, swore
I'd show them all: I would grow up.
evidence: The speaker narrates childhood humiliation with modest present distress ('I knew my body was no big deal'), then explicitly describes realistic, affect-laden planning for future vindication: 'I took the long way home, swore / I'd show them all: I would grow up.' The final resolution involves emotional preparation and forward-moving developmental work rather than erasure or fantasy.

author: Rita Dove
content:
Imagine you wake up
with a second chance: The blue jay
hawks his pretty wares
and the oak still stands, spreading
glorious shade. If you don't look back,
the future never happens.
How good to rise in sunlight,
in the prodigal smell of biscuits -
eggs and sausage on the grill.
The whole sky is yours
to write on, blown open
to a blank page. Come on,
shake a leg! You'll never know
who's down there, frying those eggs,
if you don't get up and see.
evidence: Speaker prepares emotionally for uncertain future ('you'll never know who's down there') by spreading anxiety into manageable present-tense directives ('shake a leg,' 'get up and see'), experiencing modest affective distress (uncertainty about the unknown figure) while actively planning for encounter rather than avoiding it.

author: Miller Williams
content:
No matter how she tilts her head to hear
she sees the irritation in their eyes.
She knows how they can read a small rejection,
a little judgment, in every What did you say?
So now she doesn’t say What? or Come again?
She lets the syllables settle, hoping they form
some sort of shape that she might recognize.
When they don’t, she smiles with everyone else,
and then whoever was talking turns to her
and says, “Break wooden coffee, don’t you know?”
She pulls all she can focus into the face
to know if she ought to nod or shake her head.
In that long space her brain talks to itself.
The person may turn away as an act of mercy,
leaving her there in a room full of understanding
with nothing to cover her, neither sound nor silence .
evidence: The speaker describes the painful social situation with precise, detached observation—'she sees the irritation in their eyes,' 'Break wooden coffee, don't you know?'—while maintaining emotional flatness throughout. The phrase 'neither sound nor silence' captures the affectless void where distress should register.

author: Gabriel Gomez
content:
The kinship with those humans
who speak directly to me
is webbed to the ceiling.
An economy of satellites, a cosmos,
where revision we think
comes without the benefit
of our witness. A peculiar time
when stars with modest faces
sleep in enormity and mirror
death like a child’s infirmity that
despite socio-economics
is still an illness,
definitive as fading paint
grossing a distant
understanding from a stain
pooled from its center
resonant of some terrific
nucleus making sense
of its own words
with the strangest electricity.
evidence: The speaker converts relational longing ('kinship with those humans who speak directly to me') into abstract cosmological systems ('economy of satellites, cosmos'), discussing connection through detached metaphors of distant phenomena (stars, nuclei, stains) that maintain observational distance from the emotional weight of human intimacy.

author: Rachel Loden
content:
What a giant I must seem to them, an exhausted giant who dozes above her sewing.
Asleep in mid-stitch, sorting the day’s haul of cinders, rubies, griefs—
They were laughing and carrying on, their tiny silver needles flying in and out, tiny silver thimbles on their fingers.
