It’s no use of course, keeping secrets from them, when chattering is almost their religion.
Some held corners of the flag like an enormous quilt, and some danced on little shelves above the workshop.
They were so merrie that I fell asleep again.
In the morning my beautiful flag was finished, every stitch in place and every seam.
So now I raise it—slowly, underneath a secret sky.
Near the door to the half-daft and the cradle of kleptocracy.
Where it rips and shivers, rips and shivers once more
And makes me furiously glad, and fills me up with serious pleasure.
evidence: The speaker transforms primitive impulses ('cinders, rubies, griefs') into creative production ('my beautiful flag') through dissociated fantasy labor (the tiny workers who sew while she sleeps), channeling intense feeling ('furiously glad,' 'serious pleasure') into a symbolic artifact that serves both psychological and political purposes ('near the door to the half-daft and the cradle of kleptocracy').

author: Keith Waldrop
content:
The wind dying, I find a city deserted, except for crowds of
people moving and standing.
 
 
    Those standing resemble stories, like stones, coal from the
death of plants, bricks in the shape of teeth.
 
 
    I begin now to write down all the places I have not been—
starting with the most distant.
 
 
    I build houses that I will not inhabit.
evidence: The speaker describes a deserted city 'except for crowds of people' and catalogs unvisited places and uninhabited houses with curious affective flatness—treating profound existential emptiness as mere inventory, adopting an architectural stance toward displacement without emotional resonance.

author: Kazim Ali
content:
we didn’t really speak
my summer wants to answer
the architecture doesn’t matter
this is not my real life
when I am here I want to know
why do I believe what I was taught
a storm is on the way
close all the windows
begin at the earliest hour
is there a self
evidence: The speaker poses urgent existential questions ('why do I believe what I was taught,' 'is there a self') in a curiously flat, declarative syntax without accompanying emotional resonance, and describes what should be intimate material ('this is not my real life') in the detached tone of a weather report, maintaining cognitive awareness while systematically evacuating affective engagement.

author: Kazim Ali
content:
With thick strokes of ink the sky fills with rain.
Pretending to run for cover but secretly praying for more rain.
Over the echo of the water, I hear a voice saying my name.
No one in the city moves under the quick sightless rain.
The pages of my notebook soak, then curl. I’ve written:
“Yogis opened their mouths for hours to drink the rain.”
The sky is a bowl of dark water, rinsing your face.
The window trembles; liquid glass could shatter into rain.
I am a dark bowl, waiting to be filled.
If I open my mouth now, I could drown in the rain.
I hurry home as though someone is there waiting for me.
The night collapses into your skin. I am the rain.
evidence: The speaker rehearses vulnerability with affect-laden imagery ('I am a dark bowl, waiting to be filled' / 'If I open my mouth now, I could drown'), spreading anxiety across incremental stages of exposure to the rain—pretending resistance while praying for more, then imagining total immersion. The dissociative final collapse ('The night collapses into your skin. I am the rain') transforms overwhelming merger into playful identity-dissolution rather than maintained boundaries.

author: Ezra Pound
content:
The scientists are in terror
            and the European mind stops
Wyndham Lewis chose blindness
            rather than have his mind stop.
Night under wind mid garofani,
            the petals are almost still
Mozart, Linnaeus, Sulmona,
When one’s friends hate each other
            how can there be peace in the world?
Their asperities diverted me in my green time.
A blown husk that is finished
            but the light sings eternal
a pale flare over marshes
                where the salt hay whispers to tide’s change
Time, space,
          neither life nor death is the answer.
And of man seeking good,
            doing evil.
In meiner Heimat
                   where the dead walked
                              and the living were made of cardboard.
evidence: The speaker catalogues catastrophic material—terror, blindness, death, evil—in flat declarative statements without emotional coloring ('The scientists are in terror... Wyndham Lewis chose blindness'), maintaining observational distance while cycling through grand abstractions ('Time, space, neither life nor death is the answer') that acknowledge distress cognitively without affective engagement.

poem_name: Contributions to a Rudimentary Concept of Nation
author: Omar Pérez
content:
On the volatile nights of a winter
nature corroborates with magnanimity
a Cuban is in training for amusement or amnesia,
so often and unfairly assumed as the same,
he brings candy to God, he cultivates the vernacular, he fights off
cirrhosis with fruit poached in syrup, he conducts business;
thus research has shown that The Cuban is resourceful.
In the weighty choreographies of a summer
nature authorizes already with suspicion
a Cuban meets the ocean with offerings and harpoons,
so often and unfairly assumed as the same,
he finger-counts the casualties, he commits an infraction
he slides his hands into his pockets, he avows and commits;
thus analysis has shown that The Cuban is inspired.
Let’s attend the improbable territory
where with pasty mouths a Cuban and The Cuban engage in virile
conversation
we will learn there by what voyage, by what strange condition
by what exchange
we fall prey to so much ingenuity.
evidence: The speaker adopts a pseudo-anthropological, research-report register ('thus research has shown,' 'thus analysis has shown') to describe potentially painful national identity questions, converting the lived experience of Cuban survival strategies into detached sociological observations delivered in affectless, theoretical tones like 'weather reports on the psyche.'

author: W. S. Merwin
content:
With what stillness at last
you appear in the valley
your first sunlight reaching down
to touch the tips of a few
high leaves that do not stir
as though they had not noticed
and did not know you at all
then the voice of a dove calls
from far away in itself
to the hush of the morning
so this is the sound of you
here and now whether or not
anyone hears it this is
where we have come with our age
our knowledge such as it is
and our hopes such as they are
invisible before us
untouched and still possible
evidence: The speaker consciously holds both awareness of limitation ('our knowledge such as it is / and our hopes such as they are') and future possibility ('invisible before us / untouched and still possible') in balanced tension, neither repressing difficulty nor collapsing into despair, but maintaining forward orientation with realistic acknowledgment.

author: Ralph Fletcher
content:
One container of spaghetti sauce
Grandma made before she died.
Two old pieces of wedding cake
you couldn’t pay me to eat.
Three snowballs from last winter
slightly deformed, no longer fluffy.
Four small flounder from the time
Grandpa took me deep-sea fishing.
Everything coated with a thick
white layer of sadness.
evidence: The speaker catalogs intensely grief-laden objects (Grandma's last sauce, wedding cake, Grandpa's fish) with the detached syntax of an inventory list, culminating in the only emotional acknowledgment: 'Everything coated with a thick white layer of sadness'—a metaphoric formulation that names affect while maintaining forensic distance through the concrete image of freezer frost.

author: Charles Ghigna
content:
In their yellow-most goings,
leaves of maple
ride breezes to the ground.
You can hear their sound
each autumn afternoon
as the crisp air cuts
through the trees
and hurries us along
the golden sidewalks
home.
evidence: The poem offers direct sensory observation of autumn leaves and seasonal change without psychological conflict, defense, or transformation of threatening material.

author: Barbara Jane Reyes
content:
there is ghazal swimming inside of her, wanting to be born. on the matter of foretelling, of small miracles, cactus flowers in bloom on this city fire escape, where inside your tongue touches every inch of her skin, where you lay your hand on her belly and sleep. here, she fingers the ornate remains of ancient mosques. here, some mythic angel will rise from the dust of ancestors’ bones. this is where you shall worship, at the intersections of distilled deities and memory’s sharp edges. the country is quite a poetic place; water and rock contain verse and metaphor, even wild grasses reply in rhyme. you are not broken. she knows this having captured a moment of lucidity; summer lightning bugs, sun’s rays in a jelly jar.
 
this is not a love poem, but a cove to escape the flux, however momentary. she is still a child, confabulating the fantastic; please do not erode her wonder for the liquid that is your language. there is thunderstorm in her chest, wanting to burst through her skin. this is neither love poem nor plea. this is not river, nor stone.
evidence: The speaker channels overwhelming erotic and creative impulses ('ghazal swimming inside,' 'thunderstorm in her chest') into aesthetic form and metaphor-making, maintaining emotional intensity while transforming primitive material into poetic architecture that weaves sacred, sexual, and linguistic imagery into socially valuable art.

author: Barbara Jane Reyes
content:
you dream in the language of dodging bullets and artillery fire.
new, sexy diagnoses have been added to the lexicon on your behalf
(“charlie don’t surf,” has also been added to the lexicon on your behalf).
in this home that is not our home, we have mutually exiled each
other. i walk down your street in the rain, and i do not call you. i
walk in the opposite direction of where i know to find you. that we
do not speak is louder than bombs.
 
there are times that missing you is a matter of procedure. now is
not one of those times. there are times when missing you hurts. so
it comes to this, vying for geography. there is a prayer stuck in my
throat. douse me in gasoline, my love, and strike a match. let’s see
this prayer ignite to high heaven.
evidence: The speaker directly addresses the beloved while systematically broadcasting fresh suffering ('i walk in the opposite direction of where i know to find you,' 'that we do not speak is louder than bombs') aimed at the actual source of conflict, culminating in the martyred but aggressive demand 'douse me in gasoline, my love, and strike a match'—suffering current and weaponized toward the real object.

author: Roxane Beth Johnson
content:
A boarded-up house. Ransacked inside — broken glass and toppled tables, chairs overturned, books shaken for hidden money.
 
There are mouths in dreams full of gold teeth, chewing bread and meat. The body is hollow as flame and will burn down anything if pointed straight.
 
A bird flies in through the door, then flutters at the window. Although he is tiny, I am too afraid to help him escape.
I’ve made myself another house. I hum to fill its empty rooms. I fold in like saloon doors closing, then swinging out, keeping out thieves.
evidence: The speaker reports traumatic devastation ('ransacked inside—broken glass and toppled tables') with dreamlike detachment and no corresponding emotional coloring, then describes fear ('too afraid to help') in the same observational register. The compensatory 'humming to fill empty rooms' and self-folding into protective doors suggests ritualistic attempts to counterbalance what has been violated.

author: Megan Snyder-Camp
content:
In this land the children tear their hearts in half.
Let me explain. If ten things are wanted, only ten
can be had. If a stand of birches is found to be made of tin,
the soil around them will bleed with rust. In this land children
study their magazines in broad daylight, and in their books
any soldier who stumbles will not fall. No one will fall,
a gift parents try not to make much of. At every meal
some is set aside. In every garden a patch lies fallow. At parties
there are whispers of illegal cheeses. Camembert, especially,
is said to taste alive. And so the children learn
to make room. To leave some.
Nothing will come, but nothing will go.
To love like this half must rattle in its pit.
evidence: The entire system of the land operates through compensatory ritual: 'At every meal / some is set aside. In every garden a patch lies fallow'—magical balancing gestures that neutralize desire ('if ten things are wanted, only ten / can be had'). The speaker describes a world where children enact compulsive symmetries to ward off catastrophic consequence, treating wanting itself as dangerous deed requiring atonement.

author: Megan Snyder-Camp
content:
The casseroles just showed up.
According to her sister a symbolic casting
 
of the feminine, not gender but physics, dear—
according to a friend she looked
 
just like her sister, green bathrobe mid-afternoon,
suitcase still in the trunk.
 
She’d carried him dead for days.
Out above the reeds a sphere of birds
 
stretches and knots, rises as one
brown then belly-white. Oh the hunger
 
when it came filled every chair.
evidence: The speaker reports the traumatic fact 'She'd carried him dead for days' in the same detached register as mundane details about casseroles and bathrobes, maintaining observational distance while naming profound loss. The hunger that 'filled every chair' appears as pure sensation stripped of emotional coloring.

author: Matthew Rohrer
content:
is an imaginary flower that never fades.
The amaranth is blue with black petals,
it’s yellow with red petals,
it’s enormous and grows into the shape
of a girl’s house,
the seeds nestle high in the closet
where she hid a boy.
The boy and his bike flee
the girl’s parents from the tip
of the leaves, green summer light
behind the veins.
The amaranth is an imaginary flower
in the shape of a girl’s house
dispensing gin and tonics
from its thorns, a succulent.
This makes the boy’s bike steer
off-course all summer, following
the girl in her marvelous car,
the drunken bike.
 
He was a small part of summer,
he was summer’s tongue.
evidence: The speaker constructs an elaborate, self-contained fantasy world (the amaranth flower-house with magical properties) that replaces engagement with actual relational experience; the boy becomes 'summer's tongue'—a depersonalized fragment absorbed into the fantasy system rather than a separate human subject.

author: Matthew Rohrer
content:
They learned to turn off the gravity in an auditorium
and we all rose into the air,
the same room where they demonstrated
pow-wows and prestidigitation.
 
But not everyone believed it.
That was the most important lesson
I learned—that a truck driven by a dog
could roll down a hill at dusk
and roll right off a dock into a lake
and sink, and if no one believes you
then what is the point
of telling them wonderful things?
 
I walked home from the pow-wow
on an early winter night in amazement:
they let me buy the toy tomahawk!
As soon as I got home I was going
to hit my sister with it, but I didn’t know this.
evidence: The speaker recounts childhood events saturated with aggression (buying a toy weapon to hit his sister) and betrayal (no one believing wonderful things) in a curiously flat, reportorial tone—'I was going to hit my sister with it, but I didn't know this' delivers violent intent as mechanical observation without emotional coloring or distress.

author: Matthew Rohrer
content:
It was a good day and I was about to do something important
and good, but then I unscrewed the pen I was using
to see the ink. Precision German craftsmanship.
The Germans are so persnickety and precise,
they wash their driveways. Their mountains and streams
dance around each other in a clockwork, courtly imitation
of spring. They build the Panzer tank, out of rakes
hoses and garden gnomes; they built me.
And I’ve seated myself above an avenue on the brink
of mystery, always just on the lip, with my toes over the lip
but my bowels behind.
 
When I replaced the ink the sky was socked in,
only one window of blue open in the north, directly over someone.
But that person was reading about Rosicrucians in the laundromat,
he was unaware as the blue window closed above him.
The rest of us are limp and damp,
I see a button in front of us that says “spin cycle.”
I’m going to push it.
evidence: Speaker begins noting 'I was about to do something important and good' but immediately diverts into detached observations about German precision and craftsmanship, transforming potential emotional engagement into anthropological musing. The culminating image of collective limpness and the 'spin cycle' button is delivered with curious flatness—conceptually acknowledging stuckness while remaining affectively evacuated.

poem_name: “An Archive of Confessions, A Genealogy of Confessions”
author: Joshua Clover
content:
Now the summer air exerts its syrupy drag on the half-dark
City under the strict surveillance of quotation marks.
 
The citizens with their cockades and free will drift off
From the magnet of work to the terrible magnet of love.
 
In the far suburbs crenellated of Cartesian yards and gin
The tribe of mothers calls the tribe of children in
 
Across the bluing evening. It’s the hour things get
To be excellently pointless, like describing the alphabet.
 
Yikes. It’s fine to be here with you watching the great events
Without taking part, clinking our ice as they advance
 
Yet remain distant. Like the baker always about to understand
Idly sweeping up that he is the recurrence of Napoleon
 
In a baker’s life, always interrupted by the familiar notes
Of a childish song, “no more sleepy dreaming,” we float
 
Casually on the surface of the day, staring at the bottom,
Jotting in our daybooks, how beautiful, the armies of autumn.
evidence: The speaker describes potentially weighty scenarios—surveillance, love as 'terrible magnet,' advancing 'great events,' 'armies of autumn'—in detached, ornamental language ('syrupy drag,' 'excellently pointless') that aestheticizes rather than emotionally engages. The stance is curatorial ('jotting in our daybooks') with explicit disavowal of participation ('watching...without taking part').

author: Joel Brouwer
content:
He faced the sink, one foot up
on the edge of the tub. She stood
behind him, reaching around.
In the mirror, her face rose
over his shoulder like the moon,
and like the moon she regarded him
beautifully but without feeling,
and he looked at her as he would
at the moon: How beautiful!How distant! No smiling, no weeping,
no talking. A man and a woman
transacting their magnificent business
with the usual equanimity. The man
as a passenger walking the ship’s deck
at evening and the woman as the moon
over his shoulder oiling the ocean
with light. Deep in the ship’s belly
pistons churned and sailors fed
the boilers' roar with coal. On deck
just the engine’s dull thrum and
a faint click as the woman sets her ring
on the cool white lip of the sink.
evidence: The speaker describes an intimate scene between man and woman as 'transacting their magnificent business / with the usual equanimity'—emotionally charged content delivered in detached, observational prose that strips away affective engagement while maintaining cognitive awareness of the situation.

author: Joel Brouwer
content:
Photograph found in the road: bejeweled hand
gripping a limp dick. All parties
suffering from lack of ambition. The hills
of Tuscany won’t dapple with sunlight,
and here it is nearly noon. She didn’t
much want the leather jacket, the vendor didn’t
really care to sell it, she hardly tried
it on, he barely praised her beauty, then
everyone wasn’t hungry and went to lunch.
The rubies won’t glow. The delayed train shrugs
on its siding. The penis appears at ease.Osteria, osteria, osteria, osteria.
I knew many words but preferred to say
the same ones over and over, like
a photographer shooting four frames
of the same subject, hoping for one in focus.
This clearly among the other three.
evidence: The speaker catalogs emotionally charged material (explicit sexual image, failed transactions, intimate encounters) in a detached, photographic register devoid of corresponding affect—describing 'parties / suffering from lack of ambition' and 'The penis appears at ease' with the same weather-report tone used for hills that 'won't dapple with sunlight.'

author: Rafael Campo
content:
It’s not that I don’t like the hospital.
Those small bouquets of flowers, pert and brave.
The smell of antiseptic cleansers.
The ill, so wistful in their rooms, so true.
My friend, the one who’s dying, took me out
To where the patients go to smoke, IV’s
And oxygen in tanks attached to them—
A tiny patio for skeletons. We shared
A cigarette, which was delicious but
Too brief. I held his hand; it felt
Like someone’s keys. How beautiful it was,
The sunlight pointing down at us, as if
We were important, full of life, unbound.
I wandered for a moment where his ribs
Had made a space for me, and there, beside
The thundering waterfall of his heart,
I rubbed my eyes and thought, “I’m lost.”
evidence: The speaker acknowledges the overwhelming reality of his friend's dying ('it felt / Like someone's keys,' 'skeletons') while maintaining conscious engagement with the moment ('How beautiful it was'), deliberately holding grief and beauty together without resolution—a mature postponement rather than avoidance. The momentary wandering 'where his ribs / Had made a space' and recognition 'I'm lost' signals conscious awareness of being overwhelmed while continuing to function.

author: Rafael Campo
content:
What I would like to give them for a change
is not the usual prescription with
its hubris of the power to restore,
to cure; what I would like to give them, ill
from not enough of laying in the sun
not caring what the onlookers might think
while feeding some banana to their dogs—
what I would like to offer them is this,
not reassurance that their lungs sound fine,
or that the mole they’ve noticed change is not
a melanoma, but instead of fear
transfigured by some doctorly advice
I’d like to give them my astonishment
at sudden rainfall like the whole world weeping,
and how ridiculously gently it
slicked down my hair; I’d like to give them that,
the joy I felt while staring in your eyes
as you learned epidemiology
(the science of disease in populations),
the night around our bed like timelessness,
like comfort, like what I would give to them.
evidence: The speaker transforms medical authority and its primitive impulse to control/cure ('hubris of the power to restore') into genuinely generous witness—offering patients 'astonishment at sudden rainfall,' 'joy felt while staring in your eyes,' the intimacy of shared knowledge ('epidemiology... like comfort')—channeling clinical power into emotionally authentic gift-giving that serves others while gratifying the speaker's own desire for connection.

author: Rafael Campo
content:
Not long ago, I studied medicine.
It was terrible, what the body told.
I’d look inside another person’s mouth,
And see the desolation of the world.
I’d see his genitals and think of sin.
 
Because my body speaks the stranger’s language,
I’ve never understood those nods and stares.
My parents held me in their arms, and still
I think I’ve disappointed them; they care
And stare, they nod, they make their pilgrimage
 
To somewhere distant in my heart, they cry.
I look inside their other-person’s mouths
And see the wet interior of souls.
It’s warm and red in there—like love, with teeth.
I’ve studied medicine until I cried
 
All night. Through certain books, a truth unfolds.
Anatomy and physiology,
The tiny sensing organs of the tongue—
Each nameless cell contributing its needs.
It was fabulous, what the body told.
evidence: The speaker channels profound emotional distress ('I've studied medicine until I cried / All night') into the socially valued practice of medicine itself, transforming intimate terror about sin, disappointment, and the body's strangeness into anatomical study—'tiny sensing organs of the tongue / Each nameless cell contributing its needs.' The oscillation between 'terrible' and 'fabulous' marks the transformation achieved through creative medical engagement.

author: Michelle Tea
content:
spilling water from my back,
you call and i come.
that exhausted walk to reach you
breathless and no i didn’t run
to see you, i’ve been smoking
too much, same thing.
 
another awkward hug in the car
as my face smashes your cheek
that i can feel it leaving now
is the saddest, a beautiful eruption
you could have picked it off the tree
and chowed
 
but you weren’t hungry.
feeling it dying away all day
much worse than the straining
against the leash, another gorgeous
thing that should not have happened,
gone again.
evidence: The speaker maintains full conscious awareness of desire ('you call and i come'), its frustrated trajectory ('you weren't hungry'), and the ongoing process of loss ('feeling it dying away all day'), postponing collapse into either denial or rage while tracking each stage of erotic disappointment with finite temporal markers.

author: Charles Ghigna
content:
His hand in hold so trigger-tight its blood
believes in ghosts. It clings with finger set
on steel and waits inside a dream of ducks.
The twilight burns into a rising arc
of eastern sky as sun reveals herself
too proud and instantly receives full-face
a splash of mallard flock. A shotgun blasts
the yellow into streaming pinks and gives
the creek its new-day taste of echoed blood.
Two green head ghosts fly through the pulse of dawn
upon a trigger’﻿s touch. The creek empties
of sound. In silence human fingers find
wet feet of web and carry in each hand
a bird whose only cry comes in color.
evidence: The poem observes a hunting scene with aesthetic attention to sensory detail (twilight, yellow into streaming pinks, color as cry) without revealing a psychological stance toward the violence—the speaker remains external narrator rather than subject managing conflict.

author: Charles Ghigna
content:
In the backyard
of my father’﻿s house
a hen’﻿s warm neck
once filled the center
of my pale fist.
Her place on the stump
still wears my shadow
like a stain.
evidence: The speaker recounts wringing a chicken's neck with forensic precision ('a hen's warm neck / once filled the center / of my pale fist') while maintaining complete affective distance—the violence is described as memory-object without accompanying guilt, remorse, or emotional coloring, as if cataloging rather than confessing.

author: Charles Ghigna
content:
You marched into the gray eyes of dawn
feeling older than the bones
that held their ground
like grazing, aged cattle
waiting, eyes closed to the wind,
on a winter, slaughter morning.
You searched through the fog for a sign,
but there was no sun to burn the way,
no burst of rainbow bridge
to keep you from the cattle call.
evidence: The speaker describes Berryman's final morning with affect-laden awareness of impending death ('slaughter morning,' 'cattle call'), experiencing the future discomfort through metaphors of aged cattle waiting with 'eyes closed to the wind'—realistic preparation for the feared event through present emotional engagement with what is to come.

author: Robert Creeley
content:
Tonight, nothing is long enough—
time isn’﻿t.
Were there a fire,
it would burn now.
 
Were there a heaven,
I would have gone long ago.
I think that light
is the final image.
 
But time reoccurs,
love—and an echo.
A time passes
love in the dark.
evidence: The speaker consciously holds desire ('nothing is long enough') alongside recognition of temporal limits, maintaining both wish ('Were there a heaven, I would have gone') and delay ('time reoccurs') in simultaneous awareness without repression or avoidance—acknowledging longing while postponing its resolution.

author: Catherine Wagner
content:
Raise up your back like an insect on the face of the nation
He took Miss Mousie on his knee, O say
little mouse will you marry me?
Getting hair cut this morning illegally
I can’﻿t afford it
Fourteen fifteen I depend on you and roiling unlap this morn
The mind refrigerated all night
Now to clarify the broth skim off the oil & swallow it is your oil
I must have it shorter so it grows longer in unison
A glory and stern grandeur, which men silently gazed on with wonder
    and tears
evidence: The poem enacts formal experimentation through disjunctive collage—nursery rhyme fragments, quotation, mundane declaration ('Getting hair cut')—without a coherent psychological stance toward distress or conflict that would constitute defense.

author: Francisco Aragón
content:
Amazing the mood it's put me in.
And the sky's tint at this hour—out
on my own, occasional hum or zip
of a car, August the summer month
half the city splashes about
the Mediterranean, or north:
the beach at Donostia a jewel
—its Paseo the lip of a shell to walk.
It's hearing you what really pulls
me in, soft this interior punch,
recalling the sheen of your brow—we'd talk
with our limbs, the Liffey below, have lunch...
Re-lived this evening on the phone;
the pitch of your Dublin tone.
                              Madrid
evidence: The speaker transforms intense longing for an absent lover into aesthetically crafted poetic expression, channeling erotic desire ('we'd talk / with our limbs') into formal structure (sonnet form) that maintains emotional authenticity while achieving creative distance through artistic craft.

author: Jonathan Williams
content:
Red-Wigglers, Night-Crawlers
& Other Worms
look out
into the crapulous moonlight:
 
figures of women cascading through the Sunday night;
 
no beer in sight.
 
I remember the Night-bloomingCereus by Dr. Thornton, Engraver, Blake’s
patron, it
hangs in the hall outside the bedroom
swaying hungrily like these
giant white goddesses of the dark grotto…
 
there are touring cars
and men with large guns
singing through the woods
 
behind us.
 
﻿
evidence: The speaker recounts potentially threatening imagery (touring cars, men with large guns singing through the woods behind us) and erotically charged material (women cascading, white goddesses swaying hungrily) in a curiously flat, cataloging tone that maintains technical precision ('Red-Wigglers, Night-Crawlers') while remaining disconnected from the emotional impact of danger or desire.

author: Paul Foster Johnson
content:
Their singer suffered breakdowns. In their work
there was a sense of what it was to live there at that time.
One song described the dark around the military
vehicles between them and the cocaine waiting
in Gramercy. It was about the sepsis that followed love
or love repeated as farce, the neck neck neck
damaged by an anonymous hand unstringing guitars.
They got away with it and worked to abolish youth
by knitting and paying half-attention. I thought I was
in love because my sentiments were matched
by a generic, abiding sense of unfreedom. Nothing
survives lovers descrying the red flags of old flames.
Nothing is more relatable than an unreasonable person
operating subtractively, indulgently, out of exasperation.﻿
evidence: The speaker describes intensely charged material—breakdowns, cocaine, sepsis following love, damaged necks, abolished youth—in a detached, theoretical tone that resembles a 'weather report on the psyche.' Love is analyzed as 'sentiments matched by a generic, abiding sense of unfreedom' rather than felt experience.

author: Lucille Clifton
content:
somebody coming in blackness
like a star
and the world be a great bush
on his head
and his eyes be fire
in the city
and his mouth be true as time
 
he be calling the people brother
even in the prison
even in the jail
 
i’m just only a baptist preacher
somebody bigger than me coming
in blackness like a star
evidence: The speaker attributes messianic, superhuman qualities to the coming figure ('eyes be fire,' 'mouth be true as time,' 'world be a great bush on his head') while explicitly positioning themselves as lesser ('i'm just only a baptist preacher / somebody bigger than me coming'), employing superlatives and cosmic imagery to elevate the idealized object to omnipotent status.

author: Muriel Rukeyser
content:
Deeper than sleep but not so deep as death
I lay there dreaming and my magic head
remembered and forgot. On first cry I
remembered and forgot and did believe.
I knew love and I knew evil:
woke to the burning song and the tree burning blind,
despair of our days and the calm milk-giver who
knows sleep, knows growth, the sex of fire and grass,
renewal of all waters and the time of the stars
and the black snake with gold bones.
 
Black sleeps, gold burns; on second cry I woke
fully and gave to feed and fed on feeding.
Gold seed, green pain, my wizards in the earth
walked through the house, black in the morning dark.
Shadows grew in my veins, my bright belief,
my head of dreams deeper than night and sleep.
Voices of all black animals crying to drink,
cries of all birth arise, simple as we,
found in the leaves, in clouds and dark, in dream,
deep as this hour, ready again to sleep.
evidence: The speaker transforms the primitive, physically demanding experience of night nursing—with its disruption of sleep and bodily demands—into mythic, incantatory poetry that integrates primal elements (fire, milk, sex, death) into socially valued aesthetic form while maintaining full emotional intensity and pleasure in both the maternal act and its creative rendering.

author: Kate Greenstreet
content:
Men are trading their bullets for worms.
“I spent a lifetime building.”
 
We come down from the mountains. We brought eggs, a table, a windowshade. There were times when we couldn’t bring anything. So many people.
 
As you were walking up the hill and I was walking down, we almost passed each other. But I grasped your arm and backed up. You said: “This is what I look like now.”
﻿
evidence: The speaker maintains conscious awareness of multiple contradictory elements ('We brought eggs, a table, a windowshade. There were times when we couldn't bring anything') without collapsing into resolution, holding the tension between movement, loss, and recognition across temporal markers ('spent a lifetime building,' 'walking up...walking down') while acknowledging difficulty through incremental, concrete details.

author: Kate Greenstreet
content:
She considers a field. She considers a field and buys it. Let her have the fruit of her hands.
 
We come down from the mountains. Yellow trees, green trees. I was leaving Earth but, before I did, I had to get rid of all my animals. My main one, my main model for behavior, was my snake. He was attached to the bottom of my foot but had become dangerous seeming and I was afraid he would bite me if he got the chance. My sister was there and I said, “Before I leave, I have to get rid of all my animals,” thinking maybe she’d help me. I said, “My horse, my frog, my snake…” but didn’t mention I was worried about how to dislodge my snake safely. As I was waking up, I thought of going to a place where they could give the snake a shot to knock it out or even kill it before they tried to get it off my foot. Which seemed like a pretty good idea, though inconvenient.
evidence: The speaker redirects anxious affect about a dangerous attachment ('my main model for behavior') onto symbolic animals—particularly the snake 'attached to the bottom of my foot' that threatens to bite—while the original human relationship remains unexamined. The clinical tone ('get a shot to knock it out or even kill it') strips the material of emotional resonance despite discussing elimination of her 'main model for behavior.'

poem_name: You've Ruined My Evening/You've Ruined My Life
author: Tom Raworth
content:
i would be eight people and then the difficulties vanish
only as one i contain the complications
in a warm house roofed with the rib-cage of an elephant
i pass my grey mornings re-running the reels
and the images are the same but the emphasis shifts
the actors bow gently to me and i envy them
their repeated parts, their constant presence in that world
i would be eight people each inhabiting the others’ dreams
walking through corridors of glass framed pages
telling each other the final lines of letters
picking fruit in one dream and storing it in another
only as one i contain the complications
and the images are the same, their constant presence in that world
the actors bow gently to me and envy my grey mornings
 
i would be eight people with the rib-cage of an elephant
picking fruit in a warm house above actors bowing
re-running the reels of my presence in this world
the difficulties vanish and the images are the same
eight people, glass corridors, page lines repeated
inhabiting grey mornings roofed with my complications
only as one walking gently storing my dream
evidence: The speaker retreats from the complications of unitary selfhood into an elaborate internal fantasy of multiplicity ('i would be eight people and then the difficulties vanish'), preferring imagined corridors and dream-worlds to interpersonal reality. The envious gaze directed at film actors reveals withdrawal into spectatorship of repeated, controllable scenarios rather than engagement with the unpredictable social world.

author: Tom Raworth
content:
lonely as four cherries on a tree
at night, new moon, wet roads
a moth or a snowflake
whipping past glass
 
lonely as the red noses of four clowns
thrust up through snow
their shine four whitened panes
drawn from imagined memory
 
lonely as no other lives
touching to recorded water
all objects stare
their memories aware
 
lonely as pain
recoiling from itself
imagining the cherries
and roses reaching out
evidence: The speaker enumerates extreme states of loneliness through precisely constructed visual analogies ('lonely as four cherries on a tree,' 'lonely as pain / recoiling from itself') yet maintains an observational, almost cataloging tone that strips the loneliness of its emotional weight—delivering affect as conceptual proposition rather than lived experience.

author: Landis Everson
content:
A tree that grew in the Garden of Eden
a tree of innocence called
the Tree of Good and Evil. It was harmless
 
as opposites are in balance. It was also
tasteless,
the taste of innocence before it is betrayed.
When God removed the wall
 
he gave the lemon thorns and bitterness because it had
no hostility.
It is a taste we want most to subdue. It asks
to be left alone.
We use it with fish and tea. We sugar it.
 
Look out the window. It stands with a donkey’s
stance, hoping the day will pass.
Its scent through the curtains
cuts through
mustiness, sharp
with sweet blossoms. It hides the smell
of new babies.
﻿﻿
evidence: The speaker delivers a theological-botanical lecture on the lemon's transformation ('God removed the wall / he gave the lemon thorns and bitterness') in curiously flat, theoretical tones, maintaining observational distance even when describing the tree's suffering ('hoping the day will pass') and domestication ('we want most to subdue'). The affect-drained meditation converts potential guilt about domination into abstract commentary on innocence and taste.

author: Urayoán Noel
content:
I swallow a pill but there is no cure

A city map won’t get me where I want to go

 

Scaling the scaffold,

mindless of the mall,

unaware of driveways

where housewives dodge the wrecking ball,

I crawl outside these vacant blues

 

and into the contours of your eyes
evidence: The speaker channels existential dissatisfaction ('no cure,' inability to reach desired destination) into aesthetic expression through urban imagery and romantic vision—'crawl outside these vacant blues / and into the contours of your eyes'—transforming primitive longing into poetic form with maintained emotional intensity but socially valuable creative outlet.

author: Joyce Sidman
content:
Sun slant low, chill seeps into black water. No more days of bugs and basking. Last breath, last sight of light and down I go, into the mud. Every year, here, I sink and settle, shuttered like a shed.  Inside, my eyes close, my heart slows to its winter rhythm.  Goodbye, good- bye! Remember the warmth.  Remember the quickness. Remember me. Remember.  
﻿
evidence: The speaker experiences affect-laden preparation for future dormancy ('Remember the warmth. Remember the quickness'), spreading anxiety over time through conscious acknowledgment of loss ('Last breath, last sight of light') while maintaining both awareness of what is ending and realistic planning for survival through hibernation.

author: Joyce Sidman
content:
sun
sunwarm
sunwarm on back
sunwarm on back legs
sunwarm on back legs loosens
            my heart
            my heart beats
            my heart beats faster
            in sunwarm my heart beats faster
                        I flex
                        I flex legs
                        I flex legs loose with sunwarm
                        I drink dew from dripping leaves
                        I beat
                                    flex
                                                crouch
                        leap!
 
                                                What am I?  
(grasshopper)
 
﻿
evidence: The poem enacts a grasshopper's physical awakening through incremental bodily sensations without psychological conflict, anxiety management, or relational dynamics requiring defense.

author: Joyce Sidman
content:
Water nymph, you have
climbed from the shallows to don
your dragon-colors.
Perched on a reed stem
all night, shedding your skin, you dry
your wings in moonlight.
 
Night melts into day.
Swift birds wait to snap you up.
Fly, dragonfly! Fly!
 
﻿
evidence: The speaker experiences affect-laden worry about future danger ('Swift birds wait to snap you up') while directly addressing the vulnerable dragonfly, spreading anxiety through the poem's temporal arc (night to day) and culminating in urgent, emotionally invested preparation: 'Fly, dragonfly! Fly!'

author: Stephen Sandy
content:
No one knew what the stones like squatting frogs
signified. There they were, fuming in rows, out
of the ground; every critic had his explanation
or hers. But—we had to remember—they
 
came to nothing, every one; those large stones
out of the earth served the systems
of those who considered them, as explaining
something about the past it was important
 
for the explainer to explain. And yet
no one had any idea truly; there was no
basis in fact for any view of them, and
they remained like their origins—or like
 
smiling Olmec babies, sweet but ominous figures
come from the earth to reproach us, almost
cheerfully, for our ignorance—a mystery, just
as the probe of our feelings came up with nothing.
evidence: The speaker delivers elaborate cognitive processing of mystery ('every critic had his explanation / or hers... those large stones / out of the earth served the systems / of those who considered them') with curiously flat affect despite confronting fundamental unknowing and reproach from ominous figures—discussing feelings as theoretical probe ('the probe of our feelings came up with nothing') rather than lived experience.

author: Joshua Clover
content:
In one version you must convince every living thing one by one
to weep until he climbs back into the marriage-house,
that earth about which it is said that bread is the glue of the earth.
Certainly glue is money, the phrase “the tears of things” is money,
the revelation of the Woman Clothed in the Sun is money.
The lake is a disc of bright money buying a few plain birds down,
they climb back nervously as you hurry through, plain birds like a
                                                                                                   plain song,
that moment when four or five are around your knees
like Zeno’s arrow, rising by halves, like Eurydice’s bread,
& still the possibility they might intersect,
you would be the one who was struck by a flying bird,
somewhere between a blesséd fool & village idiot,
the only one to persist outside the local economy,
drooling at travelers, holding yourself, slinging incomprehensible
                                                                                                   advice,
you would learn the trick with museum wire
where you snap the heads off quiet animals in front of the store,
tempted equally by science & dirty work. . . .
 
I am trying to invent a way for you to buy me back—
evidence: The speaker transforms desperate longing ('I am trying to invent a way for you to buy me back') into elaborate theoretical scaffolding—Zeno's paradoxes, mythic versions, economic metaphors—delivering affect-laden material (loss, longing, redemption) in densely conceptual, almost mathematical language that keeps emotional contact at arm's length while acknowledging its presence.

author: Stephen Sandy
content:
Sewn straw, exact pattern. Fields of rice-sprigs
evenly set, a mile of herringbone tweed.
The town, a sea of gunmetal, fish-scale tiles.
 
By morning each floor a casserole of pillows,
coverlets, comforters, towels:    flown nests. Imprint
of bodies, fading. They fold the beds away,
the room waits empty all day.
                                                       All day the bodies
circle, leaving no impression on each
other. Tooled in the foundry of the streets.
School-caps, factory-packed subway, miracle train:
one territorial imperative,
an emperor’s.
                          On his platform one yard square
perched on a roof that slopes in waves of tiles
up toward other tiled cascades, the karate student
for  hours does running in place, deep knee bends
on his surfboard perch. All his free time to make
his body efficient, tight, exact, rising
and falling, mint piston pumping in its shaft.
evidence: The speaker catalogs intimate domestic scenes—'imprint of bodies, fading,' 'flown nests'—and human isolation ('circle, leaving no impression on each / other') in the detached register of industrial inventory ('Tooled in the foundry of the streets,' 'mint piston pumping'). Emotional resonance of bodies disappearing, beds folding, people not touching is systematically evacuated through mechanical precision and architectural taxonomy.

author: Stephen Sandy
content:
I remember the dusty floorboards of wood in the streetcar
Of the Minneapolis Street Railway Company
And the varnished yellow banquettes of tight-knit rattan
Worn smooth by decades of passengers
The worn gleaming brass grips at the corners of the seats
And the motorman’s little bell
Windows trembling in their casings as we crossed the avenue
Liberty dimes falling softly into the steel-rimmed hour glass
The gnarled hand of the motorman near.
My grandmother arranged herself against the seat
Her back as straight as a soldier’s beside me
Her navy hat with velvet band
And net veil down making her head seem distant,
Her dreaming smile and the patient Roman nose,
A repose so deep; from my place
I watched her when we rode like princes
Rattling past traffic stopped on the granite cobbles
Riding downtown together, my hands in hers;
All that so much
That I love yet but feel no sadness for, that
Time crossed out like the trolley tracks taken up
Or entombed under the pliant blacktop of the modernized.
evidence: The speaker maintains full conscious awareness of the lost past (grandmother, streetcar, childhood intimacy) while explicitly noting 'I love yet but feel no sadness for' it—a deliberate, successful postponement of grief that acknowledges both the precious memory and its loss without collapsing into mourning.

poem_name: Photo of Melville; Back Room, Old Bookstore
author: Stephen Sandy
content:
I passed him by at first. From the photograph
Peered sepia eyes, blindered, unappeased
From a lair of brows and beard: one not amazed
At anything, as if to have looked enough
Then turned aside worked best for him—as if
Night vision was the discipline that eased
The weight of what he saw. A man’s gaze posed
Too long in the sun goes blank; comes to grief.
That face could be a focus for this back room,
For pack-rat papers strewn as if in rage,
Fond notes unread: each wary eye a phial
Unstopped to let huge Melville out, to calm
The sea of pages; Melville in older age:
The grown man’s sleepy defiance of denial.
evidence: The speaker interprets Melville's 'sleepy defiance of denial' and 'night vision' as a discipline of looking without emotional overwhelm—'A man's gaze posed / Too long in the sun goes blank; comes to grief.' The photograph becomes a model for viewing painful reality with 'blindered, unappeased' detachment, turning away rather than feeling through what is seen.

author: Nathalie Handal
content:
We say lightning has no wings
when it slides down our houses
 
We say loss is just a condition
we acquire to bury our pity further
 
We say the bleeding hands
on the table filled with red wine
imported products and passports
are just reminders of
who we have become
 
We have no titles no birthright
no groves or Shakespeare
to return to
 
We apologize for the fear
growing out of our ribs
 
Apologize for the numbers
still etched on our tongues
evidence: The speaker repeatedly constructs explanatory frameworks ('We say...') that convert traumatic realities (displacement, ancestral trauma, bodily violence) into abstracted conditions and logical propositions, attempting to make bearable what remains unmetabolized—the 'bleeding hands' become 'just reminders,' loss becomes 'just a condition we acquire.' The apologizing for 'fear growing out of our ribs' and 'numbers still etched on our tongues' reveals guilt managed through quasi-reasonable self-justification for inherited suffering.

author: Dorianne Laux
content:
Someone spoke to me last night,
told me the truth. Just a few words,
but I recognized it.
I knew I should make myself get up,
write it down, but it was late,
and I was exhausted from working
all day in the garden, moving rocks.
Now, I remember only the flavor —
not like food, sweet or sharp.
More like a fine powder, like dust.
And I wasn’﻿t elated or frightened,
but simply rapt, aware.
That’﻿s how it is sometimes —
God comes to your window,
all bright light and black wings,
and you’﻿re just too tired to open it.
evidence: The speaker consciously recognizes truth ('I knew I should make myself get up, / write it down') but chooses postponement due to exhaustion, maintaining awareness of the missed opportunity without repression or avoidance—a deliberate, semiconscious decision to defer engagement with the conflict between spiritual receptivity and bodily limits.

author: Tsering Wangmo Dhompa
content:
I am saying primroses lined the pathway of toothless hedges.
I am saying the ocean shimmered like corrugated steel in the
morning sun.
The context of my story changes when you enter. Then I am dung
on the wall of the nomad’﻿s field. Then the everyday waking person.
I am nodding in your direction like fissures between dandelion fur.
Seeing in your manner.
I am speaking your pace. Slippage of silk slippers.
I say you are losing sight. I say your breasts are dry shells.
I am afraid of what I am capable of doing.
This is all a manner of stating how I prepare myself to be loved.﻿
evidence: The speaker alternates between idealized romantic imagery ('primroses,' 'ocean shimmered') and aggressive devaluation ('dung on the wall,' 'your breasts are dry shells'), culminating in the confession 'I am afraid of what I am capable of doing'—revealing underlying hostility that the final line attempts to reverse into its opposite: 'This is all a manner of stating how I prepare myself to be loved.'

author: Tsering Wangmo Dhompa
content:
Her voice is a roundness. On full moon days, she talks about
renouncing meat but the butcher has his routine. And blood.
M’﻿s wisdom. Still reliable.
There are sounds we cannot hear but understand in motion.
Slicing of air with hips. Crushing grass, saying these are my feet.
I want my feet in my shadow. Suffice to meet desires halfway.
Quiet. We say her chakras are in place.
When the thermos shatters, she knows the direction of its spill.
She knows how to lead and follow. Know her from this.
Sounds we cannot hear. The wind blows and we say it is cool.
Night slips under the door. We are tucked into bed and kissed
a fleeting one. Through the curtains, her voice loosens like thread
from an old blanket, row upon row. We watch her teeth in the
dark and read her words. She speaks in perfect order, facing where
the breeze can tug it towards canals stretching for sound.
Her faith abides by the cycle of the moon. See how perfect she is.
evidence: The speaker attributes perfection and omniscience to the subject ('she knows the direction of its spill,' 'speaks in perfect order,' 'See how perfect she is'), constructing a portrait of someone who exists in flawless harmony with natural cycles and possesses special wisdom ('M's wisdom. Still reliable'), with idealization reaching its apex in the final declarative: 'See how perfect she is.'

author: Tsering Wangmo Dhompa
content:
A hundred and one butter lamps are offered to my uncle who
is no more.
Distraction proves fatal in death. A curtain of butter imprints
in air.
After the burning of bones, ashes are sent on pilgrimage. You are
dead, go into life, we pray. My uncle was a man given to giggles
in solemn moments.
Memory springs like crocuses in bloom. Self conscious and
precise.
Without blurring the cornea, details are resuscitated. Dried yak
meat between teeth. Semblance of what is.
Do not be distracted, Uncle who is no more.
He does not see his reflection in the river. The arching of speech
over “s” as he is becoming.
Curvature of spine as it cracked on a misty morning. A shadow
evades the wall.
You are no more, Uncle who is no more.
Every seven days he must relive his moment of expiration.
The living pray frequently amid burning juniper.
Communication efforts require the right initiative.
Somewhere along the line matters of motion and rest are resolved.
Crows pick the last offerings. You are someone else, uncle no
more.
evidence: The speaker repeatedly instructs 'Do not be distracted, Uncle who is no more' and 'Every seven days he must relive his moment of expiration. The living pray frequently amid burning juniper'—actively preparing emotionally through ritual and prayer to manage the uncle's death and journey, spreading grief across structured time periods (seven-day cycles) while experiencing present distress ('Communication efforts require the right initiative').

author: J. Patrick Lewis
content:
I was really in a muddle
looking over a mud puddle
'cause I didn't have a paddle
or a twig to ride the reef.
But I said, Oh, fiddle-faddle,
this is just a little piddle
of a second fiddle puddle
so I saddled up a leaf.
I set sail on the puddle,
but I reached the muddy middle
and I rocked the leaf a little,
then I gave it all I had.
And I solved the mighty riddle
of the whole caboodle puddle
when I hopped up on the middle
of a beetle launching pad.
evidence: The poem presents a toad's whimsical problem-solving adventure without psychological conflict, distress, or defensive operations—it is a playful narrative exercise rather than an expression of internal threat requiring defense.

author: David Ferry
content:
Alone in the library room, even when others
Are there in the room, alone, except for themselves:
There is the illusion of peace; the air in the room
Is stilled; there are reading lights on the tables,
Looking as if they're reading, looking as if
They're studying the text, and understanding,
Shedding light on what the words are saying;
But under their steady imbecile gaze the page
Is blank, patiently waiting not to be blank.
The page is blank until the mind that reads
Crosses the black river, seeking the Queen
Of the Underworld, Persephone, where she sits
By the side of the one who brought her there from Enna,
Hades the mute, the deaf, king of the dead letter;
She is clothed in the beautiful garment of our thousand
Misunderstandings of the sacred text.
evidence: The speaker observes reading as a cognitive operation with detached theoretical precision ('the mind that reads / Crosses the black river'), transforming the intimate act of meaning-making into an abstract mythological system, maintaining elaborate conceptual structures while evacuating any direct emotional engagement with what reading feels like or means to the solitary figures in the room.

author: Alison Hawthorne Deming
content:
Pomegranates fell from the trees
in our sleep. If we stayed
in the sun too long
there were aloes
to cool the burn.
Henbane for predators
and succulents when the rain was scarce.
 
There was no glorified past
to point the way
true and natural
for the sexes to meet.
He kept looking to the heavens
as if the answer were anywhere
but here. I was so bored
with our goodness
I couldn’t suck the juice
from one more pear.
 
It’s here, I kept telling him,here, rooted in the soil
like every other tree
you know. And I wove us
a bed of its uppermost branches.
evidence: The speaker directly claims agency and competence ('It's here, I kept telling him, here' and 'I wove us a bed') without guilt or apology, asserting her autonomous vision and creative action against a partner who 'kept looking to the heavens as if the answer were anywhere but here.' She expresses legitimate needs ('I was so bored with our goodness') and demonstrates forward-moving autonomy in contrast to earlier passive perfection.

author: Gerald Stern
content:
Me trying to understand say whence
say whither, say what, say me with a pencil walking,
say reading the dictionary, say learning medieval
Latin, reading Spengler, reading Whitehead,
William James I loved him, swimming breaststroke
and thinking for an hour, how did I get here?
Or thinking in line, say the 69 streetcar
or 68 or 67 Swissvale,
that would take me elsewhere, me with a textbook
reading the pre-Socratics, so badly written,
whoever the author was, me on the floor of
the lighted stacks sitting cross-legged,
walking afterwards through the park or sometimes
running across the bridges and up the hills,
sitting down in our tiny diningroom,
burning in a certain way, still burning.
evidence: The speaker catalogues intellectual pursuits (medieval Latin, Spengler, Whitehead, pre-Socratics) while attempting to understand existential questions ('whence/whither/what'), but the repeated 'say' construction and list-like recitation create affective distance from the intensity announced only at poem's end: 'burning in a certain way, still burning.' The emotion is acknowledged conceptually but processed through decades of scholarly activity.

author: J. Patrick Lewis
content:
Benny said
To Ruby Lee,
“M-A-R-R-Y M-E!”
Ruby said,
To Benny,
“Ben, wouldn't
That be F-U-N?”
Benny said,
“My Ruby Lee,
We will W-E-D
Only if you let me K-
I-S-S you
Every day!”
evidence: The poem is a straightforward courtship exchange between two children using playful spelling that demonstrates no psychological defensiveness—just innocent romantic proposal and acceptance.

author: J. Patrick Lewis
content:
It was such a lovely day
Till I stumbled in the way
Of a van that carries vinegar to stores.
When I glared up at the man—
Operator of the van—
He said, “Vinegar, it never rains but pours!”
Now a skunk was standing by.
And I thought that I would cry
From the vinegar that sprayed and soaked my clothes,
When the driver kindly yells,
“Watch that animal. It smells!”—
And the skunk ran off, a hankie to her nose.
evidence: The speaker narrates a slapstick mishap with vinegar and a skunk through wordplay and absurd reversal, but exhibits no psychological defensiveness toward the situation itself—it remains pure comic incident without underlying conflict requiring management.

author: J. Patrick Lewis
content:
Toad gots measles
Frog gots mumps
Both gots such
Disgusting bumps.
They so ugly
It's a sin.
They be jumpin'
Out that skin.
Pay no mind
These creepy items
Close my eyes
I bite 'ems, bite 'ems.
evidence: The speaker is a predatory snake narrating its hunting process toward prey (toad and frog) without psychological conflict or defensive stance—simply describing their unattractiveness as prelude to consumption.

author: Jennifer Moxley
content:
In the heavy fashion magazines strewn here and there around the house the photos of objects and people mouth the word “money,” but you, assuming no one wants you anymore, mishear the message as “meaning.” Arousal follows. The lives of the rich are so fabulous! The destruction of the poetical lies heavily on their hands, as on their swollen notion that we are always watching. There is nothing behind the mask. Nothing suffocating under its pressure, no human essence trying to get out.
     Awareness, always awareness. Don’t you see how these elaborate masks are turning you into a zombie? The private life is not for the eye but for the endless interior. It is trying to push all this crap aside and find the missing line. Nobody, least of all the future, cares about the outcome of this quest.
      It is easy to lose, through meddling or neglect, an entire aspect of existence. And sometimes, to cultivate a single new thought, you need not only silence but an entirely new life.
evidence: The speaker diagnoses cultural alienation ('elaborate masks are turning you into a zombie') and the 'atrophy of private life' through abstract theorizing about 'the destruction of the poetical' and 'nothing behind the mask,' delivering commentary on emotional impoverishment in a detached, essay-like register that analyzes rather than inhabits distress.

author: Donald Davie
content:
“stooped to truth and moralized his song”﻿



Spring pricks a little. I get out the maps.
 Time to demoralize my song, high time. Vernal a little. Primavera. First Green, first truth and last. High time, high time.   A high old time we had of it last summer? I overstate. But getting out the maps… Look! Up the valley of the Brenne, Louise de la Vallière… Syntax collapses. High time for that, high time.   To Château-Renault, the tannery town whose marquis Rooke and James Butler whipped in Vigo Bay Or so the song says, an amoral song Like Ronsard’s where we go today Perhaps, perhaps tomorrow.   Tomorrow and tomorrow and… Get well! Philip’s black-sailed familiar, avaunt Or some word as ridiculous, the whole Diction kit begins to fall apart. High time it did, high time.   High time and a long time yet, my love! Get out that blessed map. Ageing, you take your glasses off to read it. Stooping to truth, we potter to Montoire. High time, my love. High time and a long time yet.
evidence: The speaker repeatedly acknowledges affect-laden material (aging, mortality's 'tomorrow and tomorrow,' spring's erotic 'prick') while maintaining ironic, scholarly distance through literary allusions and meta-commentary on diction itself ('Syntax collapses. High time for that'). He talks about demoralization and emotional content but performs it through detached observations about maps, historical references, and the mechanics of language falling apart—acknowledging the territory of feeling while staying in the weatherman's booth.

author: Arthur Symons
content:
Side by side through the streets at midnight, Roaming together, Through the tumultuous night of London, In the miraculous April weather.
 
Roaming together under the gaslight, Day’s work over, How the Spring calls to us, here in the city, Calls to the heart from the heart of a lover!
 
Cool to the wind blows, fresh in our faces, Cleansing, entrancing, After the heat and the fumes and the footlights, Where you dance and I watch your dancing.
 
Good it is to be here together, Good to be roaming, Even in London, even at midnight, Lover-like in a lover’s gloaming.
 
You the dancer and I the dreamer, Children together, Wandering lost in the night of London, In the miraculous April weather. ﻿
evidence: The speaker transforms erotic and possessive desire into shared aesthetic experience—'You the dancer and I the dreamer'—channeling attachment intensity into collaborative urban wandering and spectatorship ('you dance and I watch your dancing') that maintains passionate investment while achieving socially valued, creative witness rather than consummation.

author: Ernest Dowson
content:
We have walked in Love's land a little way, We have learnt his lesson a little while, And shall we not part at the end of day, With a sigh, a smile?
A little while in the shine of the sun, We were twined together, joined lips, forgot How the shadows fall when the day is done, And when Love is not.
We have made no vows--there will none be broke, Our love was free as the wind on the hill, There was no word said we need wish unspoke, We have wrought no ill.
So shall we not part at the end of day, Who have loved and lingered a little while, Join lips for the last time, go our way, With a sigh, a smile? ﻿
evidence: The speaker frames parting from a love affair in emotionally detached, philosophical terms ('We have learnt his lesson a little while'), summarizing the relationship with bloodless rhetorical questions and logical accountancy ('no vows...no word said...no ill') that acknowledge feelings conceptually ('a sigh, a smile') but strip them of lived emotional weight. The tone resembles a weather report on the psyche—correct about love ending but delivered with curious affective flatness despite the intimate subject matter.

author: Edward Thomas
content:
The sorrow of true love is a great sorrow
And true love parting blackens a bright morrow:
Yet almost they equal joys, since their despair
Is but hope blinded by its tears, and clear
Above the storm the heavens wait to be seen.
But greater sorrow from less love has been
That can mistake lack of despair for hope
And knows not tempest and the perfect scope
Of summer, but a frozen drizzle perpetual
Of drops that from remorse and pity fall
And cannot ever shine in the sun or thaw,
Removed eternally from the sun’s law. ﻿
evidence: The speaker dissects love's sorrow through abstract categorization (true love versus lesser love, their respective despairs and temporal weather patterns) while maintaining conspicuous emotional distance—delivering what should be devastating emotional content in the measured, theoretical tone of taxonomic classification, as if mapping grief through meteorological metaphor rather than inhabiting its lived experience.

author: Ken Mikolowski
content:
Fighting a losing battle
 lives next door

to a vibrant woman

in her 30’s.

When he talks to her

sub-mediocre takes over

in a big way.

Zombie-ized by the big eye

she even sleeps with it on.

Just sign me: concerned.
evidence: The speaker redirects concern and judgment toward the neighbor woman's television addiction ('Zombie-ized by the big eye') while remaining conspicuously silent about the 'losing battle' fought by the figure who 'lives next door' to her—the original locus of worry is displaced onto a safer, more manageable target.

author: Joanne Kyger
content:
In Tamalpais is a big crystal. An acquaintance told
me the story. A Miwok was giving his grandfather’s medicine
bag to the Kroeber Museum in Berkeley. He said this man
took him over the mountain Tamalpais, at a certain time
in the year. I believe it was about the time of the
Winter Solstice, because then the tides are really low.
They stopped and gathered a certain plant on the way over
the mountain. On their way to the Bolinas Beach clam patch,
where there is a big rock way out there.
                                                                            Go out to
the rock. Take out of the medicine bag the crystal
that matches the crystal in Tamalpais. And
                                              if your heart is not true
                                              if your heart is not true
when you tap the rock in the clam patch
                                                            a little piece of it will fly off
                                                   and strike you in the heart
                          and strike you dead.
And that’s the first story I ever heard about Bolinas.
evidence: The speaker presents a received narrative about Indigenous sacred geography without psychological conflict—relates the story factually as heard, maintains reverent distance from the material, and integrates the crystalline legend into personal geography ('that's the first story I ever heard about Bolinas') with curiosity rather than defensive transformation.

poem_name: "When I used to focus on the worries, everybody"
author: Joanne Kyger
content:
When I used to focus on the worries, everybody
                      was ahead of me, I was the bottom
                of the totem pole,
              a largely spread squat animal.
How about a quick massage now, he said to me.
I don’t think it’s cool, I replied.
Oh, said he, after a pause, I should have waited
                     for you to ask me.
The waves came in closer and closer.
When I fall into the gap of suspicion I am no longer here.
In this world that has got closed over by houses
                       and networks, I fly out
from under the belly.     Life’s dizzy crown
of whirling lights, circles this head.    Pure
with wonder, hot
with wonder.    The streets become golden.     All
size increases, the colors glow, we are in myth.
We are in easy understanding.
Scarcely talking, thoughts pass between us.
                                    It is memory.    As I search to find
this day’s sweet drifting.    The fog out to sea, the wind.
evidence: The speaker transforms overwhelming affect ('the worries,' 'the gap of suspicion') into dreamlike altered states where consciousness fragments ('I am no longer here,' 'I fly out from under the belly') and ordinary reality becomes theatrical ('streets become golden,' 'we are in myth'), maintaining distance through imagery of flight and transcendence rather than integrated emotional processing.

author: Joanne Kyger
content:
He is pruning the privet
                of sickly sorrow   desolation
           in loose pieces of air he goes clip clip clip
       the green blooming branches fall—‘they’re getting out
             of hand’    delirious and adorable    what a switch
                               we perceive        multiple
identities     when you sing     so beautifully     the shifting
       clouds  You are not alone is this world
               not a lone  a parallel world of reflection
       in a window keeps the fire burning
                    in the framed mandala,  the red shafted flicker
               sits on the back of the garden chair in the rain
the red robed monks downtown in the rain  a rainbow arises
                   simple country      practices thunder
      lightning,  hail and rain    eight Douglas Iris
            ribbon layers of attention
              So   constant creation of ‘self’ is a tricky
       mess    He is pruning the loquat,   the olive
     which looks real enough in the damp late morning air
                                                                                          May 15, 1995
evidence: The speaker maintains metacognitive distance while observing internal states ('constant creation of self is a tricky / mess') and multiple shifting identities, reflecting on psychological patterns with curiosity rather than distortion. The pruning activity itself becomes vehicle for conscious awareness of attempts to manage internal states.

author: Robert Fernandez
content:
I
How can we accommodate these reforms? The nights of bell-flowers are as finished as the hell of water that has unrolled and become news. Pull at the ox’s ring and the wall of the sinuses falls down. Pull at the hoop in the eyelid, dormitories are felled. A marriage of fists and kites, the smile is hammered so painstakingly into the gut it forms a ring.
II
I am staring up at a boxing match in which white Everlasts and red Everlasts take on the breakneck speed of cupids. Art Deco façades hem in the open-air courtyard; a black belt of skyline circles off their incandescent white waists. The sunrise pulls level with the sea. The boxers’ shadows furl and unfurl, drawing into cups.
III
You open your heart’s wings like a bread riot, split the uncooked potatoes on the table with a glance, and eat. You make the hours work like fragile perceptions for the food they get, the warmth they get, for the variable, contradictory spontaneities imposed on their bodies as love or triumph in mistaken assertions.
evidence: The speaker transforms violence and suffering into formal, aestheticized observations ('The boxers' shadows furl and unfurl, drawing into cups'; 'the smile is hammered so painstakingly into the gut it forms a ring') that maintain conceptual engagement with brutality while stripping it of visceral emotional weight, presenting scenes of physical destruction and exploitation in detached, architectural terms.

author: Don Thompson
content:
I used to think the land
had something to say to us,
back when wildflowers
would come right up to your hand
as if they were tame.
Sooner or later, I thought,
the wind would begin to make sense
if I listened hard
and took notes religiously.
That was spring.
Now I’m not so sure:
the cloudless sky has a flat affect
and the fields plowed down after harvest
seem so expressionless,
keeping their own counsel.
This afternoon, nut tree leaves
blow across them
as if autumn had written us a long letter,
changed its mind,
and tore it into little scraps.
evidence: The speaker observes emotionally charged loss (spring's communicative intimacy replaced by autumn's torn letter, fields keeping 'their own counsel') but delivers it in affectless, clinical terms—'flat affect,' 'expressionless,' 'keeping their own counsel'—treating profound disillusionment as weather report on the psyche.

author: Will Alexander
content:
It is farming in an inclement sun system
like a powerless nether beast
fallen amidst random stellar debris
fruit changes form
light then quavers across distorted mural relics
the farmer then living as a clarified adder
his land forms compressed
his wheat suspended & flaring
his unstable forms
carving his soil with volcanic blue seeds
evidence: The poem presents a surrealist landscape of cosmic agricultural imagery without a discernible psychological stance toward conflict, loss, or threat—no defense mechanism is being deployed by the speaker.

author: Micah Ballard
content:
I see into them
as they see out of me
& dissolve the wattage
to avoid future legends
young pharaohs on Fillmore cracking dutches
it is a lonely frontier by contrast
forgotten game skulking around
big hearts, small temper
thine absence overflows
thine presence undoes
do not attempt to circle the inferno
a tremor in the throne
is a tremor in the throne
evidence: The speaker describes emotionally charged material—dissolution, loneliness, absence and presence, circling infernos—in curiously abstract, oracular language that maintains observational distance. Phrases like 'dissolve the wattage to avoid future legends' and 'thine absence overflows / thine presence undoes' deliver conceptual weight without visceral emotional engagement, creating a weather-report quality on psychic phenomena.

author: Micah Ballard
content:
Too many are leaving
     usually they greet in sleep before dashing
as in today with this gentleman
     (awkward not to type his name)
when yesterday in the shower
     I remembered his face in Aardvark
something about NWA but not about them
     just a played reference
There were newspaper clips
     all police brutality, all framed with snow
& I vaguely recalled something
     about Uma Thurman & the Menils
when the guard ushered me out
     for touching the African sculptures
I waited in the lobby for hours
     like this morning reading
that he overdosed. It was a hotel
     & I didn’t even know him
I don’t even know his work
evidence: The speaker recounts a death by overdose with affectless, chronicle-like detachment—'I remembered his face in Aardvark...It was a hotel & I didn't even know him'—delivering emotionally charged material (greeting in sleep, police brutality, overdose) in the flat tone of inventory, converting loss into factual report without accessing grief.

author: Micah Ballard
content:
I refuse to settle
out of the true
choose to communicate
through pay phones and paper
out here in the country of McAllister & Steiner
we are the only Victorians left
& I wish I wouldn’t have said
that one something that one time
when what I really wanted
was to finish cutting the okra for the gumbo
& read Baudelaire again
his L’Invitation au Voyage
backwards reveals all of the magic
in writing that one can endure
each line out loud resounds perfect
all week I’ve been thinking about printing
a pirated book of such, nothing grand
typed on the Remington, 3x4, no address
no copyright and fifty limited
for whomever I run into
evidence: The speaker channels primitive impulses (regret over 'that one something that one time,' incomplete domestic tasks) into sustained creative engagement—obsessive planning of a pirated Baudelaire book, reading backwards for magic, typing on Remington—transforming conflict into socially valued aesthetic production ('nothing grand') that maintains emotional authenticity while achieving distance through craft.

author: Diane di Prima
content:
for Jackson Allen



My friend wears my scarf at his waist
I give him moonstones
He gives me shell & seaweeds
He comes from a distant city & I meet him
We will plant eggplants & celery together
He weaves me cloth
                   Many have brought the gifts
                   I use for his pleasure
                   silk, & green hills
                   & heron the color of dawn
My friend walks soft as a weaving on the wind
He backlights my dreams
He has built altars beside my bed
I awake in the smell of his hair & cannot remember
his name, or my own.
evidence: The friend is attributed exaggerated, near-magical positive qualities (walks 'soft as a weaving on the wind,' 'backlights my dreams,' builds altars), receiving gifts from many sources for his pleasure. The speaker's self dissolves in merger-like terms ('cannot remember his name, or my own'), suggesting archaic idealization where connection to the perfect object produces loss of self-boundaries.

author: Don Bogen
content:
Air as lost time

Voice of a cloud, of a ghost crowned with nimbus

Smack-thin, it lingers forty years

I thought it came from the jeweled world we’d seen

Everything stuffed, urgent, glittering alive

But it was just pleasure, blank and sure

Now what is there to sing

From speakers, the tune folds and fades in waves

Earphones drive it through your head
evidence: The speaker transforms a forty-year emotional experience into abstract concepts ('Air as lost time,' 'Voice of a cloud'), delivering what should be affectively charged memory in curiously detached, theoretical language. The poem acknowledges pleasure and loss cognitively ('it was just pleasure, blank and sure') while maintaining observational distance through impersonal constructions and sensory metaphors that hold experience at arm's length.

author: Don Bogen
content:
Give it back—I made it all up

That alcove where surplus glowed under dust

Unfinished, an attic space with nails poking down

Khaki of sheet metal, orange flickering in tubes

Ephemeral as the smells, which were plywood, solder, and Kents

Color words, smell words—I put them in a book

Everything there is still missing
