God, the beating, lit, and doing what it does.
evidence: The speaker meticulously catalogs physical details and conceptual abstractions ('vacancy and abject,' 'philosophy of once and then not') while maintaining conspicuous emotional flatness, moving through loss-laden material (empty bed, absent child) with weather-report detachment. The final shift to 'I envy her' attempts magical reversal, imagining the lost presence alive and nurtured as if to undo the vacancy named throughout.

author: Natasha Trethewey
content:
What's left is footage: the hours before
             Camille, 1969—hurricane
                         parties, palm trees leaning
in the wind,
            fronds blown back,
a woman's hair. Then after:
            the vacant lots,
            boats washed ashore, a swamp
where graves had been. I recall
how we huddled all night in our small house,
            moving between rooms,
                        emptying pots filled with rain.
The next day, our house—
           on its cinderblocks—seemed to float
           in the flooded yard: no foundation
beneath us, nothing I could see
                          tying us                      to the land.
                          In the water, our reflection
                                                               trembled,
disappeared
when I bent to touch it.
evidence: The speaker rehearses traumatic memory of Hurricane Camille with measured affective engagement—'I recall / how we huddled all night'—spreading anxiety across temporal stages (before/during/after) through detailed cataloging of threat and survival. The flood imagery and absent foundation create realistic preparation for ongoing precarity without catastrophizing.

author: Alice Oswald
content:
Born on Monday and a tiny
world-containing grain of light
passed through each eye like heaven through a needle.
And on Tuesday
he screamed for a small ear in which to hide.
He rolled on Wednesday, rolled his whole body
full of immense salt spaces, slowly
from one horizon to the other.
And on Thursday, trembling, crippled,
broke beyond his given strength and crawled.
And on Friday he stood upright.
And on Saturday he tested a footstep
and the sky came down and alit on his shoulder
full of various languages in which one bird doesn’t answer to another.
And on Sunday he dreamed he was flying
and his mind grew gold watching the moon
and he began to sing to the brink of speaking
evidence: The speaker chronicles development day-by-day through bodily milestones (screaming, rolling, crawling, standing), experiencing and spreading out incremental doses of growth anxiety ('trembling, crippled, broke beyond his given strength') while preparing for each threshold—classic self-inoculation through realistic, affect-laden planning.

author: Joshua Edwards
content:
Wrongheaded and obsequious
on vacation, unnerved
by new surroundings, I miss
the bright feeling of belonging
and the familiar patterns of my country,
its virginity and schizophrenia,
my several stolen bicycles.
evidence: The speaker adopts a detached, self-analyzing tone ('Wrongheaded and obsequious') to describe emotional dislocation, then displaces homesickness onto an abstracted catalog of national qualities ('virginity and schizophrenia') and trivial objects ('stolen bicycles'), avoiding direct engagement with the actual affective distress of being unmoored.

author: Joshua Edwards
content:
Translation broadens language
as divorce and remarriage extend family.
Born to fade and break, facts
huddle inside black brackets.
Work means inquisition as a child
separates a cricket’s wings from thorax.
Ideas come apart as monads, metastasizing
rhapsody on the edge of delicate dusk.
Thunder sounds in the distance or television,
always on in this constant rain.
evidence: The speaker processes potentially distressing content—dissolution ('fade and break'), violent curiosity ('child separates a cricket's wings'), metastasizing disease, constant rain—through abstract conceptual frameworks ('Translation broadens language,' 'Ideas come apart as monads') that maintain analytical distance while evacuating emotional resonance.

author: Christina Davis
content:
It was something to let him go.
          It was a having to believe, furthermore,
in the voyage
of the other, a Ulysses
without an Ithaca,
was to speak
of the sea
without speech
of the shore—
and to have for a body
the going away of the body, to have for eyes
the going away of the eyes. And for hearing,
a silence, where once
were people.
And for comfort, a dwelling
before each
steps into that weather
of which all
strangers speak.
evidence: The speaker explicitly acknowledges the painful loss ('It was something to let him go') while maintaining conscious awareness of both absence and continuing relationship, postponing closure through meditation on departure as ongoing process ('to have for a body / the going away of the body'). The phrase 'furthermore' signals active cognitive work to hold the conflict rather than repress it.

author: Christina Davis
content:
Someone enters: That alone
makes me speak.
When you lived, I spoke to you.
We are transitive.
I never dream of speech:
I either speak or don’t.
And now that I know
where you will not be—
I do not go there.
evidence: The speaker explicitly states 'now that I know / where you will not be— / I do not go there,' describing deliberate avoidance of places associated with the deceased. The flat, declarative tone ('I either speak or don't') and transitive grammar ('We are transitive') maintain cognitive awareness while evacuating emotional weight from the loss.

author: Raymond McDaniel
content:
you are listening to
the transmission via seed pearl, aural irritant
clockwork and sparkgap ultra high and superlow
precious black opal crystal and glass shudders and sings
broad cast scattered the seeds among the apocrypha
each agent at land or sea satellite or space
direct conversion of royal register and groove
high in fidelity to
our regenerative radio hisses and shouts
everything that cannot be owned belongs now to us
irradiant waves oscillate below visible light
to arrive and reside requires no medium but occupies vacuum
and air transformational emission
follow your radiotelegraph
we are your conductor our amplitude varies
we fluctuate the frequency
we are not subject to static interference
we embed the subcarrierhush y’all
you need not know that language if you know this sound
evidence: The speaker claims magical control over transmission and reality itself ('we fluctuate the frequency,' 'we are not subject to static interference'), collapsing boundary between internal will and external physics. The voice asserts unlimited power over communication channels and listeners ('you need not know that language if you know this sound') with no acknowledgment of separate agency in receivers.

author: Fady Joudah
content:
My daughter
                        wouldn’t hurt a spider
That had nested
Between her bicycle handles
For two weeks
She waited
Until it left of its own accord
If you tear down the web I said
It will simply know
This isn’t a place to call home
And you’d get to go biking
She said that’s how others
Become refugees isn’t it?
evidence: The daughter turns to her father to collaborate on understanding her moral dilemma ('She said that's how others / Become refugees isn't it?'), using relational dialogue to process the conflict between her desire to bike and her empathy for the spider, seeking connection and mutual perspective-taking rather than solitary problem-solving.

author: Johannes Göransson
content: Knowledge is Power. That is what the billboard says and I agree. I am an adult, therefore I understand the threat of passengers. The threat to Our Children, who don't understand the threat of these bird-like, twitchy people. They pose two kinds of threat. To begin with, there is the one we all know about, the preda­tory threat, the hawk-like passengers that prey on children as they sit in front of their computers or televisions. The terrorist threat. That threat is easy to handle. You shoot it. You contain it. You confiscate. You stitch. You bleed from various orifices and sockets, but you survive, you rebuild house and rinse the child. The more serious threat is the diseases passengers carry with them. Internal terrorism. Children love those diseases. It makes them babble like possessed. Their make-up looks like oil in the moonlight. Such children cannot be cleaned off. Kill them. Or turn them into entertainment. Art.
evidence: The speaker misattributes internal threatening impulses to external 'passengers' who become persecutory objects carrying diseases that possess children, requiring violent elimination. The paranoid certainty ('I am an adult, therefore I understand') and totalizing division into predatory threats versus vulnerable children reveals primitive projection maintaining rigid boundaries through violence.

author: J. Patrick Lewis
content:
for Eleanor Roosevelt



Who showed the world the world itself
     Was awkward, shy and plain.
A high-born leader in a long,
     Low decade full of pain.
Poor farmers, blacks, homeless, the least
     Advantaged hoped to see,
Magnificently unarrayed,
     Pure human dignity.
A lady first, the great first lady
     Looked fear in the face,
And said, There is no room for fear
     When courage take its place.
evidence: The speaker attributes exaggerated positive qualities and transformative power to Roosevelt ('magnificently,' 'pure human dignity,' 'the great first lady'), elevating her to emblematic status as protector of the vulnerable. The idealization serves to manage anxiety about social suffering by investing hope in a perfect figure who can 'show the world the world itself.'

author: J. Patrick Lewis
content:
for Gertrude Ederle



As Europe woke from sleep,
Young Trudy Ederle
At Cap Gris Nez in France
Dived into a daunting sea.
Many had tried to make
This superhuman swim—
Thirty-five punishing miles.
Chances, at best, were slim.
When Fury found the waves,
Far from the western shore,
Her trainer shouted, "Let's turn back!"
But Trudy cried, "What for?"
Under an English moon,
The celebration began
After the fastest crossing
By woman or by man.
evidence: Trudy's 'What for?' in response to her trainer's urging to turn back demonstrates direct claiming of autonomous agency in the face of external pressure to retreat; the narrative celebrates her forward-moving competence ('fastest crossing / By woman or by man') without inflation or apology.

author: J. Patrick Lewis
content:
Jook joint's about to come alive!
It's like a bee outside a hive;
And when the hive begins to hum,
It's like a dim and distant drum;
And when the drum begins to beat,
It's like a circus down the street;
And when the street begins to fill,
It's like a sudden summer chill;
And just as summer simmers down,
It's like the fireworks over town.
Don't matter what the preachers say—
This congregation's here to stay
'Cause when the jook joint comes alive
Is when the Delta blues arrive!
evidence: The poem describes a communal scene of joyful musical gathering through serial similes without psychological conflict or defensive operations—the speaker celebrates rather than manages distress.

author: J. Patrick Lewis
content:
Superman flies onto his first comic book.
Oil bubbles up in Saudi Arabia.
Orson Welles' The War of the Worlds invades
every panicked radio along the eastern seaboard.
The Spanish Civil War rages on. Filming starts
on The Wizard of Oz. At New York City's
Carnegie Hall, John Hammond's SpiritualsTo Swing concert explodes with African chants,
the Count Basie Band, boogie-woogie,
New Orleans jazz, hot gospel, stride piano,
harmonica instrumentals, Big Bill Broonzy's
blues. The audience hears the ghost of Robert
Johnson, four months gone, easing out
of a Victrola phonograph at center stage—
the entire concert suddenly enveloped
by the man who was not there.
evidence: The poem catalogs historical events from 1938 without psychological stance—it functions as documentary ekphrasis rather than defense against internal conflict.

author: Philip Nikolayev
content:
Roaring roast cake with bean base spareribs for me,
carottes étouffées medium rare,
I like them a tad undercooked, still red
with sap, tea leaves in olive oil,
strawberry sushi flummoxed
to the point of deliquescence,
or better still, freshly picked
cucumber rolls to match
the lettuce steak, mesquite broiled
to a crunchy andante, with all
organic granola salsa, nuts
nutritious to the max, and then of course the
soypork casserole with legs
of boletus, and tofu chops on a platter
of tomato paste base salmon with
a sprinkling of beet juice droplets,
all served with a rich broccoli broth.
evidence: The poem is a playful formal experiment cataloguing absurd vegan dishes with culinary terminology ('medium rare,' 'mesquite broiled') applied to vegetables, creating ludic incongruity without psychological defensiveness.

author: Lorine Niedecker
content:
I rose from marsh mud,
algae, equisetum, willows,
sweet green, noisy
birds and frogs
to see her wed in the rich
rich silence of the church,
the little white slave-girl
in her diamond fronds.
In aisle and arch
the satin secret collects.
United for life to serve
silver. Possessed.
evidence: The speaker divides the wedding scene into all-good ('rich rich silence of the church,' 'diamond fronds') and all-bad ('slave-girl,' 'Possessed') without integration, assigning pure virtue to wealth/church aesthetics while assigning total corruption to the bride's captivity, maintaining both valuations simultaneously without synthesis.

author: Rebecca Hazelton
content:
A dirigible powered us through the first leg,
            traversing snow-capped mountains where goats
leapt from crags, and men with wrinkled
            apples faces looked up, pointed.
Smiling
            to cold air, I slept under a bear skin,
                        touched your lips in the night.
            On our pleasant soap
bubble journey, we dropped baskets down
            each morning, hooked meats, cheeses;
            in later weeks, nothing. Still, we took notes,
detailing the weather conditions, the migrating
            elk numbers and their steady decrease.
Northward by cracked compass,
            the sleet sheeting the balloon drove us low, then lower—
In time we cut through the ice, sank
fathoms into the sea, chaperoned by seals
            that are not mermaids at all.
Else you think this a hoax, know my hand—
down here still—please send help.
evidence: The speaker recounts a catastrophic journey—starvation, crashing through ice, sinking 'fathoms into the sea'—in the detached tone of scientific field notes ('took notes, / detailing the weather conditions'), maintaining observational distance even while literally drowning and pleading for rescue. The final cry 'please send help' arrives stripped of panic, as if filing a weather report on one's own demise.

author: Philip Lamantia
content:
Because the dark suit is worn it is worn warm
    with a black tie
and a kiss at the head of the stairs
When you hear the dark suit rip
on the heart’s curb the hurt is big
     rose flesh caught on the orange woman’s buttons
As you talk metropole monotone
                  antique intelligence 
as you dress wounds by peyotl looming the boulevards
women hunt their children from you
who look out
                  lit still inside of a dark suit
evidence: The speaker describes being 'lit still inside of a dark suit' while 'dress[ing] wounds by peyotl looming the boulevards'—maintaining consciousness but fragmenting experience into dreamlike, theatrical imagery. The dissociative alteration of consciousness transforms overwhelming material (ripping heart, hurt, rose flesh caught on buttons) into surreal, playful sequences that charm through their hallucinogenic quality rather than directly confronting pain.

author: Philip Lamantia
content:
Choppers in the night husk the brilliants of thought
Beyond the cities of patina grow caves of thought
Coyote Hummingbird Owl are rivers of thought
The lumens the pumpkins dance: pits of correspondence over the land
Birds the dream tongues warble Iroquois Mojavé Ohlone
Market Street of “The Mad Doctor” via the occult centers
A gang of fox spirits at the crossroadsBandoleros set between the obliteration of grizzly bears painted by an Arcimboldist and the monstrance of bleeding chains
Montezuma’s feathery headdress torn up in the boondocks of the Rosy Cross
Coyote girls in myth-time
At the central dream of edenic treasures
The irrevocable annihilation of christian civilization is taking shape with carnivorous flowers of volcanic thought
evidence: The speaker channels primitive and potentially violent imagery (obliteration of grizzly bears, annihilation of civilization, carnivorous flowers) into densely layered surrealist poetry that maintains passionate intensity while transforming historical trauma and aggressive impulses into socially valued artistic creation with real aesthetic consequences.

author: John Yau
content:
It had to be from someone whose grandparents were born in Shanghai
not the city’s greatest citizens, but certainly among the sober ones
to make their small now eroded mark
It had to be from a distant or dissolute descendant (yes, moi)
who can sing praises unworthy of even a flicker of your attention
Doesn’t this sound like it might turn into a love poem or a prayer
Well, you are wrong, because a man of the people,
which I am not nor will I ever be,
doesn’t single out one above all others
as this is a hierarchical construction
and therefore undemocratic and antihumanist
It had to be from someone whose virtues do not include stubbornness,
patience, gentleness, loyalty, or truthfulness
It had to be from someone who could take my place
after I left the room
never to return
It had to be from someone who didn’t exist
before this poem
began writing itself down
evidence: The speaker simultaneously constructs and dismantles their self-portrait with layered awareness ('a distant or dissolute descendant (yes, moi)'), watches the poem's ideological contradictions unfold ('Doesn't this sound like it might turn into a love poem or a prayer / Well, you are wrong'), and observes the speaker-self dissolve into textual process ('someone who didn't exist / before this poem / began writing itself').

author: John Yau
content:
There are rooftops
made of cloud remnants
gathered by a trader
dabbling in car parts and burlap
At night, I dive onto the breeze
fermenting above the dirt
and dream that I am a crocodile
a tin of shoe polish, an audience of two
In the morning, before the smallest yawn
becomes a noodle, I am offered
a ribbon of yellow smoke
I opt for fuzzy rocks and clawed water
and, of course, the perishable window
I am one of the last computer
chain errors to be illuminated
I tell you there are rooftops
on which the moon stops
being a cold jewel
And one by one the mountains
begin their descent from
the chambers of a lost book
evidence: The poem offers surrealist imagery ('I dive onto the breeze fermenting above the dirt / and dream that I am a crocodile / a tin of shoe polish') without discernible psychological conflict, interpersonal stance, or defensive function—it reads as formal experiment in metamorphic imagery rather than stance toward distress.

poem_name: Thought Experiment: Twin Earth and the Glass of Water*
author: Anna Moschovakis
content:
That was the day your mesmerized went awol on the shore. Each star, you said, was the same star. Beneath our sunny beach blanket an anonymous moisture spread like fur. It’s getting dark and darker.
 
Dusk is hard. Gossip travels worst when it’s low-contrast out; it stalls, and the air begins to crack. Droplets black as blood squeeze through fissures, making night.
 
What came from above—the forestscan, the cornucump, the mar—came violently on our feet. Finally, when it was time to order, you pulled yourself up from the syntax we’d shared and beckoned for me to come. It’s just like rain, I said, as if to make amends. Every arm is the same arm, you replied, and took mine.
 

* In which an unwitting traveler to a parallel universe, offered trum and toke all night, drinks himself out of his depth to drown the unknown source of his discomfort.
evidence: The speaker narrates deeply disorienting, affectively charged experiences (anonymous moisture spreading, air cracking, night squeezing through fissures, drowning) in detached, philosophical tones borrowed from thought-experiment discourse, transforming overwhelming sensory/relational breakdown into abstract conceptual play.

poem_name: Thought Experiment: Mary in the Black-and-White Room*
author: Anna Moschovakis
content:
Some things lock in competition, like an earthquake and a kiss. While a decision is waiting to be made, neither side of the argument progresses. The earthquake, though eager to prove its claim, shows valiant restraint; the kiss? It knows the power of bitten tongues.
 
Such stand-offs as these precede most gains (stance of knowing too much and fearing too late). The tongues, shaking along with the house, say nothing shattering at all.
 
With progress, not only earthquakes but kisses will be predicted. The last fine line between feeling and fact will choose a good point, and end. Flattery will continue to make us immortal in the difficult years between the first word and the lost.
 

* In which Mary, herself a palette of grays, inhabitant of a universe void of color, gains access to the complete scientific story of what makes red red—and reads it.
evidence: The speaker frames urgent emotional conflicts (earthquake vs. kiss, feeling vs. fact) in abstract philosophical language ('stand-offs precede most gains,' 'stance of knowing too much') that systematically drains affective charge. Mary's encounter with color—potentially transformative—is reduced to reading 'the complete scientific story,' rendering lived experience as theoretical construct.

author: Tyrone Williams
content:
I left my heart in the teeth of jumper-cables—
black tongue, superfluous nipples . . .By The Time I hit the yellow tape—
it was already turning red . . .
Of my fair and alabaster love?
My redundant chains drawn in chalk?Halfway to the stars I stopped—
turned, spat—it’s too late, baby . . .
evidence: Speaker recounts catastrophic romantic loss (heart in jumper-cables, yellow tape turning red, alabaster love) in detached, fragmented syntax with noir-cinematic imagery, delivering weather-report tone for traumatic material. The 'it's too late, baby' arrives as cool dismissal rather than emotionally saturated grief.

author: Lynn Xu
content:
Lie me down to heal in sleep, do not let me wake
In sin, the tongue
Cancels another year, another painted storm
In the coral caves, some pious poet
Drunk on vapors
Swatting tomb-bats in the nightwood, would that
Wayward bark sunned white
Be also thunder, a hill of bones drumming—thud
Thud, a wake
Of buzzards braiding into the loosening skull—the redoubled fists
Of students like an island in the bramble chained—I have been told
To reason, lawless, empty, without rights—
But I am old
Not age, I have been told
To match its columns by our footfall, prophet—I am not
The straw or garland of our Sirens, not the brow
Of holly, nor the warble
Of any lark
evidence: The speaker fragments across incompatible self-states ('I am old / Not age') and theatrical personae ('pious poet / Drunk on vapors') while addressing Baudelaire, creating dreamlike temporal ruptures and out-of-body imagery ('a wake / Of buzzards braiding into the loosening skull') that transforms traumatic confrontation with mortality into playful, seductive linguistic performance—watching death from aesthetic distance rather than inhabiting its terror.

poem_name: I never hear that one is dead (1325)
author: Emily Dickinson
content:
I never hear that one is dead
Without the chance of Life
Afresh annihilating me
That mightiest Belief,
Too mighty for the Daily mind
That tilling it’s abyss,
Had Madness, had it once or, Twice
The yawning Consciousness,
Beliefs are Bandaged, like the Tongue
When Terror were it told
In any Tone commensurate
Would strike us instant Dead -
I do not know the man so bold
He dare in lonely Place
That awful stranger - Consciousness
Deliberately face -
evidence: The speaker describes overwhelming terror and the threat of madness in abstract, theoretical terms ('That mightiest Belief,' 'the yawning Consciousness') while maintaining affective flatness—reporting that beliefs must be 'Bandaged' to prevent being 'struck instant Dead,' yet delivering this account without corresponding emotional engagement, like a weather report on psychic catastrophe.

poem_name: The Mushroom is the Elf of Plants - (1350)
author: Emily Dickinson
content:
The Mushroom is the Elf of Plants -
At Evening, it is not
At Morning, in a Truffled Hut
It stop opon a Spot
As if it tarried always
And yet it’s whole Career
Is shorter than a Snake’s Delay -
And fleeter than a Tare -
’Tis Vegetation’s Juggler -
The Germ of Alibi -
Doth like a Bubble antedate
And like a Bubble, hie -
I feel as if the Grass was pleased
To have it intermit -
This surreptitious Scion
Of Summer’s circumspect.
Had Nature any supple Face
Or could she one contemn -
Had Nature an Apostate -
That Mushroom - it is Him!
evidence: The speaker adopts a naturalist's observational stance toward the mushroom's fleeting lifecycle and ephemeral nature, cataloging its qualities with whimsical precision but without psychological conflict requiring defensive management.

poem_name: Glass was the Street - in Tinsel Peril (1518)
author: Emily Dickinson
content:
Glass was the Street - in Tinsel Peril
Tree and Traveller stood.
Filled was the Air with merry venture
Hearty with Boys the Road.
Shot the lithe Sleds like Shod vibrations
Emphacized and gone
It is the Past’s supreme italic
Makes the Present mean -
evidence: The poem presents a formal meditation on memory and temporal contrast through winter imagery, without evidence of psychological defense against underlying conflict.

poem_name: black herman’s last asrah levitation at magic city, Atlanta 2010
author: LaTasha N. Nevada Diggs
content:
This exclusive shit I don’t share with the world.
50 Cent



I, Herman, made medicinal — concocted potions in ways my former’s was hearsay;
Turned palomas christened Zora on to formulas husbands roll over n mitzvah.
I, a black lad, proud Virginian, selling out Liberty Hall n pinched w/ stickpins
in Woodlawn, do bequeath my next-to-last oratory:
               My roots subverted the man,
               honeys n dog voyagers to Neptune,
               who dared interfere w/ your melodious saccharine midsection.
               My cluster of tricks made chaps seek out connotation.
               Look at my magic stick.
               Not my clavicles, but my magic stick.
               Ain’t no lightness of hand but of bounce player.
               Constraints imposed by a corvid named Jim
               could not interpret my remedies.                              Jim wasn’t much of a MacGyver:                              not one skill in therapeutic thaumaturgy.                              He prescribed cowlicks for the heartsick: I mean, really.
evidence: The speaker (in persona of Black Herman) claims competence and power directly without apology: 'I, Herman, made medicinal... My roots subverted the man... My cluster of tricks made chaps seek out connotation.' The assertive first-person declarations ('Look at my magic stick') reclaim agency and skill in contexts of racial constraint, transforming historical subjugation into performative mastery.

author: Gina Franco
content:
As soon as possible, I will confront the wren’s
              doings, rinse the white streaks from the porch bricks
drawing lizards from their shade, the immediate
              smell of water too much for all of us.
But first is lunch. The remains we’ll scatter over
              the driveway away from the bricks. Wrens come,
crusts from our dishes make drama. Then history.
What is possible in memory is disingenuous.
              Limestone, impressed with the archaic smile
of bone and reptilian wrists, wishbones and feathers,
              describes. It cups the transitional form,
naturally selecting one’s best side. There was
              the time you forgot your legs no longer
could recall how to stand—then rose up straight and sang              You’ll come a-waltzing Matilda with me
Probably I’ve been thinking of that since August.
              The indelible wrens grate like shovels
outside—exhumed, one voice rises from wilderness,
              echoes,
                            settles, rests
                                          —then another, and,
between them, the keep of an unerring quiet.
evidence: The speaker explicitly postpones emotional confrontation ('As soon as possible, I will confront') while maintaining full awareness of the painful memory ('Probably I've been thinking of that since August'), choosing to tend to immediate domestic tasks (lunch, clearing wrens) before addressing the deeper grief of watching someone's dying moment of delusional standing and singing.

author: Robert Hedin
content:
I spent the winter my father died down in the basement,
under the calm surface of the floorboards, hundreds
of little plastic parts spread out like debris
on the table. And for months while the snow fell
and my father sat in the big chair by the Philco, dying,
I worked my way up deck by deck, story by story,
from steerage to first class, until at last it was done,
stacks, deck chairs, all the delicate rigging.
And there it loomed, a blazing city of the dead.
Then painted the gaping hole at the waterline
and placed my father at the railings, my mother
in a lifeboat pulling away from the wreckage.
evidence: The speaker channels overwhelming grief and helplessness about his father's dying into obsessive model-building ('worked my way up deck by deck, story by story'), transforming primitive feelings about death into socially valued creative activity that produces a tangible object with real emotional consequences—the completed model becomes a symbolic vessel for processing loss.

author: Jake Adam York
content:
And the way the jury chose to believe the ridiculous stories of the defense. . . .
     — Mamie Till, 1955
. . . with truth absent, hypocrisy and myth have flourished. . . .     —Look, January 1956
The sheriff says it wasn't Till we pulled from the river,
that man was as white as I am, white as cotton
blowed by the cotton gin fan that weighed him down,
looked like he'd lain there weeks, not a kid at all.
He was a stranger just out of Money, recalled
by a store clerk, a hobo, and a crossroad guitarist.
The reporter finds them at the once abandoned crossing.
They say it's like the sheriff says, came up one night,
headed Clarksdale way, another one, hat pulled down,
right behind. Three days later, the bluesman says,
a plague of starlings gathered into little boys
those who fished and found the dead man's foot.
The reporter stares into his cataracted, cotton eyes.
He cannot find them, no matter where he looks.
evidence: The sheriff's insistence 'it wasn't Till we pulled from the river, / that man was as white as I am' directly refuses to acknowledge the external reality of Emmett Till's lynching. The voice reports testimony that transforms the murdered Black child into nameless white men, hobos, and supernatural plague imagery ('starlings gathered into little boys'), obliterating documented facts through collective refusal.

poem_name: Confessions of a Recycled Shopping Bag
author: John Yau
content:
I used to be a plastic bottle
I used to be scads of masticated wattle
I used to be epic spittle, aka septic piddle
I used to be a pleasant colleague
I used to be a radiant ingredient
I used to be a purple polyethylene pony
I used to be a phony upload project
I used to be a stony blue inhalant
I used to be a family-size turquoise bottle
I used to be a domesticated pink bubble
I used to be a pleasant red colleague
I used to be a beaming cobalt emollient
I used to be a convenient chartreuse antidepressant
evidence: The speaker's stance is not defensive but playfully iterative, performing transformations through linguistic play rather than managing psychological conflict.

author: Ko Un
content:
All day long I've been hidden, enmeshed, locked in
the shadow of the holy mountain,
my body covered with shame.
I'd hoped there would be a dog
barking furiously.
Unable to go forward,
unable to go back,
at least with the sound of a dog barking
I'd attain something . . . something.
Instead of a dog barking there was silence,
then the sound of a drum beaten loud & fast
by a young nun.
Into this temple come none of the grand sounds,
none of the great thoughts.
In the cabbage field, heads all the same size
are profiles of young novice nuns & the priestesses
sitting in Unmun Temple.
Young faces
like dew,
like hoarfrost.
evidence: The speaker explicitly acknowledges overwhelming shame and paralysis ('covered with shame,' 'Unable to go forward, / unable to go back') while remaining consciously present to the distress, seeking—not escape—but 'something' attainable within the unbearable situation. The conflict is held in awareness, postponed rather than repressed, as attention shifts from internal torment to observing the drum, the silence, the novices.

author: Ko Un
content:
What a relief
you cannot live everywhere all at once.
Today, here in Diamond Cave,
there's no longer any reason to live.
Stay one or two days:
this world
& the Other are drained of difference.
Wind blows.
As a pearl is born at seabottom in agony
out of oyster flesh from within the most obscure darkness
here the wind blows from the depths.
I want to travel far & then return.
The wind blows as if I were eighty-five,
maybe eighty-seven.
evidence: The speaker worries realistically about aging and mortality ('The wind blows as if I were eighty-five, maybe eighty-seven'), paying dues of anxiety in advance through affect-laden contemplation of future diminishment, while sublimating this dread into poetic meditation that transforms fear into socially valuable creative expression.

author: John Barr
content:
Spiders in the cold,
bees in inarticulate bunches
hang from a day's work.
Waiting for light they wait
to see what they will be.
A tree lets down
green undersides and is maple.
A window glints—
a thing of saffron
kindles with singlehood.
In the broad yard
each thing dandles
its blue, its name, its consequence.
evidence: The poem offers descriptive observation of dawn phenomena—spiders, bees, maple, window, yard—without revealing a speaker's psychological stance toward distress or conflict.

poem_name: from Articles of War, Section V
author: John Barr
content:
I stare down into waterburn.
This urge to enter what we see.
Unrefracted tropical sun
with its whole arm
works deeply the ocean interior.
Water and light in union
make a third thing—color as fluid
deepened endlessly.
Into the quarry of aquamarine,
high-walled with light, the mind high dives.
My fingers cleave watersilk.
I breathe heavy light.
The big cavitation of the props
gone by, my struggling stops, my slowed
descent, in diminishing light,
gains the country where the shark
is eagle, fish the fishermen, and men
no more than stones along the road.
evidence: The speaker describes descent into an internal fantasy realm ('the mind high dives,' 'I breathe heavy light') that culminates in radical detachment from human engagement—men reduced to 'no more than stones along the road,' with 'struggling stops' suggesting cessation of vital investment in surface reality.

poem_name: Song VII (“My song has put off her adornments”)
author: Rabindranath Tagore
content:
My song has put off her adornments.
She has no pride of dress and decoration.
Ornaments would mar our union;
they would come between thee and me;
their jingling would drown thy whispers.
 
My poet’s vanity dies in shame before thy sight.
O master poet, I have sat down at thy feet.
Only let me make my life simple and straight,
like a flute of reed for thee to fill with music.
evidence: The speaker elevates the 'master poet' to omnipotent position ('I have sat down at thy feet') while systematically stripping away all self-worth and autonomous creative power ('My poet's vanity dies in shame before thy sight'), seeking merger with an idealized divine figure who will 'fill' the emptied, passive self. The progressive undressing and self-erasure ('put off her adornments,' 'no pride,' making life 'simple and straight') functions as ritualistic atonement for the transgression of having had poetic vanity.

author: Catherine Wagner
content:
In this poem all artifice
is stripped away
but you are held under water.
In this poem you enter a mirrored dressing room
lit so that you look more beautiful than you have ever looked.
I recognize you with surprise.
In this poem you are by yourself.
evidence: The poem oscillates between contradictory presentations of the addressed 'you' without integration: stripped of all artifice yet held under water (suffocated/punished), then shown in impossibly flattering light ('more beautiful than you have ever looked'), followed by recognition 'with surprise' and final isolation. These incompatible framings—exposure/drowning versus transfiguring beauty versus solitude—are held as separate scenarios rather than synthesized, creating compartmentalized good and bad versions of the same object.

author: David Yezzi
content:
The only time I’ve been to Switzerland
was early one spring on a train through the mountains.
There was a lake—I guess it was Lucerne?
Above me cliff tops ridged with snow fanned out
so that where I stood at the edge of the platform
light bathed the empty siding all around
with a diffused opalescence off the water.
Behind the station must have been a town,
spires of churches, municipal arcades,
and coffee squelching in the fogged cafés.
I never saw the place, though I remember
thinking this is Switzerland and took
a mind-shot of the pines, breathing in the cold
as the porter whistled at us to reboard.
evidence: The speaker consciously acknowledges incomplete experience ('I never saw the place') while maintaining awareness of both desire to see and temporal constraint ('as the porter whistled at us to reboard'), postponing fuller engagement without repression or complaint—a balanced holding of limitation and conscious memory.

author: Cleopatra Mathis
content:
We pretended to know nothing about it.
I withdrew to childhood training: stay out
of swampy undergrowth, choked edges.
This was around the time
we were too cruel to kill the mice we caught,
leaving them in the Have-a-Heart trap
under the sun-burning bramble of rugosa.
But moving up the trail, we caught a glimpse
right at the start: the fox just over the hillock
on the dune-side slope, spoiling
the grass-inscribed sand. Neither of us looked—
it seemed best to back away.
On the dune’s steep side
we surveyed what we’d come for: ocean’s
snaking blues beyond the meadow, the silvered
blade-like wands lying down. Lovely enough
to hold ourselves to that view.
But the currents of an odor wafted in and out,
until the sweep of smell grew wider, wilder.
The heat compounded, and ugliness
settled its cloud over us, profound as human speech,
although by then we were not speaking.
evidence: The speaker confesses to earlier cruelty ('too cruel to kill the mice we caught, / leaving them in the Have-a-Heart trap / under the sun-burning bramble') and then describes elaborate avoidance rituals around the dead fox ('pretended to know nothing,' 'Neither of us looked,' backing away while surveying the ocean view) that function as magical atonement for the earlier transgression—the toxic odor that cannot be escaped mirrors the inescapable guilt of the mouse-deaths.

author: Cleopatra Mathis
content:
Dead deer a week now by the snowy gate.
Do I have to watch it be eaten? Do I have to see
who comes first, who quarrels, who stays?
And there is the question of the night,
what flesh preferred by which creature—
what sinew and fat, the organs, the eyes.
These appetites: it’s enough
to know the swoop and cut of wings
over the snarl of something leaping away.
Do I have to see the icy figure fused to the ground,
scrabbled snow, not lovely or deep,
but the surface of something spoiled?
By now the rib bones arch above it all,
unbroken light shining between them,
above the black cavity.
And I hear the crows, complaint, complaint
splitting the morning, hunched over the skull.
They know their offices.
evidence: The speaker inventories decomposition with forensic precision—'who comes first, who quarrels,' 'what sinew and fat, the organs, the eyes'—maintaining obsessive cognitive engagement with the corpse while the expected emotional response (grief, horror, revulsion) remains conspicuously absent. The tone is that of a 'weather report on the psyche': factually accurate about disturbing material but delivered without corresponding affect.

author: Cleopatra Mathis
content:
She thinks it wants to kill, this wind,
the wind with no mind—a transference
to everything out there.
Because she needs waking,
the howling is meant to wake her, a self
crying underneath her sleeping self,
the stilted house groaning on the edge.
For how many days now
this dominating sea?
Wind so absolute
it rides the rain sideways and up,
crashing out far beyond her
until the anchor
breaks away—
waves take possession, smash the boat
on concrete pilings.
But isn’t this what she came for?
evidence: The speaker attributes her internal agitation to external forces ('this wind... wants to kill'), then recognizes this as 'a transference to everything out there'—locating her own distress in the natural world while remaining dimly aware of the projection. The final question 'But isn't this what she came for?' attempts to undo or neutralize the projected destructive impulse by reframing it as intentional seeking.

author: Lucie Brock-Broido
content:
No feeding on wisteria. No pitch-burner traipsing
In the nettled woods. No milk in metal cylinders, no
Buttering. No making small contusions on the page
But saying nothing no one has not said before.
No milkweed blown across your pony-coat, no burrs.
No scent of juniper on your Jacobean mouth. No crush
Of ink or injury, no lacerating wish.
                                                      Extinguish me from this.
I was sixteen for twenty years. By September I will be a ghost
And flickering in unison with all the other fireflies in Appalachia,
Blinking in the swarm of it, and all at once, above
And on a bare branch in a shepherd's sky.    No Dove.
                                                       There is no thou to speak of.
evidence: The obsessive catalog of negations ('No feeding... No pitch-burner... No milk... No making') functions as ritualistic cancellation, attempting to magically erase forbidden wishes or past experiences through compulsive verbal reversal. The culminating plea 'Extinguish me from this' and 'There is no thou to speak of' suggest desperate atonement for a transgressive attachment, treating the thought/memory of desire as requiring symbolic neutralization through repetitive denial.

poem_name: The Story of Ferdinand the Bull
author: Matt Mason
content:
Dad would come home after too long at work
and I’d sit on his lap to hear
the story of Ferdinand the Bull; every night,
me handing him the red book until I knew
every word, couldn’t read,
just recite along with drawings
of a gentle bull, frustrated matadors,
the all-important bee, and flowers—
flowers in meadows and flowers
thrown by the Spanish ladies.
Its lesson, really,
about not being what you’re born into
but what you’re born to be,
even if that means
not caring about the capes they wave in your face
or the spears they cut into your shoulders.
And Dad, wonderful Dad, came home
after too long at work
and read to me
the same story every night
until I knew every word, couldn’t read,
                                                                              just recite.
evidence: The speaker transforms childhood experience of an overworked father's limited availability into a meditation on Ferdinand's lesson—channeling potential resentment about Dad coming home 'after too long at work' (repeated twice) into appreciation for the ritual itself and its embedded wisdom about being 'what you're born to be' rather than what circumstances demand.

author: Susan Wheeler
content:
So I'll speak ill of the dead. A was crooked,
planting the small left finger of the raccoon in the upholstery
before he sold the car. B made certain to point out Celia's
bewildered look before her pink slip came in the flimsy institution.
In the videos of C, a jejune overwhelmed the cast.
D built dollhouses. Even Lonnie down at Shell
found him less a man for it, the night they went off to see the stock
cars break. I wanted E's hair, but by the end it was no more. F
refused alms, pulling the man up by his shirt in the street, and
G sought rewards. Marybeth said H fondled her for sport.
Now you, I, Smokey, hell
bent on a village version of Club 21, embarrassed by our attentions.
Mistrust it was. Dig me a chamber of preparedness.
                                                                          William T. Osborn, 1964-1993
evidence: The speaker systematically catalogs the flaws and moral failures of dead individuals (A through H) with contemptuous dismissals ('A was crooked,' 'F refused alms,' 'H fondled her for sport'), maintaining a prosecutorial inventory that diminishes each person to a single damning attribute while addressing 'you' as complicit witness.

author: Brian Russell
content:
the unthinkable prospect
of a world in which I am left
to my own devices
which are few and as soon
as the batteries die useless
first order of business
I draw a map in the sand
mark where I stand as the capital
of civilization   within me the
detailed blueprints of the pyramids
and the concept of zero
beyond me the finite frontier
the many miles of undeveloped
shoreline with spectacular views of a
sea filled with intricately depicted
monsters   I have a lot to do before
I introduce the new world
to art and astronomy and industry
medicine and technology
ethics politics democracy
by a show of hands we shall elect
which tree to burn in the first fire
evidence: The speaker positions himself as sole repository of civilization ('within me the detailed blueprints of the pyramids'), declares himself capital, and presents himself as singular source for introducing 'art and astronomy and industry / medicine and technology / ethics politics democracy'—fantasy of unlimited personal power to single-handedly recreate human knowledge without acknowledging separate agency of others or limits of individual capacity.

author: Brian Russell
content:
in a different version of this I'm the one
sitting up in bed while the nurse says
deep breaths   listens to my lungs through
the cold antenna of her stethoscope   you're the one
sitting in a chair in the corner
with a half hearted smile plastered to your face
in a different version of this
we're both in bed   hunched over
while two nurses   sisters actually   listen
to our lungs   your husband and my wife
sit in the corners of the room
both of them completely useless
in a different version of this I'm in a chair
in the corner while my sister sits up
in bed   you're the nurse who listens
to my sister breathing   you glance at me for
an instant and return my uncertain smile
in a different version of this I sit up
abruptly in bed   I'm breathing deeply   you
put your hand on my back and say
go back to sleep   you're fine   everything is fine
evidence: The speaker obsessively generates alternate scenarios that ritually reverse an unbearable present reality (illness, helplessness, mortality), each 'different version' functioning as a magical attempt to undo what cannot be faced directly. The flat, affectless catalog ('both of them completely useless') and mechanical iteration suggest isolation strips emotion while the compulsive versioning performs symbolic atonement.

author: Robert Winner
content:
Surviving in its fragile skin,
a white egret rises
from the gulf of its strength.
I want the lightest needle of a pine
to fall on my hand,
a pine with ravaged limbs.
I'd stare through salt-blind eyes
at a remote fragile sea.   I'd roar.
I'd make the skeleton of grief.
I'd roar like you, unreconciled sea.
evidence: The speaker identifies with the egret's fragile survival, the pine's ravaged limbs, and most explicitly the 'unreconciled sea,' culminating in 'I'd roar like you'—taking on the qualities of wounded natural forces to 'make the skeleton of grief' rather than expressing personal loss directly.

author: Robert Winner
content:
I try to carry the gravestone
from the darkness of my mother's sickroom—
scratches of light around drawn shades—
outside, the gold and red of autumn.
She is like a queen in exile
scraping with her nails on silk walls
her message of anger, her weak
insatiable demands and regrets.
I want her to grow rosy old
like a maple leaf, ripening,
yielding only to that ice edged wind that must come
and cut her down—like me, like everyone.
evidence: The speaker consciously acknowledges the burden ('I try to carry the gravestone') and mother's anger but expresses a wish to postpone the inevitable outcome ('I want her to grow rosy old') while maintaining full awareness that death 'must come'—demonstrating semiconscious deferral rather than denial. The clinical, observational tone when describing the mother's 'weak insatiable demands' suggests cognitive awareness of the situation while maintaining affective distance.

author: Robert Winner
content:
Opportunity I love you
Windows and watermelons march down the street
The air is nobody
Sky is in position
I am ready to endure my freedom
A riderless horse on a saffron plain
A lake that spins
A tree that lets the wind decide
evidence: The poem celebrates openness and receptivity to experience without defensive distortion—'ready to endure my freedom' and 'lets the wind decide' indicate acceptance rather than psychological management of anxiety.

author: Stephanie Burt
content:
A real one wouldn't need one,
but the one Nathan draws surely does:
four oblongs the size and color of popsicles,
green apple, toasted coconut and grape,
flanked, two per side, by billowing valentine hearts,
in a frame of Scotch tape.
Alive, it could stay off the floor,
for a few unaerodynamic minutes;
thrown as a paper airplane, for one or two more.
Very sensibly, therefore,
our son gave it something, not to keep it apart
from the ground forever, but rather to make safe its descent.
When we ask that imagination discover the limits
of the real
world only slowly,
maybe this is what we meant.
evidence: The speaker observes the child's drawing—a butterfly with parachute for 'safe descent'—and reflects on parenting philosophy: preparing for inevitable fall ('not to keep it apart from the ground forever, but rather to make safe its descent') through modest, affectively-engaged planning for future discomfort ('slowly').

author: Christian Wiman
content:
A shadow in the shape of a house
slides out of a house
and loses its shape on the lawn.
Trees seek each other
as the wind within them dies.
Darkness starts inside of things
but keeps on going when the things are gone.
Barefoot careless in the farthest parts of the yard
children become their cries.
evidence: The speaker contemplates the inevitable progression of darkness and loss with measured, affect-laden awareness: 'Darkness starts inside of things / but keeps on going when the things are gone.' The poem spreads anxiety about impermanence and dissolution across time through realistic observation, preparing emotionally for future absence while experiencing modest present distress.

author: Christian Wiman
content:
What words or harder gift
does the light require of me
carving from the dark
this difficult tree?
What place or farther peace
do I almost see
emerging from the night
and heart of me?
The sky whitens, goes on and on.
Fields wrinkle into rows
of cotton, go on and on.
Night like a fling of crows
disperses and is gone.
What song, what home,
what calm or one clarity
can I not quite come to,
never quite see:
this field, this sky, this tree.
evidence: The speaker describes what should be moments of numinous beauty—dawn breaking, fields emerging, trees carved by light—yet renders them through a series of detached, philosophical questions ('What words,' 'What place,' 'What song') that maintain curious observational distance rather than emotional engagement. The repeated 'what...can I not quite come to' structure intellectualizes an experience of perpetual near-revelation without accessing the affect of longing, awe, or frustration that would naturally accompany such repeated approach-without-arrival.

author: Christian Wiman
content:
A town so flat a grave's a hill,
            A dusk the color of beer.
A row of schooldesks shadows fill,
            A row of houses near.
A courthouse spreading to its lawn,
            A bank clock's lingering heat.
A gleam of storefronts not quite gone,
            A courthouse in the street.
A different element, almost,
            A dry creek brimming black.
A light to lure the darkness close,
            A light to keep it back.
A time so still a heart's a sound,
            A moon the color of skin.
A pumpjack bowing to the ground,
            Again, again, again.
evidence: The poem catalogs the physical features of a flat plains town at dusk through serial anaphora ('A town so flat,' 'A row of,' 'A courthouse,' etc.) without discernible psychological conflict or defensive processing—it is pure phenomenological inventory.

author: Carmen Giménez Smith
content:
Off-season and in
the burnt forest
of my nightgown, a feral
undergrowth that marks
me as burial site—
to be still enough or
just enough.
My arms become fat arms:
hearth. I eat dirt for doubt,
a secret bleached
old as lie. I out-want
like a spindly
winged monster.
If I were a bug—
were I—then you'd hope
for reparation, and paint
more brown into the plot.
evidence: The speaker systematically diminishes herself through reductive, contemptuous imagery: 'feral undergrowth,' 'burial site,' 'I eat dirt for doubt,' 'spindly winged monster,' collapsing self-worth into abject, verminous terms ('If I were a bug') while maintaining implicit awareness that this self-diminishment communicates injury to an addressee ('then you'd hope for reparation').

author: David Lau
content:
He is for a long time bleating the embassy collection:
(water-resistant cot,
a resurgent stretch of storefronts to dive into,
compadre, col legno, funding the new permits, pleasing
room, murderous rain.
Near-spring night, stars on flat blackboards, essay estate
questions passed person to person.
The lottery window
I turn red past,
hyacinth-lanced dawn.
In green tree talk
the transcriber dons a dark robe, a mirror.
The notebook discovered
the parting sun, magnificent trough, blind
hair on a head
of state, streak-woods
in the winding road atlas—stranded shore.
Pose in flight
covered by weeds, claw hair-rocks,
Pullulation. Push-up. Push-up.
Pestilentia reeds announce—trebly isolated, a closed trio,
the trinity trick-fucked—in a.
evidence: The poem presents fragmented, dislocated imagery of political protest and state apparatus through experimental juxtaposition rather than a psychological stance toward the material.

author: Lorine Niedecker
content:
Tell em to take my bare walls down
my cement abutments
their parties thereof
and clause of claws
Leave me the land
Scratch out: the land
May prose and property both die out
and leave me peace
evidence: The speaker constructs plausible philosophical justification ('May prose and property both die out / and leave me peace') for what is fundamentally a loss beyond her control—foreclosure—reframing dispossession as chosen renunciation of material attachments rather than acknowledging the distress of forced eviction.

author: Valerie Mejer Caso
content:
Nothing's in the nest. No needles. No newborn ravens.
Maybe something like night in the deep hollow,
an eggshell planet, cracked in the middle, an empty bowl of soup.
Nothing's in the nest. No thread. No webs of words.
Maybe something like my navel, the eclipse of a magnifying glass.
A slice, mute with regard to its empty depths.
In the nest, nothing. The web unwoven. Dismembered.
In the space, something, yes. A piece of cloth. Sounding like flags
taking wing, a worm in its beak and suddenly, eyes, my eyes
which, cutting across the empty air, direct themselves at something noiseless over there.
evidence: The speaker catalogues absence and emptiness with meticulous precision ('No needles. No newborn ravens... No thread. No webs of words') while maintaining an affectively flat, observational tone that resembles a 'weather report on the psyche.' The elaborately detailed inventory of what is missing—nest, navel, eclipse, worm—is delivered without emotional coloring despite imagery suggesting loss, barrenness, or void.

author: Anne Waldman
content:
(Jesse Helms & others...)



I'm coming up out of the tomb, Men of War Just when you thought you had me down, in place, hidden I'm coming up now Can you feel the ground rumble under your feet? It's breaking apart, it's turning over, it's pushing up It's thrusting into your point of view, your private property O Men of War, Censorious Ones! get ready big boys get ready I'm coming up now I'm coming up with all that was hidden Get ready, Big Boys, get ready I'm coming up with all you wanted buried, All the hermetic texts with stories in them of hot & dangerous women Women with lascivious tongues, sharp eyes & claws I've been working out, my muscles are strong I'm pushing up the earth with all you try to censor All the iconoclasm & bravado you scorn All the taunts against your banner & salute I'm coming up from Hell with all you ever suppressed All the dark fantasies, all the dregs are coming back I'm leading them back up now They're going to bark & scoff & rage & bite I'm opening the box  boo!
evidence: The speaker directly claims autonomous power and agency without apology ('I'm coming up now', 'my muscles are strong') while harnessing aggressive energy toward creative expression that confronts censorship. The voice occupies space with declarative force, transforming rage into socially engaged poetry rather than behavioral discharge.

author: Farnoosh Fathi
content:
This is not a book. Otherwise, by now
We would love each other.
You would not put me first,
Out of a kind habit, under your coat
And clutch—as a sudden rain
Spate down. For I've seen it done
For the hardly known.
No, you would know with a book you love:
How nothing held your eyes
The way the words did, with archer-focus:
How each arrow heading toward you
Was slowed by the dripping beehive
On its spike—
Nothing else could hold what you are
Still: I pressed your heart in speech and saw
What a musical you let rush, nothing
Else in the eyes. This is not a book,
But a streak—
Words cross reins—
The brow splits, veins careen.
evidence: The speaker transforms intense erotic/aggressive impulses into aesthetic production ('I pressed your heart in speech and saw / What a musical you let rush'), channeling primitive desire into creative work while maintaining full emotional intensity. The repeated insistence 'This is not a book' paradoxically produces a poem about the transfigurative power of artistic expression to hold and release affect.

author: Farnoosh Fathi
content:
Over the night a bull
Whispers into a coal
:Unmeant in the stall to sit and plate,
But sixth, with all the senses,
To consume—
Incorporate— those signal
Impressions which are (we know) its fate:
In explosions, in hard strides,
His coattails fly; to bits, to friends
Craven and brave.
Sadness undulates at their back.
His lilt's a cotillion of flies.
But how he charges, he commits!
Each to the next.It seems unfair, a target lies
Between its shoulder blades.
evidence: The poem meditates on memory through surreal imagery of a bull and its sensory consumption, but the speaker remains observational and descriptive without exhibiting a defensive stance toward psychological conflict.

author: Farnoosh Fathi
content:
Left a hole on fire agony or was it the sun
on the banks and near duets?
Eagles with the white wine of the sun
clink and spill, tall
grass over head and heels
. . . Space of hell: shy, inscribed already
but alone— I think I can be that
again, a new hole in the ongoing flute.
In a leap, the country glows— to hone
the fate that wonder exacts,
to go netted through that much,
so heavy as paperweights angels land
square on chaparral nerves.
And since names must give in spades,
out of sorts like these, your reactions
may swell great fountain lips—
a promise that a wish will purge
or pennies caravan the safe
return hearts cross.
evidence: The poem presents surreal, associative imagery ('Left a hole on fire agony or was it the sun') without discernible psychological conflict or defensive posture toward material—it is primarily aesthetic exploration rather than defense against distress.

author: Lyn Hejinian
content:
But isn’t midnight intermittent
Or was that just a whispered nine
A snap of blown light low against the flank of a cow
A likeness of something numberless that only I not knowing the sound
            might know
It may have been howled by a circling dog being chastised — threatened —
            by multiples of itself in pursuit of the consolation of knowing that
            everything is real
It was real
I don’t mean midnight — despite horizon, nipple, and fissure
I don’t mean
And yet I do — mean, I mean
A cowering animal woven real
            flickers
                        please pull over
                                    Kierkegaard
Kierkegaard says knowledge precedes every act but surely there are acts that
are not preceded by knowledge. Repetitions pass at the door from summer
to winter. Some slowly. Some quickly. Total strangers. Never saw them
before. Can’t picture them now. Umbrellas — strange totalities — upheld,
wheeling.
evidence: The speaker meticulously catalogs perceptual and cognitive phenomena (midnight, light, sound, Kierkegaard's epistemology) while maintaining conspicuous affective flatness—'It was real / I don't mean midnight' and 'And yet I do — mean, I mean' exhibit obsessive cognitive processing stripped of emotional resonance, delivering philosophical content in a tone resembling 'weather reports on the psyche.'

author: Cynthia Cruz
content:
I did not want my body
Spackled in the world’s
Black beads and broke
Diamonds. What the world
Wanted, I did not. Of the things
It wanted. The body of Sunday
Morning, the warm wine and
The blood. The dripping fox
Furs dragged through the black New
York snow—the parked car, the pearls,
To the first pew—the funders,
The trustees, the bloat, the red weight of
The world. Their faces. I wanted not
That. I wanted Saint Francis, the love of
His animals. The wolf, broken and bleeding—
That was me.
evidence: The speaker globally dismisses the social world ('the funders, / The trustees, the bloat, the red weight') with contempt while simultaneously idealizing Saint Francis and his suffering animals as pure refuge, oscillating between radical devaluation of status/materiality and archaic idealization of ascetic sanctity.

author: Carmen Tafolla
content:
He wanders through the crooked streets
that mimic river beds Before
and breathes the anxious air in traffic
filled with tension left from wooded crossroads in attack
He shops the windows, happy,
where the stalking once was good
and his kitchen floor is built on bones
of venison once gently roasted.
"It's a good place for a party!" he concurs
to friends now dressed in jeans.
The ground was already beaten smooth
and festive by the joy of ancient dances.
He feels the warmth,
and doesn't know his soul is filled
with the spirit of coyotes past.
evidence: The speaker observes a figure inhabiting contemporary urban space layered over indigenous history without psychological conflict or defensive distortion—the warmth and ease are presented as genuine encounter rather than defensive management of threat.

author: Carmen Tafolla
content:
I was the fourth ship.
          Behind Niña, Pinta, Santa María,
          Lost at sea while watching a seagull,
          Following the wind and sunset skies,
          While the others set their charts.
I was the fourth ship.
          Breathing in salt and flying with clouds,
          Sailing moonbreezes and starvision nights,
          Rolling into the wave and savoring its lull,
          While the others pointed their prows.
I was the fourth ship.
          Playfully in love with the sea,
          Eternally entwined with the sky,
          Forever vowed to my voyage,
          While the others shouted "Land."
evidence: The speaker retreats into an elaborate internal fantasy world ('Sailing moonbreezes and starvision nights') that substitutes for engagement with external reality and social goals ('While the others shouted Land'). The withdrawal is organized around a rich, self-contained imaginative voyage that explicitly rejects instrumental participation in collective endeavors.

author: Barbara Hamby
content:
A-bomb is how it begins with a big bang on page
        one, a calculator of sorts whose centrifuge
begets bedouin, bamboozle, breakdance, and berserk,
        one of my mother's favorite words, hard knock
clerk of clichés that she is, at the moment going ape
evidence: The poem playfully catalogs dictionary entries and linguistic associations, using the mother's clichés as an organizing device without revealing psychological conflict or defensive structure.

author: Jesús Papoleto Meléndez
content:
sometimes (
                            when the night air feels chévere! 
) when i can hear the real sound
of el barrio
on la conga        y timbales 
coke bottles
& garbage can tops
          when i can feel
          & reallyreally touch
          la música Latina /     africana
& the fingerpoppin' soul
emergin from tears /     sweet tears of laughter
          & i can feel
          a conglomeration of vibrations /
          heat waves
          body waves
          people waves
          of real gente
evidence: The speaker directly experiences and celebrates sensory and communal reality without distortion, displacement, or defensive maneuver—simply bearing witness to 'real sound,' 'real gente,' and embodied connection to cultural music and community.

author: Suzanne Gardinier
content:
Wasn't that your cheek against mine last night
Gin Streetlight When somebody loves you Impossible
When you reach the broken paddock fence
the sign will say Impossible
The color God painted my eyes A cross
between storm and ewerstream Impossible
All your wrong lovers without certificates
Stamped across their foreheads Impossible
Dear Torch Received your kind invitation
Regret conflagration impossible
You must mean a phantom Your hand at her waist
Your ache at her absence Not mine Impossible
A holy place in the emperor's city
A peach in a stone Impossible
You the mask of a ram I the mask of a bull
Horn chips Mischling Torn doors Impossible
Dance without footprints Dance with no name
in a room with no lovers not touching Impossible
Your eyes One protecting your sleeping son's dreaming
One torchlit and trying to close Impossible
Dear Lion Here's a gazelle Hold her
in your teeth but no biting Yours Impossible
evidence: Each couplet asserts an intimate connection or desire ('Wasn't that your cheek against mine', 'Somebody loves you', 'Dear Lion Here's a gazelle') then immediately negates it with the ritual incantation 'Impossible'—a compulsive verbal gesture that magically erases the forbidden wish the instant it's spoken.

author: Suzanne Gardinier
content:
I'm used to the emperor's bitterness
I can't find the sweet place unless you make me
This face that is not my face I may
look made but when you touch me you make me
Make the oak say blossom The stripped say swell
The avenue pavement say river Make me
This shirt I can't take off The one
the nights without you gathered to make me
The new day The sweet place Tomorrow
whispering from tonight's last light Make me
Kissing you without authorization
If you want me to stop you'll have to make me
The ruined city Or is it a woman
interrupting your sleep to say Now To say Make me
evidence: The speaker directs insistent demands ('Make me') at the addressee while framing desire as the other's responsibility ('I can't find the sweet place unless you make me'), simultaneously communicating need and covertly blaming the other for the speaker's state through the relentless repetition of 'make me' as both plea and accusation.

author: Fanny Howe
content:
The descent has deepened
the interior lengthened
designated ending
Blind
pulled down inside and then
shot up again
to see east via the plateglass
a moon a monsoon an ashram
I used time almost wantonly
in that bald but sensual sky
to give me gusts
and more measurement
not to snap the stars shut
but Joseph said
you really ought
to tender how you sail by eye
your soul is just a length of baby
evidence: The poem describes a spatial-spiritual experience ('descent deepened,' 'pulled down inside,' 'shot up again') and cosmic observation ('moon a monsoon an ashram,' 'stars') without evident psychological conflict or defensive posture. Joseph's admonition about sailing by eye reads as spiritual/philosophical counsel rather than defensive response.

author: Rusty Morrison
content:
In through our bedroom window, the full dawn-scape concusses.
Difficult to sustain sleep's equilibrium of wordlessness.
Naming anything, like stepping barefoot in wet sand up to my ankles.
Name after name, sinking me farther beneath waking's buoyancy.
House, this morning, is pale with the rush of what night siphoned off.
Objects, still emptied of resemblance, hum their chord-less cantos.
Bloodless, my knuckles knock on walls without echo, testing singularities.
Sun on the cutlery offers an ageless sheen.
Though it ages the silver relentlessly.
New, but still rudimentary tools to be gleaned from my over-used weaponry.
evidence: The speaker describes waking as 'stepping barefoot in wet sand,' sinking beneath language, with objects 'emptied of resemblance' and knuckles 'bloodless'—reporting sensory experience in curiously detached tones, as if cataloging consciousness itself without corresponding emotional weight or distress.

author: Maurice Kilwein Guevara
content:
This must happen just after I die: At sunrise
I bend over my grandparents' empty house in Hazelwood
and pull it out of the soft cindered earth by the Mon River.
Copper tubing and electric lines hang down like hairs.
The house is the size of a matchbox. I sprinkle bits
of broken pallets, seeded grass, fingernails, and tamarack
needles in the open door of the porch. I scratch a Blue Tip
and blow vowels of fire through the living room,
the tunneled hallway. Flames run up the wooden stairs.
I put my ear beside the hot kitchen window
to hear the crackling voices of cupboards and walls.
I flip the welder's mask:
Sun off the rectangular glass, a rose glint before the white torch.
evidence: The speaker exercises magical control over reality in the afterlife scenario ('I bend over my grandparents' empty house... and pull it out of the soft cindered earth'), making the house 'the size of a matchbox' and deliberately setting it aflame with ritualistic, world-creating gestures—blowing 'vowels of fire' and flipping 'the welder's mask' to command solar radiance. The speaker's wish (to transform grief over loss and absence) directly controls external outcome (the house becomes miniature, destructible, subject to creative incineration), collapsing boundary between internal desire and cosmic agency.

author: Michael Schmidt
content:
She spun a line. She knew he was listening to her.
She spun it and he took the fraying ends.
Whatever she was saying, it was cotton,
Then as he rolled the thread between
Forefinger and thumb it turned to silk,
And as he took the needle up to thread it
The line she spun became thin finest gold.
He knew not to believe her but he took it
Because she kept on spinning like the truth
Was ravelling from her lips; he watched her lips.
Cotton, silk and gold, she wanted him
To take the line and sew the wound right up
Although she held the blade still in her hand
Behind her back, and it was dripping, steaming.
There under his left arm the gash lay open
Like a mouth in disbelief. And he believed her.
evidence: The speaker maintains absolute segregation: she is all-deception (spinning lies, holding the blade behind her back), he is all-belief ('he believed her' despite the gash). No ambivalence or complexity penetrates these fixed categories—the wound remains open 'in disbelief' even as 'he believed her,' a logical impossibility sustained only by splitting consciousness into good/bad poles that cannot integrate.

poem_name: 'You with the Knees of a Fish'
author: Michael Schmidt
content:
You with the knees of a fish,
You with the fish's ears, the tongue in your round
Mouth that's nibbling the sweet air,
You with a fish's patience, on your side
Under the hot plank ot the keel, hook in your lip,
And your tail twitching like a bothered lily,
And the taste of your own bleeding on the air, salt on the sweetness,
You with a fish's voice, singing and still
Your aria, and the blue-silver flecks of light,
The water's scales, breathing the breeze
Abundant and not for you, you with a hook,
Absurd, you with lidless mercuried eyes;
And the reel whistles, the tall man's cast at you.
evidence: The speaker attributes hooked suffering and bleeding to 'you'—a fish-person hybrid—while maintaining direct accusatory address ('you with a hook, / Absurd, you with lidless mercuried eyes'). The projection externalizes internal states of being caught, wounded, and helpless onto this other, with the 'tall man's cast' representing external threat that validates the speaker's sense of persecution.

poem_name: Tell all the truth but tell it slant — (1263)
author: Emily Dickinson
content:
Tell all the truth but tell it slant —
Success in Circuit lies
Too bright for our infirm Delight
The Truth's superb surprise
As Lightning to the Children eased
With explanation kind
The Truth must dazzle gradually
Or every man be blind —
evidence: The speaker converts direct emotional contact with truth into an abstract pedagogical theory ('Success in Circuit lies,' 'dazzle gradually'), maintaining cognitive engagement while systematically distancing from visceral encounter—a meta-discourse on obliquity that enacts its own detachment through elaborate metaphor (Lightning, blindness) rather than naming what truth threatens.

poem_name: Fame is the one that does not stay — (1507)
author: Emily Dickinson
content:
Fame is the one that does not stay —
It's occupant must die
Or out of sight of estimate
Ascend incessantly —
Or be that most insolvent thing
A Lightning in the Germ —
Electrical the embryo
But we demand the Flame
evidence: The speaker dissects fame's conditions with detached logical precision ('must die / Or out of sight of estimate / Ascend incessantly'), delivering a taxonomy of impossibility in the flat, definitional tone of 'weather reports on the psyche'—conceptually mapping extinction without emotional contact with ambition's wound.

author: Juan Delgado
content:
As apparent as the rest, the asphalt cracks
are crowded with yellow weeds, the rust goes
beyond its bleeding color, and the lot's rails,
battered by cars, cast larger bars by noon.
On one side of the market someone painted
a row of flower pots, hanging geraniums
for the locals who must now go across town.
As apparent as the rest, El Tigre walks upright,
wears a tiny sombrero and sarape, and pushes
a grocery cart full of food. His painted stripes
are starting to flake like the bounty he wheels
for the families drifting into the parking lot
off 3rd Street and next to the train station
still waiting to the retrofitted for the big one.
evidence: The speaker maintains descriptive distance while documenting urban decay and displacement without psychological defensiveness—'the locals who must now go across town' presents socioeconomic reality through concrete imagery rather than intrapsychic management of conflict.

author: Hoa Nguyen
content:
Unused baby blood and this is
how you motion with hands
clotty leaving
   You have your apparatus
being the Frog Husband and I burn
your frog skin to keep you
in the shape I prefer
   Chimes   You wrote in your apple
box   Elegant neck
   I tried to glue the ripped
paper back to the religious
art but it doesn't work
   Making a mess of it
   Wasp friend landed on my
shoulder sparkle to say     This place
we are in            is a place
             Broil the asparagus
   Frog heart
apparatus
   Wash towels and rags
on Wednesdays
evidence: The speaker recounts traumatic pregnancy loss ('Unused baby blood...clotty leaving') alongside mundane domestic tasks ('Broil the asparagus...Wash towels and rags on Wednesdays') in the same affectless register, presenting catastrophic and trivial material with identical emotional flatness. The failed attempt to repair religious art and the wasp's cryptic message are delivered without accessing grief or maternal loss.

author: Hoa Nguyen
content:
Can be cracked or am that       you didn't
consider me or I thought so
recovering in a nap     You took the 4th
of July beers
   In the movie
she was Asian and playing an Asian
part   singing white on white in the white
room
      I want to strum
or mask this day
Ask a question
of the large “picture” window
like why and why and also why
to think of the napalmed girl
in the picture
evidence: The speaker juxtaposes emotionally charged content (napalmed girl, racial representation, personal hurt) with affectively flat, fragmented syntax ('Can be cracked or am that you didn't consider me') and frames trauma inquiry as abstract questioning ('Ask a question of the large picture window like why and why'), maintaining cognitive awareness while evacuating emotional resonance.

author: Ed Bok Lee
content:
All summer, the city engine's low
roar capsizes our bodies into sleep,
groans,
                evacuation—
                                                   Lost to a watery
anamnesis so warm it requires a raft
thatched from death's
                                         flotsam to necklace its shore
                              I swim on, calling your name
In my dreams, something is always deserting
But tonight, no fast shadows of birds
No oceanic flowers disrobing butterflies
or bright beach of child's porridge and bones—
                                                                                 Instead, someone weaving
a net from fallen hair in and around our bed
to catch the breath, blood, and ritual
motions that oiled us
                                            as one candle in a cave
In your dreams, someone is always resisting being savedMy teeth are on fire, you say I saidDon't fly for the labyrinth, once
I thought you were admonishing me to go away
I don't remember most others, a thousand seasons
phonographed in through a wounded windowEveryone can't have a cactus
Just o.k. empty all the rice from my legs
