evidence: The speaker recounts a spiritually charged experience (moss garden, monks, ritual prayer preparation) in a detached, reportorial tone ('she said,' 'they stumbled,' 'they entered'), maintaining syntactic distance through third-person narration until the final couplet's stark confession 'I do not want to pray' reveals affect-stripped acknowledgment without emotional engagement with the resistance itself.

author: C. Dale Young
content:
It is not the chambers of the heart that hold him
captive, but the hallways of the mind. Why
his image burning green and blue persists
—the face, the eyes questioning, the shape
of his head—is beyond anything I can understand.
evidence: The speaker maintains full affective connection to the puzzling persistence of memory ('burning green and blue persists') while openly acknowledging bewilderment without attempting to transform, displace, or defend against the experience.

author: Cate Marvin
content:
Before I go let me thank the man who mugs you,
taking your last paycheck, thank the boss who steals
your tips, thank the women who may break you.
I thank the pens that run out on you midsentence,
the flame that singes your hair, the ticket you can't
use because it's torn. Let me thank the stars
that remind you the eyes that were stars are now
holes. Let me thank the lake that drowns you, the sun
that makes your face old. And thank the street your car
dies in. And thank the brother you find unconcious
with bloody arms, thank the needle that assists in
doing him in—so much a part of you. No thanks
to the skin forgetting the hands it welcomed, your
hands refusing to recall what they happened upon.
How blessed is the body you move in—how gone.
evidence: The speaker transforms rage and grief into obsessive gratitude toward sources of harm ('thank the man who mugs you,' 'thank the needle that assists in doing him in'), delivering 'thanks' with hair-shirt intensity that reveals underlying hostility through excessive, false reversal while punishing the addressed 'you' with a catalog of suffering.

author: Lucille Clifton
content:
(at St. Mary's)



may the tide
that is entering even now
the lip of our understanding
carry you out
beyond the face of fear
may you kiss
the wind then turn from it
certain that it will
love your back     may you
open your eyes to water
water waving forever
and may you in your innocence
sail through this to that
evidence: The speaker blesses boats by naming future dangers ('the face of fear,' 'water waving forever') while offering affective preparation through imagery of navigation and trust, spreading anxiety into manageable doses of realistic worry transformed into benediction.

author: Lia Purpura
content:
In the beginning,
in the list of begats,
one begat
got forgot:
work begets work
(one poem
bears
the next).
In other words,
once there was air,
a bird
could be got.
Not taken.
Not kept.
But conjured up.
evidence: The speaker transforms the primitive impulse of acquisition ('taken,' 'kept') into creative, generative activity ('conjured up'), celebrating how 'work begets work' and 'one poem bears the next'—channeling desire into socially valued artistic production with maintained pleasure and real consequences.

author: Lia Purpura
content:
No one home.
Snow packing
the morning in.
Much white
nothing filling up.
A V of birds
pulling
the silence
until some dog
across the street
barks, and breaks
what I call my peace.
What a luxury
annoyance is.
It bites off
and keeps
just enough of
what I think
I want to be endless.
evidence: Speaker notes her own psychological process with metacognitive awareness: 'what I call my peace' and 'what I think / I want to be endless' reveal active reflection on her internal states and the constructed nature of her desires without defensiveness.

author: Linda Bierds
content:
They darken. In the sky over Florence,
the oblong clouds swell and darken.
And hailstones lift back through the updrafts,
thickening, darkening, until, swollen as bird eggs,
they drop to the cobbled streets.Horses! the child Galileo thinks, then
peeks through the doorway
to the shock of ten thousand icy hooves.
At his back, his father is tuning violins,
and because there is nothing sharper at hand
Galileo saws through a captured hailstone
with a length of E-string,
the white globe opening slowly, and the pattern inside
already bleeding its frail borders.
Layers and layers of ice—
Like what? Onion pulp? Cypress rings?
If only the room were colder, and the eye
finer. If only the hand were faster,
and the blade sharper, and firmer,
and without a hint of song . . .
evidence: The speaker dwells on the child Galileo's frustration with temporal and material limits—'If only the room were colder, and the eye finer. If only the hand were faster'—expressing realistic worry about future inadequacy through present recognition of constraints, a modest affective engagement with limitation that prepares the self (and the reader) for inevitable incompleteness.

author: Pat Mora
content:
When my aunt died,
my uncle raised his hands
like a prophet in the Bible.
“I've lost my girl,” he said,
“I've lost my girl,” over and over,
shaking his head.
I didn't know what to say,
where to look,
my quiet uncle raising his voice
to silence.
My aunt was eighty-seven.
“Listen,” my uncle said, sighing
like a tree alone at night,
“women know.
Every midnight on New Year's Eve,
when others sang
and laughed and hugged,
your aunt looked at me,
tears in her eyes.
Sixty years.
She knew.
One day, we'd kiss good-bye.”
evidence: The aunt's yearly ritual of looking at her husband with tears every New Year's Eve represents affect-laden planning for future loss—'She knew. One day, we'd kiss good-bye'—experiencing modest distress in advance through repeated anticipatory acknowledgment of death across sixty years.

author: Eileen Spinelli
content:
On Tuesday
on the way to Tween Time
Alison is all bubbly with
guess-whos
and guess-whats.
“Guess who really stole
Mrs. Bagwell's ring?”
“Guess what Mrs. Bagwell
is doing now?”
“Guess what you and I
are going to do this Friday?”
I hold my hand up. “Whoa!
One guess at a time, please.”
evidence: The poem presents a straightforward social interaction where the speaker sets a conversational boundary ('One guess at a time, please') in response to Alison's rapid-fire questions, without psychological defensiveness or unconscious conflict management.

author: Tim Nolan
content:
They grow in number all the time
The cat, the Mother, the Father
The grandparents, aunts, and uncles
Those I knew well and hardly at all
My best friend from when I was ten
The guy who sat with me in the back
Of the class where the tall kids livedBill the Shoemaker from Lyndale Avenue
The Irish poet with rounded handwriting
They live in The Land of Echo, The Land Of Reverb, and I hear them between
The notes of the birds, the plash of the wave
On the smooth rocks. They show up
When I think of them, as if they always
Are waiting for me to remember
I drive by their empty houses
I put on their old sweaters and caps
I wear their wristwatches and spend
Their money. So now I'm in six places
At once—if not eighteen or twenty
So many places to be thinking of them
Strange how quiet they are with their presence
So humble in the low song they sing
Not expecting that anyone will listen
evidence: The speaker incorporates the dead into his living self through concrete objects and multiplied presence: 'I put on their old sweaters and caps / I wear their wristwatches and spend / Their money. So now I'm in six places / At once—if not eighteen or twenty.' The dead are not mourned as absent but introjected as internal presences that expand rather than fragment the self.

author: Arden Levine
content:
She tells him she's leaving him and he
bakes a pie. His pies are exquisite,
their crusts like crinoline.
He doesn't change clothes, works
in slacks, shirtsleeves rolled.
Summer makes the kitchen unbearable
but he suffers beautifully, tenderly
cuts the strawberries, pours
into the deep curve of the bowl.
She hadn't missed his hands since
last they drew her to his body.
Now she watches them stroke the edges
of the dough, shape it like cooling glass.
When the oven opens, his brow drips,
he brings his hands to his face.
evidence: After she announces departure, he immediately bakes an exquisite pie—a compensatory ritual that attempts to counterbalance the relational rupture through beautiful suffering and tender domestic performance, though the narrative voice observes rather than shares his awareness of this pattern.

author: Cynthia Ventresca
content:
She lived there for years in a
small space in a high rise that saw
her winter years dawn. When the past
became larger than her present,
she would call and thank us for cards
we gave her when we were small;
for Christmas, Mother's Day, her birthday,
our devotion scrawled amidst depictions
of crooked hearts and lopsided lilies.
She would write out new ones,
and we found them everywhere—unsent;
in perfect cursive she wished us joy,
chains of x's and o's circling her signature.
And when her time alone was over,
the space emptied of all but sunshine, dust,
and a cross nailed above her door,
those cards held for us a bitter peace;
they had finally been delivered.
evidence: The speaker recounts profound loss—the grandmother's dementia, isolation, and death—in a detached, conceptual manner ('the past became larger than her present,' 'her time alone was over') that maintains observational distance while acknowledging emotional content only through objects (the unsent cards representing 'bitter peace'). The narrative voice reports feelings as existing in the scene but evacuates direct emotional engagement.

author: Francine Sterle
content:
Does Chang feel his teeth falling out?
One... two... three...
His mouth shuts.
How will he speak?
Like the moon
released at last and speechless,
he has lost his descendants.
Life splits—
a rift, a cleft, the half-
light between waking and sleeping.
A quartz-colored dawn rescues him.
The day clears.
Dizzy waves rush to shore.
The factory calls him to work, but
even there, the gap-toothed
partitions in the wall
where the rice bowls are kept
stay empty. It’s another
sad round of layoffs.
How many more will be lost?
How many?
evidence: The poem presents an empathic, observational meditation on Lu Chang's losses (teeth, descendants, employment) without displaying a defensive psychological stance—the speaker witnesses external suffering rather than managing internal conflict through defense.

author: Francine Sterle
content:
how much of the map
               could be labeled
                              terra incognita
how much        unknown        invisible to others
        how much of myself    could I shake off
abandon               those undiscovered places
               [I barely know]        exist
        though the map is not the territory
how I am drawn        to leave behind the pattern
               for the path        for a minute
                          an hour        for one whole day
I'd be like a Wintu               describing the body
               using cardinal directionshe touches me on the west arm
                                        the river is to the east                     when we return          his east arm
circles around me        and the river
                             stays to the west
evidence: The speaker frames a deeply personal desire for self-transformation ('how much of myself could I shake off / abandon those undiscovered places') through abstract cartographic metaphors and anthropological examples (Wintu cardinal directions), maintaining emotional distance while contemplating identity fragmentation. The shift from affectively charged questions about self-abandonment to detached linguistic exposition ('I'd be like a Wintu describing the body') demonstrates the defense's translation of emotional material into intellectual terrain.

author: Dorianne Laux
content:
Bless this boy, born with the strong face
of my older brother, the one I loved most,
who jumped with me from the roof
of the playhouse, my hand in his hand.
On Friday nights we watched Twilight Zone
and he let me hold the bowl of popcorn,
a blanket draped over our shoulders,
saying, Don't be afraid. I was never afraid
when I was with my big brother
who let me touch the baseball-size muscles
living in his arms, who carried me on his back
through the lonely neighborhood,
held tight to the fender of my bike
until I made him let go.
The year he was fourteen
he looked just like Ray, and when he died
at twenty-two on a roadside in Germany
I thought he was gone forever.
But Ray runs into the kitchen: dirty T-shirt,
torn jeans, pushes back his sleeve.
He says, Feel my muscle, and I do.
evidence: The speaker transforms grief over her brother's death at 22 into generative maternal love for Ray at 14, channeling loss into present relational engagement: 'I thought he was gone forever. But Ray runs into the kitchen... He says, Feel my muscle, and I do.' The primitive desire for reunion with the dead brother is redirected into authentic connection with the living boy who resembles him.

author: Debra Nystrom
content:
Nothing to do but scuff down
the graveyard road behind the playground,
past the name-stones lined up in rows
beneath their guardian pines,
on out into the long, low waves of plains
that dissolved time. We'd angle off
from fence and telephone line, through
ribbon-grass that closed behind as though
we'd never been, and drift toward the bluff
above the river-bend where the junked pickup
moored with its load of locust-skeletons.
Stretched across the blistered hood, we let
our dresses catch the wind while clouds above
dimmed their pink to purple, then shadow-blue—
So slow, we listened to our own bones grow.
evidence: The speaker transforms potentially ominous childhood experiences (graveyard road, locust-skeletons, junked pickup) into dreamy, entranced reverie marked by phrases like 'dissolved time,' 'closed behind as though we'd never been,' and 'drift'—the threatening content becomes play through altered consciousness and detachment from ordinary awareness, culminating in the dissociative stillness of 'So slow, we listened to our own bones grow.'

author: Barbara Crooker
content:
is a river you wade in until you get to the other side.
But I am here, stuck in the middle, water parting
around my ankles, moving downstream
over the flat rocks. I'm not able to lift a foot,
move on. Instead, I'm going to stay here
in the shallows with my sorrow, nurture it
like a cranky baby, rock it in my arms.
I don't want it to grow up, go to school, get married.
It's mine. Yes, the October sunlight wraps me
in its yellow shawl, and the air is sweet
as a golden Tokay. On the other side,
there are apples, grapes, walnuts,
and the rocks are warm from the sun.
But I'm going to stand here,
growing colder, until every inch
of my skin is numb. I can't cross over.
Then you really will be gone.
evidence: The speaker refuses to cross the river of grief ('I'm going to stand here, growing colder') as a form of self-punishment that communicates to the deceased: 'Look what you made me suffer!'—'Then you really will be gone.' The martyred suffering is directed at the actual source of loss while maintaining frozen attachment.

author: Patricia Traxler
content:
Late winter, almost spring. It's like finding a diamond;
now I don't want to leave. I sit in the dirt and put my hands
in your tracks. For the first time in a long time I don't
doubt. Now I know I always knew you were here. You
are the beginning of disclosure, the long-felt presence
Suddenly incarnate. Behind me my friend warns, If we
see the bear, get into a fetal position. No problem,
I tell her, I'm always in a fetal position—I was born
in a fetal position. Did you know, she says, the body
of a shaved bear looks exactly like a human man?
I skip a stone, feel a sudden bloat of grief, then laugh.
I ask her, Who would shave a bear? We climb
Farther up Rattlesnake Creek, watch winter sun glitter
off dark water. No matter how high we go I look higher.
Sometimes absence can prove presence. That's not exactly
faith, I know. All day, everywhere, I feel you near at hand.
There's so much to understand, and everything to prove.
Up high the air is thin and hard, roars in the ears like love.
evidence: The speaker prepares emotionally for departure ('I don't want to leave') while experiencing modest distress spread across the hike; she simultaneously acknowledges grief ('sudden bloat of grief') and engages in cognitive work about the bear encounter ('get into a fetal position') that keeps both thought and affect present without collapse or avoidance.

author: James Baldwin
content:
If the hope of giving
is to love the living,
the giver risks madness
in the act of giving.
Some such lesson I seemed to see
in the faces that surrounded me.
Needy and blind, unhopeful, unlifted,
what gift would give them the gift to be gifted?
          The giver is no less adrift
          than those who are clamouring for the gift.
If they cannot claim it, if it is not there,
if their empty fingers beat the empty air
and the giver goes down on his knees in prayer
knows that all of his giving has been for naught
and that nothing was ever what he thought
and turns in his guilty bed to stare
at the starving multitudes standing there
and rises from bed to curse at heaven,
he must yet understand that to whom much is given
much will be taken, and justly so:I cannot tell how much I owe.
evidence: The speaker transforms personal distress ('the giver risks madness') into service to others while recognizing their own neediness ('The giver is no less adrift / than those who are clamouring for the gift'), achieving vicarious gratification through giving what the self desires—connection and meaning—without acknowledging own need directly.

author: Raza Ali Hasan
content:
Humbling of Bhutto in Mecca, Bhutto kissing
Hajar-e-Aswad, half the Bhutto cabinet in Ihram,
kneeling. These were the first scenes, in the rolling
newsreel of half-closed doors, of the doorjamb
in the way of the twentieth century’s upstarts.
A nationalization, by Bhutto, of religious piety?
No, but a headlong scram into obeisance
of all and everybody and everything to the stately
rise of Islam in the neighboring, overbearing Arabia.
That year Bhutto had appointed my father
Hajj secretary, and we, the seven children and the ayah,
were present at Melody Cinema in full regalia
to see, to our amazement, on the screen,
our father in Ihram like Bhutto, and in a tent in Mina,
sitting on the ground in an ablution scene,
the humbling of our mysophobic mother,
who before her pilgrimage would have drunk water
only from a glass washed three times by a servant
and who wouldn't sit on the drawing-room sofa
unless it was draped by a freshly laundered sheet.
evidence: The speaker maintains dual awareness—simultaneously the child watching the newsreel and the adult reflecting on both political theater and familial hypocrisy ('to our amazement, on the screen, our father'), noting the mother's mysophobia with wry detachment while acknowledging the absurdity of religious posturing.

author: Dunya Mikhail
content:
A fish
meets another fish
and lays eggs.
As its fins signal to the seaweed
its colors come out
one after the other.
Its bubbles are words
meant for no one.
The world rises and falls
each day
through the eyes of a fish.
evidence: The speaker describes a complete lifecycle (meeting, mating, signaling) in detached, documentary syntax stripped of emotional resonance; 'bubbles are words / meant for no one' literalizes communication devoid of affective contact while maintaining observational clarity.

author: Dean Young
content:
I love when out of nowhere
I love when out of nowhere
my cat jumps on me
and my body isn’t even surprised.
Me who wants to be surprised by everything
like a dandelion
like a bottle cap
cricket cricket.
I keep waiting for the god under the anthill to speak up.
I keep waiting for the part of the myth
where everyone turns into a different bird
or the reeds start talking
or horses come out of the ocean
in their parliamentary regalia
and cities grow from their hoofprints.
I keep waiting for the bugle
and the jackal-headed god to weigh my heart across the river.
All this daylight in just a few moments
pours itself into darkness. More and more
I’m satisfied with partial explanations
like a fly with one wing, walking.
evidence: The speaker's repeated 'I keep waiting for' structure shows affect-laden planning for mythic transformations that never arrive, spreading anxiety about meaning and transcendence across incremental moments. The final acceptance of 'partial explanations' suggests realistic acknowledgment of what cannot be had, maintaining both desire and limitation in consciousness without collapse.

author: Raza Ali Hasan
content:
My itinerary is the eternity of exile.
Deferred is the trip back into domicile.
My marching orders lost at sea;
my papers shrouded in an immigrant's secrecy.
Lamar Avenue in Austin, Texas is wide, long and it flows.
Air-conditioned apartments allow for repose.
My transports and attachments to the past,
my dream-life, have an urgency that is never lost.
An exile's ultimate treat, tonight's dreamlike score:
a dinner with the Zaidis in their Islamabad home.
evidence: The speaker maintains conscious awareness of both present exile ('Lamar Avenue in Austin, Texas') and deferred return ('Deferred is the trip back into domicile') while experiencing affect-laden planning and persistent attachment ('my dream-life, have an urgency that is never lost'). The nocturnal dreamwork represents measured emotional preparation for impossible return, spreading longing over time through nightly rehearsals.

author: Camille Rankine
content:
Our stone wall was built by slaves and my bones, my bones
are paid for. We have two
 
of everything, twice heavy
in our pockets, warming
our two big hands.
 
This is the story, as I know it. One morning:
the ships came, as foretold, and death
pearl-handled, almost
 
and completely.
How cheap a date I turned out to be.
 
Each finger weak with the memory:
lost teeth, regret. Our ghosts
walk the shoulders of the road at night.
I get the feeling you’ve been lying to me.
evidence: The speaker frames complicity in historical exploitation through acceptable economic language ('my bones are paid for,' 'twice heavy in our pockets') while simultaneously performing compensatory recognition ('Our ghosts walk the shoulders of the road'), constructing plausible acknowledgment that masks deeper unprocessed guilt about material benefit from slavery.

poem_name: The Increasing Frequency of Black Swans
author: Camille Rankine
content:
I was listening for the dog
when the locks were pried open.
The man was dead. The dog, a survivor,
was dead. It happens
 
more often this way.
A disease left
untreated; the body,
in confusion, gives in.
 
The bomb breathes its fire down
the hallway, the son comes back
in pieces; the body,
in confusion, gives in.
 
The grief is a planet. A dust ring.
A small moon that’s been hidden
under my pillow, that’s been changing
the way my body moves this whole time.
evidence: The speaker recounts traumatic losses (the dead man, the dog, the son returning 'in pieces') using detached, clinical syntax ('the body, in confusion, gives in') and weather-report tone, acknowledging death's frequency without accessing corresponding grief until the final stanza's sudden eruption of hidden affect ('A small moon that's been hidden / under my pillow').

author: Allison Adelle Hedge Coke
content:
It wasn’t socks missing from his feet,
not elbow cloth unraveled unilaterally,
not equal displacement of chin and brow,
nor the eye that sat a bit lower on the right,
it was his knuckle that made me weep,
clove corners gone wayside, like minuscule meat
hooks clawed away bits of him each shift he made,
invisible a timeliness unfurled. It was his muscle
torn through, festering, the prosthetic hand, finger-
width dismay all across his attempted grin, left
there just like that, for anyone to see—it was his mercy.
In the end we’re rarely beautiful, mostly placed
away from compromising situations into poses
offsetting what has become of us in some gawker’s
unnerving eyes. Yet, he was, is, still here in mine,
and I’m human because of it. Maybe only. Maybe.
evidence: The speaker transforms profound distress at witnessing physical deterioration ('made me weep') into gratitude for the lesson it provides ('I'm human because of it'), correctly attributing their own need for meaning and connection to the suffering object while finding vicarious satisfaction through honoring rather than pitying him.

author: Ginger Murchison
content:
Late afternoons, we'd tuck up our hems
under Minisa Bridge, scrape our white knees
on scrub brush and drowned trees to slide
 
down the dirt bank past milk-weed
gone to seed, cattails and trash to sit on stones
at the edge of the river and giggle and smoke,
 
waiting to wolf-whistle North High's rowing team.
In the shadows where the milk-chocolate river
unfolded, ooze between our toes, we'd strip,
 
risk long-legged insects, leeches and mothers
for the silt slick on our thighs, the air thick
with the smell of honeysuckle, mud—the rest
 
of the day somewhere downstream. We didn't
know why, but none of us wanted
to go home to polite kitchens and mothers
 
patiently waiting for what happened next,
the way women have always waited for hunter husbands,
kept vigils and prayed at the entrance of mines.
evidence: The speaker describes deliberately lingering at the river to delay returning home to 'polite kitchens and mothers / patiently waiting for what happened next,' acknowledging both the inevitable future (domestic waiting, marriage, gendered roles like 'hunter husbands' and mine vigils) and the modest affective distress it evokes, while actively postponing the transition through immersive present experience ('the rest / of the day somewhere downstream').

author: Faith Shearin
content:
He was a surprise of white: his teeth
like knives, his face a triangle
of albino dislike. I had seen him before,
 
on our back porch, where my father
sometimes left watermelon rinds,
and he dipped his tongue into them,
 
his skin glowing beneath our lights,
like some four-legged relative
of the moon. I knew him
as a citizen of the night:
 
a fainting, ghostly presence
with a tail so naked it was
embarrassed to drag behind him.
 
But that morning, terrified and violent,
he was different: a hissing fury
at the bottom of the garbage can,
a vampire bathed in light.
evidence: The speaker offers a straightforward, naturalistic account of encountering a possum in two contexts—familiar nocturnal visitor vs. daytime threat—without deploying the poem's observational stance defensively to manage internal conflict.

author: Stuart Dybek
content:
Sometimes they are the only thing beautiful
about a hotel.
Like transients,
come winter they have a way of disappearing,
disguised as dirty light,
limp beside a puttied pane.
Then some April afternoon
a roomer jacks a window open,
a breeze intrudes,
resuscitates memory,
and suddenly they want to fly,
while men,
looking up from the street,
are deceived a moment
into thinking
a girl in an upper story
is waving.
evidence: The speaker offers an observational, metaphorical meditation on curtains in hotels—their disappearance in winter, revival in spring, and the optical illusion they create. No psychological defense stance is evident; the poem functions as descriptive lyric with visual metaphor.

author: Ed Roberson
content:
i must be careful about such things as these.
the thin-grained oak.    the quiet grizzlies scared
into the hills by the constant tracks squeezing
in behind them closer in the snow.    the snared
rigidity of the winter lake.    deer after deer
crossing on the spines of fish who look up and stare
with their eyes pressed to the ice.   in a sleep.  hearing
the thin taps leading away to collapse like the bear
in the high quiet.   i must be careful not to shake
anything in too wild an elation.    not to jar
the fragile mountains against the paper far-
ness.   nor avalanche the fog or the eagle from the air.
of the gentle wilderness i must set the precarious
words.   like rocks.   without one snowcapped mistake.
evidence: The speaker meticulously catalogs images of ecological fragility and death ('grizzlies scared,' 'deer crossing on spines of fish,' 'collapse like the bear') in careful, detached syntax with no corresponding emotional resonance—describing catastrophe in the controlled tone of a technical manual, converting overwhelming material into aesthetic problem-solving ('i must be careful... not to shake anything in too wild an elation').

poem_name: Curtain Design for Victory Over the Sun
author: David Lau
content:
My observation are as follows: still dirty
“in the wisdom” that is constructivist 
red theatre pieces, bike gloves all
like a mashed crop of dyed hair,
a bad crop this year.
 
We started the play at once:An Enemy of the People.
A Buddha of lapidary contrition enters,
hospice, tears, La Clemenza di Tito interlude
 
rolled in tulips of decisive consequence.
 Sprezzatura. Our row
throws that cosmic scratch,
apotropaic, scalene
back to the corner pocket
angle on the hustle—
 
“Live at the Paradox.”
evidence: The poem operates as experimental collage juxtaposing cultural references (Ibsen, Mozart, Victory Over the Sun) without a speaker navigating psychological conflict—it enacts aesthetic fragmentation rather than defensive management of affect.

author: Frederick Seidel
content:
Right now, a dog tied up in the street is barking
With the grief of being left,
A dog bereft.
Right now, a car is parking.
 
The dog emits
Petals of a barking flower and barking flakes of snow
That float upward from the street below
To where another victim sits:
 
Who listens to the whole city
And the dog honking like a car alarm,
And doesn’t mean the dog any harm,
And doesn’t feel any pity.
evidence: The speaker catalogs emotionally charged urban scenes (a 'bereft' dog's grief, its 'honking like a car alarm') but positions himself as one who 'doesn't feel any pity'—maintaining observational awareness while explicitly severing emotional response from the distressing content perceived.

author: Tess Taylor
content:
We unstave the winter’s tangle.
Sad tomatoes, sullen sky.
 
We unplay the summer’s blight.
Rotted on the vine, black fruit
 
swings free of the strings that bound it.
In the compost, ghost melon; in the fields(,)
 
grotesque extruded peppers.
We prod half-thawed mucky things.
 
In the sky, starlings eddying.
Tomorrow, snow again, old silence.
 
Today, the creaking icy puller.
Last night I woke
 
to wild unfrozen prattle.
Rain on the roof— a foreign liquid tongue.
evidence: The speaker describes seasonal tasks and weather with concrete sensory detail without any psychological conflict or defensive distortion—'We unstave the winter's tangle,' 'We prod half-thawed mucky things'—presenting mud season's ambiguous threshold as landscape phenomenon rather than psychological stance.

author: Frank Lima
content:
During the day I play at drowning
looking for the smoke
of eyelashes and faded hair
the lilac shadows of blood
and the ruins of coffee
but a night
I dream of the last syllable
in my mother's heart
the last red word in her lungs.
evidence: The speaker describes intensely charged maternal death imagery ('last syllable in my mother's heart / the last red word in her lungs') through elaborate aesthetic abstractions ('smoke of eyelashes,' 'lilac shadows of blood,' 'ruins of coffee'), converting potentially overwhelming grief into sophisticated, detached poetic constructions that acknowledge the emotional content while maintaining observational distance.

author: Frank Lima
content:
in the beginning
there was no end
the ground we
walked on was
a memory
our shadows
false stories
our clothing
space without time
darkness was the
color of angels
and the stars did
not weep
2.25.98
evidence: The poem offers a cosmological meditation on origins and timelessness through paradox and negation, without revealing psychological conflict or defensive stance toward lived experience.

author: Maggie Dietz
content:
Show's over, folks. And didn't October do
A bang-up job? Crisp breezes, full-throated cries
Of migrating geese, low-floating coral moon.
Nothing left but fool's gold in the trees.
Did I love it enough, the full-throttle foliage,
While it lasted? Was I dazzled? The bees
Have up and quit their last-ditch flights of forage
And gone to shiver in their winter clusters.
Field mice hit the barns, big squirrels gorge
On busted chestnuts. A sky like hardened plaster
Hovers. The pasty river, its next of kin,
Coughs up reed grass fat as feather dusters.
Even the swarms of kids have given in
To winter's big excuse, boxed-in allure:
TVs ricochet light behind pulled curtains.
The days throw up a closed sign around four.
The hapless customer who'd wanted something
Arrives to find lights out, a bolted door.
evidence: The speaker catalogs autumn's end and November's onset through detailed environmental observations ('bees / Have up and quit,' 'field mice hit the barns') while maintaining affective distance. The one emotional question—'Did I love it enough... Was I dazzled?'—is immediately abandoned for further detached inventory, converting potential grief about seasonal death into weather-report narration of factual changes.

author: Maggie Dietz
content:
Don't feel small. We all have
been demoted. Go on being
moon or rock or orb, buoyant
and distant, smallest craft ball
at Vanevenhoven's Hardware
spray-painted purple or day-glow
orange for a child's elliptical vision
of fish line, cardboard and foam.
No spacecraft has touched you,
no flesh met the luster of your
heavenly body. Little cold one, blow
your horn. No matter what you are
planet, and something other than
planet, ancient but not "classical,"
the controversy over what to call you
light-hours from your ears. On Earth
we tend to nurture the diminutive,
root for the diminished. None 
of your neighbors knows your name.
Nothing has changed. If Charon's
not your moon, who cares? She
remains unmoved, your companion.
evidence: Speaker offers consolation to Pluto during its demotion ('Don't feel small. We all have been demoted'), correctly identifying the object's need while transforming speaker's own experience of diminishment into vicarious comfort ('On Earth we tend to nurture the diminutive, root for the diminished'), leaving both speaker partly gratified and Pluto genuinely blessed.

author: Gail Mazur
content:
Asleep until noon, I'm dreaming
we've been granted another year.
You're here with me, healthy.
Then, half-awake, the half-truth—
this is our last day. Life's leaking
away again, and this time, we know it.
Dear body, I told you, pleading,Don't Leave! but I understand you
can't say anything. Who are we?
Are we fictional? We don't look
like our pictures, don't look like
anyone I know. Daylight
flickers through a bamboo grove,
we approach the Forbidden City,
Looking together for the Hall
of Fulfilling Original Wishes.Time is the treasure, you tell me,and the past is its hiding place.
I instruct our fictional children,The past is the treasure, time
is its hiding place. If we told him
how much we love him, how much
we miss him, he could stay.
evidence: The speaker oscillates between dreaming 'we've been granted another year' and waking to 'this is our last day,' then attempts magical reversal through instruction and pleading: 'If we told him how much we love him, how much we miss him, he could stay'—treating verbal expression as having omnipotent power to undo death itself.

author: Gail Mazur
content:
Hot hot hot, you are hot, Sun,
Glaring all over my east window
Burning, beaming, yellowing
The room. Uninterested in me
Because I'm not Mayakovsky
Although I feel you insistingI wake, that I produce right now
Or perish as my uncle used to say.
Brave Mayakovsky, doomed Mayakovsky, He could sass you, and later O'Hara
(Before they turned forty, both gone)
Sassed you and sassed Mayakovsky, too—
But when I try I know it's just another
Instance of me whistling in the dark,
Me not blazing, me not burning out.
evidence: The speaker systematically diminishes her own worth and creative power by comparing herself unfavorably to Mayakovsky and O'Hara ('I'm not Mayakovsky... when I try I know it's just another / Instance of me whistling in the dark'), framing her entire creative effort as inadequate imitation rather than authentic expression, thereby protecting against shame of ambition through preemptive self-dismissal.

author: Gail Mazur
content:
I say to the named granite stone, to the brown grass,
to the dead chrysanthemums, Mother, I still have a
body, what else could receive my mind's transmissions,
its dots and dashes of pain
evidence: The speaker describes grief as 'mind's transmissions, its dots and dashes of pain'—translating mourning into technical/mechanical language (telegraph code) that distances emotional experience through abstract conceptualization while maintaining cognitive engagement with loss.

author: Lyn Hejinian
content:
Wake up, get married, be born
First A and B pick up the trunk, then C relieves A and A wanders off, then D
         takes B’s end and B goes in search of A, but A is nowhere to be found,
         and C and D make off with the trunk
Long are the lazy man’s laws, the kittens are in the kitchen, the child’s chin aids
         pronunciation 
Maybe I’m dreaming I’m naked except for a long black t-shirt I’m dreaming 
Bring on the aspirin and bread, the vitamin C and gin 
We have fourteen names for blue and that doesn’t even count “meridian” 
Diderot, Audrey Hepburn, Hegel, Charles Dickens, and Gertrude Stein 
Shadow bird shouting 
White coral fencing 
The butcher on Sunday, Pablo Ruiz, lives south of here (in F___) and has five
         kids—how full of vitality he is
She leaves us behind in the interstices of competence
Origami, irreverence, sand on the wing of an ibis
She drops a bucket down a thick well, she whacks a golfball longer than a marble
Rude and shoed, should and lead, reed
evidence: The poem presents a sequence of disjunctive, imagistic statements without psychological conflict or emotional processing—paratactic observations that function as formal experiment rather than defensive stance.

author: Lyn Hejinian
content:
Puddings don’t have lungs, melons don’t have riders
Listen—a female seal, a seaport, and a social world
Come day’s end the top of the tree hesitates, pauses, then sweeps on like a
         blackboard eraser to clear the horizon
Sit, Shep, incognito
The lid of the sun is heavy, its lashes blink on the horizon, brushing the curve of
         the sea
So now they want to grant federal coal subsidies
I heard “suspected pipe bomb” as “suspected python”
The first nest empty and deep, at child’s eye level, in a young fir tree, of twigs
Pathos is at the front line of defense against worries as they approach
I remember almost nothing, only that I am in a room with others and we are
         reading through sacks of mail, trying to ferret out spies
She will never believe she’s too old to join a band or make quick vertical moves
         on the playing field to really quiet music—she is that still
Then the sparrow went to sleep in a lumber castle
And so we come to chapter LIX, in which I learn that I have failed
Can you believe this shit
evidence: The speaker catalogs diverse observations—from puddings lacking lungs to federal coal subsidies to suspected pythons—in affectively flat, paratactic sequences that never cohere emotionally. The final line 'Can you believe this shit' names exasperation but arrives detached from the preceding catalog, as if the affect exists separately from the content it ostensibly responds to.

author: Lyn Hejinian
content:
A star screen shimmers under the moon over the urban center flashing on it red
         and green
I’ll have a suspension, mustard, topicality, glue
Kitty, kitty, kitty, kitty
Whipped gouache just about covers the situation
In the Musée Unless there’s a fallen nest on display empty of an egg once
         belonging to a song bird, species unknown, which had sung
See style, see working late, see mismatched socks, see polyphony
It is the fate of logic infinitely to undo closure but that’s just to say that it’s the
         fate of logic infinitely to be logical
So like a man goes into a shop and there’s like this other man in there whom he
         thinks he recognizes and he says like do I know you
The fallen grass in winter sprawls its spring
Regulations state that the pier can accommodate no more than one troupe of
         acrobats, thirty fishermen, or fifty tourists
Yo
The child never gives up her secret, which—don’t tell—is that she has a secret,
         and her secret has a penis
We will lose another day from the inner picture—days are not ineradicable there
What is it that one is autobiographical about
evidence: The speaker catalogs experiences (fallen nests, mismatched socks, childhood secrets) and philosophical observations ('the fate of logic infinitely to undo closure') in affectively flat, list-like syntax that maintains cognitive connection while evacuating emotional weight—reporting potentially charged material ('the child's secret has a penis,' losing days from 'inner picture') with the detached tone of museum labels or inventory.

author: Carrie Fountain
content:
All the baby knows
is the flop of her limbs
 
and the milky blue vein
of sleep and the parking lot
 
of her animal fear,
the cars left there overnight,
 
windshields dark and thick
with dew. The rest is
 
completely unknown,
the complete darkness
 
of her white room,
where she sleeps
 
on a clean sheet
printed with baby
 
crocodiles and lions
wearing diapers.
evidence: The speaker presents a descriptive portrait of infant consciousness through precise sensory imagery without defending against conflict or managing anxiety.

author: Eloise Greenfield
content:
It takes more than a wish
to catch a fish
you take the hook
you add the bait
you concentrate
and then you wait
you wait     you wait
but not a bite
the fish don’t have
an appetite
so tell them what
good bait you’ve got
and how your bait
can hit the spot
this works a whole
lot better than
a wish
if you really
want to catch
a fish
evidence: The poem offers straightforward practical advice about fishing technique without revealing psychological conflict or defense. The speaker's stance toward the situation is instructional and matter-of-fact.

poem_name: Sonnet 134: So now I have confessed that he is thine
author: William Shakespeare
content:
So now I have confessed that he is thine,
And I my self am mortgaged to thy will,
Myself I’ll forfeit, so that other mine
Thou wilt restore to be my comfort still:
But thou wilt not, nor he will not be free,
For thou art covetous, and he is kind;
He learned but surety-like to write for me,
Under that bond that him as fast doth bind.
The statute of thy beauty thou wilt take,
Thou usurer, that put’st forth all to use,
And sue a friend came debtor for my sake;
So him I lose through my unkind abuse.
    Him have I lost; thou hast both him and me:
    He pays the whole, and yet am I not free.
evidence: The speaker enacts ritualistic atonement through self-forfeiture ('Myself I'll forfeit') attempting to magically reverse or compensate for the transgression of losing 'him' to the beloved, treating the social/sexual triangle as a debt-ledger requiring symbolic balancing ('He pays the whole, and yet am I not free').

poem_name: Sonnet 12: When I do count the clock that tells the time
author: William Shakespeare
content:
When I do count the clock that tells the time, 
And see the brave day sunk in hideous night; 
When I behold the violet past prime, 
And sable curls all silver’d o’er with white;
When lofty trees I see barren of leaves 
Which erst from heat did canopy the herd,
And summer’s green all girded up in sheaves 
Borne on the bier with white and bristly beard, 
Then of thy beauty do I question make, 
That thou among the wastes of time must go,
Since sweets and beauties do themselves forsake
And die as fast as they see others grow; 
   And nothing ‘gainst Time’s scythe can make defence
   Save breed, to brave him when he takes thee hence.
evidence: The speaker spreads temporal anxiety across catalogued observations of decay (clock, night, violet, silver hair, barren trees), experiencing modest affective distress while contemplating inevitable loss, then arrives at realistic planning ('breed') to prepare for future discomfort—the beloved's death—through present inoculation with small doses of mortality.

author: Frank Ormsby
content:
The letters still come for Mrs Watters,
who must, at one time, have warmed this house
and lived as we do. Mostly small matters —
the rolled calendar that, had she stayed,
might hang now where I drew the rusted nail,
the catalogues, the last gas bill unpaid —
and always Mrs Watters. So for me
the spirit of the house is feminine,
its whisper of the one who, constantly,
draws letters that assume she has never gone.
On which I weekly write, without conviction:
'No longer living here. Address unknown.'
evidence: The speaker recounts the ongoing arrival of letters for Mrs Watters with factual precision ('Mostly small matters — the rolled calendar... the catalogues, the last gas bill unpaid') while maintaining curious emotional distance from what could be disturbing (a disappeared woman, possible death). The meditation on 'the spirit of the house' remains abstract and aestheticized rather than affectively engaged with Mrs Watters as a real person who once lived.

author: Frank Ormsby
content:
The first act of love in a new house
is not private. Loving each other
we are half-aware of door and mirror.
Our ecstasy includes the bedside chair,
the air from the landing.
Street-lamp and elm utter leaves on walls
as in no room ever. Theirs is the tongue
our tongues join in translating. Their message
is clear: tonight you cannot ignore
the world at the window.
So we love in the knowledge of a city
at a different angle. And sharing
our bed with furniture and tree we claim
their perspective, merging our lives here
in their established frame.
evidence: The poem describes a first sexual encounter in a new house with heightened sensory awareness of the surrounding environment—furniture, street-lamp, elm tree—without distortion, conflict avoidance, or defensive transformation. The speaker integrates external reality with intimate experience through mature perception rather than defensive operation.

author: Frank Ormsby
content:
Working with one eye closed or heads buried
under their drapes, they focus to preserve
the drowned shell-hole, the salient's rubble of dead,
the bleached bones of sepoys torn from the earth.
Their stills haunt us: a stretcher piled with skulls
at Cold Harbour, graves in a barren wood
that in one hour's carnage lost its name
to history and the world's memory of death.
The worst has happened, they confirm the worst:
but show us too the makeshift hospital,
the sad errand of the hospital van
among the ruins. Also enough of sky
to suggest the infinity of angles,
that behind sandbags, under the hostile towers
someone is finding time for a wry note
on bowel movements, an entry that affirms
the loved salience of what is always there:
flower of Auschwitz, bird of the Western Front.
evidence: The speaker transforms unbearable images of mass death (drowned shell-holes, piled skulls, bleached bones) into aesthetic and historical objects through the war photographers' craft, channeling primitive horror into socially valued memorial work that produces 'stills [that] haunt us' while maintaining passionate engagement with the material—finding in extreme carnage the 'loved salience' of persistent life (flowers, birds, wry notes).

author: Elizabeth Arnold
content:
If they'd had writing in time, Cuba could have been Crete,
watery source of the Minoans and thus the Greeks.
What's lost? A possible us
growing like new foliage out of stony ground, emerging?
Last voice, first, a whole world calling—
awful, inaudible—into the unstoppable loud (roaring!)
hurricane-force sea wind.
evidence: The speaker engages in speculative historical meditation on cultural origins and loss without exhibiting psychological defensiveness—the conditional 'could have been' and ontological questions ('What's lost? A possible us') reflect philosophical contemplation rather than anxiety-driven distortion or self-protection.

author: Elizabeth Arnold
content:
I said
no to you
so often, couldn't see.
Do you know how that
changed you
—divided
what you saw in me
or anything
(if you did)
from what you
said
or mostly
didn't?
And another bottle
drained,
your head
lolling off the chair's back,
the mirror's
face blacked.
evidence: The speaker directly confronts 'you' with accusations framed as questions ('Do you know how that changed you') while documenting their own refusal ('I said no to you so often') and the other's suffering ('another bottle drained, your head lolling'), broadcasting injury and attributing causation to the target's behavior in a manner that simultaneously accuses and observes fresh suffering.

poem_name: Because We Never Practiced With The Escape Chamber
author: Alice Fulton
content:
we had to read the instructions as we sank.
In a hand like carded lace. Not nuclear warheads
on the sea's floor nor the violet flow over the reactor
will outlive this sorrowful rhyme.
evidence: The speaker narrates catastrophic sinking ('we had to read the instructions as we sank') and invokes nuclear apocalypse imagery in a curiously detached, observational register—syntax that presents mortal emergency as procedural matter ('had to read the instructions'), with the final couplet delivering cosmic stakes ('will outlive this sorrowful rhyme') in language that maintains aesthetic distance rather than visceral terror.

author: Andrew Shields
content:
The barstool's capacious, then ever more enclosed,
with every beer, as evening erodes.
A few capricious tourists off the cozy track
propose unbeaten toasts. He'll soon be going back.
Mannequin musicians play mandolins or thumb pianos,
bleat out a reggae air on ragged banjos.
The booths patter with the local lingo.
Smattered English polishes the windows.
An amble to the john, the mirror's random crack.
The urinal's askew. He'll soon be going back.
The minimal solicitudes of seven-minute flirts:
blurted-out soliloquies, well-trained parting words.
Insert pejorative for natives here,
before a sputtered call for one more beer.
Dusty carnations; carnival bric-a-brac—
long-faded revaltions. He'll soon be going back.
evidence: The speaker catalogs details of the expatriate bar scene with meticulous specificity ('Mannequin musicians,' 'seven-minute flirts,' 'dusty carnations') while maintaining affective flatness despite the refrain 'He'll soon be going back'—a significant transition narrated as mere fact. The pejorative slot ('Insert pejorative for natives here') mechanizes even racism into formal procedure, severing feeling from ideation.

author: Nikki Giovanni
content:
If Black History Month is not
viable then wind does not
carry the seeds and drop them
on fertile ground
rain does not
dampen the land
and encourage the seeds
to root
sun does not
warm the earth
and kiss the seedlings
and tell them plain:
You’re As Good As Anybody Else
You’ve Got A Place Here, Too
evidence: The speaker employs direct logical argumentation to assert cultural-historical validity, linking Black History Month to natural cycles without distortion, displacement, or defensive transformation of affect.

poem_name: The Strange Girl Asks Politely To Be Called Princess
author: Kathryn Nuernberger
content:
When the strange girl skips rope her hair flies
like a porpoise. She collects things that melt
and things that tick, circles and cubes
and checkerboards in a drawer
 
she can pull out from her navel.
Other children, alerted by the rumble
of marbles in her chest, chase her
across the field. She insists she is only
 
hungry, but they pin her down and open her
up. Cockroaches rush out and bullies run
and squeal, crushing carapaces underfoot.
She gathers as many as she can,
 
tells them she’s sorry there is no lock. She’s sorry,
but good children shouldn’t have secrets.
evidence: The strange girl's body contains impossible compartments (drawer from navel, marbles in chest, cockroaches rushing out) that literalize fragmentation of consciousness, while the theatrical, playful quality of these surreal violations transforms dangerous violation into something oddly charming. Her repeated apologies ('she's sorry there is no lock... sorry, but good children shouldn't have secrets') perform ritualistic atonement without addressing the actual violation.

author: Elizabeth Willis
content:
sleeps into heaven with his lamps on, finishing explan-
atory negotiations for a while. Desert the enemy. Star
formations, sandstone understanding, rock time in gen-
eral, whatever. Latching onto ecstasy, words that change
on waking, clover as a syrup of spring mind. Working
off a deficit of sleep or cash, you know who your friends
are. Singled out in traffic, lurching into light, having
lunch. You’re a little one with sand in your eyes, with
green on your horn, with milk on your chin. With flow-
ering ears and hearsay.
evidence: The poem presents fragmentary, associative imagery ('sleeps into heaven with his lamps on,' 'clover as a syrup of spring mind') in observational mode without a clear defensive psychological stance toward conflict or distress.

author: Elizabeth Willis
content:
It’s turneresque in twilight. The word comes at me
with its headlights on, so it’s revelation and not death.
I figure I’m halfway home though I’ve only started.
Nothing is moving but me: I’m a blackbird. The neigh-
bor’s in labor, but so am I, pushing against the road.
Physics tells us nothing is lost, but I’ve been copping
time from death and can’t relent for every job the stars
drop on my back.
evidence: The speaker engages in realistic, affect-laden planning for future discomfort ('I figure I'm halfway home though I've only started,' 'pushing against the road') while experiencing modest distress ('can't relent for every job the stars / drop on my back'), spreading anxiety through self-inoculation with the present emotional labor.

author: Rebecca Wolff
content:
Let’s go out and buy something. In the sun.
 
No, let’s stay home and make something, the sun floods the room. It
could be green, on paper. It could be money. That’s the way to create
new matter.
 
That’s how I detach boats from moorings—my boat, my mooring—
     the harbor
shallow in low tide
 
skiff propelled over buffeting sands flats on
 
sheer
puissance.
evidence: The speaker moves from mundane consumption to imagining creative acts that generate 'new matter' and culminates in detaching 'boats from moorings' through 'sheer puissance'—a fantasy of unlimited personal power where internal will directly controls external reality ('my boat, my mooring') without recognizing separate agency or natural constraints.

author: Tyehimba Jess
content:
Did a slave song at a master’s bidding
mark Tom while asleep in Charity's womb?
The whole plantation would be called to sing
and dance in Master Epps’ large parlor room—
after work sprung from dawn and dragged past dusk,
after children auctioned to parts unknown,
after funerals and whippings. Thus
was the whim of the patriarch. No groans
allowed, just high steppin’ celebration,
grins all around, gritted or sincere.
Charity threw feet, hips, arms into motion
to please the tyrant piano. Was it here
Tom learned how music can prove the master?
While he spun in a womb of slavish laughter?
evidence: The enforced performance of 'high steppin' celebration, grins all around, gritted or sincere' after trauma transforms unbearable reality into theatrical play. Charity's forced dancing to 'please the tyrant piano' creates discontinuity between body (in motion) and consciousness (in terror), with the speaker meditating on whether Tom learned in utero that survival requires splitting music from its emotional truth—'how music can prove the master' while 'spun in a womb of slavish laughter.'

author: Tyehimba Jess
content:
Our Box Henry hid    away.
John Berryman’s Ol’ Henry sulked.
I see his point—he was trying to put one over.
It was that he thought that we thought
he could do it that breaks our Henry out this-a-way.
So, here he will come out and talk.
 
All the world like a fool-bent lover
once did see from Ol’ Henry’s side.
Here comes a departure:
hereafter, something falls out. Now, it might go fraught.
Let us see how Box Henry, pried
open for all to see, survives.
 
What he has now to say is a long
wonder the world can bear and see.
Once, with his black-face worn, John was glad
all at the top. And he sang.
Here, in this land where some strong be,
let Box Henry grow in every head.
                           From Dream Song 1
evidence: The speaker redirects affect from Berryman's racist minstrelsy ('his black-face worn') toward the abstracted figure 'Box Henry,' obsessively preoccupied with this substitute persona ('let Box Henry grow in every head') rather than directly confronting the original object of anger—Berryman's appropriation and the wound it inflicted.

author: Henrietta Cordelia Ray
content:
The subtlest strain a great musician weaves,
Cannot attain in rhythmic harmony
To music in his soul. May it not be
Celestial lyres send hints to him? He grieves
That half the sweetness of the song, he leaves
Unheard in the transition. Thus do we
Yearn to translate the wondrous majesty
Of some rare mood, when the rapt soul receives
A vision exquisite. Yet who can match
The sunset’s iridescent hues? Who sing
The skylark’s ecstasy so seraph-fine?
We struggle vainly, still we fain would catch
Such rifts amid life’s shadows, for they bring
Glimpses ineffable of things divine.
evidence: The speaker transforms the frustrated desire to capture transcendent experience into the socially valued creative act itself ('We struggle vainly, still we fain would catch / Such rifts'), maintaining passionate engagement with artistic limitation while channeling primitive longing for divine merger into aesthetic production with 'real consequences'—the poem.

poem_name: 'My own heart let me more have pity on'
author: Gerard Manley Hopkins
content:
My own heart let me more have pity on; let
Me live to my sad self hereafter kind,
Charitable; not live this tormented mind
With this tormented mind tormenting yet.
I cast for comfort I can no more get
By groping round my comfortless, than blind
Eyes in their dark can day or thirst can find
Thirst's all-in-all in all a world of wet.
Soul, self; come, poor Jackself, I do advise
You, jaded, let be; call off thoughts awhile
Elsewhere; leave comfort root-room; let joy size
At God knows when to God knows what; whose smile
's not wrung, see you; unforeseen times rather — as skies
Betweenpie mountains — lights a lovely mile.
evidence: The speaker consciously advises himself to 'call off thoughts awhile / Elsewhere; leave comfort root-room' — postponing rather than eliminating attention to his torment, with explicit acknowledgment of the conflict ('this tormented mind / With this tormented mind tormenting yet') while waiting for grace 'At God knows when to God knows what' with finite, realistic hope.

author: Peter Everwine
content:
When Mrs. McCausland comes to mind
she slips through a small gap in oblivion
and walks down her front steps, in her hand
a small red velvet pillow she tucks
under the head of Old Jim Schreiber,
who is lying dead-drunk against the curb
of busy Market Street. Then she turns,
labors up the steps and is gone . . .
A small story. Or rather, the memory
of a story I heard as a boy. The witnesses
are not to be found, the steps lead nowhere,
the pillow has collapsed into a thread of dust . . .
Do the dead come back only to remind us
they, too, were once among the living,
and that the story we make of our lives
is a mystery of luminous, but uncertain moments,
a shuffle of images we carry toward sleep—
Mrs. McCausland with her velvet pillow,
Old Jim at peace—a story, like a small
clearing in the woods at night, seen
from the windows of a passing train.
evidence: The speaker anticipates oblivion and death ('a shuffle of images we carry toward sleep') while actively contemplating mortality through remembered narrative, spreading existential anxiety across the poem's duration by dwelling on how 'the dead come back only to remind us' of their lived reality, transforming potential terror into measured reflection.

author: George Bilgere
content:
I remember watching my father stop
halfway up the driveway because my tricycle
was blocking the way to the garage,
and how he solved the problem
by picking up the tricycle by the handlebars
and smashing it through the windshield
of our brand new family station wagon,
his face red with scotch, his black tie
and jacket flapping with effort, the tricycle
making its way a little farther with each blow
into the roomy interior of the latest model
as the safety glass relented, the tricycle
and the windshield both praiseworthy
in their toughness, the struggle between them
somehow making perfect sense
in midday on our quiet suburban street,
the windshield the anvil, the trike the hammer,
the marriage the forge, and failure
glowing in the heat, beaten
and tempered, slowly taking shape.
evidence: The speaker recounts devastating childhood trauma—father destroying tricycle and windshield in drunken rage—while maintaining curiously detached, almost appreciative theoretical language ('praiseworthy in their toughness,' 'making perfect sense') and transforming violence into extended metallurgical metaphor ('anvil,' 'hammer,' 'forge') that distances emotional impact through aesthetic elaboration.

author: Natasha Trethewey
content:
We mourn the broken things, chair legs
wrenched from their seats, chipped plates,
the threadbare clothes. We work the magic
of glue, drive the nails, mend the holes.
We save what we can, melt small pieces
of soap, gather fallen pecans, keep neck bones
for soup. Beating rugs against the house,
we watch dust, lit like stars, spreading
across the yard. Late afternoon, we draw
the blinds to cool the rooms, drive the bugs
out. My mother irons, singing, lost in reverie.
I mark the pages of a mail-order catalog,
listen for passing cars. All day we watch
for the mail, some news from a distant place.
evidence: The speaker maintains conscious awareness of longing ('All day we watch / for the mail, some news from a distant place') while engaging in household rituals that genuinely postpone but do not eliminate attention to what is absent—'My mother irons, singing, lost in reverie. / I mark the pages of a mail-order catalog'—waiting with incremental tasks and finite timeframes.

author: Dana Gioia
content:
Echo of the clocktower, footstep
in the alleyway, sweep
of the wind sifting the leaves.
Jeweller of the spiderweb, connoisseur
of autumn's opulence, blade of lightning
harvesting the sky.
Keeper of the small gate, choreographer
of entrances and exits, midnight
whisper traveling the wires.
Seducer, healer, deity or thief,
I will see you soon enough—
in the shadow of the rainfall,
in the brief violet darkening a sunset—
but until then I pray watch over him
as a mountain guards its covert ore
and the harsh falcon its flightless young.
evidence: The speaker directly addresses death or a deity ('I will see you soon enough') while experiencing modest affective distress in the present moment, using prayer to prepare emotionally for future loss by requesting protection for 'him' with fierce, affect-laden imagery ('harsh falcon its flightless young').

poem_name: The Man Who Gave Birth to a Panda
author: Idra Novey
content:
He had to have it, his mother told him. How could he not, with so few left in the world? He felt heaviest at night with the miracle of it. He was a vessel now. A receptacle for a threatened being. What if he rose too fast and killed it? Or maybe his stillness would do it, too much sitting around, stunned and hungry. And what if the bear emerged alive and another formed in its place, would he have to have that one, too, and another one after that? 
 
He dreamed of the panda’s tiny eyes opening inside him, the doctor’s wide incision, a whirring pain, and then the furry thing emerging, the bear turning to him as to a stranger and whether that would be it—his bit part in the history of the future.
evidence: The speaker obsessively atones for unacknowledged aggression through bearing the endangered panda—'He was a vessel now. A receptacle for a threatened being'—with magical thinking that his movements or stillness might kill it, treating hostile impulses as tantamount to dangerous deeds requiring ritualistic maternal sacrifice to neutralize guilt.

author: Marilyn L. Taylor
content:
She turns the bedlamp on. The book falls open 
in her mottled hands, and while she reads
her mouth begins to quiver, forming words 
like Breathless. Promises. Elope.
As she turns the leaves, Eudora's cheek
takes on a bit of bloom. Her frowzy hair 
thickens and turns gold, her dim eyes clear,
the wattles vanish from her slender neck.
Her waist, emerging from its ring of flesh, 
bends to the side. Breasts that used to hang 
like pockets rise and ripen; her long legs 
tremble. Her eyes close, she holds her breath— 
the steamy pages flutter by, unread,
as lover after lover finds her bed.
evidence: Eudora withdraws from the reality of her aging body and isolation into elaborate romance novel fantasy where her internal world transforms her physically ('Her frowzy hair thickens and turns gold'), substituting imagined lovers for actual relationship engagement ('lover after lover finds her bed' while 'pages flutter by, unread').

author: Faith Shearin
content:
Go north a dozen years
on a road overgrown with vines
to find the days after you were born.
Flowers remembered their colors and trees
were frothy and the hospital was
behind us now, its brick indifference
forgotten by our car mirrors. You were
revealed to me: tiny, delicate,
your head smelling of some other world.
Turn right after the circular room
where I kept my books and right again
past the crib where you did not sleep
and you will find the window where
I held you that June morning
when you opened your eyes. They were
blue, tentative, not the deep chocolate
they would later become. You were gazing
into the world: at our walls,
my red cup, my sleepless hair and though
I'm told you could not focus, and you
no longer remember, we were seeing
one another after seasons of darkness.
evidence: The speaker emotionally rehearses and prepares for the child's development ('They were blue, tentative, not the deep chocolate they would later become'), spreading awareness of time's passage across the narrative while maintaining both cognitive planning and proportionate affect about impermanence ('you no longer remember').

author: J. Michael Martinez
content:
Wherein she martyrs the mirror:                  this carnival of stone,                  her lips dilate                  the negation—space into starpoint
 
Wherein she, to be both sacrum & wrist—                  neither the fugitive epidermis,                  nor the unlocked ashblack—                  sovereigns the shadow swell as love
 
Wherein she ardors the emptiness open,                  proof the unanchored
                  Spirit of my silence,                  her revisions clothing my brightest orgasm—
 
Wherein she says, I can hear you,                  the seed under the belly’s flesh—love the far shore, 
                  she says, For She withdraws the Spring wild
                 
                  Thrust in her mother’s surrender,
                  iron ocean blackened to aurora.
evidence: The speaker constructs an exalted, omnipotent feminine figure who 'martyrs the mirror,' 'sovereigns the shadow swell as love,' and whose revisions clothe 'my brightest orgasm'—attributing transcendent, near-divine powers of transformation to this idealized other who mediates all experience and appears to possess unlimited creative and salvific capacity.

author: James K. Zimmerman
content:
this morning I felt my life
if you were dead
the expansiveness of the bed
the birds still singing
the remnants of the smell
of coffee in the morning
the emptiness of thought
the deafening silence of my heart
evidence: The speaker rehearses future loss through conditional imagining ('if you were dead'), experiencing modest affective distress ('the emptiness of thought / the deafening silence of my heart') while maintaining awareness of both the continued external world (birds, coffee) and the anticipated internal devastation—a self-inoculation with emotional pain that spreads anxiety across present consciousness.

author: Ron Koertge
content:
No one would take her when Ruth passed.
As the survivors assessed some antiques,
I kept hearing, "She's old. Somebody
should put her down."
I picked her up instead. Every night I tell her
about the fish who died for her, the ones
in the cheerful aluminum cans.
She lies on my chest to sleep, rising
and falling, rising and falling like a rowboat
fastened to a battered dock by a string.
evidence: The speaker rescues the unwanted cat ('I picked her up instead') during a period of bereavement and material assessment, transforming personal grief into caregiving. The nightly ritual of telling Lily about 'the fish who died for her' and the intimate image of the cat sleeping on the speaker's chest reveal vicarious gratification—the speaker gives the comfort and connection they themselves need while the object (Lily) is genuinely blessed by rescue from euthanasia.

author: Rachel Richardson
content:
A child climbs into a cardboard house,
shuts its doors and windows
to hold in the dark, and lies on her back
inside, looking up through its cut-out moon
and stars. She knows she is not looking
at the sky. But she calls out, still,It's nighttime! I'm looking at the sky!
evidence: The speaker transforms the child's primitive impulse (escape into darkness, retreat from reality) into creative, imaginative play with 'real consequences'—the cardboard construction, the deliberate darkness, the sustained pretense that yields genuine experience ('But she calls out, still'). The child harnesses intensity of feeling through socially valued creative activity (imaginative play) while maintaining authentic engagement.

author: Dara Wier
content:
You are a seahorse unraveling.
You are the back of a landhorse looking backward.
Gotten away form have you thrown yourself racing.
Who took what was not out of thunderous shade
In an all-knowing sycamore's branches.
What who do you make of stone steps you stepped through.
You took the boat onto flattened waters.
White wall of blue morning fog to slip into.
You withstood what is was that was wailing you through.
There you were standing on nothing, looking down at two
Blackfeathered slashes your two hands held on to.
Off were you going aloft would birds such as these take
You.
Who leaned you and stood you and shook you and shook you.
evidence: The speaker addresses a 'you' undergoing radical transformations and out-of-body experiences ('seahorse unraveling,' 'standing on nothing,' 'Off were you going aloft'), with syntax fragmenting into dreamlike discontinuity ('Gotten away form have you thrown yourself racing') that suggests altered consciousness and detachment from integrated selfhood.

author: Dara Wier
content:
You're presenting me with a telescopic line of reasoning.
You think because one dies then to die must be a good idea.
Let me get this straight. So you think to follow suit is what's
In the cards and the works and the stars. It may be that's
The next step that's clear or it may be there's another way.
You may find a friend for whom to die is not the be-all
Or the end. There were ten rooms and a thousand shelves
And ten thousand bottles filled with ten million tickets. You
Were on the end of the ladder in a blue sky filled with litter.
It was tantamount to a ticker tape parade on the streets of
A stunned city. Staccato ropes couldn't hold you any longer.
And in the evening's sudden stillness I breathed in your ear.
From now on out everything gets said in a whisper. If you like
If you want if you care to come closer. This way is better.
evidence: The speaker acknowledges another's suicidal reasoning ('you think to follow suit'), then ritually counterbalances it with compensatory whispered intimacy and alternative pathways ('there's another way... This way is better'), attempting to magically neutralize the death-wish through verbal atonement and closeness-offering.

poem_name: You'd Think the Sky Would Run Out of Water
author: Todd Colby
content:
You'd think the sky would run out of water,
but it won't; it just keeps coming down. I need someone
to marvel at the breath escaping from me.
Do you have a natural resource you prefer to exploit?
Does someone think of you and turn the channel?
How would you ever know?
Have you ever zoned out during Downton Abbey?
I'm certain of something I'd prefer not to tell you about.
"Slow down," you say. But I can already see my breath,
and its only October. Walking with you is making everything
watery and spazzed out, like a movie about sex
where I have sex and people are all like,
"He's amazing, we really like his sex style!"
But I digress. Will you please stand up when called upon to tell
the audience how wonderful I was in my best moments;
like someone in senior management delegating things and being sure
of everything but how to stop? I promise I'll make this up to 
you. I'll write your name on the menu board,
and people will come into the store all expectant of you.
evidence: The speaker solicits reassurance through recursive complaints ('You'd think the sky would run out of water, but it won't'), demands the addressee witness and validate him ('I need someone to marvel at the breath escaping from me,' 'Will you please stand up when called upon to tell the audience how wonderful I was'), yet embeds dismissive rejection in the request itself through digressive swerves and mock-grandiose fantasies that undermine genuine connection, ensuring the addressee's witnessing remains inadequate while binding them in obligation ('I promise I'll make this up to you').

author: Todd Colby
content:
Right now I'm sipping a mango lassi
in Action Town. I'm not bereft at all. In fact,
I'm pleasantly aware of people moving on all fours.
Our voice and text messages will
be erased in no time. When I walk into stores
they call me Spark Plug because I have a sparky manner.
Things get all wavy from their constant attention.
It's going to be okay. I'm a good habit:
you don't need to trick me.
I'm not sure where I should put my hands
when I walk down the street with you.
It's funny to think about who I would
call if something really awful happened.
I'd be jumping up and down not knowing who to call.
My way of doing things is scary even to me.
Remember the toothbrush, the honey bear,
and the meal that we thought would never end?
I'm counting on you to come through high water,
to come through chaos, and to Action Town,
where we'll lift our aces to skyward aviation.
evidence: The speaker insists 'I'm not bereft at all. In fact, / I'm pleasantly aware' and 'It's going to be okay' while simultaneously acknowledging catastrophic uncertainty ('if something really awful happened. / I'd be jumping up and down not knowing who to call') and a disorienting environment where 'people moving on all fours' is treated as unremarkable—the characteristic bright refusal to acknowledge threatening reality.

author: Sara Nicholson
content:
Covet not the sun its honorarium
nor authorize the stars their grants to write.
The sojournors spotted a forest
adrift with language, but couldn't make sense of it.
The woods at odds with the usual channels
and those neighboring mountains
didn't look like pyramids, no matter the scale.
Read this part as if the sum of lilac
mattered to you. For love of someone
else's vortex, toss the luminaries aside.
In lieu of flowers, please donate
and in exchange for your sympathy I'll give you
edits on the level of the line. Poems are to war
as are ghosts to the proverbial orchard. 
Headstones offer us nothing
but an end to syntax. Microsoft 
Word inverts the sea. I read
your manuscript. Reader, I married it.
I fear for the estuaries.
They are so small this time of year.
evidence: The speaker delivers emotionally charged content ('I fear for the estuaries') in a flat, theoretical register that oscillates between abstract propositions ('Poems are to war as are ghosts to the proverbial orchard') and detached meta-commentary ('edits on the level of the line'), maintaining syntactic complexity while evacuating affective engagement even when naming fear or love.

author: Julien Poirier
content:
The baboon is eating strawberries
expired on the shelf
of the Ft. Bragg Safeway.
A coastal landscape
with waves
like inexpertly torn tinfoil hangs
above his left shoulder
at the check-in desk
of an abandoned motel.
The ocean itself
is only a sound
through a torn window screen
where some hardscrabble bush
about to flower
is just a smell.
Will something appropriately
random please
happen to convince me
reality is still playing
with a full deck?
evidence: The poem presents surreal observational details (baboon, expired strawberries, torn tinfoil waves) leading to a wry request for randomness, maintaining a detached, ironic stance toward absurdity without exhibiting defensive transformation of underlying conflict.

author: Lucy Ives
content:
24.
Zachary talks about wanting to be with someone because he
feels like he can be a better person for someone else. He says he
doesn't know what he's doing, wishes he had someone to do
things for. I tell him I think we are very different people.
Theory as pure verbalism: a generated knowledge with
negative impact.
If I begin to miss someone,  I think, "Life is bad anyway."
evidence: The speaker acknowledges missing someone but immediately converts the feeling into an abstract, affectless conclusion ('Life is bad anyway'), while theorizing about relationships in detached terms ('Theory as pure verbalism: a generated knowledge with negative impact') that strip emotional resonance from the content.

author: Lucy Ives
content:
75.
Even with geographic proximity, there may exist temporal
systems such that two people cannot meet one another or will
never meet again.
Dream last night includes swimming. Something about a thick
river, keep riding boots on it, travel from station to station,
temporary home to home. It is Berlin or a group of islands
called "Connecticut." I am alone or have a child or am with
my brother. It is sunset, when one seek something.
It is possible we somehow die for a time, a year, a month, a day,
without realizing this, then awake to find ourselves, which is to
say "someone," present again, attentive, expectant, apologetic
even?
evidence: The speaker frames profoundly disorienting experiences—temporal dislocation, identity fragmentation ('I am alone or have a child or am with my brother'), possible death—in abstract, theoretical language ('temporal systems such that two people cannot meet') with a detached, observational tone that lacks emotional resonance despite the disturbing content.

author: Hannah Brooks-Motl
content:
One has some glory—brutal and rough, a kind of encounter
Fame is not pursuant
