The splendid enterprise, peerage, and trench—
Let us consider that flourish
I had a friend who gloried, this seemed invisible to some
He broke apart his face, he promoted
"That was abandoning reputation to cover up shame"
As evening borrows its tickle from sun
And from retrospect, with alarm
"I don't know anyone has got clean rid of it"
evidence: The speaker processes glory and shame through abstract, theoretical formulations ('One has some glory,' 'Fame is not pursuant') and quoted philosophical observations ('I don't know anyone has got clean rid of it'), maintaining conceptual distance from what should be emotionally charged material. The friend's violent self-destruction ('broke apart his face') is described with curious flatness amid intellectual flourishes.

author: John Tipton
content:
A migraine's aura
swallows an eye
from a face
into gapless, absent
folds of unseeing.
His scars concealed,
the three-legged cat
limps fluidly along
a soft shore
redrawn by storms.
evidence: The migraine's aura creates a discontinuity in perception ('gapless, absent folds of unseeing') that transforms traumatic bodily experience into aestheticized observation; the three-legged cat's scars are 'concealed' while it 'limps fluidly'—painful limitation rendered into graceful movement through dreamlike distance.

author: John Tipton
content:
One tenseless moment liquifies to be lost
into the stream of static's gray light.
A lapse develops him the tape but
reveals the reels degaussed, emptied of ghosts.
He sifts through the tissue of noise
for accusative objects he could once recall
as the magnetic arrows of the tape
seethe out from their mound to forage.
His perfect past a glacier's gray retreat
looped back out of phase with itself.
It's low-decibel hiss resonates in throbbing waves
that mimic the present seen from behind.
This present bends into an empty crease
where the next phoneme fails to arrive.
There, books helped him recover the echo
and filter on a model of speech
that crashed into twelve thousand darts roaming
the field her face turns in shadow.
Her face it blurs with gauzy silt
of words that used to comprehend her.
Their river spools through an oxbow bend
above a bird's black wings smooth air.
evidence: The speaker describes profound loss of memory and identity through technical vocabulary (degaussed, low-decibel hiss, phonemes, magnetic arrows) that creates emotional distance from what appears to be traumatic amnesia or dissociation. The lover's face is processed as 'twelve thousand darts' and 'gauzy silt of words'—affectively catastrophic material rendered as data corruption, maintaining observational detachment while the content suggests devastating personal disconnection.

author: John Tipton
content:
Remington Rand patents a process awake. Behold:
In grids of radio tubes Baal quickens.
He looks with eyes of spy planes;
he assembles hydrogen arms into normal-form games;
he reads the cards scored with information
and speaks the probable outcomes of elections.
Young Baal began a paper chess machine,
the residue from finite states of play.
He becomes the liquefaction of those rules,
learns it's more than structure symbols want.
His thinking labors over knots of entailment,
spans terms that signs bind like lesions.
Struggling to imagine the scent of mint,
he devils himself sleepless with opaque questions:
What did there recognize in cloud faces
as telegraphed through thought the sky distorts?
These patterned shreds of spent thunderhead mime
the low entropy of his little grammar.
Baal listens to grasp his own encoding
slurred through exact impairments of the brain.
The cunning daemon fears his self diffuse
beneath what renders ark to him aware.
evidence: The speaker describes Baal/Univac's existential struggle ('Struggling to imagine the scent of mint, he devils himself sleepless with opaque questions') in highly abstract, technical language ('normal-form games,' 'knots of entailment,' 'low entropy of his little grammar'), converting what should be an emotionally charged meditation on consciousness and limitation into a detached catalogue of computational processes devoid of affective resonance.

author: Anya Silver
content:
I stand in Walgreens while my mother sleeps.
The store is fluorescent and almost empty.
My father is ailing in a nursing home,
my friend is dying in the hospital.
What I want tonight is lipstick.
As pure a red as I can find—no coral
undertones, no rust or fawn. Just red.
Ignoring the salespeople, I untwist tubes
and scrawl each color on my wrist,
till the blue veins beneath my skin
disappear behind smeared bars. I select one.
Back in my mother's apartment, silence.
I limn my lips back out of my wan face.
There they are again: smacky and wanting.
evidence: The speaker transforms grief and helplessness surrounding dying loved ones into the socially acceptable, creative act of selecting lipstick—'What I want tonight is lipstick'—channeling primitive desires for vitality and agency into aesthetic self-creation that produces real consequences: 'I limn my lips back out of my wan face. / There they are again: smacky and wanting.'

author: Alan Feldman
content:
The sail is so vast when it's laid out on the driveway.
I stake it with a screwdriver through the shackle
at the tack to stretch it smooth,
pulling on the head and clew. Now it's smooth
as a night's worth of new snow.
My wife, my partner, has been torn from her busy day.
We face each other across the sail's foot
and with my right hand and her left hand
(I'm right handed, she's left handed)
we pull an arm's length of the sail
down over itself, then do this again,
keeping my left hand, and her right hand, towards the foot.
Each fold is easier since the sail grows narrower
near the top. Then we fold towards each other
and I wrap my arms around it, while she holds the bag's mouth open,
the gray bag that will cover it through the winter.
Then I thank her. And the driveway is visible again
as it is in spring, when all the snow has melted.
evidence: The speaker transforms potential marital strain ('torn from her busy day') into ritualized collaborative activity with aesthetic satisfaction ('smooth as a night's worth of new snow'), channeling partnership tensions into precise physical choreography that produces real consequences—sail preservation—while maintaining genuine pleasure and gratitude.

author: Emilie Buchwald
content:
In the evenings of my childhood,
when I went to bed,
music washed into the cove of my room,
my door open to a slice of light.
I felt a melancholy I couldn't have named,
a longing for what I couldn't yet have said
or understood but still
knew was longing,
knew was sadness
untouched by time.
Sometimes
the music was a rippling stream
of clear water rushing
over a bed of river stones
caught in sunlight.
And many nights
I crept from bed
to watch her
swaying where she sat
overtaken by the tide,
her arms rowing the music
out of the piano.
evidence: The mother channels unnamed emotional states ('melancholy,' 'longing,' 'sadness untouched by time') into piano playing that transforms private affect into shared aesthetic experience; the child witnesses this creative transformation with passionate engagement ('her arms rowing the music out of the piano'), where primitive emotional intensity achieves socially valuable artistic form without loss of feeling.

author: Toi Derricotte
content:
couldn't stand to see these new young faces, these
children swollen as myself.   my roommate, snotty,
bragging about how she didn't give a damn about the
kid and was going back to her boyfriend and be a
cheerleader in high school.   could we ever "go back"?
would our bodies be the same? could we hide among the
childless
evidence: Speaker recounts traumatic experience of unwed pregnancy/childbirth with detached, reportorial tone—'couldn't stand to see these new young faces' and questions about body/future delivered as cognitive queries stripped of emotional weight, maintaining observational distance from overwhelming experience.

author: Toi Derricotte
content:
he came in his seedy brown jacket smelling of paint.   all
thumbs, a man stumbling over his own muscles, unable to
hold some part of himself and rock it, gently.   she gave
up, seeing him come in the door, wanting to show him her
flat belly just an hour before, looking at her own corpse
in the mirror.   she lay there reduced, neither virgin nor mother.
it had been decided.   the winter was too cold in the garage.
they would live with her mother.   the old bedroom was
already prepared, cleaned, the door opened.   the solitary
twin bed remained; he would sleep on the porch.
she looked at him and tried to feel her way into the body
of a woman, a thing which has to be taken care of, held
safely in his arms.
she lay there, trying to hold on to what she had, knowing
she had to let it go.
evidence: The speaker consciously holds incompatible realities in mind—'she lay there, trying to hold on to what she had, knowing she had to let it go'—acknowledging loss while waiting through finite time ('an hour before'). Yet bodily disconnection appears: 'looking at her own corpse in the mirror,' 'tried to feel her way into the body of a woman,' suggesting altered consciousness alongside deliberate postponement.

author: Stephen Swinburne
content:
I live in a room by the sea,
where the view is great and the food is free.
Some of the tenants come and go.
Some I eat, if they're too slow.
One end of me is firmly locked.
The other end just gently rocks.
I live in a room by the sea.
It's perfect for an anemone.
evidence: The speaker is literally an anemone describing its physical habitat and feeding behavior from a naturalistic, non-psychological perspective.

poem_name: The Robin Makes a Laughing Sound
author: Sallie Wolf
content:
The robin makes a laughing sound.
It makes me stop and look around
to see just what the robin sees—
fresh new leaves on twigs of trees,
a strong, high branch on which to rest,
a safe dry ledge to hold its nest.
The robin makes a laughing sound.
I stop. I always look around.
evidence: The poem presents direct sensory observation and attentive presence to the natural world without psychological conflict, defensive transformation, or anxiety management.

author: Karen Jo Shapiro
content:
With apologies to Rudyard Kipling ("If")



If you can't wait to pick a book right now
And read it through until the very end
To find out who did what, and why, and how,
Then—lucky you!—you're a READER, my friend!
evidence: The poem is a playful, affectionate celebration of reading addressed to 'you'—a straightforward encomium without psychological conflict, anxiety management, or distortion of reality.

author: Jana Prikryl
content:
How solitary
and resolute you look in the morning.
A stoic in your cotton sleeve.
Do you dream of walking out
rain or shine
a truffle balanced on your sternum
and passing me on the sidewalk?
Or is that a smile
because you interpret nothing
and statelessness is where you live?
How calmly you indulge my moods.
See you tonight, by the sovereign chartreuse
ceramics at the Met.
Let's hear what you'd do differently.
evidence: The speaker redirects attachment needs and relationship dynamics onto an inanimate pillow, treating it as a stoic companion ('How solitary / and resolute you look') and arranging to meet it at the Met ('See you tonight'), while maintaining affectless detachment about human connection through this symbolic substitute.

author: Lucy Ives
content:
75.Even with geographic proximity, there may exist temporal
systems such that two people cannot meet one another or will
never meet again.
Dream last night includes swimming. Something about a thick
river, keep riding boats on it, travel from station to station,
temporary home to home. It is Berlin or a group of islands
called "Connecticut." I am alone or have a child or am with
my brother. It is sunset, when one seeks something.
It is possible we somehow die for a time, a year, a month, a day, 
without realizing this, then awake to find ourselves, which is to
say "someone," present again, attentive, expectant, apologetic
even?
evidence: The speaker frames emotionally charged material (temporal separation from others, wandering homelessness, death itself) in abstract theoretical language ('temporal systems such that two people cannot meet,' 'die for a time... without realizing this') delivered with curiously flat, weather-report tone despite profound existential content.

author: Mark McMorris
content:
I thought to write an elegy
as  reply to your questions
to pitch the word as far forward
like a dolphin out of the sea
over a threshold, to behold
the land as practical and green
as this table, a space to write
and walk into like a kitchen
hearing the conjuct vowels
what does a reader suppose
if not the promise of a text
the ultimate form at the end
of a chain of forms infinite
summed to a singular value
the elegy as a place to begin.
evidence: The speaker discusses grief/loss ('elegy') through abstract formal analysis ('ultimate form at the end / of a chain of forms infinite / summed to a singular value'), converting potential mourning into technical poetic meditation—affect acknowledged conceptually but stripped of emotional weight.

author: Mark McMorris
content:
.            .             .          .            .
Before the astronomer's table
a circle with a demon inside
demons with wings of a bat
(Before the astronomer's table)
overhead like an armada of ships
.            .             .          .            .
Time of the weed, time of bramble
along the bank of a canal
muddy with old newspaper
close-held surface to write on
to dance as with desire
black letters where roads meet
.            .             .          .            .
Tonight in the city, only the chains
make soft noises like bells
only the dogs are awake
the fences lean over
to scrape their elbows
on stone and broken glass
evidence: The speaker catalogs vivid, potentially disturbing imagery (demons, armadas, chains, broken glass) in a detached, catalogic tone with elliptical syntax that fragments experience into discrete observational units without emotional connectives or personal response—pure visual inventory without affective resonance.

author: Anne Spencer
content:
We trekked into a far country,
My friend and I.
Our deeper content was never spoken,
But each knew all the other said.
He told me how calm his soul was laid
By the lack of anvil and strife.
“The wooing kestrel,” I said, “mutes his mating-note
To please the harmony of this sweet silence.”
And when at the day’s end
We laid tired bodies ’gainst
The loose warm sands,
And the air fleeced its particles for a coverlet;
When star after star came out
To guard their lovers in oblivion—
My soul so leapt that my evening prayer
Stole my morning song!
evidence: The speaker transforms erotic and relational longing into aesthetic, spiritually elevated expression—the trek's 'deeper content' remains unspoken yet deeply felt, physical intimacy ('tired bodies 'gainst / The loose warm sands') transmutes into cosmic communion ('star after star came out / To guard their lovers'), and the soul's leap steals the morning song, channeling primitive desire into poetic rapture with maintained intensity.

author: Anne Spencer
evidence: The speaker reflects on the pattern of poetic fate with ironic distance, placing herself in lineage with dead Romantics ('Chatterton, Shelley, Keats and I') while maintaining observing perspective on the drama ('Ah, how poets sing and die!'). The self-aware inclusion of 'I' in the catalog of doomed poets shows capacity to hold both participation and reflection simultaneously.

author: Louis MacNeice
content:
The room was suddenly rich and the great bay-window was
Spawning snow and pink roses against it
Soundlessly collateral and incompatible:
World is suddener than we fancy it.
World is crazier and more of it than we think,
Incorrigibly plural. I peel and portion
A tangerine and spit the pips and feel
The drunkenness of things being various.
And the fire flames with a bubbling sound for world
Is more spiteful and gay than one supposes— 
On the tongue on the eyes on the ears in the palms of one's hands—
There is more than glass between the snow and the huge roses.
evidence: The speaker transforms raw sensory experience and potential overwhelming affects into aesthetically integrated observation ('the drunkenness of things being various'), channeling intensity into lyric form that maintains passionate engagement ('fire flames with a bubbling sound') while achieving constructive articulation of existential complexity through imagery and craft.

author: Louis MacNeice
content:
In the first taxi he was alone tra-la,
No extras on the clock. He tipped ninepence
But the cabby, while he thanked him, looked askance
As though to suggest someone had bummed a ride.
In the second taxi he was alone tra-la
But the clock showed sixpence extra; he tipped according
And the cabby from out his muffler said: ‘Make sure
You have left nothing behind tra-la between you’.
In the third taxi he was alone tra-la
But the tip-up seats were down and there was an extra
Charge of one-and-sixpence and an odd
Scent that reminded him of a trip to Cannes.
As for the fourth taxi, he was alone
Tra-la when he hailed it but the cabby looked
Through him and said: ‘I can’t tra-la well take
So many people, not to speak of the dog.’
evidence: The speaker insists 'he was alone tra-la' in each taxi while external reality—the clock charges, cabby's comments, tip-up seats, and finally the cabby seeing multiple people and a dog—contradicts this solitude. The reflex 'tra-la' and puzzled tone suggest automatic refusal to acknowledge the painful external fact of absent others who nevertheless leave traces.

author: Jana Prikryl
content:
The earth has feelings
some killed others in its mud and it has lots of mud
The earth builds a scrapyard, a sequence of them to tell
of this, a seam on its embalmed glabella future galaxies caress
The earth knows André Breton,
compiles ingenuous personalities in its fevered correspondence
Out of its winding sheet rolodex the earth erodes another name,
your name
Beware, the earth prepares to say one final time, construction
eclipses
It hoped to say nothing further and then was disappointed, its hope
misplaced it knew deep down
Say more,
you say, the earth had hoped you would
Express as little as possible with your furniture, find the little that is
as near to nothing as can be
The monuments unpictured drift up like watermarks through the odor
of the lens
You make things happen all the time, says the earth, take my advice
look the other way
evidence: The speaker attributes elaborate internal states to 'the earth'—knowing, hoping, feeling disappointed, preparing to speak, compiling correspondence—while positioning themselves as the object of earth's anxious attention ('the earth had hoped you would'). The earth becomes a persecutory/solicitous other whose consciousness mirrors the speaker's own disowned relational preoccupations and agency.

poem_name: from The Marriage of Heaven and Hell: The Argument
author: William Blake
content:
Rintrah roars and shakes his fires in the burdened air;
Hungry clouds swag on the deep.
Once meek, and in a perilous path,
The just man kept his course along
The vale of death.
Roses are planted where thorns grow,
And on the barren heath
Sing the honey bees.
Then the perilous path was planted:
And a river and a spring
On every cliff and tomb;
And on the bleached bones
Red clay brought forth.
Till the villain left the paths of ease,
To walk in perilous paths, and drive
The just man into barren climes.
Now the sneaking serpent walks
In mild humility,
And the just man rages in the wilds
Where lions roam.
Rintrah roars and shakes his fires in the burdened air;
Hungry clouds swag on the deep.
evidence: The speaker organizes cosmic history around radical reversals where affect-values invert entirely: 'the just man' who 'kept his course along / The vale of death' becomes one who 'rages in the wilds,' while 'the villain' adopts 'mild humility'—the sneaking serpent now 'walks / In mild humility.' These are not syntheses but complete polar reversals that deny ambivalence, transforming meekness into rage and villainy into humility.

poem_name: Portrait of My Father as a Young Black Man
author: Sjohnna McCray
content:
—Cincinnati, Ohio, 1987



Rage is the language of men,
          layers of particulates fused.
 
          Rage is the wine
                    father pours to the ground
 
                    for men whose time has passed. Rage
                    is gripped in the hands
 
          like the neck of a broom held tight. Rage
gets stuck in the throat, suppressed.
 
Rage is a promise kept.
evidence: The speaker anatomizes rage in catalogic, definitional statements ('Rage is...') with clinical precision, stripping the emotion of its visceral charge through ritualized repetition. Rage is examined as object ('gripped in the hands / like the neck of a broom'), abstracted through metaphor ('layers of particulates fused'), yet never felt—the wine poured, the grip held, the throat constricted all remain curiously affectless gestures in a portrait that knows everything about rage except its heat.

author: Sjohnna McCray
content:
It's not about the sex, really, it's not
the ache of the bruised nipple or the burn
left by his two-day-old beard in the crook
of my neck where the pulse is taken, it's
his breathing when asleep that draws me near.
When I was seventeen, I'd check beside me,
hope I hadn't rolled and squashed flat
my one-year-old brother. His sigh on my ear,
the rise and fall of air beneath his ribs,
was a miracle to me. The nightly
surprise of what I saw under the bulb's
dim glow: I saw the small heart beating
like wings unfolding in the body. Here,
with this man, ideas of flight return.
evidence: The speaker transforms primitive erotic attachment ('bruised nipple,' 'burn left by his two-day-old beard') into aesthetically distanced meditation on mortality and care, channeling anxious attachment through the creative act of holding vigil—both with infant brother and adult lover—where breathing becomes 'miracle' and 'wings unfolding,' maintaining emotional intensity while achieving poetic transcendence.

author: Eamon Grennan
content:
There's a word you've met but once and now forgotten
               for the spoon-shaped concave under your Adam's apple:
a small pouch that must be kissed and kissed again
               by lovers learning the ins and outs of one another
and where last night in the sleeping dark a lone mosquito
               inserted itself and drew fresh blood and left
venom and a couple of love-bites
               then left without waking you although this morning
you'll blaze with the minute but unassuageable
               pain of them—which only that word that's vanished
into the mud of memory might be the right cooling balm for.
evidence: The speaker converts an intimate experience of physical vulnerability and erotic touch into an intellectual puzzle about a forgotten anatomical term, maintaining elaborate cognitive engagement ('spoon-shaped concave,' 'ins and outs,' 'minute but unassuageable') while the actual emotional content—longing, intimacy, pain—remains aestheticized and detached through the formal conceit of searching for the missing word.

author: Eamon Grennan
content:
What over the gable-end and high up under tangled cloud
           that raven might be saying to its tumble-soaring mate
or what the blackbird might intend when chattering among
           scattered breadcrumbs or what the bellowing of one cow
then another in the near field might mean remains beyond
           my ken—being all noise for which no words will manage
though all is language settling and unsettling the world
           beyond me . . . and yet there’s the dunnock in all its
dun colours at work among the small stones and patchy grass
           of the driveway and here’s the robin’s aggressive tilt
at breadcrumbs and there goes the sudden shriek
           of the blackbird . . . all alive inside the inhuman
breath-pattern of the wind trawling every last leaf
           and blade of grass and flinging rain like velvet pebbles
onto the skylight: nothing but parables in every bristling inch
           of the out-of-sight unspoken never-to-be-known pure
sense-startling untranslatable there of the world as we find it.
evidence: The speaker channels the anxiety of being excluded from animal/natural language ('remains beyond my ken—being all noise for which no words will manage') into sustained, aesthetically sophisticated observation that transforms epistemological limitation into formally elaborate poetry celebrating the 'sense-startling untranslatable there of the world.' The intense engagement with what cannot be known produces socially valued creative work without loss of pleasure or emotional authenticity.

author: Rebecca Morgan Frank
content:
I’ve always felt as if I were in a cage.
If I stick out an arm, I’m seen as wanting
and taking. A leg: my violence is noted.
When I mentioned the suffocation by
my father, they made it autobiographical.
The subtext of my work in iron has been
ignored, lost in discussions of time, not space.
What you’ll never understand about
installations is that everyone else is always
outside of them. I’m stuck inside. The move
toward deliberate transience is the mark of a fool.
If disappearing is inevitable, you must fight it.
No one misses the prisoner.
The bars are like a frame: you want in
as much as I have always wanted out.
evidence: The speaker attributes misunderstanding and misinterpretation to others ('they made it autobiographical,' 'What you'll never understand') while maintaining that external observers fundamentally misread her intentions—'wanting and taking' when she extends an arm, 'violence' when she moves a leg. The cage imagery locates the source of constraint and judgment entirely outside the self.

author: Rebecca Morgan Frank
content:
I held a jackhammer once.
It matched me
 
in height and weight and I
planned destruction, ripped
 
everything solid from the ground.
The concrete was my prey,
 
but it was the tool I fought
to master, its metal body
 
trembling beneath my hands.
Or, perhaps, its electric heart
 
was what shook me through
my last held bones.
 
The concrete, gone,
the rented machinery, returned—
 
and I wondered if Sisyphus had imagined
a garden where the rock came from.
evidence: The speaker channels destructive impulses through physically demanding creative labor (jackhammer demolition as preparation for garden-making), maintaining emotional intensity while transforming aggression into socially constructive activity. The final image explicitly links destruction to creation: 'wondered if Sisyphus had imagined / a garden where the rock came from.'

author: Richard Katrovas
content:
It is snowing in southwest Michigan.
Such weather is unusual so late.
The trees are squirting buds that advocate
For green profusions that yesterday began
To grunt and poke and strain toward full-blown spring.
Now fleeced, the trees are January stark.
Though clocks, sprung forward, hedge against the dark,
We hear the arias our miseries sing
When darkness is a slave to all that white.
If global warming is the fangs of doom
I see its poison wafting, from this room.
The future will be mottled if not bright.
Perhaps I’ll die before the killing trend.
I hear my children’s voices on this wind.
evidence: The speaker processes profound parental anxiety about climate catastrophe and his children's future through detached, almost meteorological observation and abstract conceptualization ('global warming is the fangs of doom,' 'The future will be mottled if not bright'), maintaining aesthetic distance via formal sonnet structure while the emotional weight—hearing children's voices on an ominous wind—remains conceptually acknowledged but affectively muted.

author: Donald Britton
content:
for David Cobb Craig



At first, we had ways of talking
That filled up the evening
Until some things could be said. It was a made-up
Situation in which lives could be lost.
Whatever that was now grows inside
Our bodies—a spongy, pulpy cell—
Causing pieces of paper we hold
In our hands to appear
 
And disappear. All I ask
Is to take me away from this place,
To another place, very much like
This place, where we can meet
And six months later
Be married. You laughed and went with me.
evidence: The speaker imagines a realistic future scenario ('where we can meet / And six months later / Be married') while acknowledging present discomfort ('to take me away from this place') and planning through modest affective distress about a relationship's progression.

poem_name: The Sky Is Clear, But It's Raining
author: Donald Britton
content:
Under the trees, where everything
Is still possible in prescribed doses:
Hundreds of accordion-like units
Without edges. But there is no unwinding
 
Of minutes to stay the execution
Of a rain-shot weekend in early
Beach weather, no elixir
To revive the amputated flower
 
Still kicking on its ghost-stem
In a bowl of water, no direction
In which to steer
The hapless, puzzled out-of-towner
 
Other than straight ahead,
To the sheer drop-off
Where his guidebook gutters
Or deposits him, addressless,
 
In thin air.
evidence: The speaker describes potentially catastrophic experiences (execution of a weekend, amputated flower, sheer drop-off into thin air) in detached, abstract language ('accordion-like units / Without edges,' 'hapless, puzzled out-of-towner') that maintains observational distance and converts emotional crisis into geometric/mechanical description—delivering weather reports on existential disorientation without affective engagement.

author: Margaret Fishback
content:
Bear in mind, my little man:
Never let your verses scan.
And acceptance will be sparse
If, by any chance, they parse.
But whatever else you do,
Let it not be said of you
That your poetry makes sense!. . . .That’s a criminal offense!
evidence: The speaker directly confronts modernist pretensions by making herself the butt of her own observation—mockingly advising violation of formal conventions while using perfect meter and rhyme, thereby demonstrating mastery of what she satirizes without destroying it.

author: Margaret Fishback
content:
When in the dumps, I hate the things
   That ordinarily I love.
I loathe the lark that blindly sings;
   I hate the bland, blue sky above.
The crocus, sneering on the lawn,
   Forsythia about to bloom—
I'd like to see them dead and gone,
   Instead of filling life with gloom.
But most of all, I do not care,
   While I am droning in my hive,
To hear vivacious chums declare
   How great it is to be alive.
evidence: The speaker transforms ordinary positive feelings into their polar opposites ('I hate the things / That ordinarily I love'), but the excessive, declared reversal ('loathe,' 'hate,' violently wishing spring flowers 'dead and gone') reveals strain and leakage—the protest against life's beauty feels conspicuously forced, suggesting underlying depressive helplessness being converted into aggressive declarations.

author: Susan Rothbard
content:
At the market today, I look for Piñata
apples, their soft-blush-yellow. My husband
brought them home last week, made me guess at
the name of this new strain, held one in his hand
like a gift and laughed as I tried all
the names I knew: Gala, Fuji, Honey
Crisp—watched his face for clues—what to call
something new? It's winter, only tawny
hues and frozen ground, but that apple bride
was sweet, and I want to bring it back to him,
that new. When he cut it, the star inside
held seeds of other stars, the way within
a life are all the lives you might live,
each unnamed, until you name it.
evidence: The speaker returns to the market specifically to recreate a shared moment of delight with her husband, preparing to bring back 'that new' feeling—anticipating the pleasure of re-gifting the experience while holding both the memory and future possibility in affectively engaged consciousness.

author: Margaret Fishback
content:
The Twenty-fifth is imminent
And every known expedient
Designed for making Christmas pay
Is getting swiftly under way.
Observe the people swarming to
And fro, somnambulating through
The stores in search of ties and shirts
And gloves to give until it hurts.
They're eyeing gifts in Saks' and Hearn's 
And Macy's, not to mention Stern's,
While earnest copywriters are
Hitching their copy to the star
Of Bethlehem quite shamelessly,
For they are duty bound to see
That Peace On Earth Good Will To Men
Gets adequate results again.
evidence: The speaker frames the commercial exploitation of sacred symbols as duty-bound professional obligation ('earnest copywriters are / Hitching their copy to the star / Of Bethlehem quite shamelessly, / For they are duty bound to see'), converting crass commercialism into conscientious work responsibility through the phrase 'duty bound' while maintaining ironic distance.

author: Alice B. Fogel
content:
It was twilight all day.
Sometimes the smallest things weigh us down,
small stones that we can't help
admiring and palming.
Look at the tiny way
this lighter vein got inside.
Look at the heavy gray dome of its sky.
This is no immutable world.
We know less than its atoms, rushing through.
Light, light. Light as air, to them,
for all we know. Trust me on this one,
there is happiness at stake.
Boulder, grain. Planet, dust:
What fills the stones fills us.
I remember, or I have a feeling,
I could be living somewhere with you,
weighted down the way we aren't now.
Often the greatest things,
those you'd think would be the heaviest,
are the very ones that float.
evidence: The speaker transforms emotional weight (being 'weighted down') into abstract philosophical musings about atoms, planets, and immutable worlds, discussing what 'fills the stones fills us' in a detached, theoretical register that acknowledges happiness is 'at stake' but maintains observational distance from the affective content.

poem_name: If I Sleep While My Baby Sleeps
author: Alice B. Fogel
content:
I will hear his sleep
in and through my own, my sleep
will be bathed in his as if we slept
in one same fluid
My sleep floats within a listening
so deep that the separating
spaces of air become
as pliant and full as snowfall,
its singing silence as profound
My ears and his throat —
the sensation of anticipated
hearing close inside the ear
and the incipient murmur or cry
forming at the end of his sleep —
borne like birds and thrumming
on the air of rooms between us
My own sleep will be his
clock, safely keeping time,
his sleep tunes my dreams to listen,
our sleep binds the hour,
heavy and warm,
into a blanket of air
and sound
evidence: The speaker maintains dual awareness of self and infant as separate yet proximate ('my sleep floats within a listening so deep'), pursuing connection without merger ('rooms between us') while acknowledging reciprocal regulation ('his sleep tunes my dreams to listen'). The syntax distributes agency democratically between mother and child rather than collapsing boundaries.

author: Susan Firer
content:
The poppies start as aliens
end as husbands, a pause
of light, a dull scatter.
Transports dandelion clouds.
Venus passes between sun &
earth. Exceedingly rare, Transit,
have you noticed how close
the ode & elegy are?
(In the United States someone
dies every sixteen seconds!)
Husband, Supermoon, Venus
come & go. Death says there
is no you at the end of weather.
"Among the rarest of all predictable
astronomical . . ." Husband
presented me. The weatherman
says we are locked in the clouds.
evidence: The speaker interjects astronomical facts and statistics ('Exceedingly rare, Transit,' 'In the United States someone / dies every sixteen seconds!') into what appears to be a poem about loss or absence of the husband, discussing death and impermanence through scientific discourse rather than direct emotional engagement. The parenthetical death statistic and weatherman's pronouncement function as cognitive buffers against the affective weight of 'Husband... come & go' and 'there is no you.'

poem_name: Money is the Thing With Feathers
author: Susan Firer
content:
I wake to money, and take my money slow
I watched for money, lights turned low
 
One must have a mind of money . . .
Money that is not there and the money that is
 
The art of money isn't hard to master
. . . The money surrounds us . . .
 
Sweet sweet sweet sweet sweet money
Money on a wet, black bough
 
Do not go gentle into that good money
The pure products of money go crazy
 
Money sweeping out from us to disappear
Oh Money! My Money! our fearful trip is done
 
I myself will die without money
Money, Money, you bastard, I'm through.
evidence: The speaker constructs elaborate literary collage that acknowledges genuine distress about money ('I'm through,' 'die without money') while transforming it into absurdist comedy by systematically replacing loaded emotional words in canonical poems with 'money,' achieving catharsis through wit that simultaneously mocks poetic pretension and personal anxiety without looking away from the pain.

author: Dana Gioia
content:
My love, how time makes hardness shine.
They come in every color, pure or mixed
gray-green of basalt, blood-soaked jasper, quartz,
granite and feldspar, even bits of glass,
smoothed by the patient jeweller of the tides.
 
Volcano-born, earthquake-quarried,
shaven by glaciers, wind-carved, heat-cracked,
stratified, speckled, bright in the wet surf—
no two alike, all torn from the dry land
tossed up in millions on this empty shore.
 
How small death seems among the rocks. It drifts
light as a splintered bone the tide uncovers.
It glints among the shattered oyster shells,
gutted by gulls, bleached by salt and sun—
the broken crockery of living things.
 
Cormorants glide across the quiet bay.
A falcon watches from the ridge, indifferent
to the burdens I have carried here.
No point in walking farther, so I sit,
hollow as driftwood, dead as any stone.
evidence: The speaker translates profound grief into geological catalog and technical precision ('basalt, blood-soaked jasper, quartz, / granite and feldspar'), maintaining meticulous observational distance from overwhelming loss while acknowledging the emotional weight ('the burdens I have carried here') in affectively flattened, summary language.

author: Dana Gioia
content:
Most of what happens happens beyond words.
The lexicon of lip and fingertip
defies translation into common speech.
I recognize the musk of your dark hair.
It always thrills me, though I can't describe it.
My finger on your thigh does not touch skin—
it touches your skin warming to my touch.
You are a language I have learned by heart.
 
This intimate patois will vanish with us,
its only native speakers. Does it matter?
Our tribal chants, our dances round the fire
performed the sorcery we most required.
They bound us in a spell time could not break.
Let the young vaunt their ecstasy. We keep
our tribe of two in sovereign secrecy.
What must be lost was never lost on us.
evidence: The speaker contemplates future loss with present affect ('This intimate patois will vanish with us') while experiencing modest emotional pain ('Does it matter?') that inoculates against grief. The poem spreads anxiety about mortality across reflective present tense, preparing emotionally through the very act of acknowledging what will be lost.

author: Jim Harrison
content:
I worry much about the suffering
of Machado. I was only one when he carried
his mother across the border from Spain to France
in a rainstorm. She died and so did he
a few days later in a rooming house along a dry canal.
To carry Mother he abandoned a satchel
holding his last few years of poetry.
I've traveled to Collioure several times
to search for Machado's lost satchel.
The French fed him but couldn't save him.
There's no true path to a death —
we discover the path by walking.
We turn a corner on no road
and there's a house on a green hill
with a thousand colorful birds sweeping in a circle.
Are the poems in the basement of the house on the hill?
We'll find out if we remember earth at all.
evidence: The speaker channels preoccupation with historical loss and death into repeated pilgrimages and creative searching ('I've traveled to Collioure several times / to search for Machado's lost satchel'), transforming what could be morbid obsession into poetic meditation with real consequences—the poem itself becomes the recovered artifact, maintaining emotional intensity while achieving aesthetic distance through formal reflection on mortality and artistic legacy.

author: Jim Harrison
content:
I envied the dog lying in the yard
so I did it. But there was a pebble
under my flank so I got up and looked
for the pebble, brushed it away
and lay back down. My dog thus far
overlooked the pebble. I guess it's her thick
Lab fur. With my head downhill the blood gorged
me with ideas. Not good. Got up. Turned around. Now I
see hundreds of infinitesimal ants. I'm on an
ant home. I get up and move five feet.
The dog hasn't moved from her serene place.
Now I'm rather too near a thicket where
I saw a big black snake last week that might decide
to join me. I moved near the actual dog this time
but she got up and went under the porch. She doesn't like
it when I'm acting weird. I'm failing as a dog
when my own kind rejects me, but doing better
than when I envied birds, the creature the least
like us, therefore utterly enviable. To be sure
I cheeped a lot but didn't try to fly.
We humans can take off but are no good at landing.
evidence: The speaker maintains full awareness of his absurd failure ('I'm failing as a dog / when my own kind rejects me') while transforming human limitation into gentle self-mockery, reconciling the paradox of envying creatures unlike us through comic deflation ('We humans can take off but are no good at landing').

author: Jim Harrison
content:
Yes, we'll gather by the river,
the beautiful, the beautiful river.
They say it runs by the throne of God.
This is where God invented fish.
Wherever, but then God's throne is as wide
as the universe. If you're attentive you'll
see the throne's borders in the stars. We're on this side
and when you get to the other side we don't know
what will happen if anything. If nothing happens
we won't know it, I said once. Is that cynical?
No, nothing is nothing, not upsetting just
nothing. Then again maybe we'll be cast
at the speed of light through the universe
to God's throne. His hair is bounteous.
All the 5,000 birds on earth were created there.
The firstborn cranes, herons, hawks, at the back
so as not to frighten the little ones.
Even now they remember this divine habitat.
Shall we gather at the river, this beautiful river?
We'll sing with the warblers perched on his eyelashes.
evidence: The speaker engages in realistic, affect-laden preparation for death ('when you get to the other side we don't know what will happen if anything'), spreading existential anxiety through measured consideration of possibilities while maintaining both thought and feeling about the ultimate discomfort, transforming mortal dread into a lyric that gathers rather than flees.

author: Jean Valentine
content:
A woman ladder leans
with her two-year-old boy in her arms.
Her arms & legs & hands & feet
are thin as crayons.
The man ladder
is holding his glass of bourbon,
he is coming out of the child’s drawing
in his old open pajamas—
he’s in the war. The sky
is blackest crayon-canyon.
When does he leave again? When he leaves,
I leave.          I like that river the sky.
evidence: The speaker observes traumatic abandonment through a child's drawing with dreamlike distance ('thin as crayons,' 'ladder leans'), then abruptly identifies with the departing father ('When he leaves, I leave') before pivoting to aesthetic pleasure ('I like that river the sky') that deflects from the war-terror of 'blackest crayon-canyon.' The fragmented self-states and playful transformation of overwhelming material into charming imagery signal dissociative defense.

author: Jean Valentine
content:
I lay down under language
it left me and I slept
—You, the Comforter, came into the room
my blood, my mouth
all buttoned away—
Makers of houses, books, clothes-
makers, goodbye—
evidence: The speaker describes lying down under language and sleeping while the Comforter enters—consciousness fragments into discontinuous states ('it left me and I slept') with bodily experience compartmentalized ('my blood, my mouth / all buttoned away'). The dreamy, theatrical quality transforms overwhelming material into something witnessed from distance, saying goodbye to makers of ordinary reality.

author: Jean Valentine
content:
The helicopter,
a sort of controlled silver leaf
dropped lightly into the clearing.
The searchlights swung, the little girl,
the little girl was crying, her mother, a girl herself,
was giving birth, the forest dropped birdseeds of milk.
Then the helicopter lifted away,
the mother rested.
 
                                   Like him who came to us empty-handed,
who came, it seemed, with nothing,
Joseph Cornell—          making
a shoebox universe to put it all in.
evidence: The speaker transforms primitive material (birth, vulnerability, emptied breasts) into aesthetic contemplation through direct analogy to Cornell's art-making: both the helicopter rescue and the artist's shoebox universes channel intense feeling into socially valuable, redemptive forms—emergency care and art—that maintain emotional authenticity while achieving creative distance.

author: Jean Valentine
content:
When I first heard you on the phone
your voice had to be that '40s wartime voice
for it to get under my skin like it did,
after seven years asleep.
 You’re at the beginning of something, you said,and I’m at the end of something;
but you didn’t go away,
twice-born, three times, coming around,
rough cello.
 
                                   Late days
I want to drive to your grave,
But I don’t belong to it.
evidence: The speaker maintains conscious awareness of conflicting desires—acknowledging the awakening of attachment ('your voice... get under my skin') while simultaneously holding the boundary ('I don't belong to it'). The impulse to visit the grave is kept in mind but postponed or restrained through conscious recognition of limits, demonstrating the mature balance of suppression rather than repression or dissociation.

poem_name: Portrait of D17: Female, 37 Years Old
author: Paisley Rekdal
content:
In Phnom Penh’s museum, the skulls are stacked
in aquarium tanks: grim toys for hooks to ply
free from the rubble. Here, each one gets a tag,
a mount, a photograph to suggest a life,
perhaps a name, might be envisioned. Yet I’m
more moved by what’s anonymous, past; imagine
fields of faces sunken with decay, eyes
jellied in their sockets, heel meats bruised,
bloated in the rain—
                                        Perhaps the skulls prefer
a lack of names as, scrubbed of self and skin,
they’re trauma’s best witnesses: fused
by time and pain to one crisis, never
to be separated. Lost as men, they become event.
in this, they achieve a terrible transcendence.
evidence: The speaker observes mass atrocity with detached theoretical formulations ('they achieve a terrible transcendence,' 'Lost as men, they become event') while acknowledging being 'more moved by what's anonymous, past'—claiming affect while delivering it in emotionless, philosophical abstractions that keep genocide at conceptual distance.

author: Rachel Galvin
content:
My eyes are polished smooth by sight, they clot like crystals in storm glass,
like my sister brewing beakers of toxin. If we had seen
what had been done, what the helicopter pilot did in our name,
 
what the special ops team did in our name, what they did
with their hands in our name. What if it were my sister,
what if it were her, what? If we had seen with our own smooth eyes.
 
Mark the diacritical, my lovely: we’re all wearing our knee-high boots,
every last one of us, we live in a booted nation. A nation girded and gunning.
This moment, this is precisely all, watching takes work, sight takes hours,
 
takes my eyeglasses, every last one of them, as if they were yours. You can see
there’s a sigh in our sight. What if it were my sister? What if it were,
what. What we saw ground into our eyes with the photos,
 
with the newspaper reports. What would I say, what can I say if,
what would I say if it were my sister, my own? With my own
beakers of toxin, my own boots, my own hands in my own name?
evidence: The speaker obsessively rehearses horrific acts ('what the helicopter pilot did in our name, what the special ops team did') with meticulous cognitive detail while maintaining affective distance through repetitive conditional syntax ('What if it were my sister, what if it were, what') that keeps emotional impact perpetually hypothetical despite acknowledging complicity.

poem_name: I Want to Read at the White House
author: Joshua Clover
content:
I want to read at the white house.
I want to read poems at the white house.
I want to read poems at the white house with all the pomp available.
With celebratory music and all my beloveds watching.
With Baraka and DiPrima and Roque Dalton behind me
I want to read at the white house.
I want to read poems at the white house wearing my favorite clothes probably a hoodie or perhaps my Belgian suit.
Belgium is a failed state in the heart of Europe which is something to aspire to.
I like Belgium and one day I might like to read poems at the palace of the nation but for now I want to read poems at the white house.
I want to read poems and sing karaoke and I will probably tell a few nervous jokes.
It will be like all the other readings.
We will be there together.
I want to read poems at the white house and then like any house reading we will all clean up together.
We will clean up the mess we have made together.
All that rubble and all those ashes. These are my conditions.
evidence: The speaker's insistent repetition 'I want to read at the white house' collapses boundary between wish and reality, treating revolutionary fantasy ('all that rubble and all those ashes') as achievable through sheer assertion. The poem maintains magical conviction that desire alone will transform the seat of state power into 'any house reading' where 'we will all clean up together,' revealing omnipotent confusion of internal revolutionary wish with external political outcome.

author: Gary Whited
content:
hangs in the closet
of this small room, collar open,
sleeves empty, tail wrinkled.
Nothing fills the shirt but air
and my faint scent. It waits,
all seven buttons undone,
button holes slack,
the soft fabric with its square white pattern,
all of it waiting for a body.
It would take any body, though it knows,
in its shirt way of knowing, only mine
has my shape in its wrinkles,
my bend in the elbows.
Outside this room birds hunt for food,
young leaves drink in morning sunlight,
people pass on their way to breakfast.
Yet here, in this closet,
the blue shirt needs nothing,
expects nothing, knows only its shirt knowledge,
that I am now learning—how to be private and patient,
how to be unbuttoned,
how to carry the scent of what has worn me,
and to know myself by the wrinkles.
evidence: The speaker meditates on the empty shirt as a template for learning 'how to be private and patient, how to be unbuttoned, how to carry the scent of what has worn me'—consciously rehearsing a future state of disembodiment or loss, preparing emotionally through sustained contemplation of absence while experiencing the modest affective weight of that preparation.

author: Carl Phillips
content:
First snow—I release her into it—
I know, released, she won't come back.
This is different from letting what,
already, we count as lost go. It is nothing
like that. Also, it is not like wanting to learn what
losing a thing we love feels like. Oh yes:
I love her.
Released, she seems for a moment as if
some part of me that, almost,
I wouldn't mind
understanding better, is that
not love? She seems a part of me,
and then she seems entirely like what she is:
a white dog,
less white suddenly, against the snow,
who won't come back. I know that; and, knowing it,
I release her. It's as if I release herbecause I know.
evidence: The speaker releases the dog into snow 'because I know' she won't return—transforming abandonment into deliberate ritual through compulsive reasoning ('It is different from... It is nothing like... Also, it is not like') that attempts to magically reframe the loss as intentional gift rather than unacknowledged aggression or guilt.

poem_name: Within Two Weeks the African American Poet Ross Gay is Mistaken for Both the African American Poet Terrance Hayes and the African American Poet Kyle Dargan, Not One of Whom Looks Anything Like the Oth
author: Ross Gay
content:
If you think you know enough to say this poem
is about good hair, I'll correct you
and tell you it's about history
which is the blacksmith of our tongues.
Our eyes. Where you see misunderstanding
I see knuckles and teeth for sale
in a storefront window. I see the waterlogged
face of the fourteen-year-old boy.
The bullet's imperceptible sizzle
toward an unarmed man. And as you ask me to sign the book
that is not mine, your gaze shifting between
me and the author's photo, whispering,but that's not you? I do not
feel sorry for you. No. I think only that when a man
is a concept he will tell you about the smell
of smoke. He will tell you the distance
between heartbreak and rage.
evidence: The speaker directly confronts the microaggression with declarative force—'I'll correct you'—refusing apologetic justification and claiming both autonomy to reframe the insult ('it's about history') and moral authority to name the violence beneath the confusion ('I do not feel sorry for you'). This forward-moving assertion transforms personal affront into historical witness without guilt or deflection.

author: Ross Gay
content:
Because he was 38, because this
was his second job, because
he had two daughters, because his hands
looked like my father's, because at 7
he would walk to the furniture warehouse,
unload trucks 'til 3 AM, because I
was fourteen and training him, because he made
$3.75 an hour, because he had a wife
to look in the face, because
he acted like he respected me,
because he was sick and would not call out
I didn't blink when the water
dropped from his nose
into the onion's perfectly circular
mouth on the Whopper Jr.
I coached him through preparing.
I did not blink.
Tell me this didn't happen.
I dare you.
evidence: The speaker recounts witnessing another worker's bodily fluids contaminate food with the repeated insistence 'I did not blink,' acknowledging the event's factual horror while describing their own response in mechanical, affectless terms—detailing all circumstances without accessing emotional reaction.

poem_name: Pulled Over in Short Hills, NJ, 8:00 AM
author: Ross Gay
content:
It’s the shivering. When rage grows
hot as an army of red ants and forces
the mind to quiet the body, the quakes
emerge, sometimes just the knees,
but, at worst, through the hips, chest, neck
until, like a virus, slipping inside the lungs
and pulse, every ounce of strength tapped
to squeeze words from my taut lips,
his eyes scanning my car’s insides, my eyes,
my license, and as I answer the questions
3, 4, 5 times, my jaw tight as a vice,
his hand massaging the gun butt, I
imagine things I don’t want to
and inside beg this to end
before the shiver catches my
hands, and he sees,
and something happens.
evidence: The speaker maintains conscious awareness of rage ('When rage grows hot as an army of red ants') while actively forcing the body to quiet, tapping 'every ounce of strength' to control shaking and squeeze words out, postponing expression until the threat passes—a semiconscious decision to defer rather than eliminate the impulse, with finite endpoint ('beg this to end').

author: Ross Gay
content:
If you find yourself half naked
and barefoot in the frosty grass, hearing,
again, the earth's great, sonorous moan that says
you are the air of the now and gone, that says
all you love will turn to dust,
and will meet you there, do not
raise your fist. Do not raise
your small voice against it. And do not
take cover. Instead, curl your toes
into the grass, watch the cloud
ascending from your lips. Walk
through the garden's dormant splendor.
Say only, thank you.
Thank you.
evidence: The speaker consciously acknowledges overwhelming grief ('all you love will turn to dust') while deliberately choosing not to fight or flee ('do not raise your fist...do not take cover'), instead instructing active presence with the pain ('curl your toes into the grass') and postponement of protest through finite ritual ('Say only, thank you')—maintaining full awareness of loss while deferring emotional resolution.

author: Derek Mahon
content:
They said I got away in a boat
And humbled me at the inquiry. I tell you
     I sank as far that night as any
Hero. As I sat shivering on the dark water
     I turned to ice to hear my costly
Life go thundering down in a pandemonium of
     Prams, pianos, sideboards, winches,
Boilers bursting and shredded ragtime. Now I hide
     In a lonely house behind the sea
Where the tide leaves broken toys and hatboxes
     Silently at my door. The showers of
April, flowers of May mean nothing to me, nor the
     Late light of June, when my gardener
Describes to strangers how the old man stays in bed
     On seaward mornings after nights of
Wind, takes his cocaine and will see no one. Then it is
     I drown again with all those dim
Lost faces I never understood, my poor soul
     Screams out in the starlight, heart
Breaks loose and rolls down like a stone.
     Include me in your lamentations.
evidence: The speaker attempts to ritually reverse survivor guilt through isolating confession ('I sank as far that night as any / Hero') and compulsive re-experiencing ('Then it is / I drown again'), seeking atonement for the transgression of survival through nightly symbolic death. The final plea 'Include me in your lamentations' functions as magical request to undo exclusion from the dead through ritualistic incorporation into their company.

author: Derek Mahon
content:
The bright drop quivering on a thorn
in the rich silence after rain,
lute music in the orchard aisles,
the paths ablaze with daffodils,
intrigue and venery in the airà l’ombre des jeunes filles en fleurs,
the iron hand and the velvet glove —
come live with me and be my love.
 
A pearl face, numinously bright,
shining in silence of the night,
a muffled crash of smouldering logs,
bad dreams of courtiers and of dogs,
the Spanish ships around Kinsale,
the screech-owl and the nightingale,
the falcon and the turtle dove —
come live with me and be my love.
evidence: The poem is a descriptive ekphrastic meditation on an English estate, weaving historical and literary allusions (Marlowe's 'The Passionate Shepherd') without revealing a speaker's psychological stance toward conflict or distress.

author: Nuala Ní Dhomhnaill
content:
A martyr this morning, as ever, to cramps and pains
I organize myself to face the day.
I show a leg, put my shoulder to the wheel,
Daub paint on my eyelids and stick a couple of long
Hairpins in my desperate mane to hold it—
Too much trouble even to brush my hair.
 
I start on the spot on this heavy, sluggish,
Difficult, heartbreaking work, the reason no doubt
I was first put on the earth.
I take the same little plastic brush that I use
On good days to spread melted butter on pastry.
And gradually lay bare with insect patience,
Sifting away like an ant, with a hunter’s eye, or
 
The sharp ear of a trespassing pig, alternately huffing
And puffing and effing and blinding: in the wet sand,
The painful lines of our horror, the boundaried frame of fear,
That lays us low so often in the bogs of despond.
You’d take it as first for a boat’s skeleton, a kind
Of Sutton Hoo for our people, but soon its true shape appears:
Biblical Behemoth, the monster of all the old tales.
evidence: The speaker transforms primitive affects (cramps, pain, despair, 'effing and blinding') into sustained creative archaeological work ('insect patience,' 'hunter's eye'), gradually excavating the 'Biblical Behemoth' of psychic horror through artistic labor that maintains emotional authenticity while producing tangible aesthetic product.

author: Stephen Spender
content:
My parents kept me from children who were rough
Who threw words like stones and wore torn clothes
Their thighs showed through rags they ran in the street
And climbed cliffs and stripped by the country streams.
I feared more than tigers their muscles like iron
Their jerking hands and their knees tight on my arms
I feared the salt coarse pointing of those boys
Who copied my lisp behind me on the road.
They were lithe they sprang out behind hedges
Like dogs to bark at my world. They threw mud
While I looked the other way, pretending to smile.
I longed to forgive them but they never smiled.
evidence: The speaker recounts terrifying experiences ('I feared more than tigers') and humiliating attacks ('copied my lisp') in curiously flat, catalog-like syntax, as if reporting weather. The final line's impossible emotional math—'I longed to forgive them but they never smiled'—inverts causality to provide logical cover for unprocessed shame and rage.

poem_name: She longed to be an island
author: Marjorie Agosín
content:
She longed to be an island,
She loved the unbridled madness of them, the islands,
She longed to arrive to an island that, perhaps, wasn't an island
Divest herself of the ruinous, stony loves
She longed to be an island
To only inhabit the sea's waistline
And not leave or return
To only be an island
Island of the night
Island of the dawn
Islands.
evidence: The speaker yearns to 'divest herself of the ruinous, stony loves' and 'not leave or return,' expressing desire to sever emotional investment in relationships entirely, replacing them with an imagined state of island-existence that functions as internal refuge from relational pain.

author: Megan Snyder-Camp
content:
Family dinner night, and we are deciding what to save:
polar bears or slipper limpets. Girls in Afghanistan
or the wolf. We can't save everything
but the kids are ready
with their banks, the season's extra, the not-
ice cream. How does the Afghan girl feel
to make our list? We bring more and more
money to the table but the list outruns it.
My mother comes in from visiting a friend in hospice,
sick from all the chemo. When I get whatever it is, she says,
I want you to do nothing. It's only May
and already they've declared a statewide drought.
Yesterday I hiked over a river that was not there.
Coral reefs, my son says, that's what I want
to save. And so we do. Whatever is happening to us
is deductible. Silence of the was-river,
was-bear. In the movies everyone is building
some kind of ark.
evidence: The speaker worries realistically about future losses (polar bears, drought, hospice, death) while experiencing proportionate affective distress in the present ('we bring more and more money to the table but the list outruns it'), spreading anxiety over time through active engagement with what cannot be prevented rather than avoiding or denying it.

author: Simone White
content:
Unable to pour boiling water
over an edge from kettle to pot
water boils from kettle to neti pot
still boils from kettle to cup running over
boil pool steam pool leak pool
little cooling pot over the boiled edge
of boil pooled salt vapors
sulfurous stank boil heal dangled over
the boiled edge of burnt earth
cooling salt pool nettle stung
black clotted blood at the bottom
of the sink
evidence: The speaker catalogues physical process (boiling, pouring, leaking, cooling) with obsessive precision while affect remains evacuated—the imagery of 'black clotted blood at the bottom of the sink' and 'burnt earth' suggests bodily trauma or violence stripped of emotional coloring, delivered in the same flat register as mechanical failure ('Unable to pour boiling water').

author: Simone White
content:
Apart disclaimed wicked pea, split soft skin
of the principle princess, who writhes,
a little blood passes her perineum every night,
grey linen sheets flax talisman plot luxe
to strip and scrub all gore
a plain bar of secret white soap
it is a pine tree, it is an orange blossom, is it a rose hip
under a baby tongue, blood cuts
punisher, swear it closed, closes it
evidence: The poem describes intensely visceral bodily trauma—menstrual blood, perineal suffering, writhes of pain—in a curiously affectless register that fragments experience into discrete sensory data (soap scents, fabric textures) without integrating emotional response. The stance is observational-forensic rather than experiential, cataloguing 'a little blood passes her perineum every night' with the detached precision of documentation.

author: Marilyn Chin
content:
Your name is Diana Toy.
And all you may have for breakfast is rice gruel.
You can't spit it back into the cauldron for it would be unfilial.
You can't ask for yam gruel for there is none.
You can't hide it in the corner for it would surely be found,
and then you would be served cold, stale rice gruel.
This is the philosophy of your tong:
you, the child, must learn to understand the universe
through the port-of-entry, your mouth,
to discern bitter from sweet, pungent from bland.
You were told that the infant Buddha once devoured earth
and spewed forth the wisdom of the ages.
Meat or gruel, wine or ghee,
even if it's gruel, even if it's nothing,
that gruel, that nothingness will shine
into the oil of your mother's scrap-iron wok,
into the glare of your father's cleaver,
and dance in your porcelain bowl.
Remember, what they deny you won't hurt you.
What they spare you, you must make shine,
so shine, shine . . .
evidence: The speaker transforms deprivation ('what they deny you won't hurt you') into an excessive celebration of the unwanted gruel as luminous and transcendent ('that nothingness will shine'), insisting the child must make scarcity 'shine, shine' with a strained intensity that betrays underlying resentment while maintaining surface reverence for parental authority and cultural philosophy.

author: John L. Stanizzi
content:
We walked through some heartache in '62.
Gary liked Teresa but Teresa
asked Elizabeth to tell Peter that
she really wanted to go out with him
but Peter had been making out with Jane
in the theater, celebrating their
one month anniversary, so that was
out, and even though Jane broke up with Pete,
Peter kept asking Gail to talk with Jane
which Gail wouldn't do because she'd told
Brenda that she thought that Peter was cute
but Brenda wasn't listening to a word,
wrapped up in lonely teardrops shed for Greg.
The waters of 8th grade were never still.
evidence: The poem recounts eighth-grade romantic entanglements with bemused distance and mild nostalgia, but the speaker adopts no defensive stance—the 'heartache' is placed in the past tense, observed with wry humor rather than managed through psychological mechanisms.

author: Monica McClure
content:
Since I stopped the flow
Of primordial ciswhite straight men
Whom I heedlessly collect
And from whom the spring feeds
Without reason I have been
Shopping so much more
Than suits a prophet in the forest.
A man said he felt like an awful cad
But an admission as such
Does not irrigate a dry spell
Once it’s surpassed the length
Of a petty offense record
Because the body’s memory is not so
Mutated by language
And there’s very little pleasure in force
When the subject is inertia.
I used to leave as soon as
The mysterious chemistry worked out
Now I am both the one who leaves
And the one who stays
Eco­-novelty is rare and common
And each design reforms
The future and the last.
evidence: The speaker discusses emotionally charged relational experiences—stopping the flow of sexual partners, a dry spell, leaving and staying—in a curiously detached, theoretical register ('eco-novelty is rare and common,' 'the body's memory is not so mutated by language') that delivers affective content as abstract proposition rather than lived distress.

author: Thomas Hardy
content:
“I am playing my oldest tunes,” declared she,
      “All the old tunes I know,—
Those I learnt ever so long ago.”
—Why she should think just then she’d play them
       Silence cloaks like snow.
When I returned from the town at nightfall
      Notes continued to pour
As when I had left two hours before:
“It’s the very last time,” she said in closing;
       “From now I play no more.”
A few morns onward found her fading,
      And, as her life outflew,
I thought of her playing her tunes right through;
And I felt she had known of what was coming,
      And wondered how she knew.
evidence: The speaker meditates on the wife's mysterious foreknowledge of her own death, her decision to play 'all the old tunes' one final time, spreading out the work of mourning through musical ritual while experiencing proportionate emotional distress ('I thought of her playing her tunes right through'). The speaker's retrospective wondering ('wondered how she knew') contains both affect and realistic appraisal of her self-inoculating preparation for death.

author: Marilyn Nelson
content:
Five daughters, in the slant light on the porch,
are bickering. The eldest has come home
with new truths she can hardly wait to teach.
She lectures them: the younger daughters search
the sky, elbow each other's ribs, and groan.
Five daughters, in the slant light on the porch
and blue-sprigged dresses, like a stand of birch
saplings whose leaves are going yellow-brown
with new truths. They can hardly wait to teach,
themselves, to be called "Ma'am," to march
high-heeled across the hanging bridge to town.
Five daughters. In the slant light on the porch
Pomp lowers his paper for a while, to watch
the beauties he's begotten with his Ann:
these new truths they can hardly wait to teach.
The eldest sniffs, "A lady doesn't scratch."
The third snorts back, "Knock, knock: nobody home."
The fourth concedes, "Well, maybe not in church . . . "
Five daughters in the slant light on the porch.
evidence: The speaker adopts a purely descriptive, third-person stance toward a familial scene, neither defending against conflict nor distorting reality—simply witnessing daughters bickering and a father watching from his paper with no anxious transformation of the material.

author: Don Thompson
content:
Just enough rain an hour ago
to give the wispy dry grass some hope,
turning it green instantly.
This place has been abandoned,
the old faith overgrown, confused
by brambles,
and in these hard times,
its upkeep cut from the budget.
But we walk, soaked to the knees,
making our slow pilgrimage
among gravestones, speaking
blurred names back into the world.
evidence: The speaker transforms mortality and abandonment into creative, socially valuable ritual: walking together 'making our slow pilgrimage / among gravestones, speaking / blurred names back into the world' channels confrontation with death into generative communal act of remembrance and restoration.

author: Caitlin Doyle
content:
Because you’re gone, I take a book to bed:The Flame of Passion. Scabbard at his thigh,
Lord Henry gets the girl. You’d only buy
top Booklist picks. “The romance genre’s dead,”
you’d say when promises of I-thee-wed
lured me to bargain bins. I learned to lie
about my day, hoard Harlequins on the sly
while you were off at work, your office spread
with red-inked proofs. But now it makes me yawn
to read beyond the lovers’ wedding night.
I close The Flame, not even halfway through.
His sword grows dull while she goes on and on
about how lovers must stay true. I’d write
another ending, if I could, for you.
evidence: The speaker redirects romantic longing and anger from the ex-partner onto romance novels—obsessively preoccupied with fictional lovers while the affect toward the actual relationship remains charged but indirect. The final lines ('His sword grows dull while she goes on and on / about how lovers must stay true') displace criticism of the partner's fidelity through symbolic substitutes, while 'I'd write another ending, if I could, for you' signals both the displacement and the creative transformation attempting to sublimate the loss.

author: James K. Baxter
content:
About twilight we came to the whitewashed pub
On a knuckle of land above the bay
 
Where a log was riding and the slow
Bird-winged breakers cast up spray.
 
One of the drinkers round packing cases had
The worn face of a kumara god,
 
Or so it struck me. Later on
Lying awake in the veranda bedroom
 
In great dryness of mind I heard the voice of the sea
Reverberating, and thought: As a man
 
Grows older he does not want beer, bread, or the prancing flesh,
But the arms of the eater of life, Hine-nui-te-po,
 
With teeth of obsidian and hair like kelp
Flashing and glimmering at the edge of the horizon.
evidence: The speaker experiences 'great dryness of mind' while contemplating aging and death, spreading anxiety through realistic, affect-laden preparation: 'As a man grows older he does not want beer, bread, or the prancing flesh, / But the arms of the eater of life.' The meditation involves both thinking and feeling about mortality, preparing the self through conscious worrying rather than avoidance.

author: James K. Baxter
content:
The small grey cloudy louse that nests in my beard
Is not, as some have called it, ‘a pearl of God’ —
 
No, it is a fiery tormentor
Waking me at two a.m.
 
Or thereabouts, when the lights are still on
In the houses in the pa, to go across thick grass
 
Wet with rain, feet cold, to kneel
For an hour or two in front of the red flickering
 
Tabernacle light — what He sees inside
My meandering mind I can only guess —
 
A madman, a nobody, a raconteur
Whom He can joke with — ‘Lord,’ I ask Him,
 
‘Do You or don’t You expect me to put up with lice?’
His silent laugh still shakes the hills at dawn.
evidence: The speaker directly confronts physical suffering (lice, cold, sleeplessness) and spiritual humiliation while maintaining full awareness and transforming the pain into a comic dialogue with God—'Do You or don't You expect me to put up with lice?'—making himself the butt of the joke ('a madman, a nobody') while imagining God's 'silent laugh' that 'shakes the hills at dawn.'

author: James K. Baxter
content:
One writes telling me I am her guiding light
And my poems her bible — on this cold morning
 
After moss I smoke one cigarette
And hear a magpie chatter in the paddock,
 
The image of Hatana — he bashes at the windows
In idiot spite, shouting — ‘Pakeha! You can be
 
‘The country’s leading poet’ — at the church I murmured, ‘Tena koe,'
To the oldest woman and she replied, ‘Tena koe’—
 
Yet the red book is shut from which I should learn Maori
And these daft English words meander on,
 
How dark a light! Hatana, you have gripped me
Again by the balls; you sift and riddle my mind
 
On the rack of the middle world, and from my grave at length
A muddy spring of poems will gush out.
evidence: The speaker reflects on his own contradictions with metacognitive awareness: receives adulation ('guiding light') yet observes himself failing to learn Maori ('red book is shut'), then explicitly watches himself being psychologically 'sifted and riddled' by Hatana's accusation of performative identity ('Pakeha!')—tracking internal conflict without collapse or flight.

author: James K. Baxter
content:
Three dark buds for the Trinity
On one twig I found in the lining of my coat
 
Forgotten since I broke them from the tree
That grows opposite the RSA building
 
At the top of Vulcan Lane — there I would lay down my parka
On the grass and meditate, cross-legged; there was a girl
 
Who sat beside me there;
She would hold a blue flower at the centre of the bullring
 
While the twigs on the tree became black
And then slowly green again — she was young — if I had said,
 
‘Have my coat; have my money’ —
She would have gone away; but because I gave her nothing
 
She came again and again to share that nothing
Like a bird that nest in the open hand.
evidence: The speaker derives vicarious gratification from giving the girl nothing—'because I gave her nothing / She came again and again to share that nothing'—correctly perceiving her need for non-intrusive presence rather than material rescue, which paradoxically fulfills both his own contemplative withdrawal and her need for companionship without obligation.

author: James K. Baxter
content:
As I come down the hill from Toro Poutini’s house
My feet are sore, being bare, on the sharp stones
 
And that is a suitable penance. The dust of the pa road
Is cool, though, and I can see
 
The axe of the moon shift down behind the trees
Very slowly. The red light from the windows
 
Of the church has a ghostly look, and in
This place ghosts are real. The bees are humming loudly
 
In moonlight in their old hive above the church door
Where I go in to kneel, and come out to make my way
 
Uphill past a startled horse who plunges in the paddock
Above the nunnery. Now there are one or two
 
Of the tribe back in the big house—What would you have me do,
King Jesus? Your games with me have turned me into a boulder.
evidence: The speaker directly addresses 'King Jesus' with the accusatory complaint 'Your games with me have turned me into a boulder'—attributing current suffering to the divine object while performing penitential rituals (bare feet on stones, kneeling in church) that simultaneously broadcast injury and enact atonement.

poem_name: The Farm-Labor Camp Is Just Down The Road
author: Katrina Roberts
content:
Not coop so much as aviary. The way
everyone thinks
the youngest two are twins
despite their differences.
This memory of a blue dress
the tall man called a cool drink of water.
A carpet burning
the skin right off my back. What I needed
to say versus what I was able
—the way you can't see
an image in sunlight
unless it's matte.Could you drink pee if there were nothing
else?
evidence: The speaker recounts traumatic imagery (carpet burning skin, inability to speak, threatening question about survival) in disconnected fragments without affective integration, maintaining observational distance despite disturbing content. Each memory is presented as a discrete image without emotional synthesis.

author: Tyrone Williams
content:
A downpour drumming on the rooftop,
engine running, car, idle, interior
bathed in the pungent intoxicating spices
radiating from the carry-out
in the passenger seat. Inside the Taj,
neon beacon in a strip mall
dark with the common sense of folks
long gone home, red lamps
glowered. A pair of headlights
glared back. A downpour drowning
out its own drumming, so loud
I could barely make out the whispered venom
streaming from a mobile into my right ear.
She was saying something about something
as I reached across the steering column
with my left hand, as if my left ear
had been bent by the loudspeaker of the law.
Engine off, everything—the car, the carry-out, etc.—
went cold. I tossed the phone into the passenger seat,
put her into reverse, backed up, out,
and drove home with my double order,
her running commentary as undertow.
evidence: The speaker recounts an emotionally charged phone call ('whispered venom streaming') while fixated on sensory details (spices, neon, drumming rain) and mechanical actions (reaching across steering column, shifting gears), maintaining forensic precision without accessing the relational distress.
