evidence: The speaker transforms the primitive destruction of dolls (broken, split, burned) into transcendent creative celebration—'the bones of my skull / marvelously start to sing,' 'the whole shell / of myself invents without peril'—maintaining passionate intensity while achieving aesthetic and relational triumph ('our waking kiss,' 'the house laughs'). The sequential destruction-then-gift pattern ('I gave the fire what was left' followed by hearth flowering) suggests undoing as secondary mechanism, but the primary movement is sublimatory: loss becomes treasure, anguish becomes aubade.

author: Donald Justice
content:
It smiles to see me
Still in my bathrobe.
It sits in my lap
And will not let me rise.
Now it is kissing my eyes.
Arms enfold me, arms
Pale with a thick down.
It seems I am falling asleep
To the sound of a story
Being read me.
This is the story.
Weeks have passed
Since first I lifted my hand
To set it down.
evidence: The speaker observes lethargy as an external entity that enfolds and kisses them while they remain passive in bathrobe for weeks, describing this state with mechanical detachment ('It seems I am falling asleep') and curious affectlessness despite objective dysfunction.

author: Michele Wolf
content:
90th and Third, NYC



We almost missed him, although his face,
As blunt as a busy Picasso, all shifting
Planes, was wedged in a picture frame.  
We almost missed him, the way one can stop
Seeing hunched-over bodies along the street
Or a favorite picture above the sofa
In the living room, so familiar it seems
Invisible, until it has drifted askew
Or been removed.  "If only he had something
More contemporary," my companion offered.
The man in the frame extended his crushed
Paper coffee cup, fingers hugging its Greek
Pillars and statues, white and blue.
"Spare any change?" he asked.  I brought
Forth a quarter.  His eyes, brilliant, saidI am a masterpiece.  This is where I live.
evidence: The speaker transforms the uncomfortable encounter with homelessness into aesthetic witnessing—seeing the man's face as 'blunt as a busy Picasso' and receiving his eyes' unspoken claim 'I am a masterpiece'—channeling social distress into poetic perception that honors rather than denies the man's dignity while maintaining full awareness of the street reality.

author: Lloyd Schwartz
content:
rain on the lake
room at the lodge
alone in a room
in the lazy light
loons on the lake
geese in the air
moose in the woods
aware    awake
a cry dislodged
from the musty woods
the gamy musk
of the one aroused
the roaming moose
the rooms lit up
the woods awake
in the loony light
the moon dislodged
the lake aflame
the Muse amazed
amused     aroused
evidence: The speaker transforms raw sexual arousal ('the one aroused,' 'the gamy musk') into aesthetic linguistic play—'amazed amused aroused'—channeling primitive erotic energy into formal poetic craft through sound patterns and nature imagery that maintain emotional intensity while achieving artistic form.

author: Neil Carpathios
content:
By freezing passion at its blossoming
perhaps Rodin knew he challenged
Sophocles who said as lover you want
ice to be ice yet not melt
in your hands. How stone,
implying permanence, might let us believe,
a moment, the seated figures are beyond the leaf
that cannot keep from letting go the branch,
beyond even stupidly purpling grapes
that do not understand the process
by which they darken; darken nevertheless.
evidence: The speaker theorizes about passion through layers of artistic and philosophical reference (Rodin, Sophocles) while maintaining affective distance—describing love's impossibility via sculpture and grapes 'that do not understand the process / by which they darken' renders potentially devastating emotional content as abstract observation.

author: John Brehm
content:
I wear my heart on my sleeve,
or rather both sleeves, since
it's usually broken.
Sometimes when I join my hands
to pray, the jagged edges
briefly touch,
like a plate that fell and cracked
apart from being asked
to hold too much.
evidence: The speaker simultaneously inhabits and observes emotional experience ('I wear my heart on my sleeve, or rather both sleeves'), reflecting on patterns of vulnerability with gentle irony. The metaphor of the cracked plate transforms recurrent heartbreak into an image that holds both suffering and wry self-awareness without collapse into complaint or dramatic suffering.

author: Michele Wolf
content:
As I was guided by the director through the thick space
Of these rooms, worn sparrow brown, and strode
With the August sun on my shoulders across this particular
Acre of grass, nobody had told me this was the place
Where you had summered as a boy.  I have weathered
My fourth decade, older now than you were
When you died. I can barely remember you, yet I can see
You not as my father but as my son. You are age nine.
The downpour divides into two massive stage curtains
Parting. You bolt from the bunk, loudly racing
With your chums down the slippery hill to the dock,
Your cape of a towel flapping as if ready to lift you airborne.
You are the smallest. Still, you always run in the front.
You do not know how beautiful you are, of course, squinting
Against the sun, the flame that escapes behind the gray
Vapor for hours, sometimes for days. You cannot see
That from the beginning it has been eyeing you from afar,
That it has focused its golden spotlight just for you.
evidence: The speaker visits the father's childhood summer place, spreads anxiety over time through imaginative reconstruction of the dead father as a boy, preparing herself emotionally by envisioning him vulnerable and mortal ('You do not know... it has been eyeing you from afar') while maintaining full awareness of the loss already suffered.

author: Michele Wolf
content:
She recognizes its crest in the way he looks at her. 
The wave is as vast as the roiling mass in the Japanese
Print they had paused in front of at the museum,
Capped with ringlets of foam, all surging sinew.
That little village along the shore would be
Totally lost. There is no escaping this.
The wave is flooding his heart,
And he is sending the flood
Her way. It rushes
Over her.
Can you look at one face
For the whole of a life?
Does the moon peer down
At the tides and hunger for home?
evidence: The speaker recognizes the oncoming wave of love 'There is no escaping this' and braces herself with questions about whether one can sustain this intensity 'Can you look at one face / For the whole of a life?'—experiencing modest affective distress while preparing emotionally for future discomfort through realistic, affect-laden planning.

author: Geoffrey Brock
content:
The screamer sleeps, inside.
The desert's wide awake:
the mouse, the rattlesnake.
I've come out here to hide,
behind our house, below
the riddled sky, afraid
of what our bodies made.
To the south: Mexico...
These are the nights men run.
Guaymas before midday,
a beach-town life...I play
it out. Such things are done.
The Rincons seep like a stain
into the paling east.
The borders are policed.
The wail, nearby, of a train.
evidence: The speaker acknowledges conflicting forces simultaneously—'afraid / of what our bodies made' (the infant) while consciously contemplating flight ('These are the nights men run')—yet maintains awareness of external constraints ('The borders are policed') and finite timeframe ('Guaymas before midday'), progressing anxiously through the night without actual escape.

author: Jessica Jopp
content:
The Oldest Love Poem(For Susan)



Back from Istanbul, she gives to methe photograph she took insidethe Archaeological Museum’sblue tiled hush, of a tabletcarved in terra cotta from Nippur,written in Sumerian.Delicate etches, a lift of riverbedwhere the summer waters ranglistened on this piece of earththe earnest working hand,a pause between the lines to contemplatecedars’ ornate overhangingleaf-work become inseparablefrom the carving.  Maybe reading sky,reading wind, or tree soundsbeside the sound of clayshaped to carry a human mark.Maybe it says     we are so elegantin our exchange that looking at each otherthe trees whisper their contented greenacross any distance to be herebranches heat-satiatedfull in our veins of holding
evidence: The speaker transforms erotic-attachment affect into aesthetic contemplation of ancient love inscription, channeling longing into passionate engagement with artifact and gift-exchange ritual while maintaining emotional intensity ('branches heat-saturate / full in our veins of holding')—primitive desire finding socially valued expression through museum visit, photography, linguistic-historical meditation.

poem_name: To the Young Man on the Bridge
author: Jessica Jopp
content:
The newspaper caption read: “Two  officers talk to a young man, name withheld, as he stands on a bridge  above the Merrimack River, distraught over learning that his mother is  terminally ill.”



When I first saw you I could glance up from your image in the paperand out her kitchen doorway watchmy mother trim her artemisia,scraps of silver trailingher green wheelbarrow.And I imagined yes,if mine were dying,I would be on the abutment too,about to release the cableignoring those attempting rescue
evidence: The speaker engages in realistic, affect-laden planning for future maternal loss by imaginatively rehearsing the young man's crisis ('if mine were dying, I would be on the abutment too'), experiencing modest present distress while inoculating herself against overwhelming grief through vicarious emotional preparation.

author: Kabir
evidence: The poem catalogues impossible paradoxes as 'astonishing sights' in a teaching mode, offering itself as 'your key to the universe' without expressing psychological conflict or defensive operation—it functions as wisdom literature rather than psychological stance.

poem_name: To tonsured monks and dreadlocked Rastas
author: Kabir
content:
To tonsured monks and dreadlocked Rastas,
To idol worshippers and idol smashers,
To fasting Jains and feasting Shaivites,
To Vedic pundits and Faber poets,
The weaver Kabir sends one message:
The noose of death hangs over all.
Only Rama’s name can save you.
Say it now.
evidence: The speaker spreads anxiety across audience and time ('The noose of death hangs over all') while explicitly preparing others for future discomfort through realistic warning paired with concrete preparation ('Say it now'), demonstrating affect-laden planning that inoculates through small doses of death anxiety distributed in present moment.

author: Kabir
content:
For Geoff Dyer

his front yard
is the true Benares
      — Devara Dasimayya,
          tr. A.K. Ramanujan



His death in Benares
Won’t save the assassin
From certain hell,
Any more than a dip
In the Ganges will send
Frogs—or you—to paradise.
My home, says Kabir,
Is where there’s no day, no night,
And no holy book in sight
To squat on our lives.
evidence: The speaker transforms religious striving into vehement rejection ('no holy book in sight / To squat on our lives'), asserting spiritual purity through anti-ritualism with the excessive, hair-shirt quality characteristic of reaction formation—the sacred books that once promised transcendence are now contaminating squatters, revealing underlying ambivalence through the intensity of reversal.

poem_name: House of Cedar, Rafters of Fir
author: Paul Hoover
content:
Comfort me with apples; for I am sick of love          —Song of Solomon



scent of myrrh on the handles            when oil is in the locksilken is his mouth            when he is hard upon me young heart, green bed,            his fingers are in the streamhe eats of the bitter honey            the sweetness of cherrysacrament of the blood            and of its windingsacrament of arrival            and of its bindingexpert in earth, eager in flesh            he falls upon me and feaststhe watchmen have not seen him            nor the owl in her nesthis darkness at noon            among the white buildingsa hand that was stone            builds the inner templesacrament of what is written            on the table of the heartblueness of the wound            where he has placed his kiss
evidence: The speaker transforms erotic desire into sacred ritual through sustained religious imagery ('sacrament,' 'inner temple,' 'bitter honey') while maintaining intense sensual engagement ('he falls upon me and feasts,' 'his fingers are in the stream'), channeling sexual urgency into formal liturgical structure that achieves aesthetic distance without loss of emotional heat.

author: Gottfried Benn
content:
A drowned drayman was hoisted on to the slab.
Someone had jammed a lavender aster
between his teeth.
As I made the incision up from the chest
with a long knife
under the skin
to cut out tongue and gums,
I must have nudged it because it slipped
into the brain lying adjacent.
I packed it into the thoracic cavity
with the excelsior
when he was sewn up.
Drink your fill in your vase!
Rest easy,
little aster!
evidence: The speaker recounts dissecting a drowned corpse with procedural precision ('incision up from the chest / with a long knife'), maintaining forensic detachment while describing intimate violence to the human body, then addresses the aster with tender domesticity ('Drink your fill') without accessing grief, horror, or any proportionate affect toward the death itself.

author: Gottfried Benn
content:
The mouth of the girl who had lain long in the rushes
looked so nibbled.
When they opened her chest, her esophagus was so holey.
Finally in a bower under the diaphragm
they found a nest of young rats.
One little thing lay dead.
The others were living off kidneys and liver
drinking the cold blood and had
had themselves a beautiful youth.
And just as beautiful and quick was their death:
the lot of them were thrown into the water.
Ah, will you hearken at the little muzzles’ oinks!
evidence: The speaker describes a drowned girl's decomposed body being eaten by rats in clinical, detached language ('esophagus was so holey,' 'drinking the cold blood') without horror or grief, then ironically frames the parasitic consumption as 'beautiful youth' while maintaining flat, observational tone throughout, culminating in the aestheticized, almost whimsical 'hearken at the little muzzles' oinks' about the rats' drowning.

author: Gottfried Benn
content:
Know this:
I live beast days. I am a water hour.
At night my eyelids droop like forest and sky.
My love knows few words:
I like it in your blood.
evidence: The speaker segregates experience into absolute categories ('beast days,' 'water hour') and oscillates between primitive self-states ('I am a water hour') and human connection ('My love knows few words') without integration, maintaining contradictory aspects in separate compartments while addressing the beloved with invasive, boundary-dissolving language ('I like it in your blood').

author: Gottfried Benn
content:
A shadow on the wall
boughs stirred by the noonday wind
that’s enough earth
and for the eye
enough celestial participation.
How much further do you want to go? Refuse
the bossy insistence
of new impressions—
lie there still,
behold your own fields,
your estate,
dwelling especially
on the poppies,
unforgettable
because they transported the summer—
where did it go?
evidence: The speaker consciously postpones further striving ('How much further do you want to go? Refuse / the bossy insistence / of new impressions') while maintaining full awareness of loss ('where did it go?'), achieving finite contentment ('that's enough earth') without repression or denial of temporal limits.

author: Gottfried Benn
content:
When despair—
you who enjoyed great triumphs
and walked with confidence and the memory
of many gifts of delirium and dawns
and unexpected
turns—
when despair wants you in its grip,
and threatens you from some unfathomable depth
with destruction
and the guttering out of your flame:
then think of the unsatisfied ones,
with their migraine-prone temples and introverted dispositions,
loyal to a few memories
that held out little hope,
who still bought flowers,
and with a smile of not much luminosity
confided secret desires
to their small-scale heavens
that were soon to be extinguished.
evidence: Speaker transforms own despair by redirecting attention to 'the unsatisfied ones' with migraine-prone temples and introverted dispositions, finding vicarious comfort in their perseverance through small gestures (buying flowers, confiding desires) despite minimal hope—attributing to others the struggle with darkness that mirrors the speaker's own, while gaining implicit solace from their quiet endurance.

author: Gottfried Benn
content:
That quality of the great boxers
to be able to stand there
and take shots,
gargle with firewater,
encounter intoxication
at sub- and supra-atomic levels,
to leave one’s sandals at the crater’s lip
like Empedocles, and descend,
not say: I’ll be back,
not think: fifty-fifty,
to vacate molehills
when dwarves want space to grow,
to dine alone,
indivisible,
and able to renounce your victory—
a hymn to that man.
evidence: The speaker transforms primitive impulses (violence, intoxication, self-destruction) into a sustained meditation on endurance and renunciation that produces socially valued aesthetic output—the 'hymn' itself. The capacity 'to stand there and take shots,' 'gargle with firewater,' and 'descend' into dangerous territories without retreat channels aggression and self-destructive urges into an artistic celebration of stoic mastery, maintaining emotional intensity while achieving formal control.

author: Gottfried Benn
content:
I have met people who,
asked after their names,
shyly—as if they had no title
to an appellation all to themselves—
replied “Fräulein Christian” and added:
“like the first name,” they wanted to make it easy for the other,
not a difficult name like “Popiol” or “Babendererde”—
“like the first name”—please, don’t burden your memory overmuch!
I have met people who
grew up in a single room with their parents
and four brothers and sisters, and studied at night
with their fingers in their ears at the kitchen table,
and grew up to be beautiful and self-possessed as duchesses—
and innerly gentle and hard-working as Nausicaa,
clear-browed as angels.
I have often asked myself and never found an answer
whence kindness and gentleness come,
I don’t know it to this day, and now must go myself.
evidence: The speaker transforms social observation of humble, resilient people into aesthetically valuable reflection, maintaining emotional authenticity ('I have often asked myself and never found an answer') while channeling wonder about human dignity into poetic testimony that honors rather than exploits its subjects. The witnessing stance itself—cataloguing beauty arising from constraint—represents sublimated expression of awe without sentimentality.

author: Karen An-hwei Lee
content:
A true friend is one soul in two bodies.  —Aristotle



Kant says, transcendental
    idealism. In Aquinas, we exist apart from bodies    but only on Thursdays when his famous ox     flies by the window wiser at Cologne    where Albertus Magnus,his real name, appoints    Aquinas to magister studentium,master of students. Aquinas    is petrified but says yes.He feels his soul     sailing out of his headfloating near the roof    where a blue ox wings by.On Wednesday, two bodies     are one soul waking at sunrise    thanks to the pineal glandof Descartes, who thinks    this node in the brainis a tiny sugar cone    or salted peanut,the seat of the soul     while Aristotle points to the chopping     ax as a teleology as if the ax were a living,    breathing personwhich it isn’t.     Heraclitus, air and fire while Aquinas objects, no    not an ax but ox.If you’re a bird or soul    I am only one milefrom the sea. If you    are a soul in two bodieslife is more complex    and we must labortwice the field of sorrow    after sleep, bath, and a glassas Aquinas whispers, the things     we love tell us who we are
evidence: The speaker engages rich philosophical material (Kant, Aquinas, Descartes, Aristotle) discussing profound questions of soul, embodiment, and connection, yet processes these emotionally charged concepts—friendship, labor in 'the field of sorrow,' what we love—in detached, theoretical registers. The final whispered line 'the things we love tell us who we are' arrives as conceptual observation rather than felt recognition.

author: Sasha Dugdale
content:
March 29, 2010



Every morning since the time changedI have woken to the dawn chorusAnd even before it sounded, I dreamed of itLoud, unbelievably loud, shameless, raucousAnd once I rose and twitched the curtains apartExpecting the birds to be pressing in frightAgainst the pane like passengersBut the garden was empty and it was nightNot a slither of light at the horizonStill the birds were bawling through the mistsTerrible, invisibleA million small evangelistsHow they sing: as if each had pecked up a smoldering coalTheir throats singed and swollen with song In dissonance as befits the dark worldWhere only travelers and the sleepless belong
evidence: Speaker experiences 'woken to the dawn chorus / And even before it sounded, I dreamed of it'—preparing psychologically for disruptive intrusion through both actual and anticipatory mental rehearsal, spreading anxiety across sleep and waking states while maintaining modest affective distress ('Terrible, invisible').

author: Fanny Howe
content:
For Peter S.﻿



I saw the shrouds of prisonerslike baptismal gownsburied outside the cemetery.On the canvas frills exhaledsinged wool and cardboard.The angels arrived as lace.Took notes, then stuck. Awful residuefrom a small cut.                                 •The veil has been ripped from the skinwhere it was burned in.The skin is the veil, the baby-material,imprinted on, as ifone dropped the handkerchiefand it was one’s wrist.The cuff is frightening.  Stuffed onto oil.Water-stains might fence its ghost in.                                 ﻿•“The barbed wire complex”I understand.  Winged and flattenedat the same time, poor things!Some leftover specters of blood.Remember Blake’s figures like columnswith headslooking around for God?When events are not as randomas they seem.                                 ﻿•The article of clothingis only half there, it’s not full,but when it falls forward, it is.Terrible emptiness of the spreadneckline and little sleeve.Half-cooked squares.Was this religious fireand is this where it passed?Maybe they are floating on waterof paint, pool-sized, blue and ridged like foam.You would have to flyto see them flat as a map.The rib and hem. Rained onfor eons. Noah’s children’sfloating forms.                                 ﻿•Angels die?It’s a frightening-miracle because here they are.The Upper Godhas let them droplike centuries into space.And I recognize them!
evidence: The speaker transforms what appears to be concentration camp imagery (prisonershrouds, barbed wire complex, burned-in veils, religious fire) into aesthetic observations delivered in curiously detached, cataloging tones—'The angels arrived as lace. Took notes, then stuck' and 'You would have to fly / to see them flat as a map'—treating atrocity as formal problem while maintaining analytic distance through aesthetic framing.

author: Amrita Pritam
content:
Lots of contemporaries—
but “me” is not my contemporary.
My birth without “me”
was a blemished offering on the collection plate.
A moment of flesh, imprisoned in flesh.
And when to the tip of this tongue of flesh
some word comes, it kills itself.
If saved from killing itself,
it descends to the paper, where a murder happens.
Gunshot—
if it strikes me in Hanoi
it strikes again in Prague.
A little smoke floats up,
and my “me” dies like an eighth-month child.
Will my “me” one day be my contemporary?
evidence: The speaker divides 'me' from the self into an irreconcilable split ('me is not my contemporary'), treating the authentic self as entirely absent/dead ('my me dies') while the social self exists as 'blemished offering' and 'imprisoned flesh,' with no integration or ambivalence—only categorical separation between true and false selves that cannot coexist temporally.

author: Amrita Pritam
content:
There were two kingdoms only:
the first of them threw out both him and me.
The second we abandoned.
Under a bare sky
I for a long time soaked in the rain of my body,
he for a long time rotted in the rain of his.
Then like a poison he drank the fondness of the years.
He held my hand with a trembling hand.
“Come, let’s have a roof over our heads awhile.
Look, further on ahead, there
between truth and falsehood, a little empty space.”
evidence: The speaker consciously acknowledges suffering ('soaked in the rain of my body,' 'rotted in the rain of his') while making a deliberate, time-limited choice to postpone resolution ('let's have a roof over our heads awhile') with full awareness of the conflict between truth and falsehood that remains unresolved but must be held in mind.

author: Juhan Liiv
content:
A gust roused the waves,
leaves blew into the water,
the waves were ash-gray,
the sky tin-gray,
ash-gray the autumn.
It was good for my heart:
there my feelings were ash-gray,
the sky tin-gray,
ash-gray the autumn.
The breath of wind brought cooler air,
the waves of mourning brought separation:
autumn and autumn
befriend each other.
evidence: The speaker catalogs ash-gray and tin-gray colors with meteorological precision ('the waves were ash-gray, / the sky tin-gray') and declares 'It was good for my heart' to match this palette, yet delivers this emotional assessment in the flat, detached tone of a weather report—acknowledging feelings exist ('there my feelings were ash-gray') while evacuating their affective charge through repetitive color taxonomy and abstract seasonal personification ('autumn and autumn / befriend each other').

author: Claude Esteban
content:
Around the bend of a phrase
you return, it’s dawn in a book, it’s
a garden, one can
see everything, the dew, a moth
on a leaf and it’s you
who rises suddenly amid the pages
and the book grows more lovely
because it’s you
and you’ve not grown old, you walk
slowly to the door.
evidence: The speaker ritually reverses time's damage through the magical act of reading—'you've not grown old' functions as omnipotent erasure of loss via the book's ritual space, where encountering the beloved 'around the bend of a phrase' undoes aging and absence.

author: Abid B Al-Abras
content:
It’s as if she were an earn,
                                                                   gebidende prey for her eyrie.
Perched alertly,
                                                                   ﻿a hægtesse on their innards.
In bitter morgenceald,
                                                                   ﻿her hoar-glittered feathers.
Suddenly she sees
                                                                   ﻿a fox on the westene.
At that she rouses,
                                                                   ﻿heaved up on high,
and heads straight at him,
                                                                   ﻿in harrowing hæste.
Hearing her, he freezes
                                                                   ﻿his tail. He’s terrified.
Sees, bestelð,
                                                                   ﻿with ēagan flashing,
talons overtake him,
                                                                   ﻿﻿dash him down in torment,
overtake him again,
                                                                   ﻿﻿swengeð him on the eorðan.
One yelp as she pincers his liver.
                                                                   ﻿﻿Wyrd—pierces aorta.
evidence: The poem presents an extended simile comparing an unspecified 'she' to a bird of prey hunting a fox, using Old English vocabulary for formal effect. The speaker maintains descriptive distance without revealing psychological stance toward distressing material.

poem_name: A blind man was riding an unheated train
author: Arseny Tarkovsky
content:
A blind man was riding an unheated train,
From Bryansk he was traveling home with his fate.
Fate whispered to him so the whole car could hear:
And why should you care about blindness and war?
It’s good, she was saying, you’re sightless and poor.
If you were not blind, you’d never survive.
The Germans won’t kill you, you’re nothing to them.
Allow me to lift that bag on your shoulder—
The one with the holes, the empty torn one.
Let me just raise your eyelids wide open.
The blind man was traveling home with his fate,
Now thankful for blindness. Happy about it.
evidence: Fate constructs elaborate justifications converting catastrophic loss (blindness, poverty, war) into fortunate protection: 'It's good, she was saying, you're sightless and poor. If you were not blind, you'd never survive.' The blind man accepts this reframing, ending 'thankful for blindness. Happy about it'—transforming objective devastation into acceptable, even positive, outcome through post-hoc logical justification.

author: Eugénio de Andrade
content:
Children grow in secret. They hide themselves in the depths and darker reaches of the house to become wild cats, white birches.
One day when you’re only half-watching the herd as it straggles back in with the afternoon dust, one child, the prettiest of them all, comes close and rises up on tiptoe to whisper I love you, I’ll be waiting for you in the hay.
Shaking some, you go to find your shotgun; you spend what’s left of the day firing at rooks and jackdaws, uncountable at this hour, and crows.
evidence: Speaker redirects overwhelming incestuous anxiety (child's seductive whisper) away from the actual source onto substitute targets—'firing at rooks and jackdaws, uncountable at this hour, and crows'—maintaining affect (shaking, violent discharge) while altering object from child to birds.

author: Elena Shvarts
content:
I spoke in a hurry, in a nervous hush,
Because the time was short—
The lightning was shuddering,
Slowing down, running.
Or was that my blood,
The quiet diminishing of daily life?
It’s time for me to go forth
Into Your tiny mustard seed.
In the house of my Father, everything is fading,
In the house of the Father, all the angels are crying,
Because the anguish of a jaded, exhausted horse
Sometimes finds its way even unto them.
One gray day, I was alive on this earth,
And amid the mist of day—in triumph—
The Spirit may approach and look
So that you will see Him, without seeing.
And, so, celebrate the meager light,
Curse not the twilight.
If Christ is to visit us
It will be on such pitiful days as these.
evidence: The speaker spreads anxiety over time through affect-laden preparation for death and divine encounter ('It's time for me to go forth / Into Your tiny mustard seed'), experiencing modest distress while realistically planning for future discomfort ('Because the time was short'). The anticipatory work transforms potential terror into measured readiness, culminating in instruction to 'celebrate the meager light' rather than deny the approaching end.

author: Elsa von Freytag-Loringhoven
content:
From Daddy sprung my inborn ribaldry.
His crudeness destined me to be the same.
A seedlet, flowered from a shitty heap,
I came, the crowning glory of his aim.
From Mother I inherited ennui,
The leg irons of the queendom I once rattled.
But I won’t let such chains imprison me.
And there is just no telling what this brat’ll...!
This marriage thing? We snub our nose at it.
What’s pearl turns piss, what’s classy breeds what’s smutty.
But like it? Lump it? Neither’s exigent.
And I’m the end result of all that fucking.
Do what you will! This world’s your oyster, Pet.
But be forewarned. The sea might drown you yet.
evidence: The speaker confronts painful inheritance ('shitty heap,' 'all that fucking') with full awareness while transforming it into gallows humor that makes herself the butt of the joke ('I'm the end result'), maintaining observing ego and sophisticated timing ('what's pearl turns piss') that reconciles paradox (ribaldry/ennui, classy/smutty) without looking away from distressing reality.

author: David Ferry
content:
They said, my saints, my slogan-sayers sang,   
Be good, my child, in spite of all alarm.
They stood, my fathers, tall in a row and said,   
Be good, be brave, you shall not come to harm.
I heard them in my sleep and muttering dream,   
And murmuring cried, How shall I wake to this?
They said, my poets, singers of my song,   
We cannot tell, since all we tell you is
But history, we speak but of the dead.   
And of the dead they said such history
(Their beards were blazing with the truth of it)   
As made of much of me a mystery.
evidence: The speaker recounts childhood messages from 'saints,' 'fathers,' and 'poets' about goodness and history in detached, abstract language ('They said... They stood... They said') without accessing the emotional experience of receiving these teachings. The 'muttering dream' and 'murmuring' suggest affect is present but evacuated, while the final line—'made of much of me a mystery'—intellectualizes profound confusion about identity into conceptual formulation.

author: Kevin Young
content:
I hunted heaven
for him.
No dice.
Too uppity,
it was. Not enough
music, or dark dirt.
I begged the earth empty
of him. Death
believes in us whether
we believe
or not. For a long while
I watch the sound
of a boy bouncing a ball
down the block
take its time
to reach me. Father,
find me when
you want. I’ll wait.
evidence: The speaker consciously postpones grief's resolution ('I'll wait') while maintaining full awareness of loss and desire—acknowledging both the father's absence and the deferred hope of reunion without repression or denial, progressing 'anxiously and slowly' through mourning stages.

author: Scott Cairns
content:
προσέλθετε



For near is where you’ll meet what you have wandered far to find. And near is where you’ll very likely see how far the near obtains. In the dark katholikon
evidence: The speaker contemplates future spiritual encounter ('near is where you'll meet what you have wandered far to find') with affect-laden preparation for transformation, experiencing modest anticipatory engagement with the paradox of seeking and finding without magical thinking or avoidance.

author: A. F. Moritz
content:
For John Hollander



Although it’s likely you’re on your ownat this moment in this city of three millionreading the poems of Traherne,and there was no one till you lit your lamp,the kingdom of childhood keeps being foundedin his voice and his seeing,which are a sort of birth. A birth goes onin the dark of a poor family, or a mother alone.Then comes the small bright circle of the faces:lover pores over sleeping loved one, parent over childin their enclosure we name home,a hut in the plain so bare there’s not a tongueof grass to make the wind hiss. Unknownto the world a world exists:trees and streams, birds all the colors of the flowers.So Traherne pours over youhis wild remembrance of the world to come. And wouldeven in the silence of his bookif it were lost and lay unopenedtwo hundred years. Even if he had diedbefore he sang the Eden in his look.
evidence: The speaker transforms profound existential loneliness ('on your own...in this city of three million') into the socially valued, creative experience of reading Traherne's poetry, channeling isolation into aesthetic communion that maintains emotional intensity ('wild remembrance') while producing real consequence—a poetic meditation on reading as generative relationship that extends across centuries.

author: Marianne Boruch
content:
At the Oriental Institute, Chicago



They redid King Tut splendid, once stone-huge as this yet his wife’s feet tiny, the only thing of her nowlow, next to him. A few toes, some of the rest, a bit of ankle, that’s itin the shade of her husband’s looming, massive looking straight ahead into the future where we live and can’teye-to-eye, where to stare at himis to suffer warbler neck, head back and upà la the high just-leafing-out trees as bright bitswing their blinkand hide. Little wife, such small feet, the thought dwarfs the king as ache, as what is ever left of us and oh, I like her better.
evidence: The speaker redirects strong affect about powerlessness, erasure, and historical invisibility away from any personal source onto the museum artifact of Tut's wife—'such small feet, the thought dwarfs the king as ache'—concluding with preferential attachment to the diminished figure while the actual emotional origin remains unexamined.

author: John Rybicki
content:
For Julie



If I could tie a river around my love’s waist like ribbon,               make sails out of her bloodand pin down death like a squirming bug.If I could lift and rock each coffin in my arms              I would start with hers.
evidence: The speaker's conditional syntax ('If I could...') asserts magical, unlimited control over elemental forces and death itself—tying rivers like ribbon, making sails from blood, pinning death down—collapsing boundary between internal desire and external outcome in fantasies of total mastery over mortality and natural law. The dedication 'For Julie' and focus on 'her' coffin suggests death of beloved as context for this omnipotent wish to reverse irreversible loss.

author: Eduardo C. Corral
content:
For Arthur Russell



All that glitters isn’t music.Once, hidden in tall grass, I tossed fistfuls of dirt into the air:doe after doe of leaping. You said it was nothing but a trick of the light. Gold curves. Gold scarves. Am I not your animal?You’d wait in the orchard for hours to watch a deerbreak from the shadows.You said it was like lifting a cello out of its black case.
evidence: The speaker transforms primitive longing (animal identification, fistful-throwing desire for recognition) into aestheticized images ('Gold curves. Gold scarves') and the beloved's waiting is rendered as the reverent act of 'lifting a cello out of its black case'—raw yearning channeled into sustained, musically-structured address.

author: Eduardo C. Corral
content:
Less lonely, less . . .



I gave you a tiny box. You lifted the lid, praised the usefulness of my gift: a silver pin shaped like an amper-sand. As you fastened it to your lapel, I thought again of that motel outside of Chicago. ¿Te acuerdas?I sat on the edge of a bench, untied my shoes. Face down, eyes shut, you breathed inthe aroma of sweat & allspice coming off the sheets. I tossed my ring—gold, inscribed—toward a pile of clothes. But the ring dropped in the small of your back where it rattled & rattled like a coin in a beggar’s cup.
evidence: The speaker transforms an intensely intimate memory (ring rattling in the small of your back, face down on sheets) into a formal gift exchange and aesthetic observation, narrating charged sensory details with curiously detached precision—'I thought again,' 'breathed in the aroma'—as if cataloguing rather than inhabiting the erotic scene.

author: José Antonio Rodríguez
content:
No pitying/“Ah” for this one —Alan Shapiro



No, nor a fierce hurrahfor what it does without choice,for following the lightfor the same reason the light follows it.Just a thing rough to the touch, a facelike a thousand ticks turning their backs,suckling at something you can’t see,and a body like a tag off the earthso that my child hands couldn’t tear it outfrom the overgrown lot next door.                           My palms raw with the shockof quills and spines. Its hold like spite, and uglyexcept when seen from a distance—a whole field of them by the highway,an 80-mile-per-hour view                           like a camera’s flash.All of them like haloswithout saints to weigh them down.
evidence: The speaker recounts childhood trauma—palms raw, unable to uproot the plant, describing its hold 'like spite'—but narrates the experience with detached botanical precision and aesthetic theorizing ('ugly except when seen from a distance'), converting bodily pain into impersonal observation without accessing the fear or frustration a child would have felt.

author: Greg Glazner
content:
After Stevens



By now the snow is easingthe live nerves of the wire fenceand the firs,                              softening the distances it falls through,laying down a rightness, as in the spackled whites,the woodgrains of a room’s hush                                                                  before music,before a lush legato in whose unctionsthe excruciations ease,                                            as in the firstthick arrhythmics from the hardwoods                                                                        of the late quartets,whose dense snow of emotion,                                                         downdrifting,formal,                 whose violins and cellos,desiring the exhilarations of changes,                                                                          turn loose an infusionof wintry music, all sideslip and immense descent,repetitions, evolutionssalving down into the still air,                                                         the wound,the listening.
evidence: The speaker transforms physical and emotional pain ('excruciations,' 'the wound') into aesthetic experience through music and natural imagery, channeling distress into formal beauty ('dense snow of emotion, downdrifting, formal') with sustained passionate engagement that maintains intensity while achieving creative distance.

author: Gottfried Benn
content:
Asters—sweltering days
old adjuration/curse,
the gods hold the balance
for an uncertain hour.
Once more the golden flocks
of heaven, the light, the trim—
what is the ancient process
hatching under its dying wings?
Once more the yearned-for,
the intoxication, the rose of you—
summer leaned in the doorway
watching the swallows—
one more presentiment
where certainty is not hard to come by:
wing tips brush the face of the waters,
swallows sip speed and night.
evidence: The speaker worries realistically about future discomfort ('an uncertain hour,' 'Once more the yearned-for...where certainty is not hard to come by'), experiencing modest affective distress while contemplating mortality and transience, spreading anxiety across temporal registers through repeated 'once more' structures that inoculate against loss.

author: Gottfried Benn
content:
Renege on the rock! Smash
the oppressor cave! Sashay
out onto the floor! Scorn the cornices—
see, from the beard of drunk Silenus
from the unique uproar of his blood
the wine dribble into his genitals!
Spit on the obsession with pillars:
ancient rheumatic hands quake toward
gray skies. Bring down the temple
by the yearning of your knees
twitching with dance.
Spill, spread, unpetal, bleed
your soft flowers through great wounds.
Dove-hauled Venus girds her loins
with roses—
see the summer’s last puff of blue
drift on seas of asters to distant
pine-brown coasts; see
this final hour of our mendacious
southern happiness   
held aloft.
evidence: The speaker channels primitive destructive and sexual urges ('Smash the oppressor cave,' 'wine dribble into his genitals,' 'bleed your soft flowers through great wounds') into formally crafted aesthetic vision, transforming violent impulse into socially valued poetic proclamation without loss of emotional intensity or pleasure—the voice maintains passionate engagement while achieving aesthetic distance through mythological imagery and ekphrastic structure.

author: Gottfried Benn
content:
A day without tears is a rare occurrence
                culpable absent-mindedness
practically an episode
                                                   •
when men still wore starched collars,
and stuffed cotton wool between their toes
hobbled about in pain, pedicure hadn’t been invented,
but you would see faces that were worth a second look
those were years when something whispered
evidence: The speaker delivers a factual inventory of past suffering ('stuffed cotton wool between their toes / hobbled about in pain') in a detached, curatorial tone that strips the material of emotional resonance, treating human discomfort as historical data alongside sartorial details like starched collars.

author: Paolo Febbraro
content:
He fell and died, the skier,
high up there in the snow.
And now, spring having come,
his father leaves his home,
dark in the valley, to throw
a hook in the heavy river.
evidence: The father consciously postpones grief through the finite ritual of fishing—'spring having come' marks temporal awareness, moving from dark valley to river without forgetting the son's death, maintaining connection to loss while deferring its full emotional reckoning through the socially acceptable activity of angling.

author: Edith Södergran
content:
That the stars are adamant
everyone understands—
but I won’t give up seeking joy on each blue wave
or peace below every gray stone.
If happiness never comes, what is a life?
A lily withers in the sand
and if its nature has failed? The tide
                                         washes the beach at night.
What is the fly looking for on the spider’s web?
What does a dayfly make of its hours?
(Two wings creased over a hollow body.)
Black will never turn to white—
yet the perfume of our struggle lingers
as each morning fresh flowers
spring up from hell.
The day will come
when the earth is emptied, the skies collapse
and all goes still—
when nothing remains but the dayfly
                                         folded in a leaf.
But no one knows it.
evidence: The speaker transforms existential despair and mortality anxiety into aesthetically charged contemplation—'the perfume of our struggle lingers / as each morning fresh flowers / spring up from hell'—channeling primitive dread of meaninglessness into philosophically productive verse that maintains emotional intensity while achieving socially communicable form.

author: Edith Södergran
content:
when all the golden birds
fly home across the blue deep water;
On shore I sit rapt in its scattering
                                                       glitter;
departure rustles through the trees.
This farewell is vast and separation draws close,
but reunion, that also is certain.
My head on my arm I fall asleep easily.
On my eyes a mother’s breath,
from her mouth to my heart:
sleep, child, and dream now the sun is gone.—
evidence: The speaker acknowledges impending separation ('This farewell is vast and separation draws close') while simultaneously holding certainty of future reunion ('but reunion, that also is certain'), spreading anxiety over departure through affect-laden planning that enables calm sleep despite the loss.

author: Eugene Dubnov
content:
What is the structure of lips
That take care of sounds,
That can scream loud and long,
That can wait and be silent?
Yesterday I was mastering words
And kissing lips lightly—
Their loving weakness
Now remains on my own
Hardworking lips,
Exacting, as if forever,
My terrible punishment.
evidence: The speaker treats the past act of 'kissing lips lightly' as a transgression requiring expiation, and the 'loving weakness' absorbed from those lips now exacts 'terrible punishment' on the speaker's own lips—a magical causality where yesterday's kiss demands ritualistic atonement through hardworking speech today.

author: John Matthias
content:
In memory of Octavio Paz



not even lost in death the memory
of why we burned, and therefore still
a fire consuming all obsequious delay,
now polvo, dust, of a desire but still alive
             and aching, not even lost to you
within our common urn, urgent as an ash
still burning alma, soul, still
and moving toward you, la muerte, my amor—
             not even lost in death, memoria,
and feeling some reply, alma, memory and ash,
ash burning still, still
and moving toward you, dust and dust, ash
             alma and amor constante
             más allá de la muerte, constant
even in our common urn, polvo enamorado.
evidence: The speaker transforms primitive death-terror and erotic longing into formally crafted poetry that channels both grief and desire through sustained aesthetic engagement with Quevedo's sonnet tradition, maintaining emotional intensity while achieving social value through literary creation dedicated to Octavio Paz's memory.

author: John Matthias
content:
In memory of Anthony Kerrigan
The pure shapes of things shake and are fall
ing under the cry of bajo el cri cri
and chirping of the six margaritas
daisies that I loved but now know deflower
when the men bent upon murdering me bend
down in cabinets and on cliffs and in cafes
where some flamenco guitarist breaks his fingers
on the grave accents   /   /   /     breaks
his fingers on the acute
             \ \
                          hunting now
even in the graves
evidence: The speaker perceives external agents as 'bent upon murdering me' and 'hunting now even in the graves,' misattributing internal persecution (guilt, fear, self-attack) as originating from threatening others in the environment—men bending down 'in cabinets and on cliffs and in cafes' become persecutory figures. The paranoid stance, combined with fragmented syntax and dissociative 'breaks,' suggests boundary collapse between internal threat and external world.

author: Jorge Luis Borges
content:
Music of Japan. Parsimoniously
from the water clock the drops unfold
in lazy honey or ethereal gold
that over time reiterates a weave
eternal, fragile, enigmatic, bright.
I fear that every one will be the last.
They are a yesterday come from the past.
But from what shrine, from what mountain’s slight
garden, what vigils by an unknown sea,
and from what modest melancholy, from
what lost and rediscovered afternoon
do they arrive at their far future: me?
Who knows? No matter. When I hear it play
I am. I want to be. I bleed away.
evidence: The speaker transforms existential anxiety about temporal loss ('I fear that every one will be the last') into aesthetic contemplation of the music box's form. The final line's paradox—'I am. I want to be. I bleed away'—shows primitive terror of annihilation channeled into heightened artistic consciousness without loss of intensity.

author: Marina Tsvetaeva
content:
A kiss on the forehead—erases misery.
I kiss your forehead.
A kiss on the eyes—lifts sleeplessness.
I kiss your eyes.
A kiss on the lips—is a drink of water.
I kiss your lips.
A kiss on the forehead—erases memory.1917
evidence: The speaker performs ritualistic kisses that magically erase misery, sleeplessness, and finally memory itself—each kiss functions as a compensatory gesture that undoes psychological pain through symbolic reversal, culminating in the erasure of memory which denies the traumatic reality that necessitated the ritual.

author: Peter Cole
content:
Each day as dawn approaches,
the King sits in majesty
and blesses the holy creatures:
To you, my creatures, I speak,
before you I declare—
Creatures who bear the throne of my glory
with all your heart, and willingly with your soul—
Blessed is the hour of your creation,
and exalted is the constellation
beneath which I gave you form.
May the light of that morning continue to shine
when you came into my mind—
for you are a vessel of my desire
prepared and perfected on that day.
Be silent, creatures of my making,
so I might hear my children pray.
evidence: The speaker adopts the voice of a King blessing 'holy creatures' with superlatives ('exalted,' 'perfected,' 'vessel of my desire'), attributing omnipotent creative power to self while demanding silence to hear prayers—an archaic conviction of unlimited control over reality combined with inflated positive attribution to objects serving the self's regulatory needs.

author: Yosef Gikatilla
content:
The Nut Garden holds things felt and thought
and feeling for thought is always a palace—
Sinai with flames of fire about it,
burning though never by fire devoured.
On all four sides surrounded so—
entrance is barred to pretenders forever.
For one who learns to be wise, however,
its doors are open toward the East:
he reaches out and takes a nut,
then cracks its shell, and eats...
evidence: The speaker frames mystical experience and divine encounter (Sinai, flames, burning) through abstract architectural metaphors (palace, doors, sides) and presents spiritual wisdom as a cognitive puzzle-solving sequence (learns to be wise, reaches, cracks, eats) stripped of the overwhelming affect such experiences traditionally evoke—delivering theological content in the detached tone of instruction.

author: Peter Cole
content:
Veiled in velvet, is she here?
      Leave off, leave off:
           You shall not enter,
                you shall not emerge.
      It is neither yours nor your share.
Return...Return:
      The sea is swelling;
           its waves are calling.
                I hold to the holy portion—
      I am held in the holiness of the King.
evidence: The speaker performs ritualistic verbal counterbalancing ('Leave off, leave off' / 'Return...Return') with obsessive symmetries and magical formulations to ward off Lilith, treating the demonized feminine presence as a dangerous force requiring talismanic language to neutralize through repetitive negation ('You shall not enter, you shall not emerge') followed by compensatory assertion of holiness.

author: Mike Puican
content:
For Barry Silesky



Catalpas blooming up and down Catalpa Street, car alarms blooming up and down Waveland Avenue—an instant of nature without the narrative. O face-in-your-morning-juice, swimmer-in-an-old-wool-suit, we sit side by side on the steps smoking the same cigarette, watching children who live alone, women married to the wrong men.  Here is your little dog roaming the alley. What will he do for love this time? The gauchos sing: “The silver lights of stars hurl themselves against the open pampas of Clark Street” O tomato-in-a-woman’s-palm, one millisecond following the next millisecond, “Heal thyself,” the poem says, “Pick up your beggar’s mat and walk.” You hurl yourself into traffic. You talk to cops and street thugs;they smile at their smartphones.  They strut in the sun like jackals after a kill. And the gauchos sing: “Everyone will finally leave you, fugitive.” A cloud of pigeons cuts through the smog. Everyone will finally leave you.When the bus comes we sing like sailors. A red sky presses you to its lips. I tell you that everything has already been written. You say on a long, difficult pilgrimage Basho wrote on his hat.
evidence: The speaker transforms urban alienation and existential abandonment into lyrical art, channeling primitive material ('car alarms,' 'street thugs,' 'everyone will finally leave you, fugitive') into aesthetically wrought images ('gauchos sing,' 'red sky presses you to its lips') that maintain emotional intensity while achieving creative distance through formal composition and deliberate poetic invocation.

author: Josephine Miles
content:
When with the skin you do acknowledge drought,
The dry in the voice, the lightness of feet, the fine
Flake of the heat at every level line;
When with the hand you learn to touch without
Surprise the spine for the leaf, the prickled petal,
The stone scorched in the shine, and the wood brittle;
Then where the pipe drips and the fronds sprout
And the foot-square forest of clover blooms in sand,
You will lean and watch, but never touch with your hand.September 1934
evidence: The speaker catalogs physical sensations of drought (dry voice, light feet, heat flake, brittle wood) in clinical, weather-report tone devoid of emotional response, then predicts future restraint ('you will lean and watch, but never touch') with detached certainty—knowing without feeling the harshness, planning withdrawal without distress.

author: Joan Hutton Landis
content:
For Ann Forsythe Irwin Bourgois



Remembering AnnWhose beauty beganAt the crown of her headAnd ran to the deep underneathOf her feet—Never aware of her own élan.Now, half mad with pain, She crawls through her rooms,Calling for doctors,Falling,Forgetting,Consumed,Trepanned.Ever since the world began—Star fallNightfallBomb fallDownfall...Read the scan:Every woman and every man,Once a flowered Palestine,Falls blindly toward the Nakba—Bald catastrophe,Prescription—According to thePlan.
evidence: The speaker spreads anxiety over time through affect-laden contemplation of inevitable decline ('Read the scan: / Every woman and every man... / Falls blindly toward the Nakba'), experiencing present distress while preparing emotionally for universal catastrophe through apocalyptic imagery (star fall, nightfall, bomb fall, downfall) that keeps idea, affect, and object consciously present.

author: Laura Kasischke
content:
For G



A small thing crawling toward meacross this dark lawn. Brighteyes the only thing I’m sure I see.You’ve come back to me,haven’t you, my sweet? Fromlong ago, and very far. Through crawling dark, my sweet, you’vecome back to me, have you? Even smaller this time than the stars.
evidence: The speaker addresses a small creature in darkness with insistent declarations ('You've come back to me, haven't you') that impose a wished-for reality—return of a lost beloved—onto external stimuli (bright eyes in dark), refusing to acknowledge the obvious: this is not the person but an animal. The rapturous tone ('my sweet,' repeated) maintains exhilarated conviction despite objective circumstances.

author: Marie Ponsot
content:
“Fine bitches all, and Molly Dance...”—Djuna Barnes



Come for duty’s sake (as girls do) we watchThe sly very old woman wile away from her piousAnd stagger-blind friend, their daily split of gin.She pours big drinks. We think of whatHas crumpled, folded, slumped her flesh inAnd muddied her once tumbling blood that, young,Sped her, threaded with brave power: a Tower,Now Babel, then of ivory, of the Shulamite,Collapsed to this keen dame moving amongHerself. She hums, she plays with used brightGhosts, makes real dolls, and drinking sings Come hereMy child, and feeling it, dear. A crooking fingerShows how hot the oven is.(Also she is alive with hate.Also she is afraid of hell. Also, we wishWe might, illiberal, uncompassionate,Run from her smell, her teeth in the dish.)Even dying, her life riots in her. We stand stock stillThough aswarm with itches under her disreputable smiles.We manage to mean well. We endure, and more.We learn time’s pleasure, catch our future and its cure.We’re dear blood daughters to this every hag, and near kinTo any after this of those our mirrors tell us foolishly envy us,Presuming us, who are young, to be beautiful, kind, and sure. March 1958
evidence: The speaker repeatedly transforms revulsion into dutiful care ('We manage to mean well. We endure, and more'), with leakage visible in parenthetical confession ('Also we wish / We might, illiberal, uncompassionate, / Run from her smell, her teeth in the dish'). The excessive declaration of learning and kinship masks disgust.

author: Lisa Williams
content:
when I beheld a fire win out against a hemisphere of shadows.                           —Inferno, Dante (tr. by Allen Mandelbaum)



If I could hold a fire againsta hemisphere of shadows, hold itclose, not so that damagefinds my hands, but so fire scattersgalvanizing strands, my pupilsresponsive to the flames’ unbridled tutelage as they tell menothing but these little jumpsout of your definitions, smallor large or leaping, sinking, slumped
evidence: The speaker transforms primitive intensity ('fire against shadows') into aesthetic contemplation through poetic craft, channeling dangerous impulses ('damage finds my hands') into creative mastery ('flames' unbridled tutelage') while maintaining passionate engagement and producing socially valued work that escapes others' 'definitions.'

author: Rickey Laurentiis
content:
New Orleans, Louisiana



For the drowning, yes, there is always panic.Or peace. Your body behaving finally by instinctalone. Crossing out wonder. Crossing outa need to know. You only feel you need to live.That you deserve it. Even here. Even as your chestfills with a strange new air, you will not ask what this means. Like prey caught in the wolf’s teeth,but you are not the lamb. You are what’s in the lamb that keeps it kicking. Let it.
evidence: The speaker directly claims legitimate survival instinct and deserving without guilt or apology ('You only feel you need to live. / That you deserve it. Even here'), asserting autonomous agency ('you are not the lamb. You are what's in the lamb that keeps it kicking') while consciously postponing metaphysical questions ('Crossing out wonder. Crossing out / a need to know') in service of immediate survival.

author: Matthew Nienow
content:
gain — a bevel cut into plank ends in traditional lapstrake boat construction that allows otherwise lapped planks to lay flush at stem and transom.



There’s the paring chisel’s purpose in the steamed cedar strake, its long warp laid strong against the bench,whose pocked surface is the book of what has already been made,or marred in learning’s wake — & clamped now in the jaws one is waiting for its match, for the chisel to elaboratethe pencil’s scribed hypothesis, under whichlies another path, & through a tilting eyethe curving bevel’s made, the chisel rollingback tight scrolls of thinnest grain & what bright sleeves begin to fleece the floor; there is a lackgiven to the wood, some short song cut loosefrom the lignin’s name, that a longer &more buoyant melody be made.
evidence: The speaker transforms primitive impulses of destruction and loss ('paring,' 'marred,' 'lack given to the wood') into socially valued creative labor (boatbuilding craft), maintaining passionate engagement ('bright sleeves begin to fleece the floor') while producing tangible, functional beauty—the gain that allows 'a longer & more buoyant melody.'

author: Matthew Nienow
content:
The steam box is used for bending   frames and planks in traditional boatbuilding.



With a match I became a man who summoned dieselfrom the yellow cavernsof a ten-gallon jug, called the flamenow hissing out the hose at the small house of  water, thatrusted drum from which travelsan excruciating wetness — thisis what makes the body otherwise, what makes itsing. To take that which has decided on a shape, and bend, without breaking, the lengthening fibers. To give the straight thing curve. To make of  the tree a song grown long in a linseed skin,the slick hot strake waiting to become parcel of  the round world again.
evidence: The speaker transforms primitive impulses (summoning fire, forcing transformation, making bodies 'sing') into the socially valued craft of boatbuilding, channeling intensity ('excruciating wetness,' 'hissing flame') into creative mastery that maintains passion while producing tangible consequence—the bent plank becoming 'parcel of the round world again.'

author: Eliza Griswold
content:
Do I still long for my virginity?— Fragment 107



I never longed for my virginity.I heard it on the radio after the hurricane. There, in the aftermath, was the voice of a man —once the sweet, screwed-up boy whose hooded, jessed spirit I tried to possess with the ruthlessness I mistook for power. Here he was on NPR, so gentle, so familiar with devastation, his timbre woke the teenage falconer in me who once saw his kindness as weakness, saw a boy as an unfledged goshawk —a creature to trap and be trapped within darkened mews. I knew the rules: neither of us could sleep until the molting bird grew ravenous enough to take the raw mouse from my hand. Breaking the falconbroke us both, left us scared and less aware of  love than fear.
evidence: The speaker ritually reverses her adolescent transgression ('the ruthlessness I mistook for power') by framing her entire adult reflection as atonement—hearing his voice 'so gentle' on the radio summons confessional recognition that her falconry was cruelty requiring symbolic expiation through this poem itself, which functions as the counterbalancing gesture following damage 'committed only in thought' (the wish to possess).

author: Mary Moore Easter
content:
(there’ll be days like this.)
                                                — The Shirelles



These folks ’bout to respect me into the grave.At eighty Mama said, (mama said)            “People think you change when you’re old             but you still got a girl inside.” And men could see her, too          — that pink silk dress —soothe that hotel bellboy         “Boy, I’m old enough           to be your mama.”He coy           “well, you ain’t.”But seventy is prime time for me to own what “elder” brings.I reap myself with the respect they sow.They don’t know I got the road wide open in me.
evidence: The speaker claims autonomous competence and legitimate needs ('I reap myself with the respect they sow') while celebrating developmental achievement of separateness as empowering ('seventy is prime time for me to own what elder brings'). The forward-moving assertion 'They don't know I got the road wide open in me' expresses capacity without apology or inflation, distinguishing between respectful treatment and confining stereotype.

author: A. E. Stallings
content:
Blacksmithing demonstration, mountain arts and crafts fair, Monteagle, TN



“But can you forge a nail?” the blond boy asks,And the blacksmith shoves a length of  iron rodDeep in the coal fire cherished by the bellowsUntil it glows volcanic. He was a godBefore anachronism, before the tasksThat had been craft were jobbed out to machine.By dint of   hammer-song he makes his keen,Raw point, and crowns utility with rose:Quincunx of facets petaling its head.The breeze-made-visible sidewinds. The boy’sBlonde mother shifts and coughs. Once Work was wedTo Loveliness — sweat-faced, swarthy from soot, heReminds us with the old saw he employs(And doesn’t miss a beat): “Smoke follows beauty.”
evidence: The blacksmith transforms raw industrial labor into aesthetic creation ('crowns utility with rose'), exemplifying sublimation through craft that channels primitive force (volcanic heat, hammer-song) into socially valued art. The speaker's meditation on this process as historical loss ('before anachronism, before the task / That had been craft were jobbed out to machine') contains anticipatory mourning for vanishing sublimatory traditions.

author: Michelle Boisseau
content:
After César Vallejo



The fury that breaks a grown-up into kids,a kid into scattered birdsand a bird into limp eggs,the fury of the poortakes one part oil to two parts vinegar.The fury that breaks a tree into leaves,a leaf into deranged flowersand a flower into wilting telescopes,the fury of the poorgushes two rivers against a hundred seas.The fury that breaks the true into doubts,doubt into three matching archesand the arch into instant tombs,the fury of the poordraws a sharpening stone against two knives.The fury that breaks the soul into bodies,the body into warped organs,and the organ into eight doctrines,the fury of the poorburns with one fire in two thousand craters.
evidence: The speaker adopts a detached, formal cataloging stance toward social violence and disintegration, presenting 'the fury of the poor' through impersonal syntactic parallelism and surrealist imagery without any psychological defense mechanism governing their relationship to the material.

poem_name: Pathetic Fallacies Are Bad Science But
author: Marie Ponsot
content:
On reading Susanne K. Langer’s Mind



If  leaf-trash chokes the stream-bed,reach for rock-bottom as you rakethe muck out. Let it slump dank,and dry fading, flat above the bank.Stand back. Watch the water vault ahead.Its thrust sweeps the surface clean, shores the debris,as it debrides its stone path to the lake,clarity carrying clarity.To see clear, resist the drag of  images.Take nature as it is, not Dame nor Kind.Act in events; touch what you name. Abhoreasy obverts of natural metaphor.Let human speech breathe out its best poor bridgesfrom mind to world, mind to self, mind to mind.Yet, I admit the event of the wood thrush:     In a footnote Langer (her book rapids-clean     like the spring-water aired over sleeked rock)     says she witnessed an August bird in shock     when a hawk snatched its mate. It perched, rushed     notes fluting two life-quotas in one flood,     its lungs pushing its voice, flushing the keen     calls, pumped out as the heart pumps blood,     not in twilight or warning but noon & wrong,     its old notes whistled too fast but accurate.I read this drenched in bird-panic, its spine-fusing loss all song, all loss; that loss mineawash in unanswered unanswered song.And I cannot claim we are not desolate.
evidence: The speaker commands herself to 'resist the drag of images,' 'Take nature as it is,' and 'Abhor / easy obverts of natural metaphor'—delivering clinical imperatives about emotional distance. Yet when confronted with Langer's bird overwhelmed by mate-loss, the defensive structure collapses: 'I read this drenched in bird-panic... that loss mine / awash in unanswered unanswered song.' The poem enacts isolation's failure—affect sequestered through intellectual discipline floods back through identificatory breach.

author: Marie Ponsot
content:
“Saturday’s child must work for a living.”



“I’m moving from Grief  Street.Taxes are high herethough the mortgage’s cheap.The house is well built.With stuff to protect, thatmattered to me,the security.These things that I mind,you know, aren’t mine.I mind minding them.They weigh on my mind.I don’t mind them well.I haven’t got the knackof  kindly minding.I say Take them backbut you never do.When I throw them outit may frighten youand maybe me too.                 Maybeit will empty metoo emptilyand keep me hereasleep, at seaunder the guilt quilt,under the you tree.”
evidence: The speaker directly addresses an unnamed 'you' throughout, communicating 'Look what you made me suffer!' through accumulated burdens ('These things that I mind, you know, aren't mine') while simultaneously offering disingenuous reassurance ('it may frighten you / and maybe me too'), punishing the actual source of conflict through threats of self-emptying.

author: Michael Ryan
content:
For John Skoyles



My daughter made drawings with the pens you sent,
line drawings that suggest the things they represent,
different from any drawings she — at ten — had done,
closer to real art, implying what the mind fills in.
For her mother she made a flower fragile on its stem;
for me, a lion, calm, contained, but not a handsome one.
She drew a lion for me once before, on a get-well card,
and wrote I must be brave even when it’s hard.
Such love is healing — as you know, my friend,
especially when it comes unbidden from our children
despite the flaws they see so vividly in us.
Who can love you as your child does?
Your son so ill, the brutal chemo, his looming loss
owning you now — yet you would be this generous
to think of my child. With the pens you sent
she has made I hope a healing instrument.
evidence: The speaker transforms his own need for support during suffering into recognition of his friend's generosity despite even greater loss ('Your son so ill... yet you would be this generous / to think of my child'), experiencing vicarious gratification through giving gratitude while correctly perceiving the friend's altruistic gesture. The exchange leaves both parties blessed rather than burdened.

author: Sandra Beasley
content:
The Traveler’s Vade Mecum, line 907



The exhibition opened on a rainy Thursday, with cello suite. They hung the paintings to be viewed from both front and back. Luna moths flapped their great green sail-wings.Stingrays flapped their great gray sail-wings. Those visiting the exhibition were encouraged to touch. Captions were available in Braille and audio. The exhibition tasted like cherries. A critic asked if the exhibition was a “facile juxtaposition of   ideals.”The mother of the exhibition calls constantly and the father, never.The exhibition has taken to pouring a little scotch in the coffee. When designing layout, remember it is crucial how a bias cut fits at the exhibition’s hipsand foot traffic turns to the right, not the left. They hung the sculptures to be viewed from both above and below.They painted the walls a shade of “eggshell, minus calcium.”The exhibition did not consider itself an exhibitionistuntil the incident at the east window.The exhibition is very sorry and will refund upon request.Stingrays flapped their great gray sail-wings. Luna moths flapped their great green sail-wings.No matter how short a trip, the exhibition packs two pairs of shoes. The exhibition never knows when it is coming home again.
evidence: The speaker narrates the exhibition—which becomes a stand-in for the self—through fragmented, theatrical images that deflect emotional contact: 'The mother calls constantly and the father, never. The exhibition has taken to pouring a little scotch in the coffee.' Painful personal material (parental abandonment, substance use, exhibitionism) is transformed into playful, entertaining descriptions with dreamlike discontinuity, creating distance through charming catalog rather than integrated experience.

author: Sandra Beasley
content:
The Traveler’s Vade Mecum, line 4234



Baking two parts flour to one part water could stop a bullet. So good soldiers carried their hardtack over their hearts. Break it down with a rifle butt, flood it, fry it in pig fat to make hellfire stew.Gnaw it raw and praise the juice.Does wheat prepare for this as it grows,seeking the light in a half-thawed field?Do stalks know their strength is merely in their number? What is ground downwe name flour in promise that it will be made useful. Otherwise, it’s just dust. Sheet iron crackers. Teeth-dullers.Would you call it starving, if a man dieswith hardtack still tucked in his pocket?Can you call it food, if the bullet comes onlyat the moment he gives in and swallows?
evidence: The speaker contemplates soldiers dying with hardtack in their pockets through detached, philosophical questioning ('Do stalks know their strength...', 'Can you call it food, if...') that maintains observational distance from the human suffering at the poem's core, treating mortality as a conceptual problem about taxonomy and usefulness rather than an emotionally felt tragedy.

author: Scott Cairns
content:
A psalm of Isaak, amid uncommon darkness



O Being both far distant and most near,             O Lover embracing all unlovable, O Tender             Tether binding us together, and binding, yea             and tenderly, Your Person to ourselves,Being both beyond our ken, and kindred, One             whose dire energies invest such clay as ours             with patent animation, O Secret One secreting              life anew into our every tissue moribund,             afresh unto our stale and stalling craft, grant in this obscurity a little light.
evidence: The speaker attributes exaggerated omnipotent qualities to the divine object through escalating superlatives ('Being both far distant and most near,' 'Lover embracing all unlovable,' 'Secret One secreting life anew'), treating the deity as an archaic omniscient, omnibenevolent figure whose merger with the self counteracts internal terror ('amid uncommon darkness'). The idealization serves to manage attachment anxiety through fantasized union ('binding...Your Person to ourselves') while maintaining reverential distance.

author: Steve Gehrke
content:
The ship wherein Theseus and the youth of Athens returned had thirty oars, and was preserved by the Athenians    ...    for they took away the old planks as they decayed, putting in new and stronger timber in their place, insomuch that this ship became a standing example among the philosophers, for the logical question of things that grow; one side holding that the ship remained the same, and the other contending that it was not the same.
— Plutarch, Vita Thesel



The answer of course is that the shipdoesn’t exist, that “ship”is an abstraction, a conception,an imaginary tarp thrownacross the garden of the real.The answer is that the cheappeasantry of things toils all dayin the kingdom of  language,every ship like a casketof words: bulkhead, transom,mast steps
evidence: The speaker converts the ontological question of identity and impermanence into abstract philosophical discourse ('ship is an abstraction, a conception'), delivering existential dissolution ('the ship doesn't exist') in the affectless tone of logical demonstration rather than confronting the emotional weight of decay, loss, and the impermanence it signifies.

author: David Orr
content:
For a birth



The fact of the harp swells into the air,Alien and familiar and entirely too large,An elephant lost in the suburbs,And opens with its cry a strange passageBetween the harp itself, the fragile harp,And the almost guilty knowledgeOf   the stroke of   luck that brought it hereAnd the care with which it must depart.
evidence: The speaker contemplates the harp's arrival with 'almost guilty knowledge / Of the stroke of luck that brought it here / And the care with which it must depart,' spreading anxiety about future loss across the present moment while experiencing proportionate affective distress during realistic planning for the inevitable.

author: Carrie Fountain
content:
For Burn Construction Company



When you were building the i-10 bypass, one of   your dozers, moving earth at the center of a great pit, slipped its thick blade beneath the water table, slicing into the earth’s wet palm, and the silt moistened beneath the huge thing’s tires, and the crew was sent home for the day. Next morning, water filled the pit. Nothing anyone could do to stop it coming. It was a revelation: kidney-shaped, deep green, there between the interstate and the sewage treatment plant. When nothing else worked, you called it a lake and opened it to the public. And we were the public.
evidence: The poem recounts a concrete historical event (accidental lake formation during construction) with documentary clarity and wry irony, without exhibiting psychological defensiveness toward the material.

author: Dorothea Lasky
content:
This is a world where there are monsters
There are monsters everywhere, racoons and skunks
There are possums outside, there are monsters in my bed.
There is one monster. He is my little one.
I talk to my little monster.
I give my little monster some bacon but that does not satisfy him.
I tell him, ssh ssh, don’t growl little monster!
And he growls, oh boy does he growl!
And he wants something from me,
He wants my soul.
And finally giving in, I give him my gleaming soul
And as he eats my gleaming soul, I am one with him
And stare out his eyepits and I see nothing but white
And then I see nothing but fog and the white I had seen before was nothing but fog
And there is nothing but fog out the eyes of monsters.
evidence: The speaker divides experience into all-monster versus self-with-soul categories, then resolves the split not through integration but through complete merger—'I am one with him'—followed by affective obliteration into fog, maintaining the segregated categories (monster/soul, white/fog) without synthesis or ambivalence.

author: John Ashbery
content:
The room I entered was a dream of   this room.
Surely all those feet on the sofa were mine.
The oval portrait
of   a dog was me at an early age.
Something shimmers, something is hushed up.
We had macaroni for lunch every day
except Sunday, when a small quail was induced
to be served to us. Why do I tell you these things?
You are not even here.
