               voyage through death
                               to life upon these shores.
       “10 April 1800—
       Blacks rebellious. Crew uneasy. Our linguist says   
       their moaning is a prayer for death,
       ours and their own. Some try to starve themselves.   
       Lost three this morning leaped with crazy laughter   
       to the waiting sharks, sang as they went under.”Desire, Adventure, Tartar, Ann:
       Standing to America, bringing home   
       black gold, black ivory, black seed.
               Deep in the festering hold thy father lies,   
               of his bones New England pews are made,   
               those are altar lights that were his eyes.
evidence: The speaker adopts a documentary stance, assembling historical materials (ship names, logbook entries, transformed Ariel lyrics) to witness atrocity without positioning a personal psychology defensively toward the content. This is historical testimony, not intrapsychic management.

poem_name: I Was Sleeping Where the Black Oaks Move
author: Louise Erdrich
content:
We watched from the house
as the river grew, helpless
and terrible in its unfamiliar body.   
Wrestling everything into it,
the water wrapped around trees
until their life-hold was broken.
They went down, one by one,
and the river dragged off their covering.
Nests of the herons, roots washed to bones,   
snags of soaked bark on the shoreline:   
a whole forest pulled through the teeth   
of the spillway. Trees surfacing
singly, where the river poured off
into arteries for fields below the reservation.
When at last it was over, the long removal,   
they had all become the same dry wood.   
We walked among them, the branches   
whitening in the raw sun.
Above us drifted herons,
alone, hoarse-voiced, broken,
settling their beaks among the hollows.
Grandpa said, These are the ghosts of the tree people   
moving among us, unable to take their rest.
evidence: The speaker and community watch the flood 'helpless' yet remain present through 'the long removal,' processing catastrophic loss incrementally ('one by one') without flight or collapse. The narrative spreads distress across temporal phases—witnessing, aftermath, walking among wreckage—creating affect-laden preparation for ongoing displacement ('unable to take their rest').

poem_name: The Freaks at Spurgin Road Field
author: Richard Hugo
content:
The dim boy claps because the others clap.
The polite word, handicapped, is muttered in the stands.   
Isn’t it wrong, the way the mind moves back.
One whole day I sit, contrite, dirt, L.A.
Union Station, ’46, sweating through last night.   
The dim boy claps because the others clap.
Score, 5 to 3. Pitcher fading badly in the heat.   
Isn’t it wrong to be or not be spastic?   
Isn’t it wrong, the way the mind moves back.
I’m laughing at a neighbor girl beaten to scream   
by a savage father and I’m ashamed to look.   
The dim boy claps because the others clap.
The score is always close, the rally always short.   
I’ve left more wreckage than a quake.   
Isn’t it wrong, the way the mind moves back.
The afflicted never cheer in unison.
Isn’t it wrong, the way the mind moves back
to stammering pastures where the picnic should have worked.   
The dim boy claps because the others clap.
evidence: The speaker obsessively returns to the refrain 'The dim boy claps because the others clap' after each confession of moral failure ('laughing at a neighbor girl beaten,' 'I've left more wreckage than a quake'), using the image as ritualistic atonement for guilt about past cruelties while the emotional charge remains partially disconnected—'contrite' is stated but the confessions feel catalogued rather than viscerally remorseful.

author: William Meredith
content:
Touching your goodness, I am like a man
Who turns a letter over in his hand
And you might think this was because the hand
Was unfamiliar but, truth is, the man
Has never had a letter from anyone;
And now he is both afraid of what it means
And ashamed because he has no other means
To find out what it says than to ask someone.
His uncle could have left the farm to him,
Or his parents died before he sent them word,
Or the dark girl changed and want him for beloved.
Afraid and letter-proud, he keeps it with him.
What would you call his feeling for the words
That keep him rich and orphaned and beloved?
evidence: The speaker constructs an elaborate allegorical frame (the illiterate man with a letter) to discuss emotional receipt of goodness/love, maintaining theoretical distance through extended metaphor while discussing profoundly charged material—feeling 'rich and orphaned and beloved'—in a tone that remains curiously detached, analytical, posing the final question as intellectual puzzle rather than felt crisis.

author: William Meredith
content:
Limped out of the hot sky a hurt plane,
Held off, held off, whirring pretty pigeon,
Hit then and scuttled to a crooked stop.
The stranger pilot who emerged—this was the seashore,
War came suddenly here—talked to the still mechanics
Who nodded gravely. Flak had done it, he said,
From an enemy ship attacked.
                        They wheeled it with love
Into the dark hangar’s mouth and tended it.
Coffee and cake for the pilot then who sat alone
In the restaurant, reading the numbered sheets
That tell about weather.
                        After, toward dusk,
Mended the stranger plane went back to the sky.
His curly-headed picture, and mother’s and medal’s pictures
Were all we knew of him after he rose again,
Those few electric jewels against the moth and whining sky.
evidence: The speaker recounts a wartime emergency—'War came suddenly here'—with mechanical precision and affective flatness, providing detailed observations of the damaged plane's tending and the pilot's solitary meal while maintaining curiously detached tone. The potentially traumatic encounter is rendered as procedural sequence devoid of emotional resonance, culminating in the distanced aesthetic description of the plane's departure as 'electric jewels against the moth and whining sky' rather than acknowledging the human stakes of combat and survival.

poem_name: For the Anniversary of My Death
author: W. S. Merwin
content:
Every year without knowing it I have passed the day   
When the last fires will wave to me
And the silence will set out
Tireless traveler
Like the beam of a lightless star
Then I will no longer
Find myself in life as in a strange garment
Surprised at the earth
And the love of one woman
And the shamelessness of men
As today writing after three days of rain
Hearing the wren sing and the falling cease
And bowing not knowing to what
evidence: The speaker contemplates passing the unknown anniversary of their future death each year, experiencing modest affective distress ('I will no longer / Find myself in life as in a strange garment / Surprised') while spreading anxiety through self-inoculation with mortality, maintaining both cognitive planning and proportionate emotional engagement with future loss.

author: W. S. Merwin
content:
All these years behind windows
With blind crosses sweeping the tables
And myself tracking over empty ground
Animals I never saw
I with no voice
Remembering names to invent for them
Will any come back will one
Saying yes
Saying look carefully yes
We will meet again
evidence: The speaker spreads anxiety across time through forward-looking questions ('Will any come back will one') while experiencing modest affective distress in the present about future reconnection. The affect-laden planning ('We will meet again') combines realistic worrying about loss with emotional preparation for possible restoration of what has been missed.

author: W. S. Merwin
content:
Matches among other things that were not allowed
never would be
lying high in a cool blue box
that opened in other hands and there they all were
bodies clean and smooth blue heads white crowns
white sandpaper on the sides of the box scoring
fire after fire gone before
I could hear the scratch and flare
when they were over
and catch the smell of the striking
I knew what the match would feel like
lighting
when I was very young
a fire engine came and parked
in the shadow of the big poplar tree
on Fourth Street one night
keeping its engine running
pumping oxygen to the old woman
in the basement
when she died the red lights went on burning
evidence: The speaker recounts childhood experiences of forbidden matches and a death scene with detailed sensory precision ('bodies clean and smooth blue heads white crowns') but maintains curious emotional flatness—describing the woman's death and the fire engine's continuing red lights as pure observation without accessing or expressing grief, fear, or other proportionate affect.

author: Stephen Dunn
content:
The housework, the factory work, the work
that takes from the body
and does not put back.
The white-collar work and the dirt
of its profits, the terrible politeness
of the office worker, the work that robs
the viscera to pay the cool
surfaces of the brain. All the work
that makes love difficult, brings on
sleep, drops the body off
at the liquor cabinet. All the work
that reaches the intestines and sprawls.
And the compulsive work after the work
is done, those unfillable spaces
of the Calvinist, or certain marriage beds.
evidence: The speaker catalogs various forms of work with escalating visceral imagery ('robs the viscera,' 'reaches the intestines and sprawls'), redirecting affect about primary relational disappointments ('makes love difficult,' 'unfillable spaces of certain marriage beds') onto the safer, elaborately detailed target of work itself.

author: Mary Karr
content:
Stare hard enough at the fabric of night,   
and if you're predisposed to dark—let’s say   
the window you’ve picked is a black
postage stamp you spend hours at,
sleepless, drinking gin after the I Love   
Lucy
evidence: The speaker engages in realistic, affect-laden planning for future discomfort—staring sleepless at the window, drinking gin, preparing herself emotionally for dark outcomes through sustained worrying ('if you're predisposed to dark'). This self-inoculation spreads anxiety over time rather than avoiding it, though the gin and sleeplessness suggest the anticipatory work carries significant emotional cost.

author: Siegfried Sassoon
content:
Soldiers are citizens of death's grey land,
Drawing no dividend from time's to-morrows.   
In the great hour of destiny they stand,
Each with his feuds, and jealousies, and sorrows.   
Soldiers are sworn to action; they must win   
Some flaming, fatal climax with their lives.
Soldiers are dreamers; when the guns begin
They think of firelit homes, clean beds and wives.
I see them in foul dug-outs, gnawed by rats,
And in the ruined trenches, lashed with rain,   
Dreaming of things they did with balls and bats,
And mocked by hopeless longing to regain   
Bank-holidays, and picture shows, and spats,
And going to the office in the train.
evidence: The speaker describes soldiers' horrific reality ('foul dug-outs, gnawed by rats,' 'ruined trenches, lashed with rain') in detached, declarative sentences that read like military reports rather than horror testimony. The emotional weight of the soldiers' 'hopeless longing' is catalogued but not felt—their dreams listed with the flatness of inventory ('balls and bats,' 'picture shows, and spats').

author: Siegfried Sassoon
content:
When I have heard small talk about great men
I climb to bed; light my two candles; then
Consider what was said; and put aside
What Such-a-one remarked and Someone-else replied.
They have spoken lightly of my deathless friends,
(Lamps for my gloom, hands guiding where I stumble,)
Quoting, for shallow conversational ends,
What Shelley shrilled, what Blake once wildly muttered ....
How can they use such names and be not humble?
I have sat silent; angry at what they uttered.
The dead bequeathed them life; the dead have said
What these can only memorize and mumble.
evidence: The speaker elevates the dead poets to 'deathless friends' who are 'Lamps for my gloom, hands guiding where I stumble' while simultaneously diminishing living conversationalists who 'can only memorize and mumble' what the idealized figures have truly said, creating stark vertical distance between sacred poets and profane commentators.

author: Siegfried Sassoon
content:
Go your seeking, soul.
Mine the proven path of time’s foretelling.
Yours accordance with some mysteried whole.
I am but your passion-haunted dwelling.
Bring what news you can,
Stranger, loved of body’s humbled heart.
Say one whispered word to mortal man
From that peace whereof he claims you part.
Hither-hence, my guest,
Blood and bone befriend, where you abide
Till withdrawn to share some timeless quest.
I am but the brain that dreamed and died.
evidence: The speaker anatomizes the soul-body relationship in abstract, theoretical terms ('mysteried whole,' 'timeless quest') while describing mortality and separation with striking affective detachment—'I am but the brain that dreamed and died' delivers existential content in the tone of a categorical definition rather than felt dread.

author: Hart Crane
content:
We make our meek adjustments,
Contented with such random consolations
As the wind deposits
In slithered and too ample pockets.
For we can still love the world, who find
A famished kitten on the step, and know
Recesses for it from the fury of the street,
Or warm torn elbow coverts.
We will sidestep, and to the final smirk
Dally the doom of that inevitable thumb
That slowly chafes its puckered index toward us,
Facing the dull squint with what innocence
And what surprise!
And yet these fine collapses are not lies
More than the pirouettes of any pliant cane;
Our obsequies are, in a way, no enterprise.
We can evade you, and all else but the heart:
What blame to us if the heart live on.
The game enforces smirks; but we have seen
The moon in lonely alleys make
A grail of laughter of an empty ash can,
And through all sound of gaiety and quest
Have heard a kitten in the wilderness.
evidence: The speaker transforms humiliation and poverty into playful art ('fine collapses are not lies / More than the pirouettes of any pliant cane'), facing doom with 'innocence / And surprise,' finding transcendent beauty in degradation ('moon in lonely alleys make / A grail of laughter of an empty ash can'), maintaining both awareness of suffering and pleasure in the transformation.

author: Heather McHugh
content:
You take a rock, your hand is hard.
You raise your eyes, and there's a pair
of small beloveds, caught in pails.
The monocle and eyepatch correspond.
You take a glove, your hand is soft.
The ocean floor was done
in lizardskin. Around a log or snag
the surface currents run
like lumber about a knot. A boat
is bent to sea—we favor the medium
we're in, our shape's
around us. It takes time.
At night, the bed alive, what
teller of truth could tell
the two apart? Lover, beloved,
hope is command. Your hand
is given, when you take a hand.
evidence: The speaker converts potentially intimate experiences (hand-holding, lovers in bed) into abstract propositions and general principles ('we favor the medium we're in, our shape's around us'), discussing attachment and reciprocity ('Your hand is given, when you take a hand') in detached, theoretical tones that strip the material of emotional immediacy.

author: Theodore Roethke
content:
I wake to sleep, and take my waking slow.   
I feel my fate in what I cannot fear.   
I learn by going where I have to go.
We think by feeling. What is there to know?   
I hear my being dance from ear to ear.   
I wake to sleep, and take my waking slow.
Of those so close beside me, which are you?   
God bless the Ground!   I shall walk softly there,   
And learn by going where I have to go.
Light takes the Tree; but who can tell us how?   
The lowly worm climbs up a winding stair;   
I wake to sleep, and take my waking slow.
Great Nature has another thing to do   
To you and me; so take the lively air,   
And, lovely, learn by going where to go.
This shaking keeps me steady. I should know.   
What falls away is always. And is near.   
I wake to sleep, and take my waking slow.   
I learn by going where I have to go.
evidence: The speaker distributes anxiety across time through incremental acceptance ('I wake to sleep, and take my waking slow'), maintaining affective engagement with mortality ('I feel my fate') while using the villanelle's recursive structure to rehearse and prepare emotionally. The gradual, iterative process ('learn by going where I have to go') represents realistic planning for future discomfort through small anticipatory doses, keeping idea, affect, and object—death—in conscious mind.

author: Rita Dove
content:
Although it is night, I sit in the bathroom, waiting.
Sweat prickles behind my knees, the baby-breasts are alert.
Venetian blinds slice up the moon; the tiles quiver in pale strips.
Then they come, the three seal men with eyes as round
As dinner plates and eyelashes like sharpened tines.
They bring the scent of licorice. One sits in the washbowl,
One on the bathtub edge; one leans against the door.
“Can you feel it yet?” they whisper.
I don’t know what to say, again. They chuckle,
Patting their sleek bodies with their hands.
“Well, maybe next time.” And they rise,
Glittering like pools of ink under moonlight,
And vanish. I clutch at the ragged holes
They leave behind, here at the edge of darkness.
Night rests like a ball of fur on my tongue.
evidence: The speaker recounts a pubescent bodily event (menarche) through dreamlike fantasy of three seal men who visit and ask 'Can you feel it yet?'—transforming overwhelming physiological awakening into theatrical, detached narrative populated by imaginary beings. The pose is curious witnessing ('I sit...waiting') rather than emotional inhabitance, with vivid fantasy deflecting direct engagement with frightening bodily change.

author: Rita Dove
content:
I was ill, lying on my bed of old papers,
when you came with white rabbits in your arms;
and the doves scattered upwards, flying to mothers,
and the snails sighed under their baggage of stone . . .
Now your tongue grows like celery between us:
Because of our love-cries, cabbage darkens in its nest;
the cauliflower thinks of her pale, plump children
and turns greenish-white in a light like the ocean’s.
I was sick, fainting in the smell of teabags,
when you came with tomatoes, a good poetry.
I am being wooed. I am being conquered
by a cliff of limestone that leaves chalk on my breasts.
evidence: The speaker transforms erotic and bodily experience ('love-cries,' 'conquered,' 'chalk on my breasts') into elaborate vegetable metaphors (celery tongue, cabbage darkening, cauliflower children) and frames the beloved's arrival with tomatoes as 'a good poetry'—channeling sexual intensity into aesthetic imagery with real sensory consequences.

author: Rita Dove
content:
Nothing nastier than a white person! 
She mutters as she irons alterations
in the backroom of Charlotte’s Dress Shoppe.   
The steam rising from a cranberry wool   
comes alive with perspiration
and stale Evening of Paris.Swamp she born from, swamp
she swallow, swamp she got to sink again.
evidence: The speaker redirects rage and frustration from structural oppression onto 'a white person' as generalized target while laboring in subordinate position, with affect ('Nothing nastier') remaining conscious but attached to racial substitute rather than economic exploitation or specific power relations.

author: Stephen Dobyns
content:
I steal your mailbox, leave
gum on your sidewalk. I
seduce your sister, ignore your wife.
I tear one page from each of your books.
I convince you that I am your friend.
*
When people ask about you,
I shake my head. When they
tell about you, I nod.
*
Today, I hang myself
from a greased flagpole
outside your picture window.
Yesterday, I stole your curtains.
evidence: The speaker directly addresses 'you' with a catalog of petty sabotages ('I steal your mailbox, leave gum on your sidewalk') culminating in suicide as spectacle ('I hang myself from a greased flagpole outside your picture window'), explicitly framing self-destruction as vindictive theater designed to punish the target—the core of 'Look what you made me do!'

author: David Ferry
evidence: The speaker divides self into all-good and all-bad versions without integration: 'When I was brave he was afraid; / He told the truth, I lied'—maintaining contradictory moral assignments to what are ostensibly two halves of one person, organized into clear opposing categories (brave/afraid, liar/truth-teller, sweet/bitter) that never blend.

author: David Ferry
evidence: The speaker explicitly positions himself as 'like being a baby' in relationship to parental presences that hover protectively, regressing to infantile dependency ('the crib of knowing') while waiting passively to 'find out / Whatever is going to happen.' The anticipatory element appears in modest worry about future ('what the day / Will bring') ending in prayer for safety.

author: Amy Gerstler
content:
The dark that’s gathering strength
these days is submissive,
kinky, silken, willing;
stretched taut as a trampoline.
World events rattle by like circus
trains we wave at occasionally,
as striped, homed and spotted
heads poke out their windows.
Feels like I’m wearing a corset,
though I haven’t a stitch on.
Burn the place setting I ate from,
OK? and destroy the easy chair
I languished in. Let birds
unravel my lingerie
for nesting materials.
Fingers poised on the piano keys,
I can’t think what to play.
A dirge, a fugue?
What, exactly, are crimes
against nature
evidence: The speaker describes catastrophic world events ('Doomsday,' 'World events rattle by like circus trains') with theatrical, playful imagery (striped, horned animals) while maintaining curious detachment ('I can't think what to play'). Physical sensations are noted ('Feels like I'm wearing a corset') yet disconnected from emotional urgency, transforming apocalyptic material into aesthetic puzzlement.

author: David Ignatow
content:
Whatever we do, whether we light
strangers’ cigarettes—it may turn out
to be a detective wanting to know who is free   
with a light on a lonely street nights—
or whether we turn away and get a knife
planted between our shoulders for our discourtesy;   
whatever we do—whether we marry for love   
and wake up to find love is a task,
or whether for convenience to find love   
must be won over, or we are desperate—
whatever we do; save by dying,
and there too we are caught,
by being planted too close to our parents.
evidence: The speaker catalogs emotionally fraught scenarios (violence, marital disappointment, death) in detached, theoretical syntax ('whatever we do, whether...or whether') resembling a logic proof, delivering potentially devastating content—knives between shoulders, love as task, being trapped even in death—in the affectless tone of a weather report on the psyche.

author: David Ignatow
content:
I am looking for a past
I can rely on
in order to look to death
with equanimity.
What was given me:
my mother’s largeness
to protect me,
my father’s regularity
in coming home from work
at night, his opening the door
silently and smiling,
pleased to be back
and the lights on
in all the rooms
through which I could run
freely or sit at ease
at table and do my homework
undisturbed: love arranged
as order directed at the next day.
Going to bed was a journey.
evidence: The speaker explicitly states 'I am looking for a past / I can rely on / in order to look to death / with equanimity'—preparing emotionally for mortality by taking inventory of childhood security, spreading existential anxiety through deliberate, affect-laden memory work rather than avoidance.

author: David Ignatow
content:
For Harvey Shapiro



I stand and listen, head bowed,
to my inner complaint.
Persons passing by think
I am searching for a lost coin.
You’re fired, I yell inside
after an especially bad episode.
I’m letting you go without notice
or terminal pay. You just lost
another chance to make good.
But then I watch myself standing at the exit,
depressed and about to leave,
and wave myself back in wearily,
for who else could I get in my place
to do the job in dark, airless conditions?
evidence: The speaker maintains dual awareness—simultaneously experiencing 'inner complaint' and observing himself 'standing at the exit, depressed'—with metacognitive distance ('I watch myself') that transforms harsh self-criticism into wry recognition of inescapable self-employment: 'who else could I get in my place / to do the job in dark, airless conditions?'

author: David Ignatow
content:
I killed a fly
and laid my weapon next to it
as one lays the weapon of a dead hero
beside his body—the fly
that tried to mount the window
to its top; that was born out of a swamp
to die in a bold effort beyond itself,
and I am the one who brought it to an end.
Tired of the day and with night coming on
I lay my body down beside the fly.
evidence: The speaker transforms an act of killing into an elaborate ceremony of respect and identification ('laid my weapon next to it / as one lays the weapon of a dead hero'), correctly attributing to the fly what the speaker desires—to be honored for effort and struggle—while finding vicarious gratification in giving the fly the dignity the speaker seeks. The final gesture ('I lay my body down beside the fly') completes the altruistic stance, where serving the fly's posthumous honor allows the speaker to partly gratify their own need for recognition of daily exhaustion ('Tired of the day').

author: Babette Deutsch
content:
Fawns in the winter wood
Who feel their horns, and leap,
Swans whom the bleakening mood
Of evening stirs from sleep,
Tall flowers that unfurl
As a moth, driven, flies,
Flowers with the breasts of a girl
And sea-cold eyes.
The bare bright mirrors glow
For their enchanted shapes.
Each is a flame, and so,
Like flame, escapes.
evidence: The speaker describes ballet students through aesthetic metaphor (fawns, swans, flowers, flames) without psychological conflict or defensive operation—this is formal ekphrasis, not defense.

author: Babette Deutsch
content:
Content that now the bleeding bone be swept
Out of her reach, she lay upon her side.
In a blonde void sunk deep, she slept, she slept
Bland as a child, slept, breathing like a bride.
Color of noons that shimmer as they sing
Above the dunes, her sandy flanks heaved slow.
Between her paws curled inward, billowing
Waves of desert silence seemed to flow.
The crowd was gone, the bars were gone, the cage
Thinned into air, the sawdust and the fleas
Winnowed by sleep to nothing. After food,
Absence possessed her: bliss keener than rage,
If slumber’s prisoner at a bound could seize
This ghostly freedom, lapping it like blood.
evidence: The lioness retreats from brutal reality (bleeding bone, cage, bars, crowd) into altered consciousness where 'Absence possessed her'—the bars and cage 'thinned into air,' creating discontinuity between waking captivity and sleep's 'ghostly freedom,' transforming dangerous material into something playful ('bland as a child') while the speaker renders this fragmentation aesthetically.

author: Babette Deutsch
content:
What do we need for love—a midnight fire
Flinging itself by fistfuls up the chimney
In soft bright snatches? Do we need the snow,
Gentle as silence, covering the scars
Of weeks of hunger, years of shabby having?
Summer or winter? A heaven of stars? A room?
The smiling mouth, the sadness of desire
Are everywhere the same. If lovers go
Along an unknown road, they find no less
What is familiar. Let them stay at home,
And all will still be strange. This they know
Who with each heartbeat fight the fear of change.
evidence: The speaker catalogs emotional questions in detached, rhetorical syntax ('What do we need for love—a midnight fire... Do we need the snow') without answering or inhabiting them, converting intimate longing into abstract propositions ('The smiling mouth, the sadness of desire / Are everywhere the same') delivered as philosophical truths rather than felt experience.

author: Babette Deutsch
content:
If you press a stone with your finger,
Sir Isaac Newton observed,
The finger is also
Pressed by the stone.
But can a woman, pressed by memory’s finger,
In the deep night, alone,
Of her softness move
The airy thing
That presses upon her
With the whole weight of love? This
Sir Isaac said nothing of.
evidence: The speaker frames profound emotional suffering ('pressed by memory's finger / In the deep night, alone' with 'the whole weight of love') through the distancing apparatus of Newtonian physics, converting intimate pain into abstract theoretical inquiry ('Sir Isaac said nothing of'). The affect-laden experience is acknowledged cognitively but delivered in detached, observational tones resembling a scientific report.

author: Barbara Guest
content:
The simple contact with a wooden spoon and the word   
recovered itself, began to spread as grass, forced   
as it lay sprawling to consider the monument where   
patience looked at grief, where warfare ceased   
eyes curled outside themes to search the paper   
now gleaming and potent, wise and resilient, word   
entered its continent eager to find another as   
capable as a thorn. The nearest possession would   
house them both, they being then two might glide   
into this house and presently create a rather larger   
mansion filled with spoons and condiments, gracious
as a newly laid table where related objects might gather   
to enjoy the interplay of gravity upon facetious hints,   
the chocolate dish presuming an endowment, the ladle   
of galactic rhythm primed as a relish dish, curved   
knives, finger bowls, morsel carriages words might   
choose and savor before swallowing so much was the   
sumptuousness and substance of a rented house where words   
placed dressing gowns as rosemary entered their scent   
percipient as elder branches in the night where words   
gathered, warped, then straightened, marking new wands.
evidence: The poem explores the autonomous life and associative movement of words themselves as they 'spread as grass,' 'enter continents,' and 'gather, warp, then straighten'—a formal meditation on language's self-generative capacity rather than a psychological stance toward conflict.

author: Barbara Guest
content:
At sunset from the top of the stair watching
the castle mallets wrenched from their socket
fell from ambush into flame flew into hiding;
above the stoneware a latch like muscle hid
the green; he stood waist high under the rapt
ceiling and hanged the sparrow; where the kitchen
had been a mirror of eggs served in a tumbler he
saw the ring when a lancet pierced and threw it.
In a basket and lowered it where sails enter
the harbor over a parchment like dominoes;
the petrel-like eyelash.
To the sun and its rites were pulled the dried
banners; they flew past the ruins the tower
and window where ivory guided the mist on his back;
he rubbed his eyes and counted them kneeling
wrinkled as grass.A ghost in their nostrils put a heel at their
forehead; they saw only the moon as it
fasted.
evidence: The poem presents a surrealist landscape of fragmented images—castle mallets, hanged sparrows, lancets, petrels—without a coherent psychological stance toward distress or conflict; it functions as formal experiment with dreamlike juxtaposition rather than defensive operation.

author: Daryl Hine
content:
Echo that loved hid within a wood
Would to herself rehearse her weary woe:
O, she cried, and all the rest unsaid
Identical came back in sorry echo.
Echo for the fix that she was in
Invisible, distraught by mocking passion,
Passionate, ignored, as good as dumb,
Employed that O unchanged in repetition.
Shun love if you suspect that he shuns you,
Use with him no reproaches whatsoever.
Ever you knew, supposing him to know
No melody from which you might recover-
Cover your ears, dear Echo, do not hear.
Here is no supplication but your own,
Only your sighs return upon the air
Ere their music from the mouth be gone.
evidence: The speaker counsels Echo to 'Cover your ears, dear Echo, do not hear' and 'Shun love if you suspect that he shuns you, / Use with him no reproaches whatsoever'—consciously postponing emotional engagement ('do not hear') while maintaining full awareness of the painful reality ('Here is no supplication but your own'). The affect remains present ('weary woe,' 'mocking passion') but is held at bay through deliberate choice rather than unconscious expulsion.

author: Louis Untermeyer
content:
The brain forgets but the blood will remember.
There, when the play of sense is over,
The last, low spark in the darkest chamber
Will hold all there is of love and lover.
The war of words, the life-long quarrel
Of self against self will resolve into nothing;
Less than the chain of berry-red coral
Crying against the dead black of her clothing.
What has the brain that it hopes to last longer?
The blood will take from forgotten violence,
The groping, the break of her voice in anger.
There will be left only color and silence.
These will remain, these will go searching
Your veins for life when the flame of life smolders;
The night that you two saw the mountains marching
Up against dawn with the stars on their shoulders;
The jetting poplars’ arrested fountains
As you drew her under them, easing her pain;
The notes, not the words, of a half-finished sentence;
The music, the silence. . . . These will remain.
evidence: The speaker theorizes about memory and emotion in detached, abstract terms ('The brain forgets but the blood will remember') while discussing love and violent quarrel as if observing psychic mechanisms from outside, converting potentially painful relational material into a cognitive map of what endures versus what dissolves.

author: Kay Ryan
content:
If it please God,   
let less happen.   
Even out Earth's   
rondure, flatten   
Eiger, blanden
the Grand Canyon.
Make valleys
slightly higher,   
widen fissures   
to arable land,   
remand your
terrible glaciers   
and silence
their calving,   
halving or doubling
all geographical features   
toward the mean.
Unlean against our hearts.   
Withdraw your grandeur   
from these parts.
evidence: The speaker petitions God to flatten grandeur and 'blanden' dramatic features, transforming overwhelming magnificence (which terrifies) into its opposite—safe mediocrity—while the excessive insistence on reduction ('halving or doubling toward the mean,' 'Withdraw your grandeur') reveals strain in maintaining this preference for blandness against underlying awe or terror of divine excess.

poem_name: For Christmas Day: Hark! the Herald Angels Sing
author: Charles Wesley
content:
Hark! the herald Angels sing,Glory to the new-born King,Peace on earth and mercy mild,God and sinner reconcil’d.
   Hark! the herald Angels sing,
   Glory to the new-born King.Joyful all ye nations rise,Join the triumph of the skies,With the angelic host proclaim,Christ is born in Bethlehem.
   Hark! the herald Angels sing,
   Glory to the new-born King.Christ by highest Heaven ador’d,Christ the everlasting Lord!Late in time behold him come,Offspring of a virgin’s womb.
   Hark! the herald Angels sing,
   Glory to the new-born King.Veiled in flesh the Godhead see,Hail, the incarnate Deity,Pleased as Man with man to dwell,Jesus our Immanuel!
   Hark! the herald Angels sing,
   Glory to the new-born King.Hail the Heaven-born Prince of Peace!Hail the Sun of Righteousness!Light and life to all he brings,Risen with healing in his wings.
   Hark! the herald Angels sing,
   Glory to the new-born King.Mild he lays his glory by,Born that man no more may die,Born to raise the sons of earth,Born to give them second birth.
   Hark! the herald Angels sing,
   Glory to the new-born King.
evidence: The speaker adopts a communal, celebratory stance calling others to join in worship of the incarnation—'Joyful all ye nations rise, Join the triumph of the skies'—without discernible psychological conflict or defensive management of anxiety.

author: Louise Bogan
content:
She has attained the permanence
She dreamed of, where old stones lie sunning.
Untended stalks blow over her
Even and swift, like young men running.
Always in the heart she loved
Others had lived,—she heard their laughter.
She lies where none has lain before,
Where certainly none will follow after.
evidence: The speaker describes profound emotional material—a woman's lifelong loneliness, her unrequited longing, her death—in language that reads like a cemetery inscription: formal, measured, stripped of felt emotion. The syntactic structure maintains observational distance ('She has attained,' 'She lies where') while acknowledging psychologically charged content (loving a heart occupied by others, dying alone) in tones resembling a 'weather report on the psyche.'

author: Louise Bogan
content:
I had come to the house, in a cave of trees,
Facing a sheer sky.
Everything moved,—a bell hung ready to strike,
Sun and reflection wheeled by.
When the bare eyes were before me
And the hissing hair,
Held up at a window, seen through a door.
The stiff bald eyes, the serpents on the forehead
Formed in the air.
This is a dead scene forever now.
Nothing will ever stir.
The end will never brighten it more than this,
Nor the rain blur.
The water will always fall, and will not fall,
And the tipped bell make no sound.
The grass will always be growing for hay
Deep on the ground.
And I shall stand here like a shadow
Under the great balanced day,
My eyes on the yellow dust, that was lifting in the wind,
And does not drift away.
evidence: The speaker describes encountering Medusa and becoming frozen in a 'dead scene forever now,' transformed into a 'shadow' watching dust that 'does not drift away'—a traumatic petrification rendered with dreamlike distance and theatrical detachment, the terror transformed into aesthetic stillness where 'water will always fall, and will not fall.'

author: Louise Bogan
content:
Women have no wilderness in them,
They are provident instead,
Content in the tight hot cell of their hearts
To eat dusty bread.
They do not see cattle cropping red winter grass,
They do not hear
Snow water going down under culverts
Shallow and clear.
They wait, when they should turn to journeys,
They stiffen, when they should bend.
They use against themselves that benevolence
To which no man is friend.
They cannot think of so many crops to a field
Or of clean wood cleft by an axe.
Their love is an eager meaninglessness
Too tense, or too lax.
They hear in every whisper that speaks to them
A shout and a cry.
As like as not, when they take life over their door-sills
They should let it go by.
evidence: The speaker constructs a totalizing accusation against 'women' as a category, attributing to them constriction, stiffness, and inability ('They wait, when they should turn to journeys / They stiffen, when they should bend'), presenting these limitations as objective observations while the vehemence and absolute certainty ('Women have no wilderness in them') suggest misattribution of the speaker's own disavowed feelings about constraint, passivity, or self-limitation onto external feminine figures.

author: Robert Browning
content:
At the midnight in the silence of the sleep-time,
   When you set your fancies free,Will they pass to where—by death, fools think, imprisoned—Low he lies who once so loved you, whom you loved so,
        —Pity me?
 Oh to love so, be so loved, yet so mistaken!
   What had I on earth to doWith the slothful, with the mawkish, the unmanly?Like the aimless, helpless, hopeless, did I drivel
        —Being—who?
 One who never turned his back but marched breast forward,
   Never doubted clouds would break,Never dreamed, though right were worsted, wrong would triumph,Held we fall to rise, are baffled to fight better,
        Sleep to wake.
 No, at noonday in the bustle of man's work-time
   Greet the unseen with a cheer!Bid him forward, breast and back as either should be,"Strive and thrive!" cry "Speed,—fight on, fare ever
        There as here!"
evidence: The speaker prepares himself for death by spreading anxiety across time ('Will they pass to where...imprisoned'), maintains both affect and idea in consciousness ('Oh to love so, be so loved, yet so mistaken!'), and realistically plans for future discomfort through self-inoculation with modest affective distress about mortality while preserving forward-looking stance.

poem_name: A Boat Beneath a Sunny Sky
author: Lewis Carroll
content:
A boat beneath a sunny sky,
Lingering onward dreamily
In an evening of July —
Children three that nestle near,
Eager eye and willing ear,
Pleased a simple tale to hear —
Long has paled that sunny sky:
Echoes fade and memories die:
Autumn frosts have slain July.
Still she haunts me, phantomwise,
Alice moving under skies
Never seen by waking eyes.
Children yet, the tale to hear,
Eager eye and willing ear,
Lovingly shall nestle near.
In a Wonderland they lie,
Dreaming as the days go by,
Dreaming as the summers die:
Ever drifting down the stream —
Lingering in the golden gleam —
Life, what is it but a dream?
evidence: The speaker transforms grief over time's passage and lost children into formal poetic achievement (acrostic spelling 'ALICE PLEASANCE LIDDELL'), channeling mourning into socially valued art. 'Still she haunts me, phantomwise' acknowledges the ongoing attachment while 'Life, what is it but a dream?' achieves philosophical distance that maintains emotional intensity through creative form.

poem_name: A Coronet for his Mistress, Philosophy
author: George Chapman
content:
Muses that sing love's sensual empery,And lovers kindling your enraged firesAt Cupid's bonfires burning in the eye,Blown with the empty breath of vain desires;You that prefer the painted cabinetBefore the wealthy jewels it doth store ye,That all your joys in dying figures set,And stain the living substance of your glory;Abjure those joys, abhor their memory,And let my love the honour'd subject beOf love, and honour's complete history.Your eyes were never yet let in to see
   The majesty and riches of the mind,
   But dwell in darkness; for your god is blind.
evidence: Speaker condemns sensual love poets with moralistic fervor while claiming 'love, and honour's complete history' as his proper subject—discussing love in abstractly elevated terms ('majesty and riches of the mind') that conspicuously drain erotic content of its affective charge, delivering what should be passionate in the detached tone of philosophical superiority.

author: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
content:
"How seldom, friend! a good great man inherits
   Honour or wealth with all his worth and pains!It sounds like stories from the land of spiritsIf any man obtain that which he merits
   Or any merit that which he obtains."REPLY TO THE ABOVE   For shame, dear friend, renounce this canting strain!What would'st thou have a good great man obtain?Place? titles? salary? a gilded chain?Or throne of corses which his sword had slain?Greatness and goodness are not means, but ends!Hath he not always treasures, always friends,The good great man? three treasures, LOVE, and LIGHT,
   And CALM THOUGHTS, regular as infant's breath:And three firm friends, more sure than day and night,
   HIMSELF, his MAKER, and the ANGEL DEATH!
evidence: The speaker dismisses the friend's complaint about good men lacking worldly rewards by constructing elaborate justifications that reframe the absence of material success as irrelevant—'Greatness and goodness are not means, but ends!'—converting what might be experienced as deprivation or injustice into a philosophical principle that makes inner treasures (Love, Light, Calm Thoughts) categorically superior to external honors.

poem_name: Divine Epigrams: On the Miracle of the Multiplied Loaves
author: Richard Crashaw
content:
See here an easy feast that knows no wound,
     That under hunger’s teeth will needs be sound;A subtle harvest of unbounded bread,
     What would ye more? Here food itself is fed.
evidence: The speaker transforms alimentary/oral drive into devotional aesthetic contemplation, channeling primitive hunger and incorporation fantasies into socially valued theological poetry. The miracle becomes 'easy feast' and 'subtle harvest'—maintaining emotional intensity while achieving religious-literary transformation ('Here food itself is fed') with real artistic consequence.

author: Emily Dickinson
content:
The Bustle in a House
The Morning after Death
Is solemnest of industries
Enacted opon Earth –
The Sweeping up the Heart
And putting Love away
We shall not want to use again
Until Eternity –
evidence: The speaker describes profound grief—'Sweeping up the Heart / And putting Love away'—using domestic, procedural language ('Bustle,' 'industries,' 'Enacted') that strips away emotional resonance, converting mourning into a task resembling housekeeping with affectless precision.

author: Queen Elizabeth I
content:
I grieve and dare not show my discontent,
I love and yet am forced to seem to hate,
I do, yet dare not say I ever meant,
I seem stark mute but inwardly do prate.
I am and not, I freeze and yet am burned,
Since from myself another self I turned.
My care is like my shadow in the sun,
Follows me flying, flies when I pursue it,
Stands and lies by me, doth what I have done.
His too familiar care doth make me rue it.
No means I find to rid him from my breast,
Till by the end of things it be supprest.
Some gentler passion slide into my mind,
For I am soft and made of melting snow;
Or be more cruel, love, and so be kind.
Let me or float or sink, be high or low.
Or let me live with some more sweet content,
Or die and so forget what love ere meant.
evidence: The speaker demonstrates complete sequestration of affect from ideation: 'I grieve and dare not show my discontent, I love and yet am forced to seem to hate'—she possesses full cognitive awareness of her emotions but maintains radical disconnection between internal feeling and external expression, reporting her emotional predicament in paradoxical formulations ('I am and not, I freeze and yet am burned') that acknowledge feelings exist while severing their behavioral manifestation.

author: Robert Frost
content:
Others taunt me with having knelt at well-curbs
Always wrong to the light, so never seeing
Deeper down in the well than where the water
Gives me back in a shining surface picture
Me myself in the summer heaven godlike
Looking out of a wreath of fern and cloud puffs.Once, when trying with chin against a well-curb,
I discerned, as I thought, beyond the picture,
Through the picture, a something white, uncertain,
Something more of the depths—and then I lost it.
Water came to rebuke the too clear water.
One drop fell from a fern, and lo, a ripple
Shook whatever it was lay there at bottom,
Blurred it, blotted it out. What was that whiteness?
Truth? A pebble of quartz? For once, then, something.
evidence: The speaker frames a moment of potential emotional or spiritual insight ('Truth?') in detached, epistemological language, converting the loss of revelation into a philosophical question about perception and knowledge. The affect surrounding failure to grasp 'something white, uncertain' is evacuated through conceptual interrogation rather than felt disappointment.

author: Thomas Hardy
content:
At last I entered a long dark gallery,
   Catacomb-lined; and ranged at the side
   Were the bodies of men from far and wideWho, motion past, were nevertheless not dead.
   "The sense of waiting here strikes strong;Everyone's waiting, waiting, it seems to me;
   What are you waiting for so long? —
       What is to happen?" I said."O we are waiting for one called God," said they,
   "(Though by some the Will, or Force, or Laws;
   And, vaguely, by some, the Ultimate Cause;)Waiting for him to see us before we are clay.
   Yes; waiting, waiting, for God to know it." ...
   "To know what?" questioned I."To know how things have been going on earth and below it:
   It is clear he must know some day."
   I thereon asked them why."Since he made us humble pioneersOf himself in consciousness of Life's tears,It needs no mighty prophecyTo tell that what he could mindlessly showHis creatures, he himself will know."By some still close-cowled mysteryWe have reached feeling faster than he,But he will overtake us anon,
   If the world goes on."
evidence: The speaker confronts ultimate existential dread—humanity waiting in catacombs to be seen by an absent God—yet delivers this vision in detached, philosophical tones ('the Ultimate Cause,' 'close-cowled mystery'), treating cosmic abandonment as a theoretical problem requiring logical explanation rather than an occasion for terror or grief.

author: George Herbert
content:
A broken ALTAR, Lord, thy servant rears,Made of a heart and cemented with tears;
     Whose parts are as thy hand did frame;
     No workman's tool hath touch'd the same.
           A HEART alone
           Is such a stone,
           As nothing but
           Thy pow'r doth cut.
           Wherefore each part
           Of my hard heart
           Meets in this frame
           To praise thy name.
     That if I chance to hold my peace,
     These stones to praise thee may not cease.Oh, let thy blessed SACRIFICE be mine,And sanctify this ALTAR to be thine.
evidence: The speaker constructs an elaborate ritual offering—a broken altar made of heart and tears—to atone for the 'hard heart' and capacity to 'hold my peace' (cease praise), framing the entire poem as compensatory sacrifice that magically erases the transgression of insufficient devotion through symbolic architecture.

author: Gerard Manley Hopkins
content:
felled 1879



My aspens dear, whose airy cages quelled,   Quelled or quenched in leaves the leaping sun,   All felled, felled, are all felled;     Of a fresh and following folded rank                 Not spared, not one                 That dandled a sandalled          Shadow that swam or sank On meadow & river & wind-wandering weed-winding bank.            O if we but knew what we do          When we delve or hew —      Hack and rack the growing green!           Since country is so tender      To touch, her being só slender,      That, like this sleek and seeing ball      But a prick will make no eye at all,      Where we, even where we mean                  To mend her we end her,             When we hew or delve: After-comers cannot guess the beauty been.   Ten or twelve, only ten or twelve      Strokes of havoc unselve            The sweet especial scene,      Rural scene, a rural scene,      Sweet especial rural scene.
evidence: The speaker retrospectively constructs an explanatory framework ('O if we but knew what we do') that reframes human destructiveness as ignorance rather than deliberate harm, concluding that even constructive intentions ('where we mean / To mend her we end her') inevitably cause damage—transforming culpability into inevitable tragic consequence of human nature's relationship to 'tender' nature.

author: Gerard Manley Hopkins
evidence: The speaker attributes omnipotent, omnibenevolent qualities to divine presence ('charged with the grandeur of God,' 'Holy Ghost... broods with warm breast') to counteract existential terror about human degradation and spiritual blindness ('all is seared with trade; bleared, smeared with toil'). The total conviction in protective divine perfection despite acknowledged devastation suggests archaic merger with an idealized omnipotent figure.

poem_name: Cynthia's Revels: Queen and huntress, chaste and fair
author: Ben Jonson
content:
Queen and huntress, chaste and fair,Now the sun is laid to sleep,Seated in thy silver chairState in wonted manner keep:
     Hesperus entreats thy light,
     Goddess excellently bright.Earth, let not thy envious shadeDare itself to interpose;Cynthia's shining orb was madeHeaven to clear when day did close:
     Bless us then with wished sight,
     Goddess excellently bright.Lay thy bow of pearl apartAnd thy crystal-shining quiver;Give unto the flying hartSpace to breathe, how short soever:
     Thou that mak'st a day of night,
     Goddess excellently bright.
evidence: The speaker attributes exaggerated perfection and omnipotence to Cynthia through superlatives ('excellently bright,' 'chaste and fair') and cosmic powers ('mak'st a day of night,' 'Heaven to clear'), entreating the goddess to maintain her elevated state while organizing the entire poem around devotional petition to this idealized figure.

author: Walter Savage Landor
content:
Child of a day, thou knowest not
     The tears that overflow thy urn,The gushing eyes that read thy lot,
     Nor, if thou knewest, couldst return!And why the wish! the pure and blest
     Watch like thy mother o'er thy sleep.O peaceful night! O envied rest!
     Thou wilt not ever see her weep.
evidence: The speaker converts grief into philosophical consolation ('why the wish!'), constructing reasons the child's death is actually beneficial ('peaceful night! O envied rest!'), transforming loss into blessing by deciding the dead infant is spared from witnessing maternal weeping—a retrospective sweetening of unbearable tragedy.

poem_name: Dying Speech of an Old Philosopher
author: Walter Savage Landor
evidence: The speaker transforms primitive instinctual energy into passionate engagement with socially valued pursuits (Nature, Art) while maintaining pleasure and intensity—'I warm'd both hands before the fire of Life'—achieving creative integration of opposing forces (desire/constraint, passion/mortality) without loss of emotional authenticity.

author: Walter Savage Landor
content:
Mild is the parting year, and sweet
     The odour of the falling spray;Life passes on more rudely fleet,
     And balmless is its closing day.I wait its close, I court its gloom,
     But mourn that never must there fallOr on my breast or on my tomb
     The tear that would have soothed it all.
evidence: The speaker consciously postpones death ('I wait its close, I court its gloom') while maintaining full awareness of the longing for consolation ('mourn that never must there fall...The tear that would have soothed it all')—all components remain in consciousness with finite endpoint.

poem_name: Mother, I cannot Mind my Wheel
author: Walter Savage Landor
evidence: The speaker constructs post-hoc justification for trusting the lover ('He always said my eyes were blue, / And often swore my lips were sweet') to explain away doubt, converting flattery into logical proof of fidelity while the opening lines' physical distress suggests unacknowledged emotional pain.

poem_name: Song to Amarantha, that she would Dishevel her Hair
author: Richard Lovelace
content:
Amarantha sweet and fairAh braid no more that shining hair!
   As my curious hand or eyeHovering round thee let it fly.
   Let it fly as unconfin’dAs its calm ravisher, the wind,
   Who hath left his darling th’East,To wanton o’er that spicy nest.
   Ev’ry tress must be confestBut neatly tangled at the best;
   Like a clue of golden thread,Most excellently ravelled.
   Do not then wind up that lightIn ribands, and o’er-cloud in night;
   Like the sun in’s early ray,But shake your head and scatter day.
   See ’tis broke! Within this groveThe bower, and the walks of love,
   Weary lie we down and rest,And fan each other’s panting breast.
   Here we’ll strip and cool our fireIn cream below, in milk-baths higher:
   And when all wells are drawn dry,I’ll drink a tear out of thine eye,
   Which our very joys shall leaveThat sorrows thus we can deceive;
   Or our very sorrows weep,That joys so ripe, so little keep.
evidence: The speaker transforms erotic impulse into socially valued aesthetic courtship poetry, maintaining passionate intensity while channeling desire through elaborate metaphors of ravishment by wind, golden thread, and scattered sunlight—culminating in the sophisticated paradox that tears from joy and sorrow become interchangeable.

author: Claude McKay
content:
Although she feeds me bread of bitterness,
And sinks into my throat her tiger’s tooth,
Stealing my breath of life, I will confess
I love this cultured hell that tests my youth.
Her vigor flows like tides into my blood,
Giving me strength erect against her hate,
Her bigness sweeps my being like a flood.
Yet, as a rebel fronts a king in state,
I stand within her walls with not a shred
Of terror, malice, not a word of jeer.
Darkly I gaze into the days ahead,
And see her might and granite wonders there,
Beneath the touch of Time’s unerring hand,
Like priceless treasures sinking in the sand.
evidence: The speaker confesses love ('I love this cultured hell') for the very source of suffering that feeds him 'bread of bitterness' and 'sinks into my throat her tiger's tooth,' maintaining the awareness of persecution while reversing expected hatred into passionate attachment—the characteristic transformation of aggression into its opposite with observable strain in the paradox 'cultured hell.'

author: Edna St. Vincent Millay
content:
Love has gone and left me and the days are all alike;
   Eat I must, and sleep I will, — and would that night were here!But ah! — to lie awake and hear the slow hours strike!
   Would that it were day again! — with twilight near!Love has gone and left me and I don't know what to do;
   This or that or what you will is all the same to me;But all the things that I begin I leave before I'm through, —
   There's little use in anything as far as I can see.Love has gone and left me, — and the neighbors knock and borrow,
   And life goes on forever like the gnawing of a mouse, —And to-morrow and to-morrow and to-morrow and to-morrow
   There's this little street and this little house.
evidence: The speaker catalogues profound loss and despair in a detached, mechanical register ('Eat I must, and sleep I will,' 'This or that or what you will is all the same to me'), reporting emotional devastation as if delivering a weather report on the psyche—affectively flat despite topically accurate acknowledgment of suffering.

author: Lady Mary Wortley Montagu
content:
To all you ladies now at Bath,
   And eke, ye beaux, to you,With aching heart, and wat'ry eyes,
   I bid my last adieu.
 Farewell ye nymphs, who waters sip
   Hot reeking from the pumps,While music lends her friendly aid,
   To cheer you from the dumps.
 Farewell ye wits, who prating stand,
   And criticise the fair;Yourselves the joke of men of sense,
   Who hate a coxcomb's air.
 Farewell to Deard's, and all her toys,
   Which glitter in her shop,Deluding traps to girls and boys,
   The warehouse of the fop.
 Lindsay's and Hayes's both farewell,
   Where in the spacious hall,With bounding steps, and sprightly air,
   I've led up many a ball.
 Where Somerville of courteous mien,
   Was partner in the dance,With swimming Haws, and Brownlow blithe,
   And Britton pink of France.
 Poor Nash, farewell! may fortune smile,
   Thy drooping soul revive,My heart is full I can no more—
   John, bid the coachman drive.
evidence: The speaker recites a catalogue of Bath's social scenes—nymphs at pumps, witty critics, dance partners named—with meticulous detail and light satiric distance, yet reports only at the final moment 'My heart is full' before abruptly cutting off emotion with the command to drive away. The elaborate enumeration keeps painful departure at bay through formal inventory until the lone affective acknowledgment, immediately fled.

author: Dorothy Parker
content:
The ladies men admire, I’ve heard,
Would shudder at a wicked word.
Their candle gives a single light;
They’d rather stay at home at night.
They do not keep awake till three,
Nor read erotic poetry.
They never sanction the impure,
Nor recognize an overture.
They shrink from powders and from paints ...
So far, I’ve had no complaints.
evidence: The speaker catalogs virtuous feminine qualities men allegedly admire (single-light candles, staying home, shunning impure material) with apparent endorsement, then deflates the entire performance with 'So far, I've had no complaints'—revealing she possesses none of these qualities while maintaining full awareness of the game. The wit keeps idea, affect, and object in consciousness, making herself the butt of the joke while reconciling the paradox between social prescription and her actual libertine life.

author: Katherine Philips
content:
On her Son H.P. at St. Syth’s Church where her body also lies interred



What on Earth deserves our trust?Youth and Beauty both are dust.Long we gathering are with pain,What one moment calls again.Seven years childless marriage past,A Son, a son is born at last:So exactly lim’d and fair,Full of good Spirits, Meen, and Air,As a long life promised,Yet, in less than six weeks dead.Too promising, too great a mindIn so small room to be confined:Therefore, as fit in Heaven to dwell,He quickly broke the Prison shell.So the subtle Alchemist,Can’t with Hermes Seal resistThe powerful spirit’s subtler flight,But t’will bid him long good night.And so the Sun if it ariseHalf so glorious as his Eyes,Like this Infant, takes a shrowd,Buried in a morning Cloud.
evidence: The speaker transforms overwhelming grief over her infant son's death into elaborate philosophical and alchemical conceits ('too great a mind / In so small room to be confined'), delivering theoretically sophisticated explanations for his death while maintaining affective distance through abstract metaphors of spirits escaping vessels and celestial analogies.

poem_name: Friendship’s Mystery, To my Dearest Lucasia
author: Katherine Philips
content:
1 Come, my Lucasia, since we see
   That Miracles Mens faith do move,By wonder and by prodigy
   To the dull angry world let’s prove
   There’s a Religion in our Love.2 For though we were design’d t’ agree,
   That Fate no liberty destroyes,But our Election is as free
   As Angels, who with greedy choice
   Are yet determin’d to their joyes.3 Our hearts are doubled by the loss,
   Here Mixture is Addition grown ;We both diffuse, and both ingross :
   And we whose minds are so much one,
   Never, yet ever are alone.4 We court our own Captivity
   Than Thrones more great and innocent :’Twere banishment to be set free,
   Since we wear fetters whose intent
   Not Bondage is, but Ornament.5 Divided joyes are tedious found,
   And griefs united easier grow :We are our selves but by rebound,
   And all our Titles shuffled so,
   Both Princes, and both Subjects too.6 Our Hearts are mutual Victims laid,
   While they (such power in Friendship lies)Are Altars, Priests, and Off’rings made :
   And each Heart which thus kindly dies,
   Grows deathless by the Sacrifice.
evidence: The speaker constructs an elaborate theology of friendship that attributes transcendent, miraculous qualities to the relationship ('Religion in our Love,' 'mutual Victims,' 'Altars, Priests, and Off'rings'), employing superlatives and absolutes ('greedy choice,' 'deathless') that elevate the beloved to a salvific position where connection with Lucasia provides protection against 'the dull angry world.'

author: Edgar Allan Poe
content:
In visions of the dark night
    I have dreamed of joy departed—But a waking dream of life and light
    Hath left me broken-hearted.Ah! what is not a dream by day
    To him whose eyes are castOn things around him with a ray
    Turned back upon the past?That holy dream—that holy dream,
    While all the world were chiding,Hath cheered me as a lovely beam
    A lonely spirit guiding.What though that light, thro' storm and night,
    So trembled from afar—What could there be more purely bright
 In Truth's day-star?
evidence: The speaker explicitly retreats from waking reality ('what is not a dream by day') into an internal 'holy dream' that serves as refuge and guide ('cheered me as a lovely beam / A lonely spirit guiding'), preferring this fantasy to external engagement, treating the internal vision as 'more purely bright / In Truth's day-star' than actual reality.

author: Christina Rossetti
evidence: The speaker transforms profound personal emptiness and spiritual desolation ('numb'd too much,' 'void and brief,' 'broken bowl that cannot hold') into petition for transformation into vessel of service ('A royal cup for Him, my King: / O Jesus, drink of me'), reframing desperate neediness as vicarious offering—correctly attributing her own desire for vitality to Christ's need while partly gratifying both self (through connection) and object (genuinely blessed by devotional surrender).

author: Christina Rossetti
evidence: The speaker celebrates arrival of love through elaborate similes and imperatives for celebratory decoration, expressing unambivalent joy without defensive modification of affect or distortion of reality.

author: Christina Rossetti
content:
Where sunless rivers weepTheir waves into the deep,She sleeps a charmed sleep:
     Awake her not.Led by a single star,She came from very farTo seek where shadows are
     Her pleasant lot.She left the rosy morn,She left the fields of corn,For twilight cold and lorn
     And water springs.Through sleep, as through a veil,She sees the sky look pale,And hears the nightingale
     That sadly sings.Rest, rest, a perfect restShed over brow and breast;Her face is toward the west,
     The purple land.She cannot see the grainRipening on hill and plain;She cannot feel the rain
     Upon her hand.Rest, rest, for evermoreUpon a mossy shore;Rest, rest at the heart's core
     Till time shall cease:Sleep that no pain shall wake;Night that no morn shall breakTill joy shall overtake
     Her perfect peace.
evidence: The speaker transforms death into a 'charmed sleep,' a 'perfect rest' that 'no pain shall wake'—the traumatic finality of loss is rendered theatrical and romanticized ('Dream Land,' 'purple land,' 'mossy shore'), creating aesthetic distance from grief through dreamlike imagery while maintaining emotional disconnection from the embodied reality of the beloved's death ('cannot see,' 'cannot feel').

author: Dante Gabriel Rossetti
content:
Know'st thou not at the fall of the leafHow the heart feels a languid grief
     Laid on it for a covering,
     And how sleep seems a goodly thingIn Autumn at the fall of the leaf?And how the swift beat of the brainFalters because it is in vain,
     In Autumn at the fall of the leaf
     Knowest thou not? and how the chiefOf joys seems—not to suffer pain?Know'st thou not at the fall of the leafHow the soul feels like a dried sheaf
     Bound up at length for harvesting,
     And how death seems a comely thingIn Autumn at the fall of the leaf?
evidence: The speaker transforms autumnal melancholy into a detached interrogation ('Know'st thou not...'), cataloguing depressive affects (languid grief, brain faltering, soul as dried sheaf) in the formal, almost pedagogical tone of a weather report on the psyche—emotionally correct but delivered without gut-level engagement or personal claim.

poem_name: I Am the People, the Mob
author: Carl Sandburg
content:
I am the people—the mob—the crowd—the mass.
Do you know that all the great work of the world is done through me?
I am the workingman, the inventor, the maker of the world’s food and clothes.
I am the audience that witnesses history. The Napoleons come from me and the Lincolns. They die. And then I send forth more Napoleons and Lincolns.
I am the seed ground. I am a prairie that will stand for much plowing. Terrible storms pass over me. I forget. The best of me is sucked out and wasted. I forget. Everything but Death comes to me and makes me work and give up what I have. And I forget.
Sometimes I growl, shake myself and spatter a few red drops for history to remember. Then—I forget.
When I, the People, learn to remember, when I, the People, use the lessons of yesterday and no longer forget who robbed me last year, who played me for a fool—then there will be no speaker in all the world say the name: “The People,” with any fleck of a sneer in his voice or any far-off smile of derision.
The mob—the crowd—the mass—will arrive then.
evidence: The speaker transforms massive historical trauma and exploitation ('The best of me is sucked out and wasted. I forget. Everything but Death comes to me') into theatrical, declamatory performance that maintains disconnection from affective weight through grandiose collective identity and repetitive amnesia ('I forget'), displaying dissociation's characteristic conversion of unbearable pain into entertaining spectacle.

author: Sir Walter Scott
content:
Proud Maisie is in the wood,
 Walking so early;Sweet Robin sits on the bush,
 Singing so rarely."Tell me, thou bonny bird,
 When shall I marry me?"—"When six braw gentlemen
 Kirkward shall carry ye.""Who makes the bridal bed,
 Birdie, say truly?"—"The gray-headed sexton
 That delves the grave duly."The glowworm o'er grave and stone
 Shall light thee steady;The owl from the steeple sing,
 'Welcome, proud lady.'"
evidence: Maisie directly asks 'When shall I marry me?' and receives death as answer, yet continues questioning as if pursuing wedding plans ('Who makes the bridal bed'), maintaining exhilarated forward momentum (walking early, asking truly) while Robin's responses systematically replace marriage imagery with funeral facts she does not acknowledge.

poem_name: Sonnet 18: Shall I compare thee to a summer’s day?
author: William Shakespeare
content:
Shall I compare thee to a summer’s day?
Thou art more lovely and more temperate:
Rough winds do shake the darling buds of May,
And summer’s lease hath all too short a date;
Sometime too hot the eye of heaven shines,
And often is his gold complexion dimm'd;
And every fair from fair sometime declines,
By chance or nature’s changing course untrimm'd;
But thy eternal summer shall not fade,
Nor lose possession of that fair thou ow’st;
Nor shall death brag thou wander’st in his shade,
When in eternal lines to time thou grow’st:
   So long as men can breathe or eyes can see,
   So long lives this, and this gives life to thee.
evidence: The speaker asserts magical control over mortality itself—'thy eternal summer shall not fade,' 'Nor shall death brag thou wander'st in his shade'—through the conviction that his poetic act alone grants literal immortality ('in eternal lines to time thou grow'st'), collapsing the boundary between wish (preserving the beloved) and reality (actual control over death and time).

author: Wallace Stevens
content:
One must have a mind of winter
To regard the frost and the boughs
Of the pine-trees crusted with snow;
And have been cold a long time
To behold the junipers shagged with ice,
The spruces rough in the distant glitter
Of the January sun; and not to think
Of any misery in the sound of the wind,
In the sound of a few leaves,
Which is the sound of the land
Full of the same wind
That is blowing in the same bare place
For the listener, who listens in the snow,
And, nothing himself, beholds
Nothing that is not there and the nothing that is.
evidence: The speaker prescribes becoming 'nothing himself' to perceive reality stripped of emotional projection—'not to think / Of any misery in the sound of the wind'—achieving a stance of pure observation where affective responses to winter's harshness are systematically severed from perception itself.

poem_name: Song: Out upon it, I have lov’d
author: Sir John Suckling
content:
Out upon it, I have lov’d
   Three whole days together;And am like to love three more,
   If it prove fair weather.Time shall moult away his wings,
   Ere he shall discoverIn the whole wide world again
   Such a constant lover.But the spite on’t is, no praise
   Is due at all to me;Love with me had made no stays,
   Had it any been but she.Had it any been but she,
   And that very face,There had been at least ere this
   A dozen dozen in her place.
evidence: The speaker constructs elaborate justifications for inconstancy ('Time shall moult away his wings / Ere he shall discover... Such a constant lover' for three days of affection), then attributes all fidelity to the woman's exceptional qualities rather than any effort of will ('no praise / Is due at all to me... Had it any been but she'), converting base fickleness into reasonable response to unique circumstances while simultaneously devaluing all other potential objects ('A dozen dozen in her place').

poem_name: Sonnet 1: Dost see how unregarded now
author: Sir John Suckling
content:
Dost see how unregarded now
       That piece of beauty passes?There was a time when I did vow
       To that alone;
   But mark the fate of faces;The red and white works now no more on meThan if it could not charm, or I not see.And yet the face continues good,
       And I have still desires,Am still the selfsame flesh and blood,
       As apt to melt
   And suffer from those fires;Oh some kind pow’r unriddle where it lies,Whether my heart be faulty, or her eyes?She ev’ry day her man does kill,
       And I as often die;Neither her power then, nor my will
       Can question’d be.
   What is the mystery?Sure beauty’s empires, like to greater states,Have certain periods set, and hidden fates.
evidence: The speaker converts the painful loss of erotic response into abstract puzzles ('Oh some kind pow'r unriddle where it lies') and philosophical generalizations about 'beauty's empires' having 'certain periods set,' delivering emotional content in the detached, theoretical tone of a natural philosopher investigating mechanical causation rather than grieving diminished desire.

author: Algernon Charles Swinburne
content:
(excerpt)



I A baby's feet, like sea-shells pink,       Might tempt, should heaven see meet, An angel's lips to kiss, we think,       A baby's feet. Like rose-hued sea-flowers toward the heat       They stretch and spread and wink Their ten soft buds that part and meet. No flower-bells that expand and shrink       Gleam half so heavenly sweet As shine on life's untrodden brink       A Baby's feet. II A baby's hands, like rosebuds furled       Whence yet no leaf expands, Ope if you touch, though close upcurled,       A baby's hands. Then, fast as warriors grip their brands       When battle's bolt is hurled, They close, clenched hard like tightening bands. No rosebuds yet by dawn impearled       Match, even in loveliest lands, The sweetest flowers in all the world—       A baby's hands. III A baby's eyes, ere speech begin,       Ere lips learn words or sighs, Bless all things bright enough to win       A baby's eyes. Love, while the sweet thing laughs and lies,       And sleep flows out and in, Sees perfect in them Paradise. Their glance might cast out pain and sin,       Their speech make dumb the wise, By mute glad godhead felt within       A baby's eyes.
evidence: The poem offers pure aesthetic celebration of infant features through elaborate simile and metaphor without psychological conflict, anxiety, or defensive distortion. The speaker maintains consistent wonder and delight throughout, with no evidence of managing internal threat or external stressor.

author: J. M. Synge
content:
To a sister of an enemy of the author's who disapproved of 'The Playboy'



Lord, confound this surly sister,Blight her brow with blotch and blister,Cramp her larynx, lung, and liver,In her guts a galling give her.Let her live to earn her dinnersIn Mountjoy with seedy sinners:Lord, this judgment quickly bring,And I'm your servant, J. M. Synge.
evidence: The speaker channels raw aggressive impulse (wish for the sister's physical suffering and imprisonment) into a formally crafted comic curse structured as mock prayer—maintaining full emotional intensity while transforming primitive rage into witty, rhythmically satisfying verse that achieves real social consequence (published poem) with signature.

author: Ann Taylor
content:
WELL, what's the matter ? there's a face
   What ! has it cut a vein ?And is it quite a shocking place ?
   Come, let us look again.I see it bleeds, but never mind
   That tiny little drop ;I don't believe you'll ever find
   That crying makes it stop.'Tis sad indeed to cry at pain,
   For any but a baby ;If that should chance to cut a vein,
   We should not wonder, may be.But such a man as you should try
   To bear a little sorrow :So run along, and wipe your eye,
   'Twill all be well to-morrow.
evidence: The speaker transforms a child's pain into a mechanical problem ('that tiny little drop') and strips all emotional validation from the experience, insisting crying is inappropriate for 'such a man as you' while maintaining detached, pedagogical tone throughout. The wound is acknowledged cognitively but its distressing affect is systematically severed through minimization and appeals to maturity.

author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
content:
Where Claribel low-lieth
   The breezes pause and die,
   Letting the rose-leaves fall:But the solemn oak-tree sigheth,
   Thick-leaved, ambrosial,
   With an ancient melody
   Of an inward agony,Where Claribel low-lieth.At eve the beetle boometh
   Athwart the thicket lone:At noon the wild bee hummeth
   About the moss'd headstone:At midnight the moon cometh,
   And looketh down alone.Her song the lintwhite swelleth,The clear-voiced mavis dwelleth,
   The callow throstle lispeth,The slumbrous wave outwelleth,
   The babbling runnel crispeth,The hollow grot replieth
   Where Claribel low-lieth.
evidence: The speaker catalogues natural phenomena around Claribel's grave with meticulous, ceremonial precision ('At eve the beetle boometh / Athwart the thicket lone: At noon the wild bee hummeth') while maintaining complete affective detachment—the death is transformed into an aesthetic inventory devoid of grief, longing, or any personal emotional response.

author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
content:
Sunset and evening star,
      And one clear call for me!
And may there be no moaning of the bar,
      When I put out to sea,
   But such a tide as moving seems asleep,
      Too full for sound and foam,
When that which drew from out the boundless deep
      Turns again home.
   Twilight and evening bell,
      And after that the dark!
And may there be no sadness of farewell,
      When I embark;
   For tho' from out our bourne of Time and Place
      The flood may bear me far,
I hope to see my Pilot face to face
      When I have crost the bar.
evidence: The speaker prepares emotionally for death through affect-laden wishes ('may there be no sadness of farewell'), experiencing modest distress while planning for future discomfort, spreading anxiety across the poem's meditative structure through self-inoculation with anticipatory images of crossing, embarking, and meeting the Pilot.

author: Henry Vaughan
content:
BODY      Farewell! I go to sleep; but when
    The day-star springs, I’ll wake again.SOUL      Go, sleep in peace; and when thou liestUnnumber’d in thy dust, when all this frameIs but one dram, and what thou now descriest
    In sev’ral parts shall want a name,Then may his peace be with thee, and each dustWrit in his book, who ne’er betray’d man’s trust!BODY      Amen! but hark, ere we two stray
    How many hours dost think ’till day?SOUL      Ah go; th’art weak, and sleepy. Heav’nIs a plain watch, and without figures windsAll ages up; who drew this circle, even
    He fills it; days and hours are blinds.Yet this take with thee. The last gasp of timeIs thy first breath, and man’s eternal prime.
