evidence: The Soul prepares the Body for death with realistic, affect-laden acknowledgment of future dissolution ('when all this frame / Is but one dram'), spreading anxiety through present dialogue while maintaining conscious awareness of both the temporal process ('how many hours dost think 'till day?') and the spiritual reality that transforms mortality into 'man's eternal prime.'

author: Henry Vaughan
content:
My Soul, there is a country
    Afar beyond the stars,Where stands a winged sentry
    All skillful in the wars;There, above noise and danger
    Sweet Peace sits, crown’d with smiles,And One born in a manger
    Commands the beauteous files.He is thy gracious friend
    And (O my Soul awake!)Did in pure love descend,
    To die here for thy sake.If thou canst get but thither,
    There grows the flow’r of peace,The rose that cannot wither,
    Thy fortress, and thy ease.Leave then thy foolish ranges,
    For none can thee secure,But One, who never changes,
    Thy God, thy life, thy cure.
evidence: The speaker constructs an omnipotent, omnibenevolent protector figure ('One born in a manger / Commands the beauteous files') who is 'thy fortress, and thy ease' and 'never changes,' attributing absolute perfection and safety to this idealized object in order to manage internal terror ('above noise and danger').

author: Charles Wesley
content:
Christ, whose glory fills the skies,
   Christ, the true, the only light,Sun of Righteousness, arise,
   Triumph o’er the shades of night:Day-spring from on high, be near:Day-star, in my heart appear.Dark and cheerless is the morn
   Unaccompanied by thee,Joyless is the day’s return,
   Till thy mercy’s beams I see;Till thy inward light impart,Glad my eyes, and warm my heart.Visit then this soul of mine,
   Pierce the gloom of sin, and grief,Fill me, Radiancy Divine,
   Scatter all my unbelief,More and more thyself display,Shining to the perfect day.
evidence: The speaker attributes omnipotent, perfecting qualities to Christ using cumulative superlatives ('true, the only light,' 'Radiancy Divine') and depends entirely on merger with this idealized figure to counteract internal terror ('Dark and cheerless is the morn / Unaccompanied by thee'). The plea 'Fill me,' 'Pierce the gloom,' and 'More and more thyself display' reveals archaic conviction that safety requires fusion with an all-powerful, omnibenevolent presence.

author: John Greenleaf Whittier
content:
So fallen! so lost! the light withdrawn
   Which once he wore!The glory from his gray hairs gone
   Forevermore!Revile him not, the Tempter hath
   A snare for all;And pitying tears, not scorn and wrath,
   Befit his fall!Oh, dumb be passion’s stormy rage,
   When he who mightHave lighted up and led his age,
   Falls back in night.Scorn! would the angels laugh, to mark
   A bright soul driven,Fiend-goaded, down the endless dark,
   From hope and heaven!Let not the land once proud of him
   Insult him now,Nor brand with deeper shame his dim,
   Dishonored brow.But let its humbled sons, instead,
   From sea to lake,A long lament, as for the dead,
   In sadness make.Of all we loved and honored, naught
   Save power remains;A fallen angel’s pride of thought,
   Still strong in chains.All else is gone; from those great eyes
   The soul has fled:When faith is lost, when honor dies,
   The man is dead!Then, pay the reverence of old days
   To his dead fame;Walk backward, with averted gaze,
   And hide the shame!
evidence: The speaker transforms rage and scorn into their opposite—'pitying tears, not scorn and wrath'—while commanding others to suppress their legitimate anger ('dumb be passion's stormy rage'). The ritualistic mourning 'as for the dead' and ceremonial covering of shame ('Walk backward, with averted gaze') serve to undo and reverse the intensity of betrayal felt toward the fallen figure.

author: John Wilmot Earl of Rochester
content:
I cannot change, as others do,
     Though you unjustly scorn;
   Since that poor swain, that sighs for you
     For you alone was born.
   No, Phyllis, no, your heart to move
     A surer way I’ll try:
   And to revenge my slighted love,Will still love on, will still love on, and die.
     When, kill’d with grief, Amyntas lies;
     And you to mind shall call
   The sighs that now unpitied rise;
     The tears that vainly fall:
   That welcome hour that ends this smart,
     Will then begin your pain;
   For such a faithful, tender heartCan never break, can never break in vain.
evidence: The speaker frames his constancy as revenge ('to revenge my slighted love, / Will still love on, and die'), broadcasting current suffering directly at Phyllis while making her the cause ('Look what you made me suffer'). His martyred declaration that 'such a faithful, tender heart / Can never break in vain' punishes the actual source of conflict through self-wounding while maintaining plausible deniability as devotion.

author: John Wilmot Earl of Rochester
content:
All my past life is mine no more,
     The flying hours are gone,Like transitory dreams giv’n o’er,Whose images are kept in store
     By memory alone.The time that is to come is not;
     How can it then be mine?The present moment’s all my lot;And that, as fast as it is got,
     Phyllis, is only thine.Then talk not of inconstancy,
     False hearts, and broken vows;If I, by miracle, can beThis live-long minute true to thee,
     ’Tis all that Heav'n allows.
evidence: The speaker constructs an elaborate philosophical justification for infidelity ('The present moment's all my lot') that reframes inconstancy as metaphysical necessity rather than moral failing, converting desire for uncommitted pleasure into cosmic truth about time's nature—'If I, by miracle, can be / This live-long minute true to thee, / 'Tis all that Heav'n allows' transforms appetite into theological constraint.

author: William Wordsworth
content:
Earth has not any thing to show more fair:
Dull would he be of soul who could pass by
A sight so touching in its majesty:
This City now doth, like a garment, wear
The beauty of the morning; silent, bare,
Ships, towers, domes, theatres, and temples lie
Open unto the fields, and to the sky;
All bright and glittering in the smokeless air.
Never did sun more beautifully steep
In his first splendour, valley, rock, or hill;
Ne'er saw I, never felt, a calm so deep!
The river glideth at his own sweet will:
Dear God! the very houses seem asleep;
And all that mighty heart is lying still!
evidence: The speaker describes London at dawn with aesthetic appreciation and wonder, expressing sincere admiration without distortion, conflict avoidance, or defensive transformation of underlying anxiety.

poem_name: When Lovely Woman Stoops to Folly
author: Oliver Goldsmith
content:
When lovely woman stoops to folly,
 And finds too late that men betray,What charm can sooth her melancholy,
 What art can wash her guilt away?The only art her guilt to cover,
 To hide her shame from every eye,To give repentance to her lover,
 And wring his bosom—is to die.
evidence: The speaker frames suicide as 'the only art her guilt to cover'—a ritualistic, magical solution that will simultaneously 'give repentance to her lover / And wring his bosom,' treating the woman's death as compensatory behavior that can erase transgression and reverse shame through ultimate self-sacrifice.

author: Rabindranath Tagore
evidence: The speaker constructs an omnipotent, perfected realm ('heaven of freedom') and addresses 'thee' and 'my Father' as the guiding force capable of leading the mind forward into this ideal state, attributing salvific power to merge with through prayer while the actual country remains unawakened.

poem_name: I heard a Fly buzz - when I died - (591)
author: Emily Dickinson
content:
I heard a Fly buzz - when I died -
The Stillness in the Room
Was like the Stillness in the Air -
Between the Heaves of Storm -
The Eyes around - had wrung them dry -
And Breaths were gathering firm
For that last Onset - when the King
Be witnessed - in the Room -
I willed my Keepsakes - Signed away
What portion of me be
Assignable - and then it was
There interposed a Fly -
With Blue - uncertain - stumbling Buzz -
Between the light - and me -
And then the Windows failed - and then
I could not see to see -
evidence: The speaker recounts her own death with detailed observational precision ('I heard a Fly buzz,' 'The Stillness in the Room / Was like the Stillness in the Air') while maintaining complete affective flatness—no fear, grief, or struggle accompanies this account of ultimate dissolution. The mechanical cataloguing of death's procedural elements (willed keepsakes, signed documents, failing windows) reads as weather report rather than lived terror.

author: Emily Dickinson
content:
Success is counted sweetest
By those who ne'er succeed.
To comprehend a nectar
Requires sorest need.
Not one of all the purple Host
Who took the Flag today
Can tell the definition
So clear of victory
As he defeated – dying –
On whose forbidden ear
The distant strains of triumph
Burst agonized and clear!
evidence: The speaker adopts a philosophical stance toward success and defeat without displaying personal defensiveness; the poem meditates on abstract paradox through distanced observation rather than managing internal conflict through defense.

poem_name: There's a certain Slant of light, (320)
author: Emily Dickinson
content:
There's a certain Slant of light,
Winter Afternoons –
That oppresses, like the Heft
Of Cathedral Tunes –
Heavenly Hurt, it gives us –
We can find no scar,
But internal difference –
Where the Meanings, are –
None may teach it – Any –
'Tis the seal Despair –
An imperial affliction
Sent us of the Air –
When it comes, the Landscape listens –
Shadows – hold their breath –
When it goes, 'tis like the Distance
On the look of Death –
evidence: The speaker transforms overwhelming existential despair into formal aesthetic contemplation, converting 'Heavenly Hurt' into abstract theological-philosophical categories ('seal Despair,' 'imperial affliction') while maintaining emotional distance through capitalization and precise cataloging of an experience that 'oppresses' but produces 'no scar' only 'internal difference.' The affect is acknowledged conceptually but delivered in the detached, weather-report tone characteristic of intellectualization.

author: Ralph Waldo Emerson
evidence: The speaker adopts the voice of the Absolute (Brahma) describing metaphysical unity and transcendence of dualities; this is philosophical/theological discourse rather than psychological defense against personal distress.

author: Herman Melville
content:
About the Shark, phlegmatical one,
Pale sot of the Maldive sea,
The sleek little pilot-fish, azure and slim,
How alert in attendance be.
From his saw-pit of mouth, from his charnel of maw
They have nothing of harm to dread,
But liquidly glide on his ghastly flank
Or before his Gorgonian head;
Or lurk in the port of serrated teeth
In white triple tiers of glittering gates,
And there find a haven when peril’s abroad,
An asylum in jaws of the Fates!
They are friends; and friendly they guide him to prey,
Yet never partake of the treat—
Eyes and brains to the dotard lethargic and dull,
Pale ravener of horrible meat.
evidence: The speaker describes a predatory symbiosis involving death and survival ('charnel of maw,' 'horrible meat,' 'jaws of the Fates') in detached, almost taxonomic language that strips the scene of visceral horror, presenting mutual exploitation as merely an interesting natural arrangement to be catalogued rather than felt.

author: Herman Melville
content:
To have known him, to have loved him
   After loneness long;And then to be estranged in life,
   And neither in the wrong;And now for death to set his seal—
   Ease me, a little ease, my song!By wintry hills his hermit-mound
   The sheeted snow-drifts drape,And houseless there the snow-bird flits
   Beneath the fir-trees’ crape:Glazed now with ice the cloistral vine
   That hid the shyest grape.
evidence: The speaker acknowledges overwhelming grief ('Ease me, a little ease, my song!') while consciously maintaining attention to the painful loss through elegiac meditation, postponing emotional collapse through the semi-controlled act of writing—aware of the affect, asking for manageable doses ('a little ease') rather than total escape, progressing anxiously through the memorial.

author: Robert Louis Stevenson
content:
Grown about by fragrant bushes,Sunken in a winding valley,
   Where the clear winds blow
   And the shadows come and go,
   And the cattle stand and lowAnd the sheep bells and the linnets
   Sing and tinkle musically.Between the past and the future,
   Those two black infinities
   Between which our brief life
   Flashes a moment and goes out.
evidence: The speaker consciously holds in mind both the pastoral present ('fragrant bushes,' 'linnets sing') and the finite timeframe ('between the past and the future'), acknowledging mortality without repressing it—'our brief life flashes a moment and goes out'—while maintaining simultaneous awareness of beauty and temporal constraint.

author: Robert Bridges
content:
I love all beauteous things,
   I seek and adore them;God hath no better praise,And man in his hasty days
   Is honoured for them.I too will something make
   And joy in the making;Altho’ to-morrow it seemLike the empty words of a dream
   Remembered on waking.
evidence: The speaker channels worship of beauty into creative making ('I too will something make / And joy in the making'), transforming adoration into socially valued artistic production while maintaining emotional intensity and pleasure despite acknowledging the work's potential ephemerality.

author: Robert Bridges
content:
Love not too much. But how,When thou hast made me such,And dost thy gifts bestow,How can I love too much?
   Though I must fear to lose,And drown my joy in care,With all its thorns I chooseThe path of love and prayer.
   Though thou, I know not why,Didst kill my childish trust,That breach with toil did IRepair, because I must:
   And spite of frighting schemes,With which the fiends of HellBlaspheme thee in my dreams,So far I have hoped well.
   But what the heavenly key,What marvel in me wroughtShall quite exculpate thee,I have no shadow of thought.
   What am I that complain?The love, from which beganMy question sad and vain,Justifies thee to man.
evidence: The speaker oscillates between complaint ('what am I that complain?') and immediate retraction ('The love...Justifies thee to man'), treating his grievance as requiring magical counterbalance. Each stanza's accusation (killed trust, frightening schemes, no exculpation) triggers ritualistic self-correction, with the final reversal fully negating the entire preceding lament.

poem_name: A New York Child’s Garden of Verses
author: Franklin Pierce Adams
content:
(With the usual.)



I In winter I get up at night, And dress by an electric light. In summer, autumn, ay, and spring, I have to do the self-same thing. I have to go to bed and hear Pianos pounding in my ear, And hear the janitor cavort With garbage cans within the court. And does it not seem hard to you That I should have these things to do? Is it not hard for us Manhat- Tan children in a stuffy flat?        II It is very nice to think The world is full of food and drink; But, oh, my father says to me They cost all of his salaree.        III When I am grown to man’s estate I shall be very proud and great; E’en now I have no reverence, ’Cause I read comic supplements.        IV New York is so full of a number of kids I’m sure pretty soon we shall be invalids.        V A child should always say what’s true, And speak when he is spoken to; And then, when manhood’s age he strikes, He may be boorish as he likes.
evidence: The speaker directly confronts painful urban childhood realities (poverty, noise, overcrowding) while responding 'as if it were a game,' transforming genuine distress into wry self-mockery that makes the child-speaker the butt of the joke ('I'm sure pretty soon we shall be invalids'). The timing and sophistication reconcile paradox (innocence/cynicism, complaint/entertainment) with full observing ego, allowing pleasure to both user and reader.

poem_name: I would I might Forget that I am I
author: George Santayana
content:
Sonnet VII



I would I might forget that I am I,And break the heavy chain that binds me fast,Whose links about myself my deeds have cast.What in the body’s tomb doth buried lieIs boundless; ’tis the spirit of the sky,Lord of the future, guardian of the past,And soon must forth, to know his own at last.In his large life to live, I fain would die.Happy the dumb beast, hungering for food,But calling not his suffering his own;Blessèd the angel, gazing on all good,But knowing not he sits upon a throne;Wretched the mortal, pondering his mood,And doomed to know his aching heart alone.
evidence: The speaker explicitly wishes to escape integrated selfhood ('forget that I am I') and inhabit alternate states of consciousness—the 'dumb beast' who doesn't claim suffering as his own, or the 'angel' unaware of his throne—transforming unbearable self-awareness into fantasies of dissolution that would eliminate rather than feel the 'aching heart alone.'

author: Robert Louis Stevenson
content:
Though he, that ever kind and true,Kept stoutly step by step with you,Your whole long, gusty lifetime through,
   Be gone a while before,Be now a moment gone before,Yet, doubt not, soon the seasons shall restore
   Your friend to you.He has but turned the corner — stillHe pushes on with right good will,Through mire and marsh, by heugh and hill,
   That self-same arduous way —That self-same upland, hopeful way,That you and he through many a doubtful day
   Attempted still.He is not dead, this friend — not dead,But in the path we mortals treadGot some few, trifling steps ahead
   And nearer to the end;So that you too, once past the bend,Shall meet again, as face to face, this friend
   You fancy dead.Push gaily on, strong heart! The whileYou travel forward mile by mile,He loiters with a backward smile
   Till you can overtake,And strains his eyes to search his wake,Or whistling, as he sees you through the brake,
   Waits on a stile.
evidence: The speaker insists 'He is not dead, this friend—not dead' and constructs an elaborate spatial metaphor where death is merely turning a corner or getting 'some few, trifling steps ahead,' refusing to acknowledge the finality of loss through the conviction that reunion is guaranteed ('the seasons shall restore / Your friend to you').

author: Sara Teasdale
content:
Life has loveliness to sell,
     All beautiful and splendid things,
Blue waves whitened on a cliff,
     Soaring fire that sways and sings,
And children's faces looking up
Holding wonder like a cup.
Life has loveliness to sell,
     Music like a curve of gold,
Scent of pine trees in the rain,
     Eyes that love you, arms that hold,
And for your spirit's still delight,
Holy thoughts that star the night.
Spend all you have for loveliness,
     Buy it and never count the cost;
For one white singing hour of peace
     Count many a year of strife well lost,
And for a breath of ecstasy
Give all you have been, or could be.
evidence: The speaker offers straightforward aesthetic and spiritual counsel without defending against psychological conflict; the imperative mode ('Spend all you have') advocates for beauty as life philosophy rather than as compensation for internal distress.

author: Sara Teasdale
content:
Supper comes at five o'clock,
At six, the evening star,
My lover comes at eight o'clock—
But eight o'clock is far.
How could I bear my pain all day
Unless I watched to see
The clock-hands laboring to bring
Eight o'clock to me.
evidence: The speaker experiences affect-laden planning ('How could I bear my pain all day') while spreading anxiety over time through active mental focus on incremental progress ('watched to see / The clock-hands laboring'), preparing emotionally for the awaited event without magical thinking or avoidance.

author: Elinor Wylie
content:
For this she starred her eyes with salt
And scooped her temples thin,
Until her face shone pure of fault
From the forehead to the chin.
In coldest crucibles of pain
Her shrinking flesh was fired
And smoothed into a finer grain
To make it more desired.
Pain left her lips more clear than glass;
It colored and cooled her hand.
She lay a field of scented grass
Yielded as pasture land.
For this her loveliness was curved
And carved as silver is:
For this she was brave: but she deserved
A better grave than this.
evidence: The speaker transforms self-destructive suffering ('starred her eyes with salt,' 'scooped her temples thin,' 'shrinking flesh was fired') into an elaborate account of purification and beautification 'to make it more desired,' converting self-harm into virtue while the final couplet's protest ('she deserved / A better grave than this') leaks the underlying rage and injustice that the beautification narrative labors to reverse.

author: Annie Finch
content:
She turned to gold and fell in love.
She danced life upside down.
She opened her wild eyes again
and asked some strangers in.
The strangers felt her in and out.
They found her outsides thin.
Since her heart was still and hard,
they knocked her insides in.
evidence: The speaker narrates violation in third-person fairy-tale syntax ('turned to gold,' 'danced life upside down') with theatrical, dreamlike imagery that transforms traumatic boundary invasion ('knocked her insides in') into surreal spectacle, maintaining dissociative distance through enchanted diction while the body ('her heart was still and hard') becomes object rather than felt experience.

author: Annie Finch
content:
You may hear that your heartbeat is uneven
and let new tension climb around your shoulders,
thinking you've found the trick for going mad.
But try to keep a grip on where you are.
Remember: all around you is pure city;
try to stay alert. On the wide streets,
so empty late at night, streaking in glass,
the color of an alley, or the fall
of a sideways flicker from a neon sign
may utterly and briefly disconcert you—
but as you go, you'll find that noise is worse.
Prepare for noise. But never scream. Even tensing
ears too far in advance can sharpen sirens,
and as for horns. ... When you're back to
your normal rhythm after such encounters,
just try to stay alert. You'll never know
exactly who is coming up behind you,
but the sudden movement of pedestrians
will finally, of course, be what disarms you.
evidence: The speaker engages in realistic, affect-laden planning for future urban discomfort ('Prepare for noise. But never scream'), spreading anxiety across instructions while maintaining modest distress ('you've found the trick for going mad,' 'utterly and briefly disconcert you'), paying dues of anxiety in advance through detailed mental rehearsal.

author: Annie Finch
content:
That hour-glass-backed,
orchard-legged,
heavy-headed will,
paper-folded,
wedge-contorted,
savage—dense to kill—
pulls back on backward-moving,
arching
high legs still,
lowered through a deep, knees-reaching,
feathered down
green will,
antenna-honest,
thread-descending,
carpeted as if with skill,
a focus-changing,
sober-reaching,
tracing, killing will.
evidence: The poem offers sustained, precise observation of an insect (likely a praying mantis) with no defensiveness, emotional avoidance, or conflict—it is a formal exercise in descriptive accumulation without psychological stance.

author: Annie Finch
content:
Sir, I am not a bird of prey:
a Lady does not seize the day.
I trust that brief Time will unfold
our youth, before he makes us old.
How could we two write lines of rhyme
were we not fond of numbered Time
and grateful to the vast and sweet
trials his days will make us meet?
The Grave's not just the body's curse;
no skeleton can pen a verse!
So while this numbered World we see,
let's sweeten Time with poetry,
and Time, in turn, may sweeten Love
and give us time our love to prove.
You've praised my eyes, forehead, breast:
you've all our lives to praise the rest.
evidence: The speaker converts resistance to seduction into principled reasoning ('How could we two write lines of rhyme / were we not fond of numbered Time'), constructing elaborate justifications that reframe refusal as serving poetry and proving love through time rather than acknowledging simple unwillingness or fear.

author: Louise Imogen Guiney
content:
I: THE MOTOR: 1905
From hedgerows where aromas fain would be
    New volleyed odours execrably arise;
    The flocks, with hell-smoke in their patient eyes,
Into the ditch from bawling ruin flee:
Spindrift of one abominated sea
    Along all roads in wrecking fury flies
    Till on young strangled leaf, on bloom that dies,
In this far plot it writes a rune for me.
Vast intimate tyranny! Nature dispossessed
    Helplessly hates thee, whose symbolic flare
Lights up (with what reiterance unblest!)
    Entrails of horror in a world thought fair.
False God of pastime thou, vampire of rest,
    Augur of what pollution, what despair?
evidence: The speaker redirects affect from an unnameable source onto the motor-car itself, addressing it as 'False God' and 'vampire of rest' with disproportionate fury. The automobile becomes the symbolic target for anxieties about modernity, industrialization, and social change that remain unexamined in their primary form.

author: A. F. Moritz
content:
Her hair back from the wide round face
flows, almost a girl’s, so thick,
caught back in combs, racing
and curling through them with blackest
vigor, although it is pure white.
Cracked face, dusk-colored: not red
but with a deep red struggling under
the coming night. The eyes shift quickly,
the subway train jerks and rattles,
green vinyl, light flickering, silver poles.
Eyes driven from ancient calm,
which may fear but is never frantic
and says nothing, such as looks out
from the old Indian portraits—calm is
the one thing missing from the beauty
of her face in the black window.
Those unresting eyes there
talk plainly: there’s no money
at home, men young and old go wrong,
life almost at its end is
still day by day harried and perplexed.
evidence: The speaker provides compassionate, reality-grounded observation of the woman's face and circumstances without defensive distortion—noting both her beauty and her hardship, her vigor and her worry, maintaining integrated awareness.

author: George Elliott Clarke
content:
Wipe away tears,
Set free your fears:
Everything is free.
Only the lonely
Need much money:
Everything is free.
Don’t try to bind
The love you find:
Everyone is free.
Your lover’s yours —
Surrender force:
Everyone is free.
The sun melts down,
Spreads gold around:
Everything is free.
The rain is spent
Lending flowers scent:
Everything is free.
The love you live,
The life you give:
Everything is free.
evidence: The poem offers philosophical assertions about abundance and freedom without defending against psychological conflict; the speaker adopts a position of serene counsel rather than managing internal distress.

poem_name: The Finger Puppets in the Attic Dollhouse
author: John Reibetanz
content:
If they, more petite
   than the mice whose flittings
      have pillaged their robes’ sparkled trim,
stood tiptoe
   on the plumped felt tops
      of their thimble-sized footstools
to scrutinize
   the worn fabric
      of this room’s blue distances,
would they locate
   the source of lightning bolts
      in our faces’ wrinkled pleats
and construe the stars’
   dance from the tattered
      embroidery of our steps,
or find in our seamless
   unravelling years
      the tissue of apocalypse?
evidence: The poem is a philosophical thought experiment about perspective and scale, asking what miniature beings would perceive of human existence—a formal meditation on epistemology and temporality without evidence of psychological defense against distress.

author: John Reibetanz
content:
If I close my eyes now, I can still see them
canopied by the visor of my sunhat:
three children islanded on a narrow rim
of earth between the huge crack-willow that
they squat before, hushed, poised to net a frog,
and the pond the frog will jump to (it got away)
a glass its dive will shatter.
                                             The unbroken image
pleases my mind’s eye with its density,
such thick crisscross of tree-trunk, earth, and tall grass
I see no breach, no source for the light that steeps it
but a blue burning in the pond’s green glass.
The grass withered, the tree blew down, earth caught
the frog, the children grew. Sky’s ice-blue flame
teased along the wick it would consume.
evidence: The speaker spreads anxiety over time by holding past joy ('three children islanded') alongside present awareness of transience ('grass withered, tree blew down, earth caught / the frog, the children grew'), experiencing modest affective distress ('Sky's ice-blue flame / teased along the wick it would consume') while mentally rehearsing inevitable loss—preparing emotionally for future discomfort through affect-laden reflection rather than avoidance.

poem_name: VI Mon. August [1742] hath xxxi days.
author: Benjamin Franklin
content:
The Busy-Man's Picture



BUSINESS, thou Plague and Pleasure of my Life, Thou charming Mistress, thou vexatious Wife; Thou Enemy, thou Friend, to Joy, to Grief, Thou bring’st me all, and bring’st me no Relief, Thou bitter, sweet, thou pleasing, teazing Thing, Thou Bee, that with thy Honey wears a Sting; Some Respite, prithee do, yet do not give, I cannot with thee, nor without thee live.
evidence: The speaker systematically pairs each assertion with its immediate contradiction ('Plague and Pleasure,' 'Enemy, thou Friend,' 'bitter, sweet'), then concludes with ritual reversal 'prithee do, yet do not give'—each positive claim immediately neutralized by its negative, creating obsessive symmetry where thought and counter-thought compulsively balance, as if assertion itself requires magical atonement.

poem_name: VII Mon. September [1742] hath xxx days.
author: Benjamin Franklin
content:
The Reverse



Studious of Ease, and fond of humble Things, Below the Smiles, below the Frowns of Kings: Thanks to my Stars, I prize the Sweets of Life, No sleepless Nights I count, no Days of Strife. I rest, I wake, I drink, I sometimes love, I read, I write, I settle, or I rove; Content to live, content to die unknown, Lord of myself, accountable to none.
evidence: The speaker constructs elaborate justifications for withdrawal from ambition ('Studious of Ease,' 'Lord of myself, accountable to none') while presenting a catalog of activities in affectively flat, list-like syntax ('I rest, I wake, I drink') that converts what might be felt disappointment or fear into principled choice.

author: Thomas Hardy
content:
If but some vengeful god would call to me
From up the sky, and laugh: “Thou suffering thing,
Know that thy sorrow is my ecstasy,
That thy love's loss is my hate's profiting!” 
Then would I bear it, clench myself, and die,
Steeled by the sense of ire unmerited;
Half-eased in that a Powerfuller than I
Had willed and meted me the tears I shed.
But not so.   How arrives it joy lies slain,
And why unblooms the best hope ever sown?
—Crass Casualty obstructs the sun and rain,
And dicing Time for gladness casts a moan. . . .
These purblind Doomsters had as readily strown
Blisses about my pilgrimage as pain.
evidence: The speaker constructs an elaborate philosophical system ('Crass Casualty,' 'dicing Time,' 'purblind Doomsters') to explain suffering as mechanistic chance rather than personal failure, converting unbearable loss into abstract cosmological principles that make pain bearable through intellectual framing.

author: Robert Duncan
content:
The white peacock roosting   
might have been Christ,
         featherd robe of Osiris,
the radiant bird, a sword-flash,
         percht in the tree
and the other,    the fumed-glass slide
       —were like night and day,
the slit of an eye opening in
         time
vertical to the horizon
evidence: The poem presents visionary imagery of peacocks as mythic figures (Christ, Osiris) through direct sensory observation without psychological defensiveness—the speaker records transformation and contrast ('night and day,' 'vertical to the horizon') as aesthetic-mystical witness rather than managing internal conflict.

author: Robert Duncan
content:
It’s in the perilous boughs of the tree   
out of blue sky    the wind   
sings loudest surrounding me.
And solitude,   a wild solitude
’s reveald,   fearfully,   high     I’d climb   
into the shaking uncertainties,
part out of longing,   part     daring my self,
part to see that
widening of the world,   part
to find my own, my secret
hiding sense and place, where from afar   
all voices and scenes come back
—the barking of a dog,   autumnal burnings,
far calls,   close calls—   the boy I was
calls out to me
here the man where I am   “Look!
I’ve been where you
most fear to be.”
evidence: The adult speaker reflects on childhood tree-climbing as emotional inoculation—'I'd climb / into the shaking uncertainties' acknowledges the modest affective distress ('fearfully') while describing realistic preparation for adult fear through graduated exposure ('part out of longing, part daring my self'). The boy's triumphant call to the man—'I've been where you / most fear to be'—reveals anticipatory self-inoculation through repeatedly facing feared heights in order to build capacity for future distress.

author: Kenneth Patchen
content:
Be music, night,
That her sleep may go
Where angels have their pale tall choirs
Be a hand, sea,
That her dreams may watch
Thy guidesman touching the green flesh of the world
Be a voice, sky,
That her beauties may be counted   
And the stars will tilt their quiet faces   
Into the mirror of her loveliness
Be a road, earth,
That her walking may take thee
Where the towns of heaven lift their breathing spires
O be a world and a throne, God,
That her living may find its weather
And the souls of ancient bells in a child's book   
Shall lead her into Thy wondrous house
evidence: The speaker invokes cosmic forces (night, sea, sky, earth, God) as omnipotent protective agents who will perfectly safeguard 'her' sleep, dreams, beauty, and walking—attributing exaggerated benevolence and power to the universe itself to manage attachment anxiety and ensure the beloved's absolute safety.

author: James Laughlin
content:
You know our office on the 18th   
floor of the Salmon Tower looks   
      right out on the
Empire State and it just happened   
we were there finishing up some   
      late invoices on
a new book that Saturday morning   
when a bomber roared through the   
      mist and crashed
flames poured from the windows   
into the drifting clouds and sirens   
      screamed down in
the streets below it was unearthly   
but you know the strangest thing   
      we realized that
none of us were much surprised be-
cause we'd always known that those   
      two paragons of
progress sooner or later would per-
form before our eyes this demon-
      stration of their
      true relationship.
evidence: The speaker recounts witnessing a catastrophic bomber crash with flames and screaming sirens in a detached, almost casual tone ('you know... it just happened... we were there'), then converts the traumatic experience into an abstract observation about 'two paragons of progress' demonstrating their 'true relationship'—theoretical language that drains the horror of immediate emotional weight.

author: James Laughlin
content:
In love it may be dangerous   
to reckon on time to count
on it time’s here and then   
it’s gone   I’m not thinking
of death or disaster but of   
the slippage the unpredictable
disappearance of days on which   
we were depending for happiness.
evidence: The speaker experiences modest affective distress ('dangerous') while realistically planning for future discomfort in love, spreading anxiety over time through worrying about 'the slippage the unpredictable / disappearance of days on which / we were depending for happiness'—keeping idea, affect, and object in conscious mind while paying dues of anxiety in advance.

poem_name: Elegy with Surrealist Proverbs as Refrain
author: Dana Gioia
content:
“Poetry must lead somewhere,” declared Breton.   
He carried a rose inside his coat each day
to give a beautiful stranger—“Better to die of love   
than love without regret.” And those who loved him   
soon learned regret. “The simplest surreal act   
is running through the street with a revolver
firing at random.” Old and famous, he seemed démodé.   
There is always a skeleton on the buffet.
evidence: The speaker catalogs Breton's surrealist provocations and biographical facts (carrying roses, advocating random violence) in a detached, archival tone that drains the material of emotional resonance. The final line—'There is always a skeleton on the buffet'—delivers mortality as aesthetic aphorism rather than felt grief, summarizing the elegy as conceptual exercise.

author: Dana Gioia
content:
“We're going,” they said, “to the end of the world.”   
So they stopped the car where the river curled,   
And we scrambled down beneath the bridge   
On the gravel track of a narrow ridge.
We tramped for miles on a wooded walk
Where dog-hobble grew on its twisted stalk.
Then we stopped to rest on the pine-needle floor   
While two ospreys watched from an oak by the shore.
We came to a bend, where the river grew wide   
And green mountains rose on the opposite side.   
My guides moved back. I stood alone,
As the current streaked over smooth flat stone.
Shelf by stone shelf the river fell.
The white water goosetailed with eddying swell.   
Faster and louder the current dropped
Till it reached a cliff, and the trail stopped.
I stood at the edge where the mist ascended,   
My journey done where the world ended.
I looked downstream. There was nothing but sky,   
The sound of the water, and the water’s reply.
evidence: The speaker embarks on a literal journey to 'the end of the world,' experiencing modest affective engagement throughout ('We tramped for miles,' 'I stood alone'), culminating in emotional preparation for finality through gradual approach to the cliff's edge—spreading anxiety over time through incremental exposure to the journey's terminus rather than avoiding it.

author: Katy Giebenhain
content:
A stabbing in miniature, it is,
a tiny crime,
my own blood parceled
drop by drop and set
on the flickering tongue
of this machine.
It is the spout-punching of trees
for syrup new and smooth
and sweeter
than nature ever intended.
It is Sleeping Beauty's curse
and fascination.
It is the dipstick measuring of oil
from the Buick's throat,
the necessary maintenance.
It is every vampire movie ever made.
Hand, my martyr without lips,
my quiet cow.
I'll milk your fingertips
for all they're worth.
For what they're worth.
Something like a harvest, it is,
a tiny crime.
evidence: Speaker confronts painful daily medical necessity by transforming it into playful metaphor ('vampire movie,' 'Sleeping Beauty's curse') while maintaining full awareness of the reality ('necessary maintenance,' 'tiny crime'), allowing catharsis through wit that makes herself the subject without minimizing suffering.

author: Naomi Shihab Nye
content:
Every few minutes, he wants
to march the trail of flattened rye grass
back to the house of muttering
hens. He too could make
a bed in hay. Yesterday the egg so fresh
it felt hot in his hand and he pressed it
to his ear while the other children
laughed and ran with a ball, leaving him,
so little yet, too forgetful in games,
ready to cry if the ball brushed him,
riveted to the secret of birds
caught up inside his fist,
not ready to give it over
to the refrigerator
or the rest of the day.
evidence: The boy remains 'riveted to the secret of birds / caught up inside his fist' while other children laugh and play with a ball, demonstrating mechanical detachment from social engagement while maintaining curious intellectual connection to the egg's mystery without corresponding emotional warmth or pleasure.

poem_name: Sonnet 55: Not marble nor the gilded monuments
author: William Shakespeare
content:
Not marble nor the gilded monuments
Of princes shall outlive this powerful rhyme,
But you shall shine more bright in these contents
Than unswept stone besmeared with sluttish time.
When wasteful war shall statues overturn,
And broils root out the work of masonry,
Nor Mars his sword nor war’s quick fire shall burn
The living record of your memory.
’Gainst death and all-oblivious enmity
Shall you pace forth; your praise shall still find room
Even in the eyes of all posterity
That wear this world out to the ending doom.
    So, till the Judgement that yourself arise,
    You live in this, and dwell in lovers’ eyes.
evidence: The speaker asserts magical control over mortality itself through verse ('Not marble nor the gilded monuments / Of princes shall outlive this powerful rhyme'), collapsing boundary between wish and outcome with absolute conviction that poetic will directly commands temporal reality and grants immortality through linguistic force alone.

author: Paul Laurence Dunbar
content:
I know what the caged bird feels, alas!
    When the sun is bright on the upland slopes;   
When the wind stirs soft through the springing grass,   
And the river flows like a stream of glass;
    When the first bird sings and the first bud opes,   
And the faint perfume from its chalice steals—
I know what the caged bird feels!
I know why the caged bird beats his wing
    Till its blood is red on the cruel bars;   
For he must fly back to his perch and cling   
When he fain would be on the bough a-swing;
    And a pain still throbs in the old, old scars   
And they pulse again with a keener sting—
I know why he beats his wing!
I know why the caged bird sings, ah me,
    When his wing is bruised and his bosom sore,—
When he beats his bars and he would be free;
It is not a carol of joy or glee,
    But a prayer that he sends from his heart’s deep core,   
But a plea, that upward to Heaven he flings—
I know why the caged bird sings!
evidence: The speaker transforms personal suffering into poetic witness that maintains full emotional intensity while achieving artistic distance—'I know why the caged bird sings' channels rage and pain into a 'prayer' and 'plea' sent to Heaven, creating socially valuable testimony from brutal confinement without loss of authentic feeling.

author: Robert Hayden
content:
When it is finally ours, this freedom, this liberty, this beautiful
and terrible thing, needful to man as air,   
usable as earth; when it belongs at last to all,   
when it is truly instinct, brain matter, diastole, systole,   
reflex action; when it is finally won; when it is more   
than the gaudy mumbo jumbo of politicians:   
this man, this Douglass, this former slave, this Negro   
beaten to his knees, exiled, visioning a world   
where none is lonely, none hunted, alien,   
this man, superb in love and logic, this man   
shall be remembered. Oh, not with statues’ rhetoric,   
not with legends and poems and wreaths of bronze alone,
but with the lives grown out of his life, the lives   
fleshing his dream of the beautiful, needful thing.
evidence: The speaker engages in realistic, affect-laden planning for future justice ('when it is finally ours... when it belongs at last to all'), spreading hope across temporal distance while experiencing present urgency about collective liberation, preparing emotionally for a realized freedom not yet achieved.

poem_name: “Time does not bring relief; you all have lied”
author: Edna St. Vincent Millay
content:
Time does not bring relief; you all have lied   
Who told me time would ease me of my pain!   
I miss him in the weeping of the rain;   
I want him at the shrinking of the tide;
The old snows melt from every mountain-side,   
And last year’s leaves are smoke in every lane;   
But last year’s bitter loving must remain
Heaped on my heart, and my old thoughts abide.   
There are a hundred places where I fear   
To go,—so with his memory they brim.   
And entering with relief some quiet place   
Where never fell his foot or shone his face   
I say, “There is no memory of him here!”   
And so stand stricken, so remembering him.
evidence: The speaker directly confronts ongoing grief without distortion, displacement, or defensive transformation—acknowledging both the persistence of pain and the paradox that even memory-free spaces evoke remembering.

author: Sara Teasdale
content:
Since there is no escape, since at the end
My body will be utterly destroyed,
This hand I love as I have loved a friend,
This body I tended, wept with and enjoyed;
Since there is no escape even for me
Who love life with a love too sharp to bear:
The scent of orchards in the rain, the sea
And hours alone too still and sure for prayer—
Since darkness waits for me, then all the more
Let me go down as waves sweep to the shore
In pride, and let me sing with my last breath;
In these few hours of light I lift my head;
Life is my lover—I shall leave the dead
If there is any way to baffle death.
evidence: The speaker channels death-anxiety into aesthetic resolve ('let me sing with my last breath'), transforming mortality's terror into creative assertion ('Life is my lover') and passionate engagement with sensory experience ('scent of orchards in the rain, the sea'). The primitive fear is not suppressed but harnessed into intensified living and art-making.

author: Margaret Fuller
content:
We deemed the secret lost, the spirit gone,
Which spake in Greek simplicity of thought,
And in the forms of gods and heroes wrought
Eternal beauty from the sculptured stone,—
A higher charm than modern culture won
With all the wealth of metaphysic lore,
Gifted to analyze, dissect, explore.
A many-colored light flows from one sun;
Art, ’neath its beams, a motley thread has spun;
The prism modifies the perfect day;
But thou hast known such mediums to shun,
And cast once more on life a pure, white ray.
Absorbed in the creations of thy mind,
Forgetting daily self, my truest self I find.
evidence: The speaker attributes exaggerated, perfected qualities to Flaxman ('thou hast known such mediums to shun, / And cast once more on life a pure, white ray'), positioning him as one who has recovered lost Greek perfection and stands above modern culture's 'motley' complexity, while the final couplet reveals the speaker's merger fantasy: finding 'truest self' by being 'Absorbed in the creations of thy mind.'

author: Sarah Helen Whitman
content:
Vainly my heart had with thy sorceries striven:
It had no refuge from thy love,—no Heaven
But in thy fatal presence;—from afar
It owned thy power and trembled like a star
O’erfraught with light and splendor. Could I deem
How dark a shadow should obscure its beam?—
Could I believe that pain could ever dwell
Where thy bright presence cast its blissful spell?
Thou wert my proud palladium;—could I fear
The avenging Destinies when thou wert near?—Thou wert my Destiny;—thy song, thy fame,
The wild enchantments clustering round thy name,
Were my soul’s heritage, its royal dower;
Its glory and its kingdom and its power!
evidence: The speaker attributes absolute power to the beloved ('thy sorceries,' 'thy fatal presence,' 'Thou wert my Destiny'), elevating them to palladium status with superlative language ('my soul's heritage, its royal dower; Its glory and its kingdom and its power') while describing total merger that leaves the self without refuge or heaven beyond this figure.

author: Li-Young Lee
content:
I buried my father
in the sky.
Since then, the birds
clean and comb him every morning   
and pull the blanket up to his chin   
every night.
I buried my father underground.   
Since then, my ladders
only climb down,
and all the earth has become a house   
whose rooms are the hours, whose doors   
stand open at evening, receiving   
guest after guest.
Sometimes I see past them
to the tables spread for a wedding feast.
I buried my father in my heart.
Now he grows in me, my strange son,   
my little root who won’t drink milk,   
little pale foot sunk in unheard-of night,   
little clock spring newly wet
in the fire, little grape, parent to the future   
wine, a son the fruit of his own son,   
little father I ransom with my life.
evidence: The speaker transforms unbearable grief over the father's death into layered poetic metaphors (sky burial, underground burial, internal burial) that produce socially valued art while maintaining emotional authenticity and intensity. The final internalization—'Now he grows in me, my strange son'—achieves creative reversal of loss into generative presence, channeling primitive mourning into formal beauty with real aesthetic consequences.

author: Gertrude Stein
content:
Why am I if I am uncertain reasons may inclose.   
Remain remain propose repose chose.   
I call carelessly that the door is open   
Which if they may refuse to open   
No one can rush to close.   
Let them be mine therefor.   
Everybody knows that I chose.   
Therefor if therefore before I close.   
I will therefore offer therefore I offer this.
Which if I refuse to miss may be miss is mine.
I will be well welcome when I come.   
Because I am coming.
Certainly I come having come.
                           These stanzas are done.
evidence: The poem enacts a formal experiment with repetition, syntactic variation, and linguistic assertion ('I chose,' 'I am coming') without defensive distortion of reality or psychological conflict—it is a compositional exercise rather than a stance toward emotional threat.

author: Gertrude Stein
content:
Sweet sweet sweet sweet sweet tea.
       Susie Asado.
Sweet sweet sweet sweet sweet tea.
       Susie Asado.
Susie Asado which is a told tray sure.
A lean on the shoe this means slips slips hers.
When the ancient light grey is clean it is yellow, it is a silver seller.
This is a please this is a please there are the saids to jelly. These are the wets these say the sets to leave a crown to Incy.
Incy is short for incubus.
A pot. A pot is a beginning of a rare bit of trees. Trees tremble, the old vats are in bobbles, bobbles which shade and shove and render clean, render clean must.   
       Drink pups.   
Drink pups drink pups lease a sash hold, see it shine and a bobolink has pins. It shows a nail.
What is a nail. A nail is unison.
Sweet sweet sweet sweet sweet tea.
evidence: The poem operates through pure linguistic play and sonic repetition ('Sweet sweet sweet sweet sweet tea') without a discernible psychological stance toward conflict, loss, or relationship—it is formal experiment in language materiality rather than defensive negotiation of affect.

author: Ezra Pound
content:
No, no! Go from me. I have left her lately.
I will not spoil my sheath with lesser brightness,   
For my surrounding air hath a new lightness;
Slight are her arms, yet they have bound me straitly   
And left me cloaked as with a gauze of æther;   
As with sweet leaves; as with subtle clearness.   
Oh, I have picked up magic in her nearness
To sheathe me half in half the things that sheathe her.   
No, no! Go from me. I have still the flavour,   
Soft as spring wind that’s come from birchen bowers.   
Green come the shoots, aye April in the branches,
As winter’s wound with her sleight hand she staunches,   
Hath of the trees a likeness of the savour:
As white their bark, so white this lady’s hours.
evidence: The speaker attributes magical, transformative powers to the beloved ('I have picked up magic in her nearness'), describing her in superlatives and nature imagery that elevate her beyond human dimension—she becomes seasons, healing force, and source of æther-like transformation that 'sheathes' and protects the speaker from all lesser experiences.

author: Walt Whitman
content:
I hear America singing, the varied carols I hear,
Those of mechanics, each one singing his as it should be blithe and strong,
The carpenter singing his as he measures his plank or beam,
The mason singing his as he makes ready for work, or leaves off work,
The boatman singing what belongs to him in his boat, the deckhand singing on the steamboat deck,
The shoemaker singing as he sits on his bench, the hatter singing as he stands,
The wood-cutter’s song, the ploughboy’s on his way in the morning, or at noon intermission or at sundown,
The delicious singing of the mother, or of the young wife at work, or of the girl sewing or washing,
Each singing what belongs to him or her and to none else,
The day what belongs to the day—at night the party of young fellows, robust, friendly,
Singing with open mouths their strong melodious songs.
evidence: The poem catalogs workers singing without distress, conflict, or psychological need to manage anxiety. It is descriptive celebration of democratic labor, not a defensive stance toward emotional threat.

author: Molly Peacock
content:
What if we got outside ourselves and there   
really was an outside out there, not just   
our insides turned inside out? What if there   
really were a you beyond me, not just   
the waves off my own fire, like those waves off   
the backyard grill you can see the next yard through,   
though not well -- just enough to know that off   
to the right belongs to someone else, not you.   
What if, when we said I love you, there were   
a you to love as there is a yard beyond   
to walk past the grill and get to? To endure   
the endless walk through the self, knowing through a bond   
that has no basis (for ourselves are all we know)   
is altruism: not giving, but coming to know   
someone is there through the wavy vision   
of the self's heat, love become a decision.
evidence: The speaker defines altruism as 'not giving, but coming to know / someone is there through the wavy vision / of the self's heat, love become a decision'—accurately recognizing the other's separate existence beyond projection ('What if there / really were a you beyond me, not just / the waves off my own fire') and choosing connection despite knowledge that 'ourselves are all we know.'

author: William Carlos Williams
content:
If I when my wife is sleeping
and the baby and Kathleen
are sleeping
and the sun is a flame-white disc
in silken mists
above shining trees,—
if I in my north room
dance naked, grotesquely
before my mirror
waving my shirt round my head
and singing softly to myself:
“I am lonely, lonely.
I was born to be lonely,
I am best so!”
If I admire my arms, my face,
my shoulders, flanks, buttocks
against the yellow drawn shades,—
Who shall say I am not
the happy genius of my household?
evidence: The speaker's private, naked dancing ritual while others sleep—'Who shall say I am not / the happy genius of my household?'—reveals grandiose self-investment coupled with magical belief in his centrality and sufficiency. The assertion of being 'born to be lonely, / I am best so!' while simultaneously claiming genius-status suggests omnipotent conviction that his solitary will and self-regard constitute household mastery.

author: William Carlos Williams
content:
Her body is not so white as
anemony petals nor so smooth—nor
so remote a thing. It is a field
of the wild carrot taking
the field by force; the grass
does not raise above it.
Here is no question of whiteness,
white as can be, with a purple mole
at the center of each flower.
Each flower is a hand’s span
of her whiteness. Wherever
his hand has lain there is
a tiny purple blemish. Each part
is a blossom under his touch
to which the fibres of her being
stem one by one, each to its end,
until the whole field is a
white desire, empty, a single stem,
a cluster, flower by flower,
a pious wish to whiteness gone over—
or nothing.
evidence: The speaker transforms sexual desire and physical possession ('Wherever his hand has lain there is a tiny purple blemish') into sustained aesthetic contemplation of botanical imagery, channeling erotic intensity into socially valued poetic form while maintaining the passion ('white desire') without adverse consequence.

author: David Baker
content:
It was midday before we noticed it was morning.
The boy cousins brought us a tray—soup and cheese,
warm soda, and a soft cloth and candy for her fever.
They wouldn’t come in, the tray weighing between them.
They stood like woodwork inside the door frame.
By afternoon the old procession—silence at the lip
of a dozen night travelers tired and grieving, one
by one, or pairs floating to the bed and back
with a touching of hands like humming,
and the one we gathered for slipping farther
for all the good we could do. She lay in her shadow.
She looked to no one. Her daylilies bobbed wide
open out in the wild, blue sun and the same bee
kept nosing her window to reach them.
Dusk: even the boys were back watching it try.
evidence: The speaker maintains full awareness of the dying woman ('slipping farther / for all the good we could do') while consciously attending to small details—the tray, the daylilies, the bee—postponing but not denying the grief that hovers over the scene. The temporal progression ('midday,' 'afternoon,' 'Dusk') marks incremental movement through the vigil, acknowledging both the inevitable loss and the finite waiting period with stoic observation.

author: David Baker
content:
If things were worse, this cursed rain
would soak me unto sickness,
so Samuel Sewall might have
written in his vespers journal.
I have it on my writing desk
inside. For three days I have
labored with a saw and plane
and many boards to make my girl
a swingset near her mother’s
lilac shrubs, as rain has drizzled
cold and meaningless. How
coherent was his world of works
and days, when Plentifull Rains
might connote a coming
providence—so Sewall notes
of Her Majesty’s Court, June
the eighteenth, seventeen twelve.            We are well satisfyed with the Layin out
            of our Money—
evidence: The speaker constructs an elaborate justificatory frame for his outdoor labor in cold rain ('If things were worse... would soak me unto sickness'), anchoring the project in historical precedent (Sewall's journals) to convert what might register as obsessive or driven behavior into something meaningful and providential. The nested quotations create cognitive distance from the affect of three days spent building a swingset in 'cold and meaningless' rain.

author: Virginia Hamilton Adair
content:
Past the fourth cloverleaf, by dwindling roads   
At last we came into the unleashed wind;
The Chesapeake rose to meet us at a dead end   
Beyond the carnival wheels and gingerbread.
Forsaken by summer, the wharf. The oil-green waves   
Flung yellow foam and sucked at disheveled sand.   
Small fish stank in the sun, and nervous droves   
Of cloud hastened their shadows over bay and land.
Beyond the NO DUMPING sign in its surf of cans   
And the rotting boat with nettles to the rails,   
The horse dung garlanded with jeweling flies   
And papers blown like a fleet of shipless sails,
We pushed into an overworld of wind and light   
Where sky unfettered ran wild from earth to noon,   
And the tethered heart broke loose and rose like a kite   
From sands that borrowed diamonds from the sun.
We were empty and pure as shells that air-drenched hour,   
Heedless as waves that swell at the shore and fall,   
Pliant as sea-grass, the rapt inheritors
Of a land without memory, where tide erases all.
evidence: The speaker transforms the experience of confronting squalid, desolate material reality (rotting boats, stinking fish, horse dung, garbage) into transcendent aesthetic experience through passionate, sustained poetic engagement—'the tethered heart broke loose and rose like a kite'—achieving genuine catharsis and creative transformation without loss of emotional authenticity or denial of the primitive content.

author: Eva Gore-Booth
content:
The princess in her world-old tower pined
A prisoner, brazen-caged, without a gleam
Of sunlight, or a windowful of wind;
She lived but in a long lamp-lighted dream.
They brought her forth at last when she was old;
The sunlight on her blanched hair was shed
Too late to turn its silver into gold.
“Ah, shield me from this brazen glare!” she said.
evidence: The princess, freed from lifelong imprisonment in darkness, recoils from liberation ('Ah, shield me from this brazen glare!'), transforming freedom into threat and requesting return to the very confinement that damaged her—the defense reveals itself in the excessive, strained reversal where rescue becomes assault.

author: Rhina P. Espaillat
content:
My father liked them separate, one there,
one here (allá y aquí), as if aware
that words might cut in two his daughter’s heart
(el corazón) and lock the alien part
to what he was—his memory, his name
(su nombre)—with a key he could not claim.
“English outside this door, Spanish inside,”
he said, “y basta.” But who can divide
the world, the word (mundo y palabra) from
any child? I knew how to be dumb
and stubborn (testaruda); late, in bed,
I hoarded secret syllables I read
until my tongue (mi lengua) learned to run
where his stumbled. And still the heart was one.
I like to think he knew that, even when,
proud (orgulloso) of his daughter’s pen,
he stood outside mis versos, half in fear
of words he loved but wanted not to hear.
evidence: The speaker consciously acknowledges the father's prohibition ('English outside this door, Spanish inside') yet deliberately postpones compliance ('late, in bed, / I hoarded secret syllables'), maintaining awareness of both loyalty to father and desire for linguistic integration without eliminating either—a finite, volitional deferral that preserves the conflict in consciousness while progressing toward resolution.

poem_name: “I think I should have loved you presently”
author: Edna St. Vincent Millay
content:
I think I should have loved you presently,
And given in earnest words I flung in jest;
And lifted honest eyes for you to see,
And caught your hand against my cheek and breast;
And all my pretty follies flung aside
That won you to me, and beneath your gaze,
Naked of reticence and shorn of pride,
Spread like a chart my little wicked ways.
I, that had been to you, had you remained,
But one more waking from a recurrent dream,
Cherish no less the certain stakes I gained,
And walk your memory’s halls, austere, supreme,
A ghost in marble of a girl you knew
Who would have loved you in a day or two.
evidence: The speaker converts a painful romantic loss into a cool, hypothetical scenario ('I think I should have loved you presently... in a day or two'), delivering affect-laden material in the detached conditional mood, transforming actual grief into theoretical calculus about what 'would have' occurred.

poem_name: “What lips my lips have kissed, and where, and why”
author: Edna St. Vincent Millay
content:
What lips my lips have kissed, and where, and why,
I have forgotten, and what arms have lain
Under my head till morning; but the rain
Is full of ghosts tonight, that tap and sigh
Upon the glass and listen for reply,
And in my heart there stirs a quiet pain
For unremembered lads that not again
Will turn to me at midnight with a cry.
Thus in the winter stands the lonely tree,
Nor knows what birds have vanished one by one,
Yet knows its boughs more silent than before:
I cannot say what loves have come and gone,
I only know that summer sang in me
A little while, that in me sings no more.
evidence: The speaker retains powerful affect ('quiet pain,' 'rain full of ghosts') while explicitly forgetting the ideational content—'I have forgotten' the specific lovers, cannot name 'what loves have come and gone.' The emotional music remains (loneliness, longing) but the words (identities, particulars) are genuinely lost, creating the hysteric's signature pattern of feeling without remembered content.

author: Marilyn Nelson
content:
It was like soul-kissing, the way the words
filled my mouth as Mrs. Purdy read from her desk.
All the other kids zoned an hour ahead to 3:15,
but Mrs. Purdy and I wandered lonely as clouds borne
by a breeze off Mount Parnassus. She must have seen
the darkest eyes in the room brim: The next day
she gave me a poem she’d chosen especially for me
to read to the all except for me white class.
She smiled when she told me to read it, smiled harder,
said oh yes I could. She smiled harder and harder
until I stood and opened my mouth to banjo playing
darkies, pickaninnies, disses and dats. When I finished
my classmates stared at the floor. We walked silent
to the buses, awed by the power of words.
evidence: The speaker recounts a traumatic racist humiliation with poetic, aestheticized language ('soul-kissing,' 'wandered lonely as clouds') and describes the classroom silence as 'awed by the power of words'—cognitively accurate about what happened but affectively detached, converting emotional devastation into an ironic literary observation.

poem_name: Oh, Hope! thou soother sweet of human woes
author: Charlotte Smith
content:
Oh, Hope! thou soother sweet of human woes!
      How shall I lure thee to my haunts forlorn!
For me wilt thou renew the withered rose,
      And clear my painful path of pointed thorn?
Ah come, sweet nymph! in smiles and softness drest,
      Like the young hours that lead the tender year
Enchantress come! and charm my cares to rest:
      Alas! the flatterer flies, and will not hear!
A prey to fear, anxiety, and pain,
      Must I a sad existence still deplore?
Lo! the flowers fade, but all the thorns remain,
      ‘For me the vernal garland blooms no more.’
Come then, ‘pale Misery’s love!’ be thou my cure,
And I will bless thee, who though slow art sure.
evidence: The speaker attributes exaggerated, omnipotent powers to Hope ('soother sweet,' 'Enchantress,' capable of renewing roses and clearing thorns), maintaining desperate conviction that merger with this idealized figure will counteract internal terror, then pivots to embrace 'pale Misery's love' when Hope disappoints—the archaic split between all-good savior and compensatory attachment.

author: Amy Uyematsu
content:
So by sixteen we move in packs
learn to strut and slide
in deliberate lowdown rhythm
talk in a syn/co/pa/ted beat
because we want so bad
to be cool, never to be mistaken
for white, even when we leave
these rowdier L.A. streets—
remember how we paint our eyes
like gangsters
flash our legs in nylons
sassy black high heels
or two inch zippered boots
stack them by the door at night
next to Daddy’s muddy gardening shoes.
evidence: The speaker claims agency and cultural identity through deliberate stylistic choices ('we move in packs / learn to strut and slide / in deliberate lowdown rhythm') that assert autonomy and group belonging without apology, directly expressing 'we want so bad / to be cool, never to be mistaken / for white'—a developmental achievement of separateness as empowering rather than threatening.

author: Philip Appleman
content:
I heard a child, a little under four years old, when asked what was meant by being in good spirits, answer, “It is laughing, talking, and kissing.”
—CHARLES DARWIN, The Expression of the Emotions in Man and Animals



1.   WALDORF-ASTORIA EUPHORIA,
THE JOY OF BIG CITIES
       Joy, when intense, leads to various purposeless
       movements—to dancing about, clapping the
       hands, stamping, etc.
evidence: The poem title and epigraph frame a descriptive exploration of joy's physical manifestations, citing Darwin on spontaneous movement; no psychological conflict or defensive stance emerges in this fragment.

author: Philip Appleman
content:
In Indianapolis they drive
five hundred miles and end up
where they started: survival
of the fittest. In the swamps
of Auburn and Elkhart,
in the jungles of South Bend,
one-cylinder chain-driven runabouts fall
to air-cooled V-4’s, a-speed gearboxes,
16-horse flat-twin midships engines—
carcasses left behind
by monobloc motors, electric starters,
3-speed gears, six cylinders, 2-chain drive,
overhead cams, supercharged
to 88 miles an hour in second gear, the age
of Leviathan ...    There is grandeur in this view of life,
    as endless forms
    most beautiful and wonderful
    are being evolved.
evidence: The speaker processes automotive industrial history through elaborate technical catalogs ('one-cylinder chain-driven runabouts fall / to air-cooled V-4's, a-speed gearboxes') delivered in detached, taxonomic tones that evacuate human consequence, culminating in Darwin's quoted grandeur applied to machines—a conceptually sophisticated operation that transforms economic displacement and obsolescence into bloodless evolutionary spectacle.

author: Rae Armantrout
content:
So these are the hills of home. Hazy tiers
nearly subliminal. To see them is to see
double, hear bad puns delivered with a wink.
An untoward familiarity.
Rising from my sleep, the road is more
and less the road. Around that bend are pale
houses, pairs of junipers. Then to look
reveals no more.
evidence: The speaker observes the doubling of perception ('to see them is to see double') and the gap between expectation and reality ('to look reveals no more') without deploying a defense mechanism—this is phenomenological description of how familiar places produce uncanny recognition effects, not a psychological stance toward conflict.

author: Lucille Clifton
content:
for some
it is stone
bare smooth
as a buttock
rounding
into the crevasse
of the world
for some
it is extravagant
water   mouths wide
washing together
forever   for some
it is fire
for some air
and for some
certain only of the syllables
it is the element they
search their lives for
eden
for them
it is a test
evidence: The speaker catalogs diverse experiences of pleasure/desire in detached, abstract categories (stone, water, fire, air, syllables) without affective engagement, delivering a taxonomy of erotic or spiritual longing in the emotionless tone of a weather report—'for some it is stone... for some it is extravagant water... for some it is fire'—maintaining observational distance from what should be charged material.

author: B. H. Fairchild
content:
Like the blue angels of the nativity, the museum patrons
hover around the art historian, who has arrived frazzled
and limp after waking late in her boyfriend’s apartment.And here, she notes, the Procession of St. Gregory,
where atop Hadrian’s mausoleum the angel of death
returns his bloody sword to its scabbard
evidence: The poem offers descriptive observation of a scene—museum patrons, an art historian, and her lecture on medieval imagery—without revealing psychological conflict or defensive stance in the speaker toward any distressing material.

poem_name: Early Occult Memory Systems of the Lower Midwest
author: B. H. Fairchild
content:
In his fifth year the son, deep in the backseat   
of his father’s Ford and the mysterium
of time, holds time in memory with words,night, this night, on the way to a stalled rig south   
of Kiowa Creek where the plains wind stacks   
the skeletons of weeds on barbed-wire fences   
and rattles the battered DeKalb sign to make   
the child think of time in its passing, of death.
Cattle stare at flat-bed haulers gunning clumps   
of black smoke and lugging damaged drill pipe   
up the gullied, mud-hollowed road. Road, this   
road
evidence: The child actively holds time in memory through words, engaging in realistic, affect-laden contemplation of future mortality ('think of time in its passing, of death') while experiencing modest present distress in the backseat mysterium—spreading anxiety through language itself as self-inoculation.

poem_name: On the Gift of a Book to a Child
author: Hilaire Belloc
content:
Child! do not throw this book about!   
   Refrain from the unholy pleasure   
Of cutting all the pictures out!
   Preserve it as your chiefest treasure.
Child, have you never heard it said   
   That you are heir to all the ages?
Why, then, your hands were never made
   To tear these beautiful thick pages!
Your little hands were made to take
   The better things and leave the worse ones:   
They also may be used to shake
   The Massive Paws of Elder Persons.
And when your prayers complete the day,   
   Darling, your little tiny hands
Were also made, I think, to pray   
   For men that lose their fairylands.
evidence: The speaker constructs elaborate justifications for why the child should preserve the book—framing it as cosmic inheritance ('heir to all the ages'), divine purpose ('your hands were never made / To tear'), and moral duty to pray for lost adults—converting what is likely anxiety about possessions being damaged into high-minded pedagogical imperatives about the child's destiny.

author: Hilaire Belloc
content:
As a friend to the children commend me the Yak.   
   You will find it exactly the thing:
It will carry and fetch, you can ride on its back,   
   Or lead it about with a string.
The Tartar who dwells on the plains of Thibet   
   (A desolate region of snow)
Has for centuries made it a nursery pet,   
   And surely the Tartar should know!
Then tell your papa where the Yak can be got,   
   And if he is awfully rich
He will buy you the creature—or else he will not.
   (I cannot be positive which.)
evidence: The speaker maintains full awareness of the absurd impracticality of acquiring a Yak while delivering the pitch with deadpan enthusiasm, culminating in the self-deflating parenthetical '(I cannot be positive which.)'—acknowledging reality while treating the whole proposition as play, making himself the butt of the joke through comic timing.

author: Hilaire Belloc
content:
Be kind and tender to the Frog,
   And do not call him names,
As ‘Slimy skin,’ or ‘Polly-wog,’
   Or likewise ‘Ugly James,’
Or ‘Gape-a-grin,’ or ‘Toad-gone-wrong,’   
   Or ‘Billy Bandy-knees’:
The Frog is justly sensitive
   To epithets like these.
No animal will more repay
   A treatment kind and fair;
At least so lonely people say
Who keep a frog (and, by the way,   
They are extremely rare).
evidence: The speaker confronts social cruelty toward the vulnerable (frogs, lonely frog-keepers) by responding as if it were a game, maintaining full awareness of the absurdity while inviting pleasure in both user and reader; the final parenthetical '(and, by the way, / They are extremely rare)' lands with perfect timing that deflates pretension without withdrawing attention from genuine isolation.

author: Hilaire Belloc
content:
The Bison is vain, and (I write it with pain)
   The Door-mat you see on his head
Is not, as some learned professors maintain,   
The opulent growth of a genius’ brain;
   But is sewn on with needle and thread.
evidence: The speaker confronts the absurd pretension of the bison's 'opulent growth of a genius' brain' by revealing it as a sewn-on door-mat, making the vain animal itself (not displaced others) the butt of the joke while maintaining full observing ego awareness of the playful deflation.

author: Billy Collins
content:
I pour a coating of salt on the table
and make a circle in it with my finger.
This is the cycle of life
I say to no one.
This is the wheel of fortune,
the Arctic Circle.
This is the ring of Kerry
and the white rose of Tralee
I say to the ghosts of my family,
the dead fathers,
the aunt who drowned,
my unborn brothers and sisters,
my unborn children.
This is the sun with its glittering spokes
and the bitter moon.
This is the absolute circle of geometry
I say to the crack in the wall,
to the birds who cross the window.
This is the wheel I just invented
to roll through the rest of my life
I say
touching my finger to my tongue.
evidence: The speaker transforms potential grief about family deaths and unborn children into abstract geometric propositions ('This is the cycle of life,' 'the absolute circle of geometry'), delivering emotionally charged content in a detached, theoretical manner like 'weather reports on the psyche'—conceptually acknowledging loss while remaining affectively evacuated.

author: W. S. Di Piero
content:
The whole world was there, plucking their linen,   
half-bald, mumbling, sucking on their moustache tips.   
Broadway was still in business and they asked no favors.
All the cracked ribs of Fredericksburg,
the boys who held their tongues at Chancellorsville   
as the bandages, mule shit, skin and shot
overran the Rappahannock’s banks   
and poured it in our mouths   
that summer.
He sat up half the night reading to the Army of the Potomac   
poems about trooping goats and crazy fathers   
chewing grass in the wilderness.
         It’s me that saved his life, dear mother,
         he had dysentery, bronchitis, and something else   
         the doctors couldn
evidence: The speaker transforms witnessing of Civil War trauma into caretaking service ('It's me that saved his life, dear mother'), finding gratification through tending wounded soldiers ('sat up half the night reading to the Army of the Potomac') while correctly attributing need to the suffering objects—the soldiers genuinely blessed by Whitman's nursing presence.

author: Sophie Jewett
evidence: The speaker explicitly acknowledges both the present truce ('This hour we will not care') and the awaiting battle ('just beyond to-morrow's gate'), consciously postponing attention to conflict with finite timeframe while maintaining full awareness of what is being deferred—'fickle flag of truce' and 'sweet abuse' reveal she knows the reprieve is temporary and illusory.

author: Sophie Jewett
content:
A noisome thing that crawls by covert path,
   For glad, unfearing feet to lie in wait;
No part in summer’s fellowship it hath,
   From mirth and love and music alienate.
Yet once it flashed across the close, brown grass
   In the noon sun, and, as it quivered there,
The spell of beauty over it did pass,
   Making it kin with earth and light and air.
I knew that Life’s imperial self decrees
   That this, the loathliest of living things,
By patient ways of cycled centuries,
   Slow creeping, shall at last attain to wings.
evidence: The speaker transforms revulsion toward a 'noisome' crawling creature into evolutionary meditation on beauty and destiny, channeling primitive disgust into philosophical vision that finds transcendent meaning ('Life's imperial self decrees / That this, the loathliest of living things... shall at last attain to wings'). The intensity of feeling—'loathliest,' 'noisome'—is maintained but redirected into socially valued reflection on transformation rather than simple avoidance.

author: Rudyard Kipling
content:
(‘“The Finest Story in the World”’—Many Inventions)



We pulled for you when the wind was against us and the sails were low.            Will you never let us go?
We ate bread and onions when you took towns, or ran aboard quickly when you were beaten back by the foe.
The Captains walked up and down the deck in fair weather singing songs, but we were below.
We fainted with our chins on the oars and you did not see that we were idle, for we still swung to and fro.            Will you never let us go?
The salt made the oar-handles like shark-skin; our knees were cut to the bone with salt-cracks; our hair was stuck to our foreheads; and our lips were cut to the gums, and you whipped us because we could not row.
            Will you never let us go?
But, in a little time, we shall run out of the port-holes as the water runs along the oar-blade, and though you tell the others to row after us you will never catch us till you catch the oar-thresh and tie up the winds in the belly of the sail. Aho!
            Will you never let us go?
