evidence: The speaker describes traumatic questioning (bridge fall/jump, identity interrogation) in fragmented, dreamlike syntax with theatrical distance ('My tongue is a spoon,' 'mouth is a splinter not telling'), converting trauma into riddle-like play while maintaining amnesia about her own name and actions—charm and fragmentation characteristic of dissociative consciousness.

poem_name: From the Lost Diary of Anna Anderson: II. Dalldorf Asylum: House 4, Ward B
author: Allison Titus
content:
II.   Dalldorf Asylum: House 4, Ward B
      The white coats still come.     Fraulein Unbekaant,
      they call me.
          Miss Unknown.
      One asks, Do I hear         voices       Do I          see things.
      The doctors here are not very well informed.
      When they appear at the foot of my bed,
      alabaster and looming        I hide my face
      in the sheets coarsened and brailled as if
      boiled in the well of a thousand drowned
      birds.
                                                                 So much worse
                                  is this quiet than the river’s drift and silt.
                                  Here I have only the rasp and cough of the woman
                                  bunked nearest me drooled to choking.
                                  On the floor, a puddle of saliva thickens.
evidence: The speaker recounts traumatic hospitalization with theatrical distance ('alabaster and looming') and sensory detachment, hiding face in sheets while narrating asylum conditions with curiously affectless, observational tone—describing saliva pooling without emotional bridge, creating fragmented consciousness that watches rather than inhabits experience.

author: James Joyce
content:
My love is in a light attire
     Among the apple trees,
Where the gay winds do most desire
     To run in companies.
There, where the gay winds stay to woo
     The young leaves as they pass,
My love goes slowly, bending to
     Her shadow on the grass.
And where the sky’s a pale blue cup
     Over the laughing land,
My love goes lightly, holding up
     Her dress with dainty hand.
evidence: The speaker observes his beloved moving through an idyllic landscape with direct pleasure and appreciation, maintaining full awareness of affect and object without distortion, displacement, or defensive transformation.

author: Donald (Grady) Davidson
content:
“The chambers of the sun, that nowFrom ancient melody have ceased.”



The doorways of the Sun were closed;    Its muted bells gave forth no sound.But while the windy prophets dozed    A child a little crevice found.He pulled with one small straining hand;    The massy door moved willingly.And he has wakened all the band    Of singers—they rise eagerly.Let now again the hinges move    In sweetly clanging melody;Unseat the dark blind from the groove;    Unleash the struggling harmony.The golden doors are opening    To ancient sounds of loveliness;The Sons of Light are issuing,    Winged with their antique mightiness.Who can sing the House of the Sun?    Who shall frame its dreadful art?His childhood never must be done!    And he must have a wondering heart!Burn all the manuscripts of shame!    Break every lute of brazen string!Utter, O living tongues, the flame!    Up, Dust, into the Sun, and sing!
evidence: The speaker transforms primitive, overwhelming energies ('the flame,' 'Dust') into 'ancient sounds of loveliness' and poetic creation itself, channeling raw intensity ('Burn all the manuscripts of shame!') into socially valued artistic activity ('Who can sing the House of the Sun?') while maintaining passionate engagement and real aesthetic consequences.

poem_name: Fortune [There’s only one horizon, yet it can be found]
author: Dobby Gibson
content:
There’s only one horizon, yet it can be found
in every direction we look.
You’d think it would be easier to get the hell out of here.
Just ask an iceberg.
In any Chinese restaurant, never order the 42.
Never answer your door during dinner,
it’s probably another little shit peddling Snickers.
Posing behind their windows,
the mannequins remind us of their absent stylist.
This is all hero worship.
This poem ends the same way they all do—
list everyone you’ve ever had sex with here:
evidence: The speaker delivers potentially emotional material—limits on escape, cynicism about human connection, culminating in a prompt about sexual history—in a detached, list-like 'weather report' tone that strips away affective resonance while maintaining cognitive engagement with these themes.

poem_name: Fortune [The neighbors will soon spread their confounding potluck before you.]
author: Dobby Gibson
content:
The neighbors will soon spread their confounding potluck before you.
Dressed in period garb, they wear sandals with socks.
They subscribe to Life magazine to experience
the present as if it were already the past.
Their flowering trees were engineered to never drop fruit.
Overhead, constellations of stickers glow from bedroom
ceilings as souvenirs from a time when life was lived outdoors.
All conversations end in silence. The trick is to make it purposeful.
It’s not going to get any easier, for these are the CliffsNotes.
evidence: The speaker catalogs surreal domestic details (sandals with socks, Life magazine subscriptions, fruitless trees, glow-in-the-dark stickers) in a detached, forensic tone, concluding with abstracted maxims ('All conversations end in silence. The trick is to make it purposeful') that convert potentially distressing alienation into affectless observation delivered like a weather report on suburban ennui.

author: Fred Marchant
content:
I woke to more rain, and felt in the dark
for how wet the sill was, then rolled back
to my radio, and a midnight preacher
in my earphone teaching about sin.
 
I learned that punishment would come
like lightning that surprises an innocent shore.
Thunder would follow me all my days,
stern reminder and sharp rebuke.
 
The long, sleek, and pointed call
that rose, as if in response, out of the estuary
of night and storm, said it knew well
what the given world gave, and wanted more.
evidence: The speaker wakes to prophetic warning about punishment coming 'like lightning' and 'thunder would follow me all my days,' engaging in realistic, affect-laden contemplation of future discomfort—classic anticipatory worry. The heron's call then transforms this anxious rehearsal into poetic witness, channeling primitive dread into creative observation.

author: Fred Marchant
content:
What god was it that would open
earth’s picture book and see the two
of us on a road, snowfields glittering
on every side and poplars bent like
the fingers of an old man clutching
what he loved about the sun?
 
Which one was it that would peer
into our thatched, white-washed
farmhouse, and see the fur, flies,
and shit-stained walls? Which one
laughed at the barbed wire fences,
the wall topped with broken glass?
 
Which of the many who came then,
gleaming and rimed in hard sunlight?
Which of those who bobbed like ice
along the winter shore? What did
we have that any god would want?
Quick, if you can find it, hide it.
evidence: The speaker transforms internal feelings of vulnerability, violation, and paranoid exposure into elaborate accusations that multiple external gods are maliciously surveilling and desiring what the speaker possesses ('Which one was it that would peer... and see the fur, flies, and shit-stained walls? Which one laughed... What did we have that any god would want?'), while maintaining the persecutory gaze throughout—the gods become repositories of the speaker's own intrusive scrutiny and critical judgment, culminating in the defensive command to 'hide it,' treating divine attention as external threat rather than internal shame.

author: Leslie Adrienne Miller
content:
He arrives in the courtyard with two cartons
of juice, each of which he’ll tip and drain
at one go in the heat, and a sack of food
for the roses. He looms over his tools,
blond and dusty as a stalk of ripe wheat,
surely someone’s prized lover. Centuries
bask among his hybrid teas, and he shakes
his capable handfuls of food into their beds
until nothing but roses nose the blues between lake
and garden, lake and sky, the lapse of lawn
where a party could be if those who lived here once
returned to pour the wine. She’d be the sort
to tuck a bud behind her ear, and he to catch
one in his teeth. But alas, we’re guests
of the present, expectant and sultry; all
graciousness is fled, and rain fills the spent
blooms, tumbles their tops, weighted with ruffles
and shocks of pink. The gardener too disappears
with his breeches the color of mustard and cinched
with a string, gone back into the pages of Hardy
or Lawrence. Perhaps, he’ll appear again Tuesday next,
but he won’t look any of us living in the eye.
evidence: The speaker populates an elaborate fantasy world where 'those who lived here once / returned to pour the wine,' imagining elegant gestures ('She'd be the sort / to tuck a bud behind her ear'), while maintaining observational distance from actual present relationships ('he won't look any of us living in the eye'). The gardener becomes a fantasy object 'gone back into the pages of Hardy / or Lawrence' rather than an interactable person.

author: Jason Shinder
content:
Many of my friends are alone
and know too much to be happy
though they still want to dive
to the bottom of the green ocean
and bring back a gold coin
in their hand. A woman I know wakes
in the late evening and talks
to her late husband,
the windows blank photographs.
On the porch, my brother,
hands in pockets,
stares at the flowing stream.
What’s wrong? Nothing.
The cows stand
in their own slow afternoons.
The horses gather
wild rose hips in the sun
the way I longed for someone
long ago. What was it like?
The door opening
and no one on either side.
evidence: The speaker catalogs emotionally significant material—friends who are alone and unhappy, a widow talking to her dead husband, a brother's silent distress, loneliness past and present—in declarative, detached syntax that maintains affective flatness. The brother's 'What's wrong? Nothing.' crystallizes the defense: awareness without emotional engagement.

author: Jason Shinder
content:
While lovers sleep, I dig my nails into the earth,
 
holding up traffic. Just now a cloud has pulled up
while I was talking to the Emptiness
 
of the Universe and my voice plugged into the waves
 
at the bottom of the ocean.
My heart is taped up like a child’s drawing
 
of the moon over the broken window of the sky
 
where the wind always comes back to fill my lungs.
I will dance on my shadow. I will open my mouth
 
with the air inside my mother’s coffin.
 
I will be the arrow breaking apart in the body
of the blackbird, which appears at my window, singing.
evidence: The speaker transforms primitive grief and dissolution anxiety (mother's death, bodily fragmentation) into sustained poetic utterance with real aesthetic consequences, maintaining emotional intensity while channeling death-wishes and mourning into formally controlled imagery: 'I will be the arrow breaking apart in the body / of the blackbird, which appears at my window, singing.'

author: Jason Shinder
content:
I will dive to the bottom of the hotel pool and find my mother’s hairpin.
 
With the mouth of a drowning woman on my lap,
I will add her breath to mine. In the dark, I will lay the thin white sheet
 
of the moonlight over the blue plums of my wife’s breasts.
 
With the new planet I discovered just when I thought I was losing my sight,
I will love another man because I will be a woman.
 
Everything important will never as yet have happened. Let it happen.
 
I will throw a lit match on the secrets my body
has kept from me and stand in the fire. The people I have sawed in half
 
will appear in my bedroom mirror, getting dressed.
evidence: The speaker enacts a series of ritualistic reparative gestures—diving for mother's hairpin, restoring the drowning woman's breath, covering the wife's breasts—that function as symbolic atonement for unspoken transgressions. The compulsive catalog of future acts ('I will...I will...I will') suggests magical thinking where each vow neutralizes some prior guilt, culminating in the violent self-immolation ('throw a lit match on the secrets') that promises to restore wholeness to those the speaker has 'sawed in half.'

author: Jason Shinder
content:
Because I am not married, I have the skin of an orange
 
that has spent its life in the dark. Inside the orange I am blind.
I cannot tell when a hand reaches in and breaks
 
the atoms of the blood. Sometimes a blackbird will bring the wind
 
into my hair. Or the yellow clouds falling on the cold floor
are animals fighting each other
 
out of their drifting misery. All the women I have known
 
have been ruined by fog and the deer crossing the field at night.
evidence: The speaker attributes his internal deadness and blindness to being unmarried (external circumstance), then projects causation outward: 'All the women I have known / have been ruined by fog and the deer'—locating damage in external agents rather than acknowledging internal states. The mechanical, affectless delivery of disturbing imagery ('atoms of the blood' breaking, women 'ruined') suggests isolation of affect operating alongside the projective attribution.

author: Tess Gallagher
content:
Heartbeat trembling
your kingdom
of leaves
near the ceremony
of water, I never
insisted on you. I admit
I delayed. I was the Empress
of Delay. But it can’t be
put off now. On the sacred branch
of my only voice – I insist.
Insist for us all,
which is the job
of the voice, and especially
of the poet. Else
what am I for, what use
am I if I don’t
insist?
There are messages to send.
Gatherings and songs.
Because we need
to insist. Else what are we
for? What use
are we?
evidence: The speaker moves from admission of past delay to urgent, repetitive insistence on voice—'I insist. / Insist for us all, / which is the job / of the voice'—claiming poetic autonomy and social function without apology, framing speech itself as necessary agency ('Else / what am I for, what use / am I if I don't / insist?').

author: Richard Garcia
content:
Both lying on our sides, making love in
spoon position when she’s startled, What’s that?
She means the enormous ship passing before you—
maybe not that large, is it a freighter
 
or a passenger ship? But it seems huge in the dark
and it’s so close. That’s a poem you say, D. H.
Lawrence—Have you built your ship of death,
have you? O build your ship of death,
 
for you will need it. Right here it would be good
if there were a small orchestra on board, you’d hear
them and say to her, That piece is called Autumn,
 
that’s what the brave musicians played as the Titanic
went under—and then you could name this poem “Autumn.”
But no, the ship is silent, its white lights glow in the darkness.
evidence: Speaker responds to partner's startled question about an enormous passing ship by citing D.H. Lawrence's 'Ship of Death' poem in detached, literary terms, then elaborates a hypothetical Titanic scenario complete with named musical piece, converting intimate vulnerability into aesthetic construction without emotional engagement.

author: Rachel Loden
content:
After the offending bit is popped out
these tiny stitches on your neck
are exquisite. Lips of the slit
don’t speak the way you think they should,
break into stupid song, blow kisses
at the doctor. Some piece
that kept insisting on itself
will spend a few weeks in a jar on holiday
with strangers, stained and diced
and separated neatly
from its secrets. You can only
wait, reading your book about the sex
lives of the saints, the lance
that pierced and then pulled slowly out
of Saint Teresa’s heart. A slice
is venerated in Milan, they say, an arm
in Lisbon, a single breast in Rome;
but her heart’s enthroned
behind the convent walls at Avila. Pink
under glass, it wears a tiny crown.
evidence: The speaker addresses medical trauma (cancer biopsy, waiting for pathology results) through elaborate cultural-historical meditation on Saint Teresa's dismembered relics, maintaining detached, almost scholarly tone ('they say,' cataloguing body parts in cities) while the body undergoes invasive procedures. Affect is drained into aesthetic observation ('tiny stitches...are exquisite') and literary reference rather than engaging the terror of potential diagnosis.

author: Mary Ruefle
content:
A boy from Brooklyn used to cruise on summer nights.
As soon as he’d hit sixty he’d hold his hand out the window,
cupping it around the wind. He’d been assured
this is exactly how a woman’s breast feels when you put
your hand around it and apply a little pressure. Now he knew,
and he loved it. Night after night, again and again, until
the weather grew cold and he had to roll the window up.
For many years afterwards he was perpetually attempting
to soar. One winter’s night, holding his wife’s breast
in his hand, he closed his eyes and wanted to weep.
He loved her, but it was the wind he imagined now.
As he grew older, he loved the word etcetera and refused
to abbreviate it. He loved sweet white butter. He often
pretended to be playing the organ. On one of his last mornings,
he noticed the shape of his face molded in the pillow.
He shook it out, but the next morning it reappeared.
evidence: The boy redirects sexual longing away from its natural object (woman's body) toward the wind, then in marriage 'holding his wife's breast / in his hand, he closed his eyes and wanted to weep. / He loved her, but it was the wind he imagined now'—the affect remains conscious but has been permanently reattached to a substitute.

author: Cole Swensen
content:
amid the growing craze for automatons
The voice within the device that moves      is not
                                                              (as if nothing human
could be quite that moving) My precious edgling: though                                                                                          some                                                                                          believethe answers be given by a man concealed,                                        these are speaking machines.
 
                                        They were risking their lives.
Usually a woman or a child, who woke up inside the oracle,
who swallowed the burning oil, and who forces the idols to speak?
 
                                        Though when the bishop Theophilus
broke open the statues at Alexandria, he found them hollow
 
                                        it does not necessarily follow that
 
The penalty for trickery was death.
                                        Such is the wealth of belief.
                                        Behind a finely painted sheet of shell
a voice unlatched surrounds the world.
evidence: The poem discusses deeply charged material—risk of death, burning oil, trickery punished by execution—in a curiously detached, documentary tone with fragmented syntax that strips emotional resonance from what should be viscerally disturbing content about bodies used as oracles.

poem_name: Variations on Some of Dante’s Last Lines
author: Norma Cole
content:
And move and hold back
entering by the highroad through the words
and fall like a person hit by sleep
arriving at the place without light
 
And fall like a dead body falls
and find there the great enemy
and come to a tower all of stones
such that through it the earth opens
 
We pass between the martyrs and the high walls
even up there water is pouring out
then turning and fording again
sling the noose from the roof of the house
 
And each and every vapor spent
over winning and not losing
in which it stands caught out
fleet then catapults like a stone
 
Filling our view
whereupon another valley is revealed
evidence: The poem presents a series of formal variations on Dante's imagery (descending, towers, martyrs, valleys) without a speaker's psychological stance toward stress or conflict—it functions as ekphrastic experimentation with source material.

author: Norma Cole
content:
“and then looks at
the stars” from the
bed in the ambulance
 
looks up at boughs of
trees shifting quickly
lit in blackness
 
blackening soft, deep
siren’s song—she died
several times that night
 
and only in the weeks
to come started and
started to come back
 
then forward which is
real life
evidence: The speaker recounts a near-death experience with cinematic detachment ('looks up at boughs of trees shifting quickly lit in blackness') and temporal fragmentation ('she died several times that night'), transforming overwhelming trauma into dream-like observation viewed from emotional distance.

author: Stuart Dybek
content:
A drain spout splashing
rusty stains on concrete,
 
the taste of doorknobs
you kiss before squinting
 
through the musty keyhole
at the knife-sharpener’s daughter,
 
while across the city
the knife-sharpener
 
limps his pushcart
with its dinging axles,
 
with its screeching whetstone
up wet alleys
 
crying: scissors! knives! axes!
evidence: The speaker's erotic/voyeuristic impulse toward the daughter is held in consciousness but redirected onto ritualistic sensory fixations (taste of doorknobs, drain spouts) and displaced outward onto the father's distant, mechanical labor; affect remains but reattaches to substitute objects while the knife-sharpener's screeching whetstone carries the symbolic cargo of forbidden desire.

author: Stuart Dybek
content:
It’s more than silhouettes tonight,
every window in the city lit,
shades lifted, curtains open.
As one suspected, the dark buildings
full of lovers, undressing
beneath light bulbs, before mirrors.
Men & women, men & men, women & women, embracing.
Even the loners visible, flickered
by single candles, touching
places they’ve usually chosen to keep secret.
So much nakedness!
And the streets empty
except for the newsboys moving through shadows,
leafless trees snatching underclothes
out of wind, the El clattering
above the roofs
like a strip of blue movie.
evidence: The speaker adopts a detached, cinematic gaze upon urban intimacy with curiosity rather than defensiveness—'As one suspected' suggests confirmation of hypothesis rather than management of internal conflict, and the inventory of exposed lovers creates a catalog unmarked by anxiety, guilt, or compensatory maneuver.

author: Jennifer Scappettone
content:
Lavenderish dusk
strapped for stays,
pomegranates under the rubberband
chucked for a glass Oz,
 
letdown
splayed by the pillar-shelves
to page upon the Ottoman:
 
his talk has wrought suit
amid citrus gapes
and pall dunked in the bowl
and grated sage
or cleaved clear paleo-pines.
 
Postgeist, upcast
California upon weed,
what banker yields
so fragrant a cant
as this vagrant cant?
evidence: The poem presents a fragmented aesthetic collage of images (poppies, pomegranates, Ottoman, citrus) without evident psychological conflict or defensive stance—language itself becomes the object of formal experiment rather than vehicle for managing internal distress.

author: Averill Curdy
content:
The cheap dropped ceiling 
     jumped like a pot-lid boiling when our upstairs neighbor 
   chased his girl that winter. 
        Falling out of summer’s skimpy tops 
  she’d want our phone. Her plush lips creased. Not exactly blonde, 
    but luteous, we thought, 
             pleased the right word was there for that shade 
    of slightly slutty mermaid. Wincing, we’d hear him punch 
   along the floor on crutch- 
         es, a giant bat trying to mince 
     a mayfly. Sex and Violence you called them; Blondie with 
     Dagwood on crystal meth, 
         I’d tell our friends over dinners stewed 
    in noise. Even his truck cowed. Black, smoked glass, outsized wheels 
    flaunted like chrome knuckles 
          we shrank from, ducked, afraid we’d find her 
     later, knocking at our door. Some nights we waited through 
   like captured prey. To you 
         I’d turn in bed, saying the furtive
     words against your back, I love ... You’d stroke my hair, or hip, 
     all our years the same flip 
           crack, I do, too.
evidence: The speaker transforms ongoing domestic violence into clever wordplay ('luteous,' 'Sex and Violence,' 'Dagwood on crystal meth') and dinner-party anecdotes, maintaining detached observational tone while the visceral threat goes emotionally unprocessed. The violence becomes aesthetic problem-solving ('pleased the right word was there') rather than felt distress.

author: William Carlos Williams
content:
Now that I have cooled to you
Let there be gold of tarnished masonry,
Temples soothed by the sun to ruin   
That sleep utterly.
Give me hand for the dances,            
Ripples at Philae, in and out,         
And lips, my Lesbian,         
Wall flowers that once were flame.         
Your hair is my Carthage         
And my arms the bow,         
And our words arrows         
To shoot the stars         
Who from that misty sea         
Swarm to destroy us.         
But you there beside me—         
Oh how shall I defy you,         
Who wound me in the night         
With breasts shining         
Like Venus and like Mars?         
The night that is shouting Jason         
When the loud eaves rattle         
As with waves above me         
Blue at the prow of my desire.
evidence: The speaker transforms emotional cooling into an elaborate aesthetic meditation on classical ruins, mythology, and celestial imagery, discussing the end of passion in detached, literary terms ('gold of tarnished masonry,' 'Temples soothed by the sun') while the affect remains evacuated despite topically addressing desire and loss.

author: Sara Teasdale
content:
In my heart the old love  
 Struggled with the new,  It was ghostly waking  
 All night through.   Dear things, kind things  
 That my old love said,  Ranged themselves reproachfully  
 Round my bed.   But I could not heed them,  
 For I seemed to see  Dark eyes of my new love  
 Fixed on me.   Old love, old love,  
 How can I be true?  Shall I be faithless to myself  
 Or to you?
evidence: The speaker vacillates between old and new love through compulsive questioning and symmetrical balancing ('Old love, old love, / How can I be true?'), treating the emotional betrayal as requiring ritualistic expiation through the very act of address to the abandoned object, seeking magical neutralization of guilt through formal acknowledgment.

author: Sara Teasdale
content:
Lyric night of the lingering Indian Summer,Shadowy fields that are scentless but full of singing, Never a bird, but the passionless chant of insects, 
    Ceaseless, insistent.  The grasshopper’s horn, and far-off, high in the maples, The wheel of a locust leisurely grinding the silence Under a moon waning and worn, broken, 
    Tired with summer.   Let me remember you, voices of little insects,Weeds in the moonlight, fields that are tangled with asters, Let me remember, soon will the winter be on us,
    Snow-hushed and heavy.  Over my soul murmur your mute benediction,While I gaze, O fields that rest after harvest,As those who part look long in the eyes they lean to, 
    Lest they forget them.
evidence: The speaker explicitly prepares for future loss ('Let me remember, soon will the winter be on us') while experiencing proportionate emotional distress in the present ('Let me remember... Lest they forget them'), spreading anxiety over time through conscious, affect-laden attention to what will be lost.

author: Rupert Brooke
content:
If I should die, think only this of me:
      That there’s some corner of a foreign field
That is for ever England. There shall be
      In that rich earth a richer dust concealed;
A dust whom England bore, shaped, made aware,
      Gave, once, her flowers to love, her ways to roam;
A body of England’s, breathing English air,
      Washed by the rivers, blest by suns of home.
And think, this heart, all evil shed away,
      A pulse in the eternal mind, no less
            Gives somewhere back the thoughts by England given;
Her sights and sounds; dreams happy as her day;
      And laughter, learnt of friends; and gentleness,
            In hearts at peace, under an English heaven.Poetry Out Loud Note: This poem has had two titles: “The Soldier” and “Nineteen-Fourteen: The Soldier”. The student may give either title during the recitation.
evidence: The speaker transforms primitive terror of death and bodily dissolution into elevated, aestheticized vision of death-as-consecration ('richer dust'), channeling mortality anxiety into socially valued patriotic poetry that maintains emotional intensity while achieving formal beauty. The impulse toward self-preservation is harnessed into enduring artistic legacy.

author: Amy Lowell
content:
The chatter of little people   
Breaks on my purpose
Like the water-drops which slowly wear the rocks to powder.
And while I laugh
My spirit crumbles at their teasing touch.
evidence: The speaker maintains observational distance while describing emotional erosion ('My spirit crumbles'), using geological metaphor (water-drops/rocks) to theorize distress without accessing its affect directly. The laugh signals awareness of the irritation but frames the corrosive process as detached phenomenon rather than felt experience.

poem_name: On the Birth of a Son
author: Su Tung-Po
content:
Families when a child is born
Hope it will turn out intelligent.
I, through intelligence
Having wrecked my whole life,
Only hope that the baby will prove
Ignorant and stupid.
Then he'll be happy all his days
And grow into a cabinet minister.
evidence: Speaker makes himself the butt of the joke ('I, through intelligence / Having wrecked my whole life'), directly confronting his painful reality while transforming it into paradoxical play that instructs and entertains. The bitter wisdom ('Then he'll be happy all his days / And grow into a cabinet minister') reconciles love and cynicism, naming political corruption while maintaining wry detachment.

author: Carl Sandburg
content:
(From tablet writing, Babylonian excavations of the 4th millennium B.C.)



Bilbea, I was in Babylon on Saturday night.I saw nothing of you anywhere.I was at the old place and the other girls were there, But no Bilbea.Have you gone to another house? or city?Why don’t you write?I was sorry. I walked home half-sick.Tell me how it goes.Send me some kind of a letter.And take care of yourself.
evidence: The speaker directly expresses disappointment, worry, and care toward an absent friend without distortion, displacement, or defensive transformation—'I was sorry. I walked home half-sick' maintains affective authenticity and relational concern without employing mechanisms to manage anxiety.

author: Edna St. Vincent Millay
content:
We were very tired, we were very merry—
We had gone back and forth all night on the ferry.
It was bare and bright, and smelled like a stable—
But we looked into a fire, we leaned across a table,
We lay on a hill-top underneath the moon;
And the whistles kept blowing, and the dawn came soon.
We were very tired, we were very merry—
We had gone back and forth all night on the ferry;
And you ate an apple, and I ate a pear,
From a dozen of each we had bought somewhere;
And the sky went wan, and the wind came cold,
And the sun rose dripping, a bucketful of gold.
We were very tired, we were very merry,
We had gone back and forth all night on the ferry.
We hailed, “Good morrow, mother!” to a shawl-covered head,
And bought a morning paper, which neither of us read;
And she wept, “God bless you!” for the apples and pears,
And we gave her all our money but our subway fares.
evidence: The speaker transforms exhaustion from the night's wandering into generous action—'we gave her all our money but our subway fares'—meeting the woman's emotional need (blessing them for fruit) while finding vicarious gratification in spontaneous giving. The narrative describes service to another during personal depletion without acknowledging own need, presenting the act as natural response rather than compensation.

author: Louis Untermeyer
content:
Eleven o’clock, and the curtain falls.
The cold wind tears the strands of illusion;
The delicate music is lost
In the blare of home-going crowds
And a midnight paper.
The night has grown martial;
It meets us with blows and disaster.
Even the stars have turned shrapnel,
Fixed in silent explosions.
And here at our door
The moonlight is laid
Like a drawn sword.
evidence: The poem describes an external transformation from theatrical illusion to martial reality without revealing the speaker's psychological stance toward this shift—it reports sensory and metaphorical change ('curtain falls,' 'wind tears strands of illusion,' 'stars have turned shrapnel') but does not demonstrate how the speaker manages internal conflict through defensive operations.

author: Léonie Adams
content:
These lovers’ inklings which our loves enmesh,   
Lost to the cunning and dimensional eye,   
Though tenemented in the selves we see,   
Not more perforce than azure to the sky,   
Were necromancy-juggled to the flesh,   
And startled from no daylight you or me.
For trance and silvemess those moons commend,   
Which blanch the warm life silver-pale; or look   
What ghostly portent mist distorts from slight   
Clay shapes; the willows that the waters took   
Liquid and brightened in the waters bend,   
And we, in love’s reflex, seemed loved of right.
Then no more think to net forthwith love’s thing,   
But cast for it by spirit sleight-of-hand;
Then only in the slant glass contemplate,   
Where lineament outstripping line is scanned,   
Then on the perplexed text leave pondering,   
Love’s proverb is set down transliterate.
evidence: The speaker transforms the experience of love into abstract philosophical puzzles ('trance and silverness,' 'necromancy-juggled,' 'spirit sleight-of-hand'), delivering elaborate theoretical constructs about love's nature in emotionless, highly conceptual language that strikes as a weather report on the psyche rather than lived emotional experience.

author: Basil Bunting
content:
Muzzle and jowl and beastly brow,
bilious glaring eyes, tufted ears,
recidivous criminality in the slouch,
—This is not the latest absconding bankrupt
but a ‘beautiful’ tiger imported at great expense from   
Kuala Lumpur.
7 photographers, 4 black-and-white artists and an R.A.   
are taking his profitable likeness;   
28 reporters and an essayist
are writing him up.
Sundry ladies think he is a darling   
especially at mealtimes, observing
that a firm near the docks advertises replicas
fullgrown on approval for easy cash payments.
♂Felis Tigris (Straits Settlements) (Bobo) takes exercise   
up and down his cage before feeding
in a stench of excrements of great cats
indifferent to beauty or brutality.
He is said to have eaten several persons
but of course you can never be quite sure of these things.
evidence: The speaker catalogs brutal facts—criminality, stench of excrement, reports of the tiger eating persons—in a detached, inventory-like tone devoid of emotional response, culminating in 'indifferent to beauty or brutality' which mirrors the speaker's own affective evacuation.

author: Janet Loxley Lewis
content:
Below the gardens and the darkening pines
The living water sinks among the stones,
Sinking yet foaming till the snowy tones
Merge with the fog drawn landward in dim lines.
The cloud dissolves among the flowering vines,
And now the definite mountain-side disowns
The fluid world, the immeasurable zones.
Then white oblivion swallows all designs.
But still the rich confusion of the sea,
Unceasing voice, sombre and solacing,
Rises through veils of silence past the trees;
In restless repetition bound, yet free,
Wave after wave in deluge fresh releasing
An ancient speech, hushed in tremendous ease.
evidence: The speaker channels primitive dissolution-anxiety ('white oblivion swallows all designs') into carefully wrought poetic form that transforms annihilation into aesthetic achievement—the sonnet's formal containment and elevated diction ('sombre and solacing,' 'tremendous ease') harness chaos into socially valued art without loss of emotional intensity.

author: Malcolm Cowley
content:
Not that the pines were darker there,   
nor mid-May dogwood brighter there,   
nor swifts more swift in summer air;
    it was my own country,
having its thunderclap of spring,   
its long midsummer ripening,   
its corn hoar-stiff at harvesting,
    almost like any country,
yet being mine; its face, its speech,   
its hills bent low within my reach,   
its river birch and upland beech
    were mine, of my own country.
Now the dark waters at the bow
fold back, like earth against the plow;   
foam brightens like the dogwood now
    at home, in my own country.
evidence: The speaker channels loss and separation (implied voyage away from homeland) into aesthetic contemplation and lyric craft, transforming longing into formal poetry that maintains emotional authenticity while achieving beauty; the present-tense images ('foam brightens like the dogwood now') sustain both the intensity of attachment and productive creative expression with real consequences.

author: E. E. Cummings
content:
love is more thicker than forget
more thinner than recall
more seldom than a wave is wet
more frequent than to fail
it is most mad and moonly
and less it shall unbe
than all the sea which only
is deeper than the sea
love is less always than to win
less never than alive
less bigger than the least begin
less littler than forgive
it is most sane and sunly
and more it cannot die
than all the sky which only
is higher than the sky
evidence: The poem is a formal meditation on the paradoxical nature of love using logical syntax and comparative structures—it exhibits no psychological defense stance toward conflict, trauma, or relational distress.

author: Weldon Kees
content:
“A equals X,” says Mister One.   
“A equals B,” says Mister Two.   
“A equals nothing under the sun   
But A,” says Mister Three. A few   
Applaud; some wipe their eyes;   
Some linger in the shade to see   
One and Two in neat disguise   
Decapitating Mister Three.
“This age is not entirely bad.”
It’s bad enough, God knows, but you   
Should know Elizabethans had   
Sweeneys and Mrs. Porters too.   
The past goes down and disappears,   
The present stumbles home to bed,   
The future stretches out in years
That no one knows, and you’ll be dead.
evidence: The speaker delivers philosophical abstractions about epistemology and historical continuity ('A equals X,' 'This age is not entirely bad') in detached, summary tones, culminating in the utterly affectless announcement 'you'll be dead'—treating mortality and violence as weather reports rather than emotionally charged material.

author: Weldon Kees
content:
Plurality is all. I walk among the restaurants,
the theatres, the grocery stores; I ride the cars
and hear of Mrs. Bedford’s teeth and Albuquerque,
strikes unsettled, someone’s simply marvelous date,
news of the German Jews, the baseball scores,
storetalk and whoretalk, talk of wars. I turn
the pages of a thousand books to read
the names of Buddha, Malthus, Walker Evans, Stendhal, André Gide,
Ouspenski; note the terms: obscurantism,
factorize, fagaceous, endocarp; descend
the nervous stairs to hear the broken ends
of songs that float through city air.
In Osnabrück and Ogden, on the Passamaquoddy Bay,
in Ahmednagar, Waco (Neb.), in Santa Fé,
propelled by zeros, zinc, and zephyrs, always I’m pursued
by thoughts of what I am, authority, remembrance, food,
the letter on the mezzanine, the unemployed, dogs’ lonely faces, pianos and decay.
Plurality is all. I sympathize, but cannot grieve
too long for those who wear their dialectics on their sleeves.
The pattern’s one I sometimes rather like; there’s really nothing wrong
with it for some. But I should add: It doesn’t wear for long,
before I push the elevator bell and quickly leave.
evidence: The speaker catalogs vast accumulations of experience, knowledge, and emotional material ('German Jews, baseball scores, storetalk and whoretalk, talk of wars') but processes it all in the same detached, list-making register—'weather reports on the psyche' that acknowledge content without emotional engagement, culminating in 'I sympathize, but cannot grieve.'

author: Mary Barnard
content:
Rotting in the wet gray air
the railroad depot stands deserted under
still green trees. In the fields
cold begins an end.
There were other too-long-postponed departures.
They left, finally, because of well water
gone rank, the smell of fungus, the chill
of rain in chimneys.
The spot is abandoned even in memory.
They knew, locking doors upon empty houses,
to leave without regret is to lose
title to one home forever.
evidence: The speaker acknowledges 'too-long-postponed departures' and frames leaving as conscious, deliberate act ('They knew, locking doors') that balances loss against necessity—maintaining full awareness of both attachment ('to lose title to one home forever') and the concrete reasons for departure (well water, fungus, chill) without repression or avoidance.

author: Babette Deutsch
content:
Into this net of leaves, green as old glass   
That the sun fondles, trembling like images
In water, this live net, swung overhead
From branch to branch, what swam? The spider’s thread
Is less passive, where it appears to float
Like a bright hair clinging to the wind’s coat.
Hot at work, history neither schemes nor grieves   
Here where the soaking dead are last year’s leaves,
And over them slung, meshed with sun, a net   
No creature wove, none frantically tried to fret.
The huge weight of time without its sting   
Hangs in that greenly cradling woof. A wing
Has caught there, held. Held. But not to stay,   
We know, who, how slowly, walk away.
evidence: The speaker contemplates mortality and time's passage with measured affect—'The huge weight of time without its sting'—acknowledging the wing 'caught there, held' and recognizing 'But not to stay, / We know, who, how slowly, walk away.' This realistic, affect-laden preparation for future loss spreads anxiety over time through contemplative observation.

author: Richard Wilbur
content:
A striped blouse in a clearing by Bazille   
Is, you may say, a patroness of boughs   
Too queenly kind toward nature to be kin.   
But ceremony never did conceal,
Save to the silly eye, which all allows,
How much we are the woods we wander in.
Let her be some Sabrina fresh from stream,
Lucent as shallows slowed by wading sun,
Bedded on fern, the flowers’ cynosure:
Then nymph and wood must nod and strive to dream   
That she is airy earth, the trees, undone,
Must ape her languor natural and pure.
Ho-hum. I am for wit and wakefulness,   
And love this feigning lady by Bazille.   
What's lightly hid is deepest understood,   
And when with social smile and formal dress   
She teaches leaves to curtsey and quadrille,   
I think there are most tigers in the wood.
evidence: The speaker processes the tension between nature and civilization through abstract aesthetic discourse ('ceremony never did conceal'), maintaining cognitive engagement with human-nature relations while the affect remains theoretical. The 'Ho-hum' signals affective flatness despite topical engagement, converting what could be visceral encounter into bloodless meditation on concealment.

author: Weldon Kees
content:
September was when it began.
Locusts dying in the fields; our dogs
Silent, moving like shadows on a wall;
And strange worms crawling; flies of a kind
We had never seen before; huge vineyard moths;
Badgers and snakes, abandoning
Their holes in the field; the fruit gone rotten;
Queer fungi sprouting; the fields and woods
Covered with spiderwebs; black vapors
Rising from the earth - all these,
And more began that fall. Ravens flew round
The hospital in pairs. Where there was water,
We could hear the sound of beating clothes
All through the night. We could not count
All the miscarriages, the quarrels, the jealousies.
And one day in a field I saw
A swarm of frogs, swollen and hideous,
Hundreds upon hundreds, sitting on each other,
Huddled together, silent, ominous,
And heard the sound of rushing wind.
evidence: The speaker catalogs apocalyptic natural phenomena in flat, documentary syntax without expressing personal emotion, distress, or psychological management of the material—this is descriptive witness, not defensive transformation.

author: Alan Dugan
content:
The skin ripples over my body like moon-wooed water,
rearing to escape me. Where could it find another
animal as naked as the one it hates to cover?
Once it told me what was happening outside,
who was attacking, who caressing, and what the air
was doing to feed or freeze me. Now I wake up
dark at night, in a textureless ocean of ignorance,
or fruit bites back and water bruises like a stone.
It’s jealousy, because I look for other tools to know
with, and other armor, better girded to my wish.
So let it lie, turn off the clues or try to leave:
sewn on me seamless like those painful shirts
the body-hating saints wore, the sheath of hell
is pierced to my darkness nonetheless: what traitors
labor in my face, what hints they smuggle through
its arching guard! But even in the night it jails,
with nothing but its lies and silences to feed upon,
the jail itself can make a scenery, sing prison songs,
and set off fireworks to praise a homemade day.
evidence: The speaker rigidly divides the skin into all-bad betrayer ('traitors labor in my face,' 'arching guard,' 'lies and silences') while the imprisoned interior self remains separate, capable of pure creative transcendence ('make a scenery, sing prison songs'). The body-envelope is wholly treacherous prison; the contained consciousness wholly generative—no integration or ambivalence between these segregated aspects of self.

author: Frank O'Hara
content:
The white chocolate jar full of petals
swills odds and ends around in a dizzying eye   
of four o’clocks now and to come. The tiger,   
marvellously striped and irritable, leaps   
on the table and without disturbing a hair   
of the flowers’ breathless attention, pisses   
into the pot, right down its delicate spout.
A whisper of steam goes up from that porcelain   
urethra. “Saint-Saëns!” it seems to be whispering,   
curling unerringly around the furry nuts   
of the terrible puss, who is mentally flexing.   
Ah be with me always, spirit of noisy   
contemplation in the studio, the Garden   
of Zoos, the eternally fixed afternoons!   
There, while music scratches its scrofulous   
stomach, the brute beast emerges and stands,   
clear and careful, knowing always the exact peril   
at this moment caressing his fangs with   
a tongue given wholly to luxurious usages;   
which only a moment before dropped aspirin   
in this sunset of roses, and now throws a chair   
in the air to aggravate the truly menacing.
evidence: The speaker narrates a tiger urinating into a teapot as if watching from dreamlike distance, transforming violence ('throws a chair / in the air to aggravate the truly menacing') into theatrical performance with no emotional alarm—the stance is charmed witness to chaotic domestic menace rendered as entertainment rather than threat.

poem_name: For a Girl Killed at Sea
author: Calvin Thomas Jr.
content:
I see the ships, the plotted crash,
The stateroom’s purgatory trash,
The waiting wedged and still no splash.
There is the torch that burns not through
Unless it drowns the sailor crew
Shoring the bulkhead pinning you.
And then the priest who, being ill,
Intones through steel the bitter pill:
This tomb is your last confession grille.
I think of you awake in bed,
Praying what all the voyage said:
Have done with dying and be dead.
It is a pride in loneliness
Like some propriety of dress
That shuns the water meant to bless;
My hand as from a magnet pole
Works to the Sunday dipping bowl
To spot my tie and cross my soul.
evidence: The speaker witnesses catastrophic death ('This tomb is your last confession grille') then ritually dips fingers in Sunday water 'To spot my tie and cross my soul'—a compulsive gesture that treats the holy-water blessing as magical atonement for witnessing or imagining the girl's suffering, with the superstitious 'cross my soul' revealing omnipotent fantasy that ritual can neutralize guilt over thoughts of her drowning.

author: Edgar Bowers
content:
I’ll go among the dead to see my friend.
The place I leave is beautiful: the sea
Repeats the winds’ far swell in its long sound,
And, there beside it, houses solemnly
Shine with the modest courage of the land,
While swimmers try the verge of what they see.
I cannot go, although I should pretend
Some final self whose phantom eye could see
Him who because he is not cannot change.
And yet the thought of going makes the sea,
The land, the swimmers, and myself seem strange,
Almost as strange as they will someday be.
evidence: The speaker worries about a future encounter with his dead friend, spreading anxiety across the present moment ('the thought of going makes the sea, / The land, the swimmers, and myself seem strange') while experiencing modest affective distress—not catastrophizing but realistically preparing for the eventual inevitability ('Almost as strange as they will someday be').

author: George Oppen
content:
Her arms around me—child—
Around my head, hugging with her whole arms,
Whole arms as if I were a loved and native rock,
The apple in her hand—her apple and her father,
                   and my nose pressed
Hugely to the collar of her winter coat—. There
                   in the photograph
It is the child who is the branch
We fall from, where would be bramble,
Brush, bramble in the young Winter
With its blowing snow she must have thought
Was ours to give to her.
evidence: The speaker acknowledges the child's winter illusion ('she must have thought / Was ours to give to her') with modest affective distress, spreading anxiety over mortality and generational limits through contemplation of the photograph—realistic worrying about future separation ('the child who is the branch / We fall from') while experiencing proportionate sadness in the present.

author: Alan Dugan
content:
For old Billy Dugan, shot in the ass in the Civil War, my father said.



The old wound in my ass has opened up again, but I am past the prodigiesof youth’s campaigns, and weepwhere I used to laughin war’s red humors, halfin love with silly-assed painsand half not feeling them. I have to sit up with an indoor unsittable itch before I go down lateand weeping to the storm-cellar on a dirty nightand go to bed with the worms.So pull the dirt up over me and make a family joke for Old Billy Blue Balls,the oldest private in the worldwith two ass-holes and noplace more to go to for a laughexcept the last one. Say:The North won the Civil Warwithout much help from me although I wear a proof of the war’s obscenity.
evidence: The speaker directly confronts mortality and bodily degradation ('old wound in my ass,' 'go to bed with the worms') while simultaneously transforming the material into dark comedy ('Old Billy Blue Balls,' 'two ass-holes') that makes himself the butt of the joke. The voice maintains full awareness of death's approach yet responds 'as if it were a game,' crafting a family joke from obscene suffering without looking away from the painful reality.

author: Robert Creeley
content:
As soon as   
I speak, I   
speaks. It
wants to   
be free but   
impassive lies
in the direction   
of its
words. Let
x equal x, x   
also
equals x. I
speak to   
hear myself   
speak? I
had not thought   
that some-
thing had such
undone. It   
was an idea   
of mine.
evidence: The speaker converts a crisis of selfhood ('I speaks' the moment speech begins) into algebraic equations ('Let x equal x') and abstract linguistic analysis, maintaining cognitive engagement while evacuating emotional resonance from the profound dissociation between 'I' and 'speaks.'

author: Thomas McGrath
content:
I see him moving, in his legendary fleece,
Between the superhighway and an Algonquin stone axe;
Between the wild tribes, in their lost heat,
And the dark blizzard of my Grandfather’s coat;
Cold with the outdoor cold caught in the curls,
Smelling of the world before the poll tax.
And between the new macadam and the Scalp Act
They got him by the short hair; had him clipped
Who once was wild—and all five senses wild—
Printing the wild with his hoof’s inflated script
Before the times was money in the bank,
Before it was a crime to be so mild.
But history is a fact, and moves on feet
Sharper than his, toward wallows deeper than.
And the myth that covered all his moving parts,
Grandfather’s time had turned into a coat;
And what kept warm then, in the true world’s cold
Is old and cold in a world his death began.
evidence: The speaker constructs an elaborate historical-conceptual framework ('between the superhighway and an Algonquin stone axe,' 'Before the times was money in the bank') to meditate on loss and extinction, yet the poem's tone remains curiously detached—a theoretical weather report on cultural death and personal heritage that acknowledges emotional territory ('what kept warm then') without accessing gut-level grief or mourning.

author: Amiri Baraka
content:
She stands beside me, stands away,   
the vague indifference
of her dreams. Dreaming, to go on,   
and go on there, like animals fleeing   
the rise of the earth. But standing   
intangible, my lust a worked anger
a sweating close covering, for the crudely salty soul.
Then back off, and where you go? Box of words   
and pictures. Steel balloons tied to our mouths.   
The room fills up, and the house. Street tilts.   
City slides, and buildings slide into the river.   
What is there left, to destroy? That is not close,   
or closer. Leaning away in the angle of language.   
Pumping and pumping, all our eyes criss cross
and flash. It is the lovers pulling down empty structures.   
They wait and touch and watch their dreams   
eat the morning.
evidence: The speaker's stance oscillates between embodied desire ('my lust a worked anger / a sweating close covering') and dreamlike fragmentation ('Box of words / and pictures. Steel balloons tied to our mouths'), with catastrophic imagery that transforms overwhelming intimacy into playful apocalypse ('City slides, and buildings slide into the river') while maintaining theatrical distance from emotional contact.

author: George Starbuck
content:
“Stephen Smith, University of Iowa sophomore, burned what he said was his draft card”
and Norman Morrison, Quaker, of Baltimore Maryland, burned what he said was himself.
You, Robert McNamara, burned what you said was a concentration
of the Enemy Aggressor.
No news medium troubled to put it in quotes.
And Norman Morrison, Quaker, of Baltimore Maryland, burned what he said was himself.
He said it with simple materials such as would be found in your kitchen.
In your office you were informed.
Reporters got cracking frantically on the mental disturbance angle.
So far nothing turns up.
Norman Morrison, Quaker, of Baltimore Maryland, burned, and while burning, screamed.
No tip-off. No release.
Nothing to quote, to manage to put in quotes.
Pity the unaccustomed hesitance of the newspaper editorialists.
Pity the press photographers, not called.
Norman Morrison, Quaker, of Baltimore Maryland, burned and was burned and said
all that there is to say in that language.
Twice what is said in yours.
It is a strange sect, Mr. McNamara, under advice to try
the whole of a thought in silence, and to oneself.
evidence: The speaker maintains dual awareness—observing both the historical event and the psychological architecture of silence around it ('Pity the unaccustomed hesitance of the newspaper editorialists'), while directly asserting moral clarity to McNamara without aggression or distortion: 'It is a strange sect, Mr. McNamara, under advice to try the whole of a thought in silence, and to oneself.'

author: Thomas McGrath
content:
In the chill rains of the early winter I hear something—
A puling anger, a cold wind stiffened by flying bone—
Out of the north ...
and remember, then, what’s up there:
That ghost-bank: home: Amchitka: boot hill ....
They must be very tired, those ghosts; no flesh sustains them
And the bones rust in the rain.
Reluctant to go into the earth
The skulls gleam: wet; the dog-tag forgets the name;
The statistics (wherein they were young) like their crosses, are weathering out,
They must be very tired.
But I see them riding home,
Nightly: crying weak lust and rage: to stand in the dark,
Forlorn in known rooms, unheard near familiar beds:
Where lie the aging women: who were so lovely: once.
evidence: The speaker experiences affect-laden planning for future discomfort ('I hear something... Out of the north'), spreading anxiety through self-inoculation with anticipatory doses of emotional pain while maintaining conscious awareness of idea, affect, and object—the dead soldiers, their degradation, and his own mortality—keeping distress modest but present throughout ('They must be very tired').

author: Constance Urdang
content:
If I am in the house
beams posts planks siding slate
protect us
                Wall
guard us against the night-terrors
Floor shore us up above the void below
cover us roof
enclose us from the void above
door keep out the angry stranger
Hearth cherish the fire
windows be beacons
breathe out my warm air chimney
while I am in the house
In this room
my eyes be twelve-paned windows
clock pump my blood
cover my nakedness, rug
curtains be eyelids
sofa, I rest on your strong thighs
Where is the soul’s seat?
Doctors have cut up frogs and not found love.
Is this my reason?
I in myself
evidence: The speaker transforms vulnerability and terror into an elaborate system of protective imperatives ('protect us,' 'guard us,' 'cover us'), constructing exaggerated safety through architectural reverence while leakage appears in the obsessive repetition of commands and the void's persistent presence. The strained tone ('cherish the fire,' 'be beacons') carries the excessive quality of reaction formation against underlying abandonment terror.

author: Sandra McPherson
content:
I accompany this life’s events like a personal journalist:
“Little did she know when she got in the car that afternoon ...”;
or “Despite inauspicious beginnings,
this was to be their happiest year.”
Little did I expect that our horoscopes would prove true.
And how could we foresee an answer to
that frankly secular prayer, we with so little faith
as to be false prophets to our most fortunate gifts.
I am glad when doom fails. Inept apocalypse
is a specialty of the times: the suffering of the rich
at the hand of riches; the second and third comings of wars.
Shouldn’t we refuse prediction
that the untried today is guilty, that immeasurable
as this child’s hope is, it will break tomorrow?
evidence: The speaker engages in explicit anticipatory thinking ('Little did I expect that our horoscopes would prove true' / 'how could we foresee') about future outcomes while simultaneously observing this defensive process with metacognitive awareness ('I accompany this life's events like a personal journalist'). The affect-laden planning is evident in the worry about prediction and breaking hope, yet this is tempered by self-reflective distance that notes the pattern itself.

author: Brenda Hillman
content:
In movies when the hero is about to die,
He scatters a few phrases in a place like this,
Hoping the words will come up again
Immortal, or the grasses will reach out for him
As now they do for us.
Someone has planted a row of little trees
To stop the wind. Instead they’ve learned
To bend like the elect
In one direction only; they know
The sea will shatter them.
Isn’t it always like this?
Something uncontrollable becomes the hero,
Taking off its dress, the ice plants
Sunburn from the center out
So we can see that their deaths
Of splendid rust and yellow are not ours,
We are allowed again the glare
Of the sand, the druid hills,
The grasses brushing the legs, though
Just to have felt it once would have been enough.
evidence: The speaker spreads anxiety over time through realistic worrying about future discomfort ('the sea will shatter them'), experiencing modest affective distress while contemplating mortality and loss, yet maintaining calm acknowledgment that 'Just to have felt it once would have been enough'—preparing emotionally for inevitable endings.

author: Gary Soto
content:
The clouds shouldered a path up the mountains
East of Ocampo, and then descended,
Scraping their bellies gray on the cracked shingles of slate.
They entered the valley, and passed the roads that went
Trackless, the houses blown open, their cellars creaking
And lined with the bottles that held their breath for years.
They passed the fields where the trees dried thin as hat racks
And the plow’s tooth bit the earth for what endured.
But what continued were the wind that plucked the birds spineless
And the young who left with a few seeds in each pocket,
Their belts tightened on the fifth notch of hunger—
Under the sky that deafened from listening for rain.
evidence: The speaker maintains consistent third-person descriptive distance throughout, documenting environmental devastation and human suffering without revealing personal psychological investment, conflict, or defensive operation. No defense mechanism is employed—this is witness reportage.

author: John Ciardi
content:
A boy came up the street and there was a girl.   
"Hello," they said in passing, then didn’t pass.   
They began to imagine. They imagined all night   
and woke imagining what the other imagined.   
Later they woke with no need to imagine.
They were together. They kept waking together.   
Once they woke a daughter who got up
and went looking for something without looking back.   
But they had one another. Then one of them died.   
It makes no difference which. Either. The other   
tried to imagine dying, and couldn’t really,
but died later, maybe to find out,
though probably not. Not everything that happens   
is a learning experience. Maybe nothing is.
evidence: The speaker narrates profound life events—meeting, intimacy, parenthood, death—in detached, summary form ('It makes no difference which. Either') and concludes with abstract generalization about learning experiences, converting emotionally charged material into affectless reportage resembling 'weather reports on the psyche.'

author: Dannie Abse
content:
Some prowl sea-beds, some hurtle to a star
and, mother, some obsessed turn over every stone
or open graves to let that starlight in.
There are men who would open anything.
Harvey, the circulation of the blood,
and Freud, the circulation of our dreams,
pried honourably and honoured are
like all explorers. Men who’d open men.
And those others, mother, with diseases
like great streets named after them: Addison,
Parkinson, Hodgkin—physicians who’d arrive
fast and first on any sour death-bed scene.
I am their slowcoach colleague, half afraid,
incurious. As a boy it was so: you know how
my small hand never teased to pieces
an alarm clock or flensed a perished mouse.
And this larger hand’s the same. It stretches now
out from a white sleeve to hold up, mother,
your X-ray to the glowing screen. My eyes look
but don’t want to; I still don’t want to know.
evidence: The speaker catalogs explorers and physicians with elaborate historical detail ('Harvey, the circulation of the blood, / and Freud, the circulation of our dreams') while delivering the emotional core—reading his mother's X-ray—in a curiously detached tone that acknowledges fear ('half afraid') yet maintains clinical distance even in direct address.

author: Gerald Stern
content:
I wanted to know what it was like before we
had voices and before we had bare fingers and before we
had minds to move us through our actions
and tears to help us over our feelings,
so I drove my daughter through the snow to meet her friend
and filled her car with suitcases and hugged her
as an animal would, pressing my forehead against her,
walking in circles, moaning, touching her cheek,
and turned my head after them as an animal would,
watching helplessly as they drove over the ruts,
her smiling face and her small hand just visible
over the giant pillows and coat hangers
as they made their turn into the empty highway.
evidence: The speaker deliberately strips away human symbolic capacities ('before we had voices...minds...tears') and enacts farewell through animal behaviors—'pressing my forehead against her, walking in circles, moaning'—inhabiting a preverbal, pre-reflective mode to manage separation distress that adult language cannot adequately contain.

author: Rita Dove
content:
After all, there’s no need
to say anything
at first. An orange, peeled
and quartered, flares
like a tulip on a wedgewood plate
Anything can happen.
Outside the sun
has rolled up her rugs
and night strewn salt
across the sky. My heart
is humming a tune
I haven’t heard in years!
Quiet’s cool flesh—
let’s sniff and eat it.
There are ways
to make of the moment
a topiary
so the pleasure’s in
walking through.
evidence: The speaker experiences modest affective pleasure while contemplating future intimacy ('Anything can happen'), spreading the erotic moment through careful attention to present sensory detail (orange, night, heart humming) that prepares for what may unfold, maintaining both awareness and proportionate emotional engagement.

author: Timothy Steele
content:
The nominalist in me invents
A life devoid of precedents.
The realist takes a different view:
He claims that all I feel and do
Billions of others felt and did
In history’s Pre-me period.
Arguing thus, both voices speak
A partial truth. I am unique,
Yet the unceasing self-distress
Of desire buffets me no less
Than it has other sons of man
Who’ve come and gone since time began.
The meaning, then, of this dispute?
My life’s a nominal/real pursuit,
Which leaves identity clear and blurred,
In which what happens has occurred
Often and never—which is to say,
Never to me, or quite this way.
evidence: Speaker converts existential distress ('unceasing self-distress / Of desire') into philosophical debate between nominalist/realist voices, maintaining detached theoretical tone ('arguing thus, both voices speak / A partial truth') while acknowledging emotional suffering only as abstract proposition.

author: Susan Stewart
content:
Before balance, before counting, before
The record glistens and the needle slides,
Grating, into the overture, there is the end
Of weight, the leaning into nothing and then
A caught breath, the record listens, the needle slides
Over slowly, and all at once around us a woman’s voice
Stretches weightless, leaning into nothing.
Like a clothesline, the taut chorus: oh, hilarious
Oh baby, all around us, over slowly, a woman’s voice
Gathers above the pick me up, pick me up
And the desperate put, put me down. First the tightrope,
Then the light foot, and the taunting chorus
Pick me up, pick me up. Oh, oh baby.
The slippery floor shimmers and spins like a record while
The light is swinging footloose on its rope
Out of time. The shadows
Slip, shimmering black, and spin across the floor,
Then turn back and pick up again. Oh seedpod stuck for just
One moment on the cattail, out of time, out of shadows,
Downy cheek against a beard: oh scratches
On the record, oh baby, oh measure
Oh strange balance that grips us
On this side of the world.
evidence: The speaker transforms primitive bodily impulses (weight, balance, desperation in 'pick me up, pick me up') and the precarious terror of helplessness into an aesthetic meditation on father-infant dance, channeling anxiety about trust and control into rhythmic, musical imagery that preserves emotional intensity while achieving formal beauty ('oh measure / Oh strange balance that grips us').

author: Alice Fulton
content:
You sway like a crane to the tunes of tossed stones.
I am what you made to live in
from what you had: hair matted as kelp, bad schools.
Oh, you will never know me. I wave and you go
on playing in the clouds
boys clap from erasers. I am the pebble
you tossed on the chalked space and war-
danced toward, one-leg two-leg, arms treading air.
In this, your future, waves rechristen the sea
after its tiny jeweled lives
that hiss “Us Us” to the shore all day.
Where’s the kid called Kateydid? the moonfaced
Kewpiedoll? The excitable pouting
Zookie? The somber O-Be-Joyful?
Lost girl, playing hopscotch, I will do what you could.
Name of father, son, ghost. Cross my heart and hope.
While the sea’s jewels build shells and shells
change to chalk and chalk to loam and gold
wheat grows where oceans teetered.
evidence: The speaker transforms childhood suffering ('hair matted as kelp, bad schools') into creative witness and promise ('I will do what you could'), channeling primitive material into socially valuable expression through the poem itself, which maintains emotional authenticity while achieving aesthetic transformation through formal craft and metaphor.

author: Maxine Kumin
content:
From a documentary on marsupials I learn
that a pillowcase makes a fine
substitute pouch for an orphaned kangaroo.
I am drawn to such dramas of animal rescue.
They are warm in the throat. I suffer, the critic proclaims,
from an overabundance of maternal genes.
Bring me your fallen fledgling, your bummer lamb,
lead the abused, the starvelings, into my barn.
Advise the hunted deer to leap into my corn.
And had there been a wild child—filthy and fierce as a ferret, he is called
in one nineteenth-century account—
a wild child to love, it is safe to assume,
given my fireside inked with paw prints,
there would have been room.
Think of the language we two, same and not-same,
might have constructed from sign,
scratch, grimace, grunt, vowel:
Laughter our first noun, and our long verb, howl.
evidence: The speaker frames a compulsive caretaking stance ('Bring me your fallen fledgling, your bummer lamb') not as compensatory or defensive but as natural overflow of 'maternal genes,' finding vicarious gratification in rescuing vulnerable creatures while appearing genuinely to bless them with care ('fireside inked with paw prints'). The critic's accusation becomes a point of pride rather than shame.

author: Lisel Mueller
content:
When I am asked   
how I began writing poems,   
I talk about the indifference of nature.   
It was soon after my mother died,   
a brilliant June day,   
everything blooming.   
I sat on a gray stone bench   
in a lovingly planted garden,   
but the day lilies were as deaf   
as the ears of drunken sleepers   
and the roses curved inward.   
Nothing was black or broken   
and not a leaf fell   
and the sun blared endless commercials   
for summer holidays.   
I sat on a gray stone bench   
ringed with the ingenue faces   
of pink and white impatiens   
and placed my grief   
in the mouth of language,   
the only thing that would grieve with me.
evidence: The speaker transforms unbearable grief over mother's death into socially valued creative work ('placed my grief in the mouth of language'), channeling primitive emotional pain into poetry that 'would grieve with me'—maintaining intensity while achieving productive expression.

author: Stephen Kuusisto
content:
Finnish rural life, ca. 1910



Fires, always fires after midnight,
the sun depending in the purple birches
and gleaming like a copper kettle.
By the solstice they’d burned everything,
the bad-luck sleigh, a twisted rocker,
things “possessed” and not-quite-right.
The bonfire coils and lurches,
big as a house, and then it settles.
The dancers come, dressed like rainbows
(if rainbows could be spun),
and linking hands they turn
to the melancholy fiddles.
A red bird spreads its wings now
and in the darker days to come.
evidence: The poem offers ethnographic description of Finnish midsummer ritual (burning bad-luck objects, dancing to fiddles) with aesthetic distance and folkloric focus, without psychological conflict or defensive stance toward threatening material.

author: D. Nurkse
content:
After death, my father
practices meticulously
until the Bach is seamless,
spun glass in a dream,
you can no longer tell
where the modulations are,
or the pedal shifts
or the split fingerings . . .
if he rests
it’s to wind the metronome
or sip his cup of ice . . .
but who is the other old man
in the identical flannel gown,
head cocked, listening
ever more critically,
deeper in the empty room?
evidence: The father splits into two identical figures—one who practices and one who listens 'ever more critically'—maintaining opposed aspects (creator/judge, performer/critic) in separate compartments 'deeper in the empty room' without integration.

author: Charles Wright
content:
Dove-twirl in the tall grass.
                                              End-of-summer glaze next door
On the gloves and split ends of the conked magnolia tree.
Work sounds: truck back-up beep, wood tin-hammer, cicada, fire horn.
History handles our past like spoiled fruit.
Mid-morning, late-century light
                                                 calicoed under the peach trees.
Fingers us here. Fingers us here and here.
The poem is a code with no message:
The point of the mask is not the mask but the face underneath,
Absolute, incommunicado,                                                                                                   
                                       unhoused and peregrine.
The gill net of history will pluck us soon enough
From the cold waters of self-contentment we drift in
One by one
                  into its suffocating light and air.
Structure becomes an element of belief, syntax
And grammar a catechist,
Their words what the beads say,
                                        words thumbed to our discontent.
evidence: The speaker theorizes about poetry, history, and language in detached, abstract terms ('The poem is a code with no message,' 'Structure becomes an element of belief') while treating impending mortality and historical violence as conceptual problems rather than emotionally engaging realities—delivering 'weather reports on the psyche' about being plucked by history's gill net into 'suffocating light.'

author: William Matthews
content:
I was miserable, of course, for I was seventeen,
and so I swung into action and wrote a poem,
and it was miserable, for that was how I thought
poetry worked: you digested experience and shat
literature. It was 1960 at The Showplace, long since
defunct, on West 4th St., and I sat at the bar,
casting beer money from a thin reel of ones,
the kid in the city, big ears like a puppy.
And I knew Mingus was a genius. I knew two
other things, but they were wrong, as it happened.
So I made him look at the poem.
“There’s a lot of that going around,” he said,
and Sweet Baby Jesus he was right. He laughed
amiably. He didn’t look as if he thought
bad poems were dangerous, the way some poets do.
If they were baseball executives they’d plot
to destroy sandlots everywhere so that the game
could be saved from children.   Of course later
that night he fired his pianist in mid-number
and flurried him from the stand.
“We’ve suffered a diminuendo in personnel,”
he explained, and the band played on.
evidence: The speaker directly confronts his adolescent misery and pretentious poetry ('you digested experience and shat literature') while transforming the humiliation into shared comedy through timing and self-mockery, making himself the butt of the joke ('big ears like a puppy') without minimizing the pain or looking away from it.

author: Elizabeth Alexander
content:
In white pleated trousers, peering through green
sunshades, looking for the way the sun is red
noise, how locusts hiss to replicate the sun.
What is the visual equivalent
of syncopation? Rows of seared palms wrinkle
in the heat waves through green glass. Sprinklers
tick, tick, tick. The Watts Towers aim to split
the sky into chroma, spires tiled with rubble
nothing less than aspiration. I’ve left
minarets for sun and syncopation,
sixty-seven shades of green which I have
counted, beginning: palm leaves, front and back,
luncheon pickle, bottle glass, etcetera.
One day I will comprehend the different   
grades of red. On that day I will comprehend
these people, rhythms, jazz, Simon Rodia,
Watts, Los Angeles, aspiration.
evidence: The speaker catalogs sensory experiences ('sixty-seven shades of green which I have / counted') and seeks visual equivalents of syncopation with taxonomic detachment, maintaining observational distance from the cultural-emotional content of jazz, Watts, and 'these people' through enumeration and theoretical framing ('What is the visual equivalent / of syncopation?') rather than affective engagement.

author: Elizabeth Alexander
content:
Now is the time of year when bees are wild
and eccentric. They fly fast and in cramped
loop-de-loops, dive-bomb clusters of conversants
in the bright, late-September out-of-doors.
I have found their dried husks in my clothes.
They are dervishes because they are dying,
one last sting, a warm place to squeeze
a drop of venom or of honey.
After the stroke we thought would be her last
my grandmother came back, reared back and slapped
a nurse across the face. Then she stood up,
walked outside, and lay down in the snow.
Two years later there is no other way
to say, we are waiting. She is silent, light
as an empty hive, and she is breathing.
evidence: The speaker spreads out grief through natural observation and realistic acknowledgment: 'Two years later there is no other way / to say, we are waiting.' She experiences modest affective distress (the dying bees as metaphor) while preparing emotionally for imminent loss, holding both thought and feeling in consciousness.

author: Elizabeth Alexander
content:
Looking at the photograph is somehow not
unbearable: My friends, two dead, one low
on T-cells, his white T-shirt an X-ray
screen for the virus, which I imagine
as a single, swimming paisley, a sardine
with serrated fins and a neon spine.
I’m on a train, thinking about my friends
and watching two women talk in sign language.
I feel the energy and heft their talk
generates, the weight of their words in the air
the same heft as your presence in this picture,
boys, the volume of late summer air at the beach.
Did you tea-dance that day? Write poems
in the sunlight? Vamp with strangers? There is
sun under your skin like the gold Sula
found beneath Ajax’s black. I calibrate
the weight of your beautiful bones, the weight
of your elbow, Melvin,
                                  on Darrell’s brown shoulder.
evidence: The speaker processes devastating loss (two friends dead, one dying of AIDS) through detailed, aesthetic observation—transforming the virus into imagined 'swimming paisley' with 'serrated fins and neon spine,' measuring 'the weight of your beautiful bones' with scientific precision ('calibrate'), all delivered in emotionally flattened, analytical tone despite catastrophic content.

author: Ha Jin
content:
We used to like talking about grief
Our journals and letters were packed
with losses, complaints, and sorrows.
Even if there was no grief
we wouldn’t stop lamenting
as though longing for the charm
of a distressed face.
Then we couldn’t help expressing grief
So many things descended without warning:
labor wasted, loves lost, houses gone,
marriages broken, friends estranged,
ambitions worn away by immediate needs.
Words lined up in our throats
for a good whining.
Grief seemed like an endless river—
the only immortal flow of life.
