    My Conversation make to be thy Reele
    And reele the yarn thereon spun of thy Wheele.Make me thy Loome then, knit therein this Twine:
    And make thy Holy Spirit, Lord, winde quills:Then weave the Web thyselfe. The yarn is fine.
    Thine Ordinances make my Fulling Mills.
    Then dy the same in Heavenly Colours Choice,
    All pinkt with Varnisht Flowers of Paradise.Then cloath therewith mine Understanding, Will,
    Affections, Judgment, Conscience, MemoryMy Words, and Actions, that their shine may fill
    My wayes with glory and thee glorify.
    Then mine apparell shall display before yee
    That I am Cloathd in Holy robes for glory.
evidence: The speaker employs extended metaphor to articulate a theological desire for divine transformation, maintaining full conscious integration of wish, affect, and object throughout—no psychological conflict is defended against, only pious aspiration expressed.

author: Adelaide Crapsey
content:
The poet pursues his beautiful theme;The preacher his golden beatitude;
  And I run after a vanishing dream—
  The glittering, will-o’-the-wispish gleamOf the properly scholarly attitude—The highly desirable, the very advisable,The hardly acquirable, properly scholarly attitude.
  I envy the savage without any clothes,Who lives in a tropical latitude;
  It’s little of general culture he knows.
  But then he escapes the worrisome woesOf the properly scholarly attitude—The unceasingly sighed over, wept over, cried over,The futilely died over, properly scholarly attitude.
  I work and I work till I nearly am dead,And could say what the watchman said—that I could!
  But still, with a sigh and a shake of the head,
  “You don’t understand,” it is ruthlessly said,“The properly scholarly attitude—The aye to be sought for, wrought for and fought for,The ne’er to be caught for, properly scholarly attitude—”
  I really am sometimes tempted to sayThat it’s merely a glittering platitude;
  That people have just fallen into the way,
  When lacking a subject, to tell of the swayOf the properly scholarly attitude—The easily preachable, spread-eagle speechable,In practice unreachable, properly scholarly attitude.
evidence: The speaker directly confronts her distress at failing to achieve scholarly standards ('I work and I work till I nearly am dead') while transforming the pain into playful self-mockery through elaborate rhyme schemes and absurd comparisons ('I envy the savage without any clothes'), maintaining full awareness of the conflict and making herself the butt of the joke.

author: Robert Browning
content:
Nay but you, who do not love her,
    Is she not pure gold, my mistress?Holds earth aught—speak truth—above her?
    Aught like this tress, see, and this tress,And this last fairest tress of all,So fair, see, ere I let it fall?Because, you spend your lives in praising;
    To praise, you search the wide world over:Then why not witness, calmly gazing,
    If earth holds aught—speak truth—above her?Above this tress, and this, I touchBut cannot praise, I love so much!
evidence: Speaker attributes exaggerated perfection to the beloved ('pure gold,' 'fairest tress of all'), challenging the addressee to find 'aught above her' while simultaneously wielding this idealization as aggressive reproach against the unloving listener. The paradoxical claim 'cannot praise, I love so much' reveals the speaker's fusion with the idealized object.

author: Robert Duncan
content:
We have gone out in boats upon the sea at night,   
lost, and the vast waters close traps of fear about us.
The boats are driven apart, and we are alone at last   
under the incalculable sky, listless, diseased with stars.
Let the oars be idle, my love, and forget at this time
our love like a knife between us
defining the boundaries that we can never cross   
nor destroy as we drift into the heart of our dream,   
cutting the silence, slyly, the bitter rain in our mouths   
and the dark wound closed in behind us.
Forget depth-bombs, death and promises we made,   
gardens laid waste, and, over the wastelands westward,   
the rooms where we had come together bombd.
But even as we leave, your love turns back. I feel
your absence like the ringing of bells silenced. And salt   
over your eyes and the scales of salt between us. Now,   
you pass with ease into the destructive world.   
There is a dry crash of cement. The light fails,   
falls into the ruins of cities upon the distant shore   
and within the indestructible night I am alone.
evidence: The speaker directly instructs 'Let the oars be idle' and 'forget' multiple traumatic realities (depth-bombs, death, promises, bombed rooms) while maintaining full conscious awareness of what is being postponed—the couple drifts together acknowledging 'our love like a knife between us' that defines boundaries 'we can never cross nor destroy,' holding both attachment and separation in mind without resolution.

author: David Bottoms
content:
We have all seen them circling pastures,
have looked up from the mouth of a barn, a pine clearing,   
the fences of our own backyards, and have stood   
amazed by the one slow wing beat, the endless dihedral drift.
But I had never seen so many so close, hundreds,   
every limb of the dead oak feathered black,
and I cut the engine, let the river grab the jon boat   
and pull it toward the tree.
The black leaves shined, the pink fruit blossomed   
red, ugly as a human heart.
Then, as I passed under their dream, I saw for the first time   
its soft countenance, the raw fleshy jowls
wrinkled and generous, like the faces of the very old   
who have grown to empathize with everything.
And I drifted away from them, slow, on the pull of the river,   
reluctant, looking back at their roost,   
calling them what I'd never called them, what they are,
those dwarfed transfiguring angels,
who flock to the side of the poisoned fox, the mud turtle
crushed on the shoulder of the road,
who pray over the leaf-graves of the anonymous lost,
with mercy enough to consume us all and give us wings.
evidence: The speaker transforms primitive revulsion at carrion birds into aesthetic and spiritual contemplation ('calling them what I'd never called them...those dwarfed transfiguring angels'), channeling disgust into a meditative vision that finds mercy and sacred function in death-feeding, producing socially valuable insight without loss of emotional intensity.

poem_name: The Snow Is Deep on the Ground
author: Kenneth Patchen
content:
The snow is deep on the ground.   
Always the light falls
Softly down on the hair of my belovèd.
This is a good world.
The war has failed.
God shall not forget us.
Who made the snow waits where love is.
Only a few go mad.
The sky moves in its whiteness
Like the withered hand of an old king.   
God shall not forget us.
Who made the sky knows of our love.
The snow is beautiful on the ground.   
And always the lights of heaven glow   
Softly down on the hair of my belovèd.
evidence: Speaker insists 'This is a good world. The war has failed' and repeats 'God shall not forget us' while imagery of whiteness, old king's withered hand, and those who 'go mad' suggests objective circumstances contradict the asserted benevolence—refusing to acknowledge external painful reality.

author: Kenneth Patchen
content:
We must be slow and delicate; return
the policeman's stare with some esteem,   
remember this is not a shadow play
of doves and geese but this is now
the time to write it down, record the words—
I mean we should have left some pride
of youth and not forget the destiny of men   
who say goodbye to the wives and homes   
they've read about at breakfast in a restaurant:   
"My love."—without regret or bitterness   
obtain the measure of the stride we make,   
the latest song has chosen a theme of love   
delivering us from all evil—destroy. . . ?
why no. . . this too is fanciful. . . funny how   
hard it is to be slow and delicate in this,   
this thing of framing words to mark this grave   
I mean nothing short of blood in every street   
on earth can fitly voice the loss of these.
evidence: The speaker attempts to 'be slow and delicate' and 'frame words to mark this grave' regarding catastrophic loss ('blood in every street'), but the discourse remains curiously detached—a theoretical exercise in 'record[ing] the words' and 'obtain[ing] the measure' while the enormity of 'the loss of these' (men saying goodbye to wives) is acknowledged cognitively but processed through abstract imperatives ('We must...', 'we should...') rather than direct emotional engagement.

author: James Laughlin
content:
a sentimental curator has placed   
two fragments of bronze Grecian   
    heads together boy
and girl so that the faces black-
ened by the three thousand years of   
    desert sand & sun
seem to be whispering something   
that the Gurgan lion & the wing-
    ed dog of Azerbaijan
must not hear but I have heard   
them as I hear you now half way   
    around the world
so simply & so quietly more like
a child than like a woman making   
    love say to me in
that soft lost near and distant voice   
I’m happy now I’m happy oh don’t   
    move don’t go away.
evidence: The speaker redirects erotic longing for an absent lover onto ancient bronze fragments, investing the museum artifacts with the affect properly belonging to the relationship: 'as I hear you now half way around the world' explicitly links the whispered intimacy of the bronze heads to the distant beloved's voice during lovemaking.

author: James Laughlin
content:
Life kept rolling her over   
like a piece of driftwood
in the surf of an angry sea   
she was intelligent and beau-
tiful and well-off she made   
friends easily yet she wasn’t
able to put the pieces to-
gether into any recognizable
shape   she wasn’t sure who   
she wanted to be   so she
ended up being no one in par-
ticular   she made herself al-
most invisible   she was the   
person you loved so much who
really wasn’t there at all.
evidence: The speaker describes someone who 'made herself almost invisible' and 'ended up being no one in particular' despite intelligence and beauty—a systematic retreat from emotional investment in relationships where 'she wasn't sure who she wanted to be' producing observable detachment ('the person you loved so much who really wasn't there at all').

poem_name: O Best of All Nights, Return and Return Again
author: James Laughlin
content:
How she let her long hair down over her shoulders, making a love cave around her face. Return and return again.
How when the lamplight was lowered she pressed against him, twining her fingers in his. Return and return again.
How their legs swam together like dolphins and their toes played like little tunnies. Return and return again.
How she sat beside him cross-legged, telling him stories of her childhood. Return and return again.
How she closed her eyes when his were open, how they breathed together, breathing each other. Return and return again.
How they fell into slumber, their bodies curled together like two spoons. Return and return again.
How they went together to Otherwhere, the fairest land they had ever seen. Return and return again.
O best of all nights, return and return again.
evidence: The obsessive ritual refrain 'Return and return again' functions as magical incantation to counterbalance inevitable loss—each anaphoric repetition attempts to reverse time's forward motion through compulsive verbal formula, treating memory as dangerous deed requiring ritualistic preservation.

author: George Oppen
content:
The householder issuing to the street
Is adrift a moment in that ice stiff   
Exterior. ‘Peninsula
Low lying in the bay
And wooded—’ Native now
Are the welder and the welder’s arc
In the subway’s iron circuits:
We have not escaped each other,
Not in the forest, not here. The crippled girl hobbles   
Painfully in the new depths   
Of the subway, and painfully   
We shift our eyes. The bare rails   
And black walls contain
Labor before her birth, her twisted
Precarious birth and the men   
Laborious, burly—She sits   
Quiet, her eyes still. Slowly,   
Deliberately she sees
An anchor’s blunt fluke sink   
Thru coins and coin machines,   
The ancient iron and the voltage
In the iron beneath us in the child’s deep
Harbors into harbor sand.
evidence: The speaker delivers emotionally charged material—workers' bodies, the crippled girl's suffering, collective shame ('painfully / We shift our eyes')—in detached, technical syntax ('iron circuits,' 'voltage,' 'anchor's blunt fluke') that maintains observational distance while acknowledging pain theoretically. The poem maps suffering onto industrial diagrams rather than inhabiting its emotional weight.

author: Jean Toomer
content:
Black reapers with the sound of steel on stones
Are sharpening scythes. I see them place the hones   
In their hip-pockets as a thing that’s done,   
And start their silent swinging, one by one.   
Black horses drive a mower through the weeds,   
And there, a field rat, startled, squealing bleeds.   
His belly close to ground. I see the blade,   
Blood-stained, continue cutting weeds and shade.
evidence: The speaker maintains documentary distance, recording the reapers' methodical labor and the incidental death of the field rat with sequential precision and no psychological stance toward the violence observed.

author: Dana Gioia
content:
Now you hear what the house has to say.  
Pipes clanking, water running in the dark,   
the mortgaged walls shifting in discomfort,   
and voices mounting in an endless drone
of small complaints like the sounds of a family   
that year by year you’ve learned how to ignore.
But now you must listen to the things you own,   
all that you’ve worked for these past years,   
the murmur of property, of things in disrepair,   
the moving parts about to come undone,   
and twisting in the sheets remember all
the faces you could not bring yourself to love.
How many voices have escaped you until now,   
the venting furnace, the floorboards underfoot,   
the steady accusations of the clock
numbering the minutes no one will mark.   
The terrible clarity this moment brings,   
the useless insight, the unbroken dark.
evidence: The speaker catalogues emotionally devastating material—'faces you could not bring yourself to love,' 'terrible clarity,' marital and financial regret—in the flat, impersonal register of inventory ('the murmur of property,' 'moving parts,' 'the steady accusations'), maintaining syntactic distance through third-person constructions ('you') and converting relational failure into mechanical breakdown without accessing proportionate grief or panic.

author: Dana Gioia
content:
And in the end, all that is really left
Is a feeling—strong and unavoidable—
That somehow we deserved something better.   
That somewhere along the line things
Got fouled up. And that letter from whoever’s   
In charge, which certainly would have set   
Everything straight between us and the world,
Never reached us. Got lost somewhere.   
Possibly mislaid in some provincial station.   
Or sent by mistake to an old address   
Whose new tenant put it on her dresser   
With the curlers and the hairspray forgetting   
To give it to the landlord to forward.   
And we still wait like children who have sent   
Two weeks’ allowance far away   
To answer an enticing advertisement   
From a crumbling, yellow magazine,
Watching through years as long as a childhood summer,   
Checking the postbox with impatient faith   
Even on days when mail is never brought.
evidence: The speaker constructs an elaborate explanatory narrative for disappointment—a lost letter from 'whoever's in charge'—converting existential dissatisfaction ('we deserved something better') into a plausible external mishap ('mislaid in some provincial station'), thereby justifying ongoing passive waiting rather than confronting the absence of cosmic fairness or entitlement.

author: Eamon Grennan
content:
Acorn-brown, the girl's new nipples
draw the young men's rooster eyes
where a woman is fitting a man to her mouth,   
breathing fire, holding for dear life.
Green almonds in their shells:
she knifes them open one at a time and   
hands him a slick teardrop, cool white   
tasting cool white. Nothing
compares with such austerities, although   
the skull's honeycomb of bone
will break their hearts, who need hearts   
like a bird's wishbone, to bend, unbend
at every feathery beat—wishbone hearts,
or something fleet and light as an ostrich's   
leg-bone, bearing him to where, panicked   
with grief, he can bury his head in sand.Papyrus light: a scarf with black parrots on it   
lifts in the breeze, and a real rare bird
is about to fly—his head in the clouds, his life   
shrouded in daylight he keeps breaking.
evidence: The speaker redirects erotic and mortal anxieties onto emblematic objects and animal imagery (acorn nipples, rooster eyes, wishbone hearts, ostrich leg-bone) rather than directly confronting the underlying sexual and death terrors; the emotional charge adheres to these symbolic substitutes while the primary threat remains unexamined.

author: Jane Kenyon
content:
A fly wounds the water but the wound   
soon heals. Swallows tilt and twitter   
overhead, dropping now and then toward   
the outward-radiating evidence of food.
The green haze on the trees changes   
into leaves, and what looks like smoke   
floating over the neighbor’s barn   
is only apple blossoms.
But sometimes what looks like disaster   is disaster: the day comes at last,
and the men struggle with the casket   
just clearing the pews.
evidence: The speaker spreads anxiety over time through measured observation of transient wounds that heal and illusions that resolve, inoculating herself with small doses of impermanence before arriving at the unavoidable reality: 'the day comes at last, and the men struggle with the casket.' The meditation holds both present sensory engagement and future dread simultaneously.

poem_name: Portrait of a Figure near Water
author: Jane Kenyon
content:
Rebuked, she turned and ran
uphill to the barn. Anger, the inner   
arsonist, held a match to her brain.   
She observed her life: against her will   
it survived the unwavering flame.
The barn was empty of animals.   
Only a swallow tilted
near the beams, and bats
hung from the rafters
the roof sagged between.
Her breath became steady
where, years past, the farmer cooled   
the big tin amphoræ of milk.
The stone trough was still
filled with water: she watched it   
and received its calm.
So it is when we retreat in anger:   
we think we burn alone
and there is no balm.
Then water enters, though it makes   
no sound.
evidence: The speaker consciously postpones anger's destructive force ('against her will / it survived the unwavering flame'), deliberately retreating to a space where breath becomes steady and calm is actively received from water, maintaining awareness of the anger while choosing to defer its expression through finite waiting.

author: Jane Kenyon
content:
Let the light of late afternoon
shine through chinks in the barn, moving   
up the bales as the sun moves down.
Let the cricket take up chafing   
as a woman takes up her needles   
and her yarn. Let evening come.
Let dew collect on the hoe abandoned   
in long grass. Let the stars appear
and the moon disclose her silver horn.
Let the fox go back to its sandy den.   
Let the wind die down. Let the shed   
go black inside. Let evening come.
To the bottle in the ditch, to the scoop   
in the oats, to air in the lung   
let evening come.
Let it come, as it will, and don’t   
be afraid. God does not leave us   
comfortless, so let evening come.
evidence: The speaker prepares emotionally for death ('let evening come') through realistic, affect-laden contemplation that spreads anxiety over incremental images (light moving up bales, dew collecting, shed going black), experiencing modest distress while rehearsing the inevitable without catastrophizing, culminating in 'don't be afraid'—anxiety paid in advance through repeated inoculation.

author: Stevie Smith
content:
Highlight Actions
Enable or disable annotations




Nobody heard him, the dead man,   
But still he lay moaning:
I was much further out than you thought   
And not waving but drowning.
Poor chap, he always loved larkinglarking Playing tricks, kidding, fooling around.
And now he’s dead
It must have been too cold for him his heart gave way,   
They said.
Oh, no no no, it was too cold always   
(Still the dead one lay moaning)   
I was much too far out all my life   
And not waving but drowning.
evidence: The dead speaker moans accusatorily to the living who misjudged his distress signals—'I was much further out than you thought'—transforming his lifetime of unmet need into a posthumous reproach that ensures no genuine understanding or rescue was ever possible while maintaining surface performance ('larking').

author: Eva Gore-Booth
content:
The grand road from the mountain goes shining to the sea,
And there is traffic in it and many a horse and cart,
But the little roads of Cloonagh are dearer far to me,
And the little roads of Cloonagh go rambling through my heart.
A great storm from the ocean goes shouting o’er the hill,
And there is glory in it and terror on the wind,
But the haunted air of twilight is very strange and still,
And the little winds of twilight are dearer to my mind.
The great waves of the Atlantic sweep storming on their way,
Shining green and silver with the hidden herring shoal,
But the Little Waves of Breffny have drenched my heart in spray,
And the Little Waves of Breffny go stumbling through my soul.
evidence: The speaker repeatedly redirects overwhelming affect away from its unnamed source toward safer symbolic objects (little roads, winds, waves) while maintaining heightened emotional investment: 'the little roads of Cloonagh go rambling through my heart' and 'the Little Waves of Breffny go stumbling through my soul' reveal disproportionate intensity attached to landscape features that carry covert cargo from unexamined primary attachments or losses.

author: Eva Gore-Booth
content:
Lo, in my soul there lies a hidden lake,
High in the mountains, fed by rain and snow,
The sudden thundering avalanche divine,
And the bright waters’ everlasting flow,
Far from the highways’ dusty glare and heat.
Dearer it is and holier, for Christ’s sake,
Than his own windy lake in Palestine,
For there the little boats put out to sea
Without him, and no fisher hears his call,
Yea, on the desolate shores of Galilee
No man again shall see his shadow fall.
Yet here the very voice of the one Light
Haunts with sharp ecstasy each little wind
That stirs still waters on a moonlit night,
And sings through high trees growing in the mind,
And makes a gentle rustling in the wheat. . . .
Yea, in the white dawn on this happy shore,
With the lake water washing at his feet,
He stands alive and radiant evermore,
Whose presence makes the very East wind kind,
And turns to heaven the soul’s green-lit retreat.
evidence: The speaker attributes exaggerated positive qualities and sacred omnipotence to an internal Christ-figure ('alive and radiant evermore,' 'the very voice of the one Light') whom she explicitly elevates above the historical Christ of Galilee, positioning this idealized internal object as protection against desolation and source of transformation ('turns to heaven the soul's green-lit retreat').

author: Emma Lazarus
content:
Not like the brazen giant of Greek fame,
With conquering limbs astride from land to land;
Here at our sea-washed, sunset gates shall stand
A mighty woman with a torch, whose flame
Is the imprisoned lightning, and her name
Mother of Exiles. From her beacon-hand
Glows world-wide welcome; her mild eyes command
The air-bridged harbor that twin cities frame.
“Keep, ancient lands, your storied pomp!” cries she
With silent lips. “Give me your tired, your poor,
Your huddled masses yearning to breathe free,
The wretched refuse of your teeming shore.
Send these, the homeless, tempest-tost to me,
I lift my lamp beside the golden door!”
evidence: The speaker voices America's welcoming proclamation ('Give me your tired, your poor') in which the nation's own need—to define itself through generosity and distinguish itself from Old World conquest—is correctly attributed to the exiles' need for refuge, leaving both self partly gratified (national identity affirmed) and object genuinely blessed (refugees given sanctuary).

author: Marvin Bell
content:
Ethics without faith, excuse me,
is the butter and not the bread.
You can’t nourish them all, the dead
pile up at the hospital doors.
And even they are not so numerous
as the mothers come in maternity.
The Provider knows his faults—
love of architecture and repair—
but will not fall into them for long:
he can’t afford the adolescent luxury,
the fellowship of the future
looks greedily toward his family.
The black keys fit black cylinders
in the locks in holes in the night.
He had a skeleton key once,
a rubber arm and complete confidence.
Now, as head of the family, he is
inevitably on the wrong side looking out.
evidence: The speaker converts profound personal distress about inadequacy and failure into abstract economic-theological language ('Ethics without faith...is the butter and not the bread,' 'The Provider knows his faults'), discussing family responsibility and moral burden in a detached, aphoristic register that drains affective weight from what appears to be paternal anxiety and guilt.

author: Marvin Bell
content:
As simply as a self-effacing bar of soap
escaping by indiscernible degrees in the wash water
is how a man may change
and still hour by hour continue in his job.
There in the mirror he appears to be on fire
but here at the office he is dust.
So long as there remains a little moisture in the stains,
he stands easily on the pavement
and moves fluidly through the corridors. If only one
cloud can be seen, it is enough to know of others,
and life stands on the brink. It rains
or it doesn’t, or it rains and it rains again.
But let it go on raining for forty days and nights
or let the sun bake the ground for as long,
and it isn’t life, just life, anymore, it’s living.
In the meantime, in the regular weather of ordinary days,
it sometimes happens that a man has changed
so slowly that he slips away
before anyone notices
and lives and dies before anyone can find out.
evidence: The speaker describes profound existential disappearance ('a man may change / so slowly that he slips away / before anyone notices / and lives and dies before anyone can find out') in the detached, observational tone of a weather report, using extended metaphors (soap dissolving, dust, moisture, rain cycles) that convert lived human anguish into impersonal natural phenomena—acknowledging the ideational content of erasure while evacuating its emotional weight.

author: Robert W. Service
evidence: The speaker adopts a detached chronicler's stance, narrating the brothers' contrasting fates through sequential events without revealing any psychological defense against the material's injustice or pathos.

author: Lucille Clifton
content:
hey music and
me
only white,
hair a flutter of
fall leaves
circling my perfect
line of a nose,
no lips,
no behind, hey
white me
and i’m wearing
white history
but there’s no future
in those clothes
so i take them off and
wake up
dancing.
evidence: The speaker transforms profound racial pain into playful self-mockery ('no lips, / no behind, hey / white me'), maintaining full awareness of the absurdity and danger ('no future / in those clothes') while reconciling the paradox through comic catharsis—waking up 'dancing' after stripping off the fantasy, making herself the butt of the joke without dissociating from the wound.

poem_name: my dream about the second coming
author: Lucille Clifton
content:
mary is an old woman without shoes.
she doesn’t believe it.
not when her belly starts to bubble
and leave the print of a finger where
no man touches.
not when the snow in her hair melts away.
not when the stranger she used to wait for
appears dressed in lights at her
kitchen table.
she is an old woman and
doesn’t believe it.
when Something drops onto her toes one night
she calls it a fox
but she feeds it.
evidence: Mary's repeated refusal to acknowledge miraculous reality—'she doesn't believe it' despite belly bubbling, snow melting from hair, stranger in lights—meets the core definition of denial: automatic refusal to acknowledge external reality that is unpleasant or threatening, treating impossible events as nonexistent even while they materially occur.

author: B. H. Fairchild
content:
As a kid sitting in a yellow vinyl
booth in the back of Earl’s Tavern,
you watch the late-afternoon drunks
coming and going, sunlight breaking
through the smoky dark as the door
opens and closes, and it’s the future
flashing ahead like the taillights
of a semi as you drop over a rise
in the road on your way to Amarillo,bright lights and blonde-haired women,
as Billy used to say, slumped over
his beer like a snail, make a real man
out of you
evidence: The speaker rehearses future transformation ('bright lights and blonde-haired women...make a real man out of you') with modest affective engagement—the vision is both hoped-for and cautiously observed, spreading anxiety about masculinity across time through imaginative rehearsal of what awaits on the Amarillo road.

author: B. H. Fairchild
content:
I am so young that I am still in love
with Battle Creek, Michigan: decoder rings,
submarines powered by baking soda,   
whistles that only dogs can hear. Actually,   
not even them. Nobody can hear them.
Mrs. Hill from next door is hammering   
on our front door shouting, and my father
in his black and gold gangster robe lets her in   
trembling and bunched up like a rabbit in snow   
pleading, oh I’m so sorry, so sorry,
so sorry,
evidence: The speaker recounts a moment of adult terror and distress (Mrs. Hill's traumatized arrival) through the detached lens of childhood trivia about decoder rings and dog whistles, maintaining curious, affectless observation ('Actually, not even them') while Mrs. Hill enters 'trembling and bunched up like a rabbit' in obvious crisis.

author: Billy Collins
content:
In the dining room there is a brown fish
hanging on the wall who swims along
in his frame while we are eating dinner.
He swims in candlelight for all to see,
as if he has been swimming forever, even
in the darkness of the ink before someone thought
to draw him and the thin reeds waving in his stream
and the clear pebbles strewn upon the sand.
No wonder he continues his swimming
deep into the night, long after we have
blown out the candles and gone upstairs to bed.
No wonder I find him in the pale morning
light, still swimming, still looking out at me
with his one, small, spellbound eye.
evidence: The speaker channels primitive fascination with permanence and mortality into sustained aesthetic contemplation of the printed fish, transforming existential anxiety about the passage of time into socially valued creative observation that maintains emotional authenticity while achieving formal distance.

author: Billy Collins
content:
I ask them to take a poem
and hold it up to the light
like a color slide
or press an ear against its hive.
I say drop a mouse into a poem
and watch him probe his way out,
or walk inside the poem’s room
and feel the walls for a light switch.
I want them to waterski
across the surface of a poem
waving at the author’s name on the shore.
But all they want to do
is tie the poem to a chair with rope
and torture a confession out of it.
They begin beating it with a hose
to find out what it really means.
evidence: The speaker directly instructs students on experiential approaches to poetry versus their violent literalism, showing pedagogical frustration rather than intrapsychic defense.

author: W. S. Di Piero
content:
You’re still there in the spectral impress,   
the plied afterimage grid of trucks
and buses, diesel fume and bloodspoor streaked   
on wet streets, and cars biting evening papers
from the black newsstand. Above, the trestle’s   
gravel bed hums expectantly, or with relief,
and the gritty pinpoints of snow, at rest   
on silver rails, flare into the coming dark,
while everywhere your hungry light still tries   
to reconstruct itself, charm the space
in and around the looseknit ironworks,   
winter’s checkered yellowings glaring past
the dark. From here, two years away, I see   
in your middle distance a trestle stretched
between two brownstones, the whole scene   
droning deep: the train tears through the gap,
ratcheting the space with green aquatic squares   
that flick past like old sluggish film,
each frame a piece of failing, played-back fact,   
and the unseen wheels click, mumble, click
in flukes of young clean snow fountaining up   
around those strangers abiding in the glass.
evidence: The speaker addresses the abandoned city as 'you're still there' and obsessively reconstructs it through layered sensory details ('spectral impress,' 'plied afterimage grid'), treating the act of detailed memorial description as ritualistic compensation for having left—the poem functions as atonement for departure through elaborate aesthetic preservation.

author: W. S. Di Piero
content:
Snarls, bread trucks, yeast
breathing inside huddled bags,
and sleepers completing lives
behind their gray windows.
A whistle on the phonewires,
feathers, twitches, whistling
down to the hot loaves.
Reeds everywhere, pulse,
flesh, flutes, and wakened sighs.
An answer. Radio news
and breathers behind our windows,
birds’ new voices changing,changed, to the unforgiving
hunger screech of immigrants.
evidence: The poem offers layered sensory observation of urban morning—starlings, bread trucks, sleepers, phonewires—building through incremental imagery without psychological conflict or defensive positioning.

author: Sophie Jewett
content:
“O Love, thou art winged and swift,
   Yet stay with me evermore!”
And I guarded my house with bolt and bar
   Lest Love fly forth at the door.
Without, in the world, ’t was cold,
   While Love and I together
Laughed and sang by my red hearth-fire,
   Nor knew it was winter weather.
Sweet Love would lull me to sleep,
   In his tireless arm caressed;
His shadowing wings and burning eyes
   Like night and stars wrought rest.
And ever the beat of Love’s heart
   As a chime rang at my ear;
And ever Love’s bending, beautiful face
   Covered me close from fear.
Was it long ere I waked alone?
   A snow-drift whitened the floor;
I saw spent ashes upon my hearth
   And Death in my open door.
evidence: Speaker insists 'Nor knew it was winter weather' while barricading Love inside with 'bolt and bar,' refusing to acknowledge external reality (Death approaching) until forced awakening reveals snow-drift and spent ashes—the Pollyanna conviction that 'everything is perfect' while circumstances deteriorate.

author: Sophie Jewett
content:
The leaves talked in the twilight, dear;
   Hearken the tale they told:
How in some far-off place and year,
   Before the world grew old,
I was a dreaming forest tree,
   You were a wild, sweet bird
Who sheltered at the heart of me
   Because the north wind stirred;
How, when the chiding gale was still,
   When peace fell soft on fear,
You stayed one golden hour to fill
   My dream with singing, dear.
To-night the self-same songs are sung
   The first green forest heard;
My heart and the gray world grow young—
   To shelter you, my bird.
evidence: The speaker transforms intense protective/nurturing affect toward the child into aesthetically valuable lyric poetry, channeling primitive merger wishes (dreaming tree sheltering wild bird, heart as nest) into socially valued creative form while maintaining emotional authenticity and pleasure in the imaginative conceit.

author: Sophie Jewett
content:
All night I dreamed of roses,
   Wild tangle by the sea,
And shadowy garden closes.
   Dream-led I met with thee.
Around thee swayed the roses,
   Beyond thee sang the sea;
The shadowy garden closes
   Were Paradise to me.
O Love, ’mid the dream-roses
   Abide to heal, to save!
The world that day discloses
   Narrows to one white grave.
evidence: The speaker responds to waking reality—'The world that day discloses / Narrows to one white grave'—by pleading for the beloved to remain in the dream-roses to 'heal, to save,' insisting 'Oh, no!' to death's finality through desperate invocation of a paradisiacal dream-state as counter-reality.

author: Sophie Jewett
content:
Mown meadows skirt the standing wheat;
I linger, for the hay is sweet,
New-cut and curing in the sun.
Like furrows, straight, the windrows run,
Fallen, gallant ranks that tossed and bent
When, yesterday, the west wind went
A-rioting through grass and grain.
To-day no least breath stirs the plain;
Only the hot air, quivering, yields
Illusive motion to the fields
Where not the slenderest tassel swings.
Across the wheat flash sky-blue wings;
A goldfinch dangles from a tall,
Full-flowered yellow mullein; all
The world seems turning blue and gold.
Unstartled, since, even from of old,
Beauty has brought keen sense of her,
I feel the withering grasses stir;
Along the edges of the wheat,
I hear the rustle of her feet:
And yet I know the whole sea lies,
And half the earth, between our eyes.
evidence: The speaker transforms intense longing for an absent beloved into aesthetically powerful contemplation of natural beauty—'the whole sea lies, / And half the earth, between our eyes'—yet this acknowledgment of geographic separation produces not collapse but sustained passionate engagement with harvest imagery that holds both desire and constraint simultaneously.

author: Josephine Miles
content:
This face had no use for light, took none of it,
Grew cavernous against stars, bore into noon
A dark of midnight by its own resources.
Yet where it lay in sleep, where the pillows held it
With the blind plaster over it and the four walls
Keeping the night carefully, it was undone.
Sixty-watt light, squared to a window frame,
Across a well of air, across wind and window
Leaped and made shine the dark face in its sleep.
evidence: The poem presents an external, witnessing perspective on a sleeping face rather than a psychological stance toward conflict. The speaker observes transformation through literal light without defensive distortion.

author: Josephine Miles
content:
To this man, to his boned shoulders
Came the descent of pain.
All kinds,
Cruel, blind, dear, horrid, hallowed,
Rained, again, again.
To this small white blind boned face,
Wherever it was,
Descended
The blows of pain, it took as it were blinded,
As it were made for this.
We were there. We uneasy
Did not know if it were.
Knew neither
The reason nor the man nor whether
To share, or to beware.
evidence: The speakers witness horrific suffering ('Cruel, blind, dear, horrid, hallowed' pain descending 'again, again') yet maintain affective distance through abstraction and detached observation ('We uneasy / Did not know if it were'), reporting emotional content in a curiously flat, observational register without corresponding emotional engagement or empathic response.

author: Josephine Miles
content:
One rat across the floor and quick to floor's a breeze,
But two a whisper of a human tongue.
One is a breath, two voice;
And one a dream, but more are dreamed too long.
Two are the portent which we may believe at length,
And two the tribe we recognize as true.
Two are the total, they saying and they saying,
So we must ponder what we are to do.
For every scuttle of motion in the corner of the eye
Some thought of thought is asked in us indeed,
But of two, more: there we have likeness moving,
And there knowledge therefore, and therefore creed.
evidence: The speaker constructs an elaborate cognitive framework ('Two are the total, they saying and they saying, / So we must ponder what we are to do') to justify treating paired phenomena as epistemologically privileged ('there we have likeness moving, / And there knowledge therefore, and therefore creed'), converting what may be simple pattern-recognition into a logical system of belief requiring deliberation.

author: Josephine Miles
content:
When I think of my kindness which is tentative and quiet
And of yours which is intense and free,
I am in elaboration of knowledge impatient
Of even the patientest immobility.
I think of my kind, which is the human fortune
To live in the world and make war among its friends,
And of my version, which is to be moderately peaceful,
And of your version; and must make amends
By my slow word to your wish which is mobile,
Active and moving in its generous sphere.
This is the natural and the supernatural
Of humankind of which I grow aware.
evidence: The speaker converts potentially painful comparisons between self and other ('my kindness which is tentative and quiet' versus 'yours which is intense and free') into abstract categorization ('the natural and the supernatural / Of humankind'), discussing emotional inadequacy in detached, theoretical language like a taxonomy of types rather than acknowledging felt distress.

author: Josephine Miles
content:
After her pills the girl slept and counted
Pellet on pellet the regress of life.
Dead to the world, the world's count yet counted
Pellet on pill the antinomies of life.
Refused to turn, the way's back, she counted
Her several stones across the mire of life.
And stones away and sticks away she counted
To keep herself out of the country of life.
Lost tally. How the sheep return to home
Is the story she will retrieve
And the only story believe
Of one and one the sheep returning home
To take the shapes of life,
Coming and being counted.
evidence: The speaker recounts a girl's suicidal overdose ('After her pills') and dissociative counting ritual in a mechanical, affectless manner—'dead to the world, the world's count yet counted'—treating life-and-death material as neutral inventory. The obsessive tallying of pills/pellets/stones functions as undoing, magically keeping 'herself out of the country of life' through ritual enumeration that attempts to reverse or neutralize the death wish.

author: Stevie Smith
content:
There is far too much of the suburban classes   
Spiritually not geographically speaking. They’re asses.   
Menacing the greatness of our beloved England, they lie   
Propagating their kind in an eightroomed stye.   
Now I have a plan which I will enfold   
(There’s this to be said for them, they do as they’re told)   
Then tell them their country’s in mortal peril   
They believed it before and again will not cavil   
Put it in caption form firm and slick
If they see it in print it is bound to stick:   
‘Your King and your Country need you Dead’
You see the idea? Well, let it spread.
Have a suitable drug under string and label   
Free for every Registered Reader’s table.
For the rest of the gang who are not patriotic   
I’ve another appeal they’ll discover hypnotic:   
Tell them it’s smart to be dead and won’t hurt   
And they’ll gobble up drug as they gobble up dirt.
evidence: The speaker systematically strips the suburban classes of all human worth ('They're asses,' 'propagating their kind in an eightroomed stye'), reducing an entire social category to contemptible livestock, while maintaining omnipotent fantasies of control ('Now I have a plan,' 'tell them... they do as they're told') that position the speaker as architect of mass elimination.

author: Stevie Smith
content:
My life is vile
         I hate it so
         I’ll wait awhile   
         And then I’ll go.
         Why wait at all?
         Hope springs alive,
         Good may befall   
         I yet may thrive.
It is because I can’t make up my mind   
If God is good, impotent or unkind.
evidence: The speaker converts suicidal ideation ('My life is vile...I'll go') into a theological problem-solving exercise about God's nature, delivering existential crisis in the affectless tone of a logical proposition ('I can't make up my mind / If God is good, impotent or unkind').

author: Stevie Smith
content:
The lions who ate the Christians on the sands of the arena
By indulging native appetites played what has now been seen a   
Not entirely negligible part
In consolidating at the very start
The position of the Early Christian Church.
Initiatory rites are always bloody
And the lions, it appears
From contemporary art, made a study
Of dyeing Coliseum sands a ruddy
Liturgically sacrificial hue
And if the Christians felt a little blue—
Well people being eaten often do.
Theirs was the death, and theirs the crown undying,
A state of things which must be satisfying.
My point which up to this has been obscured
is that it was the lions who procured
By chewing up blood gristle flesh and bone
The martyrdoms on which the Church has grown.
I only write this poem because I thought it rather looked   
As if the part the lions played was being overlooked.
By lions’ jaws great benefits and blessings were begotten   
And so our debt to Lionhood must never be forgotten.
evidence: The speaker looks directly at martyrdom's horrific violence ('chewing up blood gristle flesh and bone') while responding to it as intellectual game ('my point which up to this has been obscured'), maintaining full observing ego through rhyme and wit that transforms sadomasochistic horror into comedy without looking away. The timing ('And if the Christians felt a little blue— / Well people being eaten often do') deflates terror through unexpected understatement exactly where pathos is expected.

author: Stevie Smith
content:
Tender only to one   
Tender and true   
The petals swing   
To my fingering
Is it you, or you, or you?
Tender only to one
I do not know his name
And the friends who fall   
To the petals’ call
May think my love to blame.
Tender only to one   
This petal holds a clue   
The face it shows
But too well knows   
Who I am tender to.
Tender only to one,
Last petal’s latest breath
Cries out aloud
From the icy shroud
His name, his name is Death.
evidence: The speaker performs an extended justification ritual (daisy oracle game) to explain or legitimate an attachment that is actually to Death—constructing a socially acceptable framework ('tender only to one,' searching among friends) for what is fundamentally a morbid fixation, while the petal-plucking serves as both magical thinking and compensatory gesture for the forbidden truth.

author: Stevie Smith
content:
In the flame of the flickering fire   
The sins of my soul are few
And the thoughts in my head are the thoughts of a bed   
With a solitary view.
But the eye of eternal consciousness   
Must blink as a bat blinks bright
Or ever the thoughts in my head be stilled
On the brink of eternal night.
Oh feed to the golden fish his egg
Where he floats in his captive bowl,
To the cat his kind from the womb born blind,   
And to the Lord my soul.
evidence: The speaker consciously holds death and solitude in mind ('thoughts of a bed / With a solitary view,' 'brink of eternal night') while actively postponing resolution through finite rituals ('feed to the golden fish his egg') and maintaining awareness of both immediate concerns and ultimate surrender ('to the Lord my soul').

author: Stevie Smith
content:
In my dreams I am always saying goodbye and riding away,   
Whither and why I know not nor do I care.
And the parting is sweet and the parting over is sweeter,   
And sweetest of all is the night and the rushing air.
In my dreams they are always waving their hands and saying goodbye,
And they give me the stirrup cup and I smile as I drink,   
I am glad the journey is set, I am glad I am going,
I am glad, I am glad, that my friends don't know what I think.
evidence: The speaker describes repetitive dream departures with theatrical pleasure ('sweetest of all') while maintaining curious affective distance from the content—'Whither and why I know not nor do I care'—and relief that friends 'don't know what I think,' suggesting fragmented consciousness where escape is performed with charming detachment rather than integrated emotional processing.

author: Jonathan Galassi
content:
Heartworn happiness, fine line that winds
among the tapestry’s old blacks and blues,
bright hair blazing in the theater,
red hair raving in the bar—as now
the little leaves shoot veils of gold
across the trees’ bones, shroud of spring,
ghost of summer, shadblow snow, blood-
russet spoor spilled prodigal on last year’s leaves . . .
When your yellows, greens, and yellow-greens,
your ochres and your umbers have evolved
nearly to hemlock blackness, cypress blackness,
when the woods are rife with soddenness
(unfolded ferns, skunk cabbage by the stream,
barberry by the trunks, and bitter
watercress inside the druid pool)
will your thin, still-glinting thread insist
to catch the eye in filigreed titrations
stitched along among beneath the branches,
in the branches where it lives all winter,
occulted fire, brief constant fleeting gold . . .
evidence: The speaker transforms what appears to be erotic or romantic intensity ('bright hair blazing,' 'red hair raving,' 'heartworn happiness') into intricate aesthetic observation of natural cycles, channeling desire into elaborate descriptive language that maintains emotional authenticity while achieving creative distance through formal craft ('filigreed titrations,' 'occulted fire').

author: Jonathan Galassi
content:
FOR B.



Somewhere ahead I see you
watching something out your window,
what I don’t know. You’re tall,
not on your tiptoes, green,
no longer yellow,
no longer little, little one,
but the changeless changing
seasons are still with us.
Summer’s back,
so beautiful it always reeks of ending,
and now its breeze is stirring
in your room commanding the lawn,
trying to wake you to say the day is wasting,
but you’re north of childhood now and out of here,
and I’ve gone south.
evidence: The speaker consciously holds in mind both the child's presence ('watching something out your window') and their absence ('you're north of childhood now and out of here'), acknowledging loss while maintaining awareness of the developmental progression through explicit temporal markers ('Somewhere ahead,' 'now') rather than denying or avoiding the separation.

poem_name: The Moment When Your Name is Pronounced
author: Forrest Gander
content:
This high up, the face
eroding; the red cedar slopes
over. An accident chooses a stranger.
Each rain unplugs roots
which thin out like a hand.
Above the river, heat
lightning flicks silently
and the sound holds, coiled in air.
Some nights you are here
dangling a Valpolicella bottle,
staring down at the flat water
that slides by with its mouth full of starlight.
It is always quiet
when we finish the wine.
While you were a living man
how many pictures were done
of you. Serious as an angel,
lacing up your boots. Ice
blows into my fields.
evidence: The speaker describes profound loss ('While you were a living man') and intimate ritual ('Some nights you are here / dangling a Valpolicella bottle') in the same affectless, observational register used for geological erosion ('the face / eroding; the red cedar slopes / over'), as if reporting weather rather than grief—cognition intact, emotion evacuated.

author: Forrest Gander
content:
—for Pilar Coover 
 



Me, when I think of you I see
Alley cats in your kitchen,
God weeping at your openings,
Individual acts of imagination, never
Culligan men under
Floorboards slipping hallucinogens into your water.
Let me say I have imagined you
Undressing guests before mirrors
To let their dragonfly bodies
Escape from human shells.
evidence: The speaker retreats from concrete interpersonal engagement with Pilar into an elaborate private fantasy world populated by alley cats, weeping gods, and dragonfly transformations—a rich internal imaginarium that substitutes for authentic relational description, maintaining affective distance through ornate, dreamlike imagery.

author: Aileen Lucia Fisher
content:
(FOR HANUKKAH)



Light the first of eight tonight—
the farthest candle to the right.
Light the first and second, too,
when tomorrow's day is through.
Then light three, and then light four—
every dusk one candle more
Till all eight burn bright and high,
honoring a day gone by
When the Temple was restored,
rescued from the Syrian lord,
And an eight-day feast proclaimed—
The Festival of Lights—well named
To celebrate the joyous day
when we regained the right to pray
to our one God in our own way.
evidence: The poem is a pedagogical instruction about Hanukkah ritual—lighting candles sequentially over eight nights—culminating in historical exposition about the Temple's restoration and religious freedom. No psychological stance toward conflict or distress is evident; this is ceremonial description.

author: Harry Graham
content:
Billy, in one of his nice new sashes,
Fell in the fire and was burned to ashes;
Now, although the room grows chilly,
I haven't the heart to poke poor Billy.
evidence: The speaker recounts Billy's immolation and current state as ashes with complete affective flatness, treating the catastrophic death as equivalent to a domestic inconvenience; the only acknowledged 'feeling' is tender-hearted reluctance to disturb the remains for warmth, delivering horror in the register of etiquette.

author: Phil Bolsta
content:
Last night when I was sound asleep,
My little brother Keith
Tiptoed into my bedroom
And pulled out all my teeth.
You’d think that I would be upset
And jump and spit and swear.
You’d think that I would tackle Keith
And pull out all his hair.
But no!   I’m glad he did it.
So what if people stare.
Now, thanks to the Tooth Fairy,
I’ll be a millionaire!
evidence: The speaker converts what should be a violent violation ('pulled out all my teeth') into a financial opportunity ('thanks to the Tooth Fairy, I'll be a millionaire'), constructing a plausible justification that masks the expected outrage and transforms loss into gain through magical compensation.

author: Kalli Dakos
content:
Don’t tell me the cat ate your math sheet,
And your spelling words went down the drain,
And you couldn’t decipher your homework,
Because it was soaked in the rain.
Don’t tell me you slaved for hours
On the project that’s due today,
And you would have had it finished
If your snake hadn’t run away.
Don’t tell me you lost your eraser,
And your worksheets and pencils, too,
And your papers are stuck together
With a great big glob of glue.
I’m tired of all your excuses;
They are really a terrible bore.
Besides, I forgot my own work,
At home in my study drawer.
evidence: The teacher catalogues elaborate student excuses with mounting exasperation ('Don't tell me... Don't tell me'), positioning them as tiresome rationalizations ('I'm tired of all your excuses; / They are really a terrible bore'), then reveals her own identical behavior ('Besides, I forgot my own work, / At home in my study drawer'), retrospectively justifying her impatience as projection of her own guilt-inducing failure.

author: Mother Goose
content:
There was a crooked man, and he walked a crooked mile,
He found a crooked sixpence against a crooked stile;
He bought a crooked cat which caught a crooked mouse,
And they all lived together in a little crooked house.
evidence: The poem is a descriptive nursery rhyme cataloging a world of 'crooked' objects and beings without psychological conflict, emotional distress, or defensive posture from a speaker—it presents a whimsical portrait rather than managing anxiety or unwanted affect.

poem_name: "Hush little baby, don't say a word,"
author: Mother Goose
content:
Hush little baby, don't say a word,
Papa's gonna buy you a mockingbird.
And if that mockingbird won't sing,
Papa's gonna buy you a diamond ring.
And if that diamond ring turns to brass,
Papa's gonna buy you a looking glass.
And if that looking glass gets broke,
Papa's gonna buy you a billy goat.
And if that billy goat won't pull,
Papa's gonna buy you a cart and bull.
And if that cart and bull turn over,
Papa's gonna buy you a dog named Rover.
And if that dog named Rover won't bark,
Papa's gonna buy you a horse and cart.
And if that horse and cart fall down,
You'll still be the sweetest little baby in town!
evidence: The speaker ritualistically counterbalances each anticipated failure or disappointment with a compensatory promise ('And if that mockingbird won't sing, Papa's gonna buy you a diamond ring'), treating potential loss as requiring immediate magical replacement through an escalating chain of substitutions that culminates in the omnipotent reassurance 'You'll still be the sweetest little baby in town'—regardless of external reality.

author: Sarah Josepha Hale
content:
Mary had a little lamb,
Its fleece was white as snow;
And everywhere that Mary went
The lamb was sure to go.
It followed her to school one day,
Which was against the rule;
It made the children laugh and play
To see a lamb at school.
And so the teacher turned it out,
But still it lingered near,
And waited patiently about
Till Mary did appear.
Why does the lamb love Mary so?
The eager children cry;
Why, Mary loves the lamb, you know,
The teacher did reply.
evidence: The poem is a simple narrative observation of a lamb's attachment behavior, explained through reciprocal affection ('Mary loves the lamb'). No psychological defense stance is evident—the speaker remains external to any internal conflict.

author: Mother Goose
content:
Ladybird, ladybird,
Fly away home,
Your house is on fire
And your children all gone;
All except one
And that's little Ann,
And she has crept under
The warming pan.
evidence: The poem is a traditional nursery rhyme addressing a ladybird insect, delivering urgent news about fire and children in a formulaic folk pattern without psychological stance from a human speaker managing internal conflict.

poem_name: "There was an old woman who lived in a shoe."
author: Mother Goose
content:
There was an old woman who lived in a shoe.
She had so many children, she didn't know what to do.
She gave them some broth without any bread;
And whipped them all soundly and put them to bed.
evidence: The poem presents a third-person narrative describing an old woman's situation and actions without revealing her psychological stance or internal conflict; it functions as folk description rather than psychological self-disclosure.

author: Mother Goose
content:
Polly, put the kettle on,
Polly, put the kettle on,
Polly, put the kettle on,
We'll all have tea.
Sukey, take it off again,
Sukey, take it off again,
Sukey, take it off again,
They've all gone away.
evidence: The poem is a simple nursery rhyme depicting a cyclical domestic action (preparing tea, then removing the kettle when guests leave) without psychological conflict or defensive stance.

author: Frank Asch
content:
If sunlight fell like snowflakes,
gleaming yellow and so bright,
we could build a sunman,
we could have a sunball fight,
we could watch the sunflakes
drifting in the sky.
We could go sleighing
in the middle of July
through sundrifts and sunbanks,
we could ride a sunmobile,
and we could touch sunflakes—
I wonder how they'd feel.
evidence: The speaker engages in realistic, affect-laden planning about a counterfactual future experience ('I wonder how they'd feel'), imagining specific sensory and physical consequences of transformed sunlight with curious rather than anxious tone, spreading out the imaginative work across detailed scenarios without magical omnipotence or dissociative flight.

author: Lilian Moore
content:
If you catch a firefly
            and keep it in a jar
You may find that
            you have lost
A tiny star.
If you let it go then,
            back into the night,
You may see it
            once again
Star bright.
evidence: The speaker engages in realistic, affect-laden planning for future discomfort—imagining the consequence of capturing the firefly ('you may find that you have lost / A tiny star') and preparing emotionally for the possibility of loss, then considering corrective action ('If you let it go then') with measured anxiety about whether restoration is possible ('You may see it once again').

author: Lilian Moore
content:
I left my head
somewhere
today.
Put it down for
just
a minute.
Under the
table?
On a chair?
Wish I were
able
to say
where.
Everything I need
is
in it!
evidence: The poem is a playful, literal depiction of absentmindedness (losing one's head) without psychological defensiveness—the speaker openly acknowledges the loss and searches for it with no anxiety management or reality distortion.

author: Lilian Moore
content:
I made a sand castle.
In rolled the sea.
            "All sand castles
            belong to me—
            to me,"
said the sea.
I dug sand tunnels.
In flowed the sea.
            "All sand tunnels
            belong to me—
            to me,"
said the sea.
I saw my sand pail floating free.
I ran and snatched it from the sea.
            "My sand pail
            belongs to me—
            to ME!"
evidence: The speaker progresses from passive observation of the sea's claims ('All sand castles belong to me') to direct, forceful reclamation of agency: 'I ran and snatched it from the sea... My sand pail belongs to ME!' The escalating caps and repetition mark developmental triumph of autonomous assertion against engulfing authority.

poem_name: Wade Seego Believes Soylent Green Is People
author: R. T. Smith
content:
Down here we say we dare defend our rights,
our state motto. I’d back Charlton Heston
for any office in the land. A Christian,
he speaks right up. He’s got his head on straight,
and people listen. Even on the screen
of a honky-tonk TV he still looks
like a hero, and he wouldn’t let freaks
take over our country. If it takes firepower
to keep us free, I say stock up. Keep your
powder dry. Everything is dangerous
these days. Life sucks. We suck too. Disaster
is coming. Even God’s gone spleenish. Bless
the common man against the government.
They lie. They grind us up. Winchesters
might be our last resort. Hellfire preachers
say we best prepare for a dark event,
but maybe Charlie Heston could keep death
off our backs and tone down Jehovah’s wrath.
Sweet Jesus—and this is the gospel truth—
is pissed off at our newfangled unfaith.
He’s coming back, and he’s armed to the teeth.
evidence: The speaker attributes his own rage, fear, and aggressive impulses outward to external threats ('They lie. They grind us up'), to God ('Even God's gone spleenish... Jehovah's wrath'), and to Jesus ('Sweet Jesus... is pissed off... He's coming back, and he's armed to the teeth'), transforming internal hostility into justified defense against a persecutory world while maintaining empathic connection to the projected anger.

author: Sharon Olds
content:
I could not tell I had jumped off that bus,
that bus in motion, with my child in my arms,
because I did not know it. I believed my own story:   
I had fallen, or the bus had started up
when I had one foot in the air.
I would not remember the tightening of my jaw,   
the irk that I’d missed my stop, the step out   
into the air, the clear child
gazing about her in the air as I plunged
to one knee on the street, scraped it, twisted it,   
the bus skidding to a stop, the driver
jumping out, my daughter laughingDo it again.
I have never done it   
again, I have been very careful.
I have kept an eye on that nice young mother
who lightly leapt
off the moving vehicle
onto the stopped street, her life
in her hands, her life’s life in her hands.
evidence: The speaker describes motivated forgetting of a dangerous impulsive act ('I could not tell... because I did not know it. I believed my own story: I had fallen'), with the affect (jaw-tightening irk, risky leap) remaining present but the volitional idea excluded from awareness until retrospective recognition. The gap between what happened and what she could consciously know is the poem's central drama.

author: Sharon Olds
content:
As the guests arrive at our son’s party   
they gather in the living room—
short men, men in first grade
with smooth jaws and chins.
Hands in pockets, they stand around
jostling, jockeying for place, small fights
breaking out and calming. One says to anotherHow old are you? —Six. —I’m seven. —So?
They eye each other, seeing themselves   
tiny in the other’s pupils. They clear their   
throats a lot, a room of small bankers,
they fold their arms and frown. I could beat youup, a seven says to a six,
the midnight cake, round and heavy as a
turret behind them on the table. My son,
freckles like specks of nutmeg on his cheeks,   
chest narrow as the balsa keel of a   
model boat, long hands
cool and thin as the day they guided him   
out of me, speaks up as a host
for the sake of the group.We could easily kill a two-year-old,
he says in his clear voice. The other   
men agree, they clear their throats
like Generals, they relax and get down to   
playing war, celebrating my son’s life.
evidence: The speaker describes first-graders casually discussing killing a two-year-old ('We could easily kill a two-year-old, he says in his clear voice') with the clinical detachment of an anthropologist observing 'small bankers' or 'Generals'—the violence is cognitively registered but stripped of horror or maternal alarm, delivered in measured, observational syntax.

author: Sharon Olds
content:
At dusk, on those evenings she does not go out,   
my mother potters around her house.   
Her daily helpers are gone, there is no one   
there, no one to tell what to do,
she wanders, sometimes she talks to herself,   
fondly scolding, sometimes she suddenly   
throws out her arms and screams—high notes   
lying here and there on the carpets   
like bodies touched by a downed wire,
she journeys, she quests, she marco-polos through   
the gilded gleamy loot-rooms, who is she.   
I feel, now, that I do not know her,
and for all my staring, I have not seen her
—like the song she sang, when we were small,   I wonder as I wander, out under the sky,   
how Jesus, the Savior, was born for, to die,   
for poor lonely people, like you, and like I
evidence: Speaker observes mother's lonely wandering and screaming with detached, almost anthropological distance ('she wanders,' 'she quests,' 'she marco-polos'), maintaining emotional remove even while acknowledging profound unknowing ('I feel, now, that I do not know her'). The affect-laden reality of a deteriorating parent is converted into aesthetic description ('high notes lying here and there on the carpets like bodies') that preserves cognitive engagement while evacuating emotional contact.

author: Tom Clark
content:
The angel asked, as his shoulders were pressed into the stone
Why me? And taken away from the inhabited body,
Like the lyric voice rustling from memory forests,
Childhood rushes toward death, a wind in those woods,
Crashing through trees, dying out,
Settling like a white mist over everything.
evidence: The speaker contemplates mortality through the angel figure pressed into stone, experiencing modest affective distress (the white mist settling over everything) while holding both childhood vitality and death in conscious awareness—spreading anxiety across the temporal span from 'childhood rushes toward death' without avoiding the emotional weight.

author: Tom Clark
content:
Always behind my back I hear
The spastic clicking of jerked knees
And other automatic reactions
Tracking me through the years to where
Time’s winged chariot is double
Parked near the eternity frontier
And in such moments I want to participate
In human life less and less
But when I do the obligatory double take
And glance behind me into the dark green future
All I see stretching out are vast
Arizona republics of more
evidence: The speaker describes wanting 'to participate / In human life less and less' while tracking automatic reactions 'behind my back,' expressing detached immunity to connection and reducing existence to mechanical processes ('spastic clicking of jerked knees') viewed from observational distance.

author: Tom Clark
content:
Nice spring day off big white cloud
At Inspiration Point escaping time wars
Poet takes book & wine bottle up into Mist Mountains
Since only available agenda is rhyming with silence
Seeking window of opportunity on a wall
I disguise what I have to say by sounding Chinese
Such as stars are now darker and farther away
They take deeper drinks because space is
Drying out afraid to think own thoughts
Administered citizen achieving condition of robot
In public mind things not so good these days
Nor in wrong run will it matter to Tu Fu
evidence: The speaker converts distress about 'administered citizen achieving condition of robot' and 'in public mind things not so good these days' into detached philosophical observation delivered in a flat, theoretical register ('disguise what I have to say by sounding Chinese'), discussing cosmic alienation ('stars are now darker and farther away') without emotional engagement despite naming fear ('afraid to think own thoughts').

author: Tom Clark
content:
The smashed weirdness of the raving cadenzas of God
Takes over all of a sudden
In our time. It speaks through the voices of talk show moderators.
It tells us in a ringing anthem, like heavenly hosts uplifted,
That the rhapsody of the pastoral is out to lunch.
We can take it from there.
We can take it to Easy Street.
But when things get tough on Easy Street
What then? Is it time for realism?
And who are these guys on the bus
Who glide in golden hats past us
On their way to Kansas City?
evidence: The poem satirizes media voices and consumer-culture promises ('talk show moderators,' 'Easy Street') without revealing a defensive psychological stance. The speaker observes cultural absurdity with ironic distance but displays no internal conflict requiring defense.

author: Constance Urdang
content:
Travel is a vanishing act
Only to those who are left behind.
What the traveler knows
Is that he accompanies himself,
Unwieldy baggage that can’t be checked,
Stolen, or lost, or mistaken.
So one took, past outposts of empire,
“Calmly as if in the British Museum,”
Not only her Victorian skirts,
Starched shirtwaists, and umbrella, but her faith
In the civilizing mission of women,
Her backaches and insomnia, her innocent valor;
Another, friend of witch-doctors,
Living on native chop,
Trading tobacco and hooks for fish and fetishes,
Heralded her astonishing arrival
Under shivering stars
By calling, “It’s only me!” A third,
Intent on savage customs, and to demonstrate
That a woman could travel as easily as a man,
Carried a handkerchief damp with wifely tears
And only once permitted a tribal chieftain
To stroke her long, golden hair.
evidence: The speaker reflects on the psychological paradox of travel—that one 'accompanies himself, / Unwieldy baggage that can't be checked'—and observes through historical examples how travelers carry their internal worlds (faith, backaches, wifely tears) unchanged across external geography, demonstrating metacognitive awareness of psychological constancy without excessive detachment.

author: Linda Gregg
content:
I would like to decorate this silence,   
but my house grows only cleaner
and more plain. The glass chimes I hung   
over the register ring a little
when the heat goes on.
I waited too long to drink my tea.   
It was not hot. It was only warm.
evidence: The speaker acknowledges desire ('I would like to decorate this silence') but accepts postponement without eliminating awareness, noting incremental disappointments ('waited too long...not hot...only warm') while maintaining conscious attention to the conflict between desire for warmth/ornamentation and austere reality.

author: Linda Gregg
content:
It was a picture I had after the war.
A bombed English church. I was too young   
to know the word English or war,
but I knew the picture.
The ruined city still seemed noble.   
The cathedral with its roof blown off
was not less godly. The church was the same   
plus rain and sky. Birds flew in and out   
of the holes God’s fist made in the walls.   
All our desire for love or children   
is treated like rags by the enemy.
I knew so much and sang anyway.   
Like a bird who will sing until
it is brought down. When they take   
away the trees, the child picks up a stick   
and says, this is a tree, this the house
and the family. As we might. Through a door   
of what had been a house, into the field   
of rubble, walks a single lamb, tilting   
its head, curious, unafraid, hungry.
evidence: The speaker transforms childhood war trauma into sustained creative witness—'I knew so much and sang anyway. Like a bird who will sing until it is brought down'—maintaining emotional authenticity while channeling primitive awareness of destruction ('All our desire for love or children / is treated like rags by the enemy') into artistically rendered testimony with real consequences.

author: Robert Bly
content:
Inside the veins there are navies setting forth,
Tiny explosions at the waterlines,
And seagulls weaving in the wind of the salty blood.
It is the morning. The country has slept the whole winter.
Window seats were covered with fur skins, the yard was full
Of stiff dogs, and hands that clumsily held heavy books.
Now we wake, and rise from bed, and eat breakfast!
Shouts rise from the harbor of the blood,
Mist, and masts rising, the knock of wooden tackle in the sunlight.
Now we sing, and do tiny dances on the kitchen floor.
Our whole body is like a harbor at dawn;
We know that our master has left us for the day.
evidence: The speaker channels primitive bodily and instinctual energy ('navies setting forth,' 'explosions,' 'harbor of the blood') into exuberant, creative celebration ('we sing, and do tiny dances') that transforms biological awakening into socially shareable aesthetic experience without loss of vitality or pleasure.

author: Carolyn Forché
content:
I take off my shirt, I show you.
I shaved the hair out under my arms.
I roll up my pants, I scraped off the hair   
on my legs with a knife, getting white.
My hair is the color of chopped maples.   
My eyes dark as beans cooked in the south.   
(Coal fields in the moon on torn-up hills)
Skin polished as a Ming bowl
showing its blood cracks, its age, I have hundreds   
of names for the snow, for this, all of them quiet.
In the night I come to you and it seems a shame   
to waste my deepest shudders on a wall of a man.
You recognize strangers,
think you lived through destruction.
You can’t explain this night, my face, your memory.
You want to know what I know?   
Your own hands are lying.
evidence: The speaker attributes dissociation and falsehood to the addressed 'you' ('You recognize strangers, think you lived through destruction... Your own hands are lying') while maintaining certainty about the other's internal confusion and deception, characteristic of projection's misattribution of internal states as originating externally.

author: Robert Herrick
evidence: The speaker directly addresses Julia, offering protective reassurances and inviting her to come to him through the night. The imagery of guiding lights and protective spirits serves courtship rather than psychological defense.

poem_name: To Anthea, who may Command him Anything
author: Robert Herrick
content:
Bid me to live, and I will live
     Thy protestant to be;Or bid me love, and I will give
     A loving heart to thee.A heart as soft, a heart as kind,
     A heart as sound and free,As in the whole world thou canst find,
     That heart I'll give to thee.Bid that heart stay, and it will stay,
     To honour thy decree;Or bid it languish quite away,
     And 't shall do so for thee.Bid me to weep, and I will weep,
     While I have eyes to see;And having none, yet I will keep
     A heart to weep for thee.Bid me despair, and I'll despair,
     Under that cypress tree;Or bid me die, and I will dare
     E'en death, to die for thee.Thou art my life, my love, my heart,
     The very eyes of me;And hast command of every part,
     To live and die for thee.
evidence: The speaker attributes omnipotent control to Anthea ('hast command of every part'), treating her as possessing magical power over his existence, life, and death. The totality of surrender ('bid me die, and I will dare / E'en death') and absence of any negative qualities in the beloved reveal archaic idealization requiring merger with an omnipotent figure.

poem_name: Song: “Hark, hark! the lark at heaven's gate sings”
author: William Shakespeare
content:
(from Cymbeline)



Hark, hark! the lark at heaven's gate sings, And Phoebus 'gins arise, His steeds to water at those springs On chaliced flowers that lies; And winking Mary-buds begin To ope their golden eyes: With every thing that pretty is, My lady sweet, arise: Arise, arise.
evidence: The speaker delivers a straightforward morning song praising natural beauty to wake a beloved—no psychological conflict, anxiety management, or defensive transformation is evident in the stance.

poem_name: Song: “O Mistress mine where are you roaming?”
author: William Shakespeare
content:
(from Twelfth Night)



O Mistress mine where are you roaming? O stay and hear, your true love's coming,       That can sing both high and low. Trip no further pretty sweeting. Journeys end in lovers' meeting,       Every wise man's son doth know. What is love, 'tis not hereafter, Present mirth, hath present laughter:       What's to come, is still unsure. In delay there lies no plenty, Then come kiss me sweet and twenty:       Youth's a stuff will not endure.
evidence: The speaker explicitly acknowledges competing forces—desire ('come kiss me') and temporal constraint ('Youth's a stuff will not endure')—while advocating for deliberate postponement of hesitation rather than elimination of awareness: 'In delay there lies no plenty' frames waiting itself as the recognized problem, urging finite deferral of caution in favor of immediate action with full consciousness of both impulse and limits.

poem_name: Song: “Orpheus with his lute made trees”
author: William Shakespeare
content:
(from Henry VIII)



Orpheus with his lute made trees, And the mountain tops that freeze, Bow themselves when he did sing:To his music plants and flowers Ever sprung; as sun and showers There had made a lasting spring. Every thing that heard him play, Even the billows of the sea, Hung their heads, and then lay by. In sweet music is such art, Killing care and grief of heart Fall asleep, or hearing, die.
evidence: The speaker celebrates how primitive emotional forces ('care and grief of heart') are transformed through artistic expression ('sweet music') into something that produces pleasure and relief for both creator and audience, with real consequences ('Killing care...Fall asleep, or hearing, die') rather than mere displacement.

poem_name: Song: “Take, oh take those lips away”
author: William Shakespeare
content:
(from Measure for Measure)



Take, oh take those lips away,       That so sweetly were forsworn, And those eyes: the breake of day,       Lights that do mislead the Morn; But my kisses bring again, bring again, Seals of love, but sealed in vain, sealed in vain.
evidence: The speaker commands 'Take, oh take those lips away' (banishment), then immediately reverses with 'But my kisses bring again'—a ritualistic oscillation between rejection and restoration. The kisses function as 'Seals of love' that attempt to counterbalance the forsworn betrayal, though acknowledged as 'sealed in vain,' revealing the magical, compensatory logic of undoing.

author: Alfred Noyes
content:
Now, in a breath, we’ll burst those gates of gold,
   And ransack heaven before our moment fails.
Now, in a breath, before we, too, grow old,
