The pigeons observation delivers emotional content (closeness/distance paradox) in curiously flat, philosophical syntax.

author: Louise Glück
content:
—After Robert Pinsky



Defier of closed space, such as the head, opener Of the sealed passageways, so that Sunlight entering the nose can once again Exit the ear, vaporizer, mist machine, whose Soft hiss sounds like another human being But less erratic, more stable, or, if not like a human being, Carried by one, by my mother to the sick chamber Of my childhood — as Freud said, Why are you always sick, Louise? his cigar Confusing mist with smoke, interfering With healing—Embodied Summoner of these ghosts, white plastic tub with your elegant Clear tub, the water sanitized by boiling, Sterile, odorless, In my mother’s absence Run by me, the one machine I understand: what Would life be if we could not buy Objects to care for us And bear them home, away from the druggists’ pity, If we could not carry in our own arms Alms, alchemy, to the safety of our bedrooms, If there were no more Sounds in the night, continuous Hush, hush of warm steam, not Like human breath though regular, if there were nothing in the world More hopeful than the self, Soothing it, wishing it well.
evidence: The speaker redirects affect from complex maternal ambivalence ('in my mother's absence / Run by me') onto the machine itself, which becomes the stable, reliable caretaker—'the one machine I understand'—with disproportionate emotional investment in the humidifier as substitute object that 'care[s] for us' and provides 'continuous / Hush, hush' unlike erratic human presence.

author: Averill Curdy
content:
Never the bark and abalone mask
cracked by storms of a mastering god,
never the gods’ favored glamour, never
the pelagic messenger bearing orchards
in its beak, never allegory, not wisdom
or valor or cunning, much less hunger
demanding vigilance, industry, invention,
or the instinct to claim some small rise
above the plain and from there to assert
the song of another day ending;
lentil brown, uncounted, overlooked
in the clamorous public of the flock
so unlikely to be noticed here by arrivals,
faces shining with oils of their many miles,
where it hops and scratches below
the baggage carousel and lights too high,
too bright for any real illumination,
looking more like a fumbled punch line
than a stowaway whose revelation
recalls how lightly we once traveled.
evidence: The speaker redirects anxiety about human insignificance and overlooked suffering onto the sparrow, concentrating emotional energy on this 'lentil brown, uncounted, overlooked' creature rather than confronting the self's own displacement and diminishment in 'the clamorous public.' The sparrow becomes the less threatening target for meditating on being 'unlikely to be noticed' amid 'faces shining with oils of their many miles.'

author: Stephen Dunn
content:
Finally, I gave up on obeisance,
and refused to welcome
either retribution or the tease
of sunny days. As for the can’t-be-
seen, the sum-of-all-details,
the One—oh, when it came
to salvation I was only sure
I needed to be spared
someone else’s version of it.
The small prayers I devised
had in them the hard sounds
of split and frost.
I wanted them to speak
as if it made sense to speak
to what isn’t there
in the beaconless dark.
I wanted them to startle
by how little they asked.
evidence: The speaker directly claims autonomy ('Finally, I gave up on obeisance') and asserts legitimate needs without guilt ('I was only sure I needed to be spared someone else's version'), refusing imposed religious structures while crafting personal prayers that claim spiritual self-determination.

author: Kevin A. González
content:
Beginning on a line by Silvio Rodríguez



How will it taste—the beer the gravedigger will drink after bestowing your dirt coat? What will he say—you keeled the outrigger too south, & when the breakers rolled, no boats heard your Mayday? & will he ask his friends at the bar—if someone calls a Mayday & there is no one at the other end of any radio, did Kevin A. González really exist? O second person, what would you do without you? Where would Kevin A. González hide? Our bond is over. The red of the rockets’ glare has faded. Your grave has been dug. Gone too are the days when I tried to speak through you.
evidence: The speaker reflects with metacognitive awareness on the psychological function of second-person address itself ('O second person, what would you do without you? Where would Kevin A. González hide?'), observing their own defensive use of 'you' as a distancing mechanism ('Gone too are the days when I tried to speak through you').

author: Jody Gladding
content:
We'd  like  to  talk  with  you  about  fear they  said  so
many  people  live  in  fear  these  days  they  drove  up
all  four  of  them  in  a  small  car nice   boy  they  said
beautiful  dogs they  said  so  friendly  the  man  ahead
of  the  woman  the other  two  waiting  in  the  drive  I
was  outside digging up the garden no one home I said
what   are  you  selling   anyway  I'm   not  interested  I
said  well  you  have  a  nice  day  they  said  here's  our
card  there's   a  phone  number  you  can  call  anytime
any  other   houses  down  this  road  anyone  else   live
here  we'd  like  to  talk  to  them  about  living  in  fear
evidence: The speaker sets clear boundaries with strangers who arrive uninvited ('no one home I said... I'm not interested I said'), claiming autonomous space and directly refusing unwanted interaction without guilt or apology despite their persistence. The narrative presents this boundary-setting as appropriate self-protection rather than aggression.

author: Gwendolyn Brooks
content:
They eat beans mostly, this old yellow pair.   
Dinner is a casual affair.
Plain chipware on a plain and creaking wood,   
Tin flatware.
Two who are Mostly Good.
Two who have lived their day,
But keep on putting on their clothes   
And putting things away.
And remembering ...
Remembering, with twinklings and twinges,
As they lean over the beans in their rented back room that is full of beads and receipts and dolls and cloths, tobacco crumbs, vases and fringes.
evidence: The poem adopts an external, documentarian stance toward its subjects ('They eat beans mostly, this old yellow pair'), presenting their material poverty and accumulated life without psychological defensiveness—it is descriptive portraiture rather than a defensive operation by the speaker.

author: Hailey Leithauser
content:
Philosophic
in its complex, ovoid emptiness,
a skillful pundit coined it as a sort
    of stopgap doorstop for those
           quaint equations           Romans never
dreamt of. In form completely clever
and discrete—a mirror come unsilvered,     loose watch face without the works,              a hollowed globe            from tip to toe
unbroken, it evades the grappling
hooks of mass, tilts the thin rim of no thing,     remains embryonic sum,             non-cogito.
evidence: The speaker delivers a lengthy, philosophically abstract discourse on the concept of zero using detached, theoretical language ('Philosophic in its complex, ovoid emptiness,' 'non-cogito') that treats what could be emotionally resonant material (emptiness, nothingness, absence) as purely intellectual puzzle-solving without affective engagement.

author: Jody Gladding
content:
We'd  like  to  talk  with  you  about  fear they  said  so
many  people  live  in  fear  these  days  they  drove  up
all  four  of  them  in  a  small  car nice   boy  they  said
beautiful  dogs they  said  so  friendly  the  man  ahead
of  the  woman  the other  two  waiting  in  the  drive  I
was  outside digging up the garden no one home I said
what   are  you  selling   anyway  I'm   not  interested  I
said  well  you  have  a  nice  day  they  said  here's  our
card  there's   a  phone  number  you  can  call  anytime
any  other   houses  down  this  road  anyone  else   live
here  we'd  like  to  talk  to  them  about  living  in  fear
evidence: The speaker directly refuses unwanted solicitation with clear boundary-setting ('what are you selling anyway I'm not interested I said'), owning their refusal without apology while simultaneously exposing the absurd irony of strangers arriving to 'talk about fear' then asking intrusive questions. The self-assertion maintains relational authenticity without aggression, while the wry framing of the card '1-800-FEAR' and the recursive last line create dark humor that holds both the threat and the speaker's composure in view.

author: Hailey Leithauser
content:
Philosophic
in its complex, ovoid emptiness,
a skillful pundit coined it as a sort
    of stopgap doorstop for those
           quaint equations           Romans never
dreamt of. In form completely clever
and discrete—a mirror come unsilvered,     loose watch face without the works,              a hollowed globe            from tip to toe
unbroken, it evades the grappling
hooks of mass, tilts the thin rim of no thing,     remains embryonic sum,             non-cogito.
evidence: The speaker delivers an emotionally evacuated lecture on zero using abstract philosophical diction ('ovoid emptiness,' 'non-cogito') and technical imagery ('mirror come unsilvered,' 'loose watch face without the works'), maintaining detached, theoretical distance while discussing what is fundamentally a void—the affect-stripped meditation reads like a conceptual weather report on nothingness itself.
